# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of IFRS, US GAAP, and Ind AS

This comparative table is **extremely high-yield** for KTU questions on "Differences between GAAP systems."

| Parameter | **IFRS** (International) | **US GAAP** (American) | **Ind AS** (Indian) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Issuing Body** | IASB (London) | FASB (Norwalk, CT, USA) | MCA, on ICAI recommendation |
| **Principle-Based vs Rule-Based** | **Principle-based** (broad guidance) | **Rule-based** (extensive rules) | **Principle-based** (converged with IFRS) |
| **Inventory Valuation** | LIFO **NOT allowed** | LIFO permitted | LIFO **NOT allowed** (lower of cost & NRV) |
| **R&D Treatment** | Development costs → Capitalized | R&D typically expensed | Development costs → Capitalized (if criteria met) |
| **Inventory Measurement** | Lower of cost & NRV | Lower of cost or market | Lower of cost & NRV |
| **Development Costs** | Capitalized if certain criteria met | Generally expensed | Capitalized if criteria met |
| **Lease Accounting** | IFRS 16 — single model (right-of-use) | ASC 842 — dual model | Ind AS 116 — converged with IFRS 16 |
| **Financial Instruments** | IFRS 9 (3 categories) | Complex 5-category model | Ind AS 109 (converged with IFRS 9) |
| **Revenue Recognition** | IFRS 15 — 5-step model | ASC 606 — 5-step model | Ind AS 115 — 5-step model |
| **Number of Standards** | ~17 IFRS + ~28 IAS | ~90+ ASC topics | ~40 Ind AS (converged) |
| **Use in India** | Not directly applicable | Not applicable | **Mandatory** for specified companies |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Ind AS" with "Indian GAAP (AS)." Remember: 
> - **Old regime:** Companies Accounting Standards Rules, 2006 → **AS** series
> - **New regime:** Ind AS notified by MCA → **Ind AS** series
> - The "convergence" is in progress; some companies still follow the older AS, but most large and listed entities have migrated to Ind AS.

---

## 3.2 Derivations: Working Out GAAP-Based Calculations

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the Accounting Equation

The **Dual Aspect Principle** is the foundation of double-entry bookkeeping. Let us derive the basic accounting equation step-by-step:

**Step 1:** A business has assets (resources owned). These assets are financed by:
- **External Sources** (Creditors) → Called **Liabilities**
- **Internal Sources** (Owners) → Called **Capital / Owner's Equity**

**Step 2:** Therefore, at any point in time:

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Claims of Creditors} + \text{Claims of Owners}$$

**Step 3:** In equation form:

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$$

**Step 4:** Recognizing the effects of business operations (Revenue and Expenses):

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital} + \text{Revenue} - \text{Expenses}$$

**Step 5:** Profits increase capital; losses/drawings decrease capital:

$$\text{Net Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Expenses}$$

**Step 6:** The final expanded form:

$$\boxed{\text{Assets} + \text{Expenses} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital} + \text{Revenue} - \text{Drawings}}$$

---

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example — Application of GAAP Principles

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
> A startup was incorporated on 1st April 2023. The following transactions occurred during the first month:
>
> (i) Owners invested ₹10,00,000 cash.
> (ii) Purchased machinery for ₹6,00,000 (paid 30% cash, balance on credit).
> (iii) Purchased raw material ₹2,00,000 on credit.
> (iv) Sold goods for ₹3,50,000 cash (cost of goods sold: ₹2,00,000).
> (v) Paid salaries of ₹50,000.
> (vi) Owner withdrew ₹20,000 for personal use.
> (vii) Outstanding rent for the month: ₹10,000 (not yet paid).
>
> **Required:** Compute (a) Total Assets, (b) Total Liabilities, (c) Net Profit, and (d) Closing Capital, applying GAAP principles.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the **Business Entity Principle** to separate owner's transactions from the business.
- Owners' personal use (transaction vi: ₹20,000) is recorded as **Drawings**, not a business expense.

**Step 2:** Apply the **Accrual Principle** and **Matching Principle** to recognize the **Outstanding Rent (₹10,000)** as an expense for April 2023, even though not yet paid.

**Step 3:** Apply the **Historical Cost Principle** — Machinery is recorded at ₹6,00,000 (purchase cost), not its market value.

**Step 4:** Apply the **Going Concern Principle** — Machinery is depreciated over useful life, not recorded at scrap value.

**Step 5:** Calculate Revenue, Expenses, and Net Profit:

$$\text{Revenue} = 3,50,000 \text{ (Sales)}$$

$$\text{Expenses} = \text{Cost of Goods Sold} + \text{Salaries} + \text{Rent} = 2,00,000 + 50,000 + 10,000$$

$$\text{Total Expenses} = 2,60,000$$

$$\text{Net Profit} = 3,50,000 - 2,60,000 = 90,000$$

**Step 6:** Compute Total Assets using the accounting equation:

| Asset | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Cash (10,00,000 - 1,80,000 - 2,00,000 - 50,000 - 20,000 + 3,50,000) | 9,00,000 |
| Machinery (Historical Cost) | 6,00,000 |
| Raw Material (Stock) | 2,00,000 |
| **Total Assets** | **17,00,000** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cash Calculation Verification:**
> +10,00,000 (capital) − 1,80,000 (30% of machinery) − 0 (raw material on credit) − 50,000 (salaries) − 20,000 (drawings) + 3,50,000 (sales receipt) = **9,00,000** ✓

**Step 7:** Compute Total Liabilities:

| Liability | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Creditors for Machinery (70% of 6,00,000) | 4,20,000 |
| Creditors for Raw Material | 2,00,000 |
| Outstanding Rent | 10,000 |
| **Total Liabilities** | **6,30,000** |

**Step 8:** Compute Closing Capital:

$$\text{Closing Capital} = \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Drawings}$$

$$\text{Closing Capital} = 10,00,000 + 90,000 - 20,000 = 10,70,000$$

**Step 9:** Verify Accounting Equation:

$$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$$

$$17,00,000 = 6,30,000 + 10,70,000$$

$$17,00,000 = 17,00,000 \quad \checkmark$$

**The Accounting Equation balances — confirming the Dual Aspect Principle.**

---

### 3.2.3 Revenue Recognition — Application of Percentage of Completion Method

**Problem:**
> A construction company has a 3-year contract worth ₹30,00,000. Estimated cost = ₹24,00,000. 
> - Year 1: Cost incurred = ₹4,80,000 
> - Year 2: Cost incurred = ₹9,60,000 
> - Year 3: Cost incurred = ₹9,60,000
>
> **Required:** Compute revenue to be recognized each year using the Percentage of Completion Method (as per AS-7 / Ind AS 11 principles).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate percentage of completion each year:

$$\text{% Completion (Year 1)} = \frac{4,80,000}{24,00,000} \times 100 = 20\%$$

$$\text{% Completion (Year 2)} = \frac{4,80,000 + 9,60,000}{24,00,000} \times 100 = 60\%$$

$$\text{% Completion (Year 3)} = \frac{4,80,000 + 9,60,000 + 9,60,000}{24,00,000} \times 100 = 100\%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate cumulative revenue (Applying Revenue Recognition Principle over the contract life):

$$\text{Cumulative Revenue (Year 1)} = 30,00,000 \times 20\% = 6,00,000$$

$$\text{Cumulative Revenue (Year 2)} = 30,00,000 \times 60\% = 18,00,000$$

$$\text{Cumulative Revenue (Year 3)} = 30,00,000 \times 100\% = 30,00,000$$

**Step 3:** Calculate year-wise revenue (GAAP's Matching Principle — revenue in the period it is earned):

| Year | Cumulative Revenue (₹) | Previous Year's Revenue (₹) | Current Year Revenue (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6,00,000 | 0 | **6,00,000** |
| 2 | 18,00,000 | 6,00,000 | **12,00,000** |
| 3 | 30,00,000 | 18,00,000 | **12,00,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Percentage of Completion?** Under the **Matching Principle**, the revenue of a 3-year project must be matched with the costs incurred in those 3 years — NOT recognized entirely in Year 1 (when work begins) or Year 3 (when delivery happens). This is GAAP's interpretation of "effort and reward over time."

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary — When Each Principle Applies

| GAAP Principle | Triggered When... | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Going Concern | Preparing regular financial statements | Misclassification of assets/liabilities |
| Historical Cost | Recording asset acquisition | Over/understatement of asset values |
| Matching | Preparing the Profit & Loss Account | Distorted profit figures |
| Revenue Recognition | Recording sales | Premature or delayed profit recognition |
| Prudence | Identifying potential losses | Overstatement of assets and profits |
| Materiality | Deciding disclosure detail | Information overload or omission |
| Consistency | Comparing multi-year financials | Inconsistency in trend analysis |
| Substance Over Form | Sale-and-leaseback, securitization, etc. | Misleading financial position |
| Full Disclosure | Notes to accounts, contingencies | Hidden risks |
| Accrual | All regular transactions | Cash-based distortion |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Accounting Authorities in India

This diagram shows the formal regulatory hierarchy that KTU students must understand.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PARLIAMENT OF INDIA"] --> nodeB["COMPANIES ACT 2013"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MINISTRY OF CORPORATE AFFAIRS MCA"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["NATIONAL FINANCIAL REPORTING AUTHORITY NFRA"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["NOTIFIED INDIAN ACCOUNTING STANDARDS IND AS"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["ENFORCEMENT AND QUALITY REVIEW"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["ACCOUNTING STANDARDS BOARD ASB UNDER ICAI"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DRAFTING OF IND AS AND AS STANDARDS"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ICAI COUNCIL APPROVAL"]
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeJ["APPLIED BY REPORTING COMPANIES"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["AUDITED BY CHARTERED ACCOUNTANTS"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["FINANCIAL STATEMENTS PUBLISHED"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["STAKEHOLDERS INVESTORS LENDERS GOVT PUBLIC"]
    nodeC --> nodeN["SEBI RBI IRDA SECTORAL REGULATORS"]
    nodeN --> nodeJ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Parliament** enacts the **Companies Act 2013**, the supreme source of corporate law in India.
> - **MCA (Ministry of Corporate Affairs)** is the nodal ministry that notifies accounting standards.
> - **NFRA** is the enforcement body that oversees accounting and auditing quality.
> - **ICAI's ASB (Accounting Standards Board)** is the technical drafting body for Ind AS / AS.
> - **SEBI, RBI, IRDA** add sector-specific reporting requirements.
> - The cycle ends with **financial statements reaching stakeholders**.

## 4.4 GAAP vs IFRS vs Ind AS — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeGlobal["IASB BASED IN LONDON"] --> nodeIFRS["IFRS INTERNATIONAL FINANCIAL REPORTING STANDARDS"]
    nodeIFRS --> nodeConverge["CONVERGENCE FRAMEWORK POST 2007"]
    nodeConverge --> nodeIndAS["IND AS INDIAN ACCOUNTING STANDARDS"]
    nodeConverge --> nodeIFRS15["IFRS 15 REVENUE"]
    nodeConverge --> nodeIFRS16["IFRS 16 LEASES"]
    nodeIndAS --> node115["IND AS 115 REVENUE"]
    nodeIndAS --> node116["IND AS 116 LEASES"]
    nodeConverge --> nodeIFRS9["IFRS 9 FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS"]
    nodeIndAS --> node109["IND AS 109 FINANCIAL INSTRUMENTS"]
    nodeUS["FASB BASED IN USA"] --> nodeUSGAAP["US GAAP GENERALLY ACCEPTED ACCOUNTING PRINCIPLES USA"]
    nodeUSGAAP --> nodeASC606["ASC 606 REVENUE"]
    nodeUSGAAP --> nodeASC842["ASC 842 LEASES"]
    nodeIFRS -.->|DIFFERENCES| nodeUSGAAP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:**
> - **IFRS** (international) and **Ind AS** (Indian) are largely **converged** after 2007.
> - **US GAAP** remains a **separate, more rule-based** system, with similar but technically different standards (e.g., ASC 606 ≈ IFRS 15 ≈ Ind AS 115).
> - Indian companies listing on US stock exchanges sometimes need to prepare **dual reports** (Ind AS + US GAAP) for compliance.
