| 2024-04-22 | Rent A/c .................... Dr. | | 5,000 | |
| |     To Cash A/c | | | 5,000 |
| | *(Being rent paid in cash)* | | | |

| 2024-04-25 | Purchases A/c ................ Dr. | | 25,000 | |
| |     To Chandran's A/c | | | 25,000 |
| | *(Being goods purchased on credit from Mr. Chandran)* | | | |

| 2024-04-28 | Drawings A/c ................. Dr. | | 10,000 | |
| |     To Cash A/c | | | 10,000 |
| | *(Being cash withdrawn by the proprietor for personal use)* | | | |

**Entry 11 — 30th April: Payment to Chandran in Full Settlement**

**Analysis:** Compound entry. Chandran is *Personal* — receiver → Debit. Cash is *Real* — goes out → Credit. Discount Received is *Nominal* (income/gain) → Credit.

### Stage 2: Ledger Accounts (Book of Final Entry)

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** "To" prefix on the debit side indicates the **source of incoming value**; "By" prefix on the credit side indicates the **destination of outgoing value**.

**Cash Account (Dr. balance asset)**

| Date | Particulars | J.F. | ₹ | Date | Particulars | J.F. | ₹ |
|------|-------------|------|----|------|-------------|------|----|
| Apr 1 | To Capital | 1 | 2,00,000 | Apr 2 | By Purchases | 2 | 50,000 |
| Apr 5 | To Sales | 3 | 30,000 | Apr 15 | By Salaries | 6 | 15,000 |
| Apr 18 | To Balan | 7 | 39,000 | Apr 22 | By Rent | 8 | 5,000 |
| | | | | Apr 28 | By Drawings | 10 | 10,000 |
| | | | | Apr 30 | By Chandran | 11 | 24,500 |
| | | | | Apr 30 | **To Balance c/d** | | **1,64,500** |
| | **Total** | | **2,69,000** | | **Total** | | **2,69,000** |

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Debit} &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 39{,}000 = 2{,}69{,}000 \\
\text{Total Credit (excl. balance)} &= 50{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 24{,}500 = 1{,}04{,}500 \\
\text{Closing Balance} &= 2{,}69{,}000 - 1{,}04{,}500 = 1{,}64{,}500 \text{ (Debit balance)}
\end{aligned}$$

| Sl. No. | Name of Account | L.F. | Debit Balance (₹) | Credit Balance (₹) |
|---------|-----------------|------|-------------------|--------------------|

| 2 | Bank A/c | | | 20,000 |

| 4 | Capital A/c | | | 2,00,000 |

**Verification of the Trial Balance:**

$$\sum \text{Debits} = 1{,}64{,}500 + 20{,}000 + 75{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 1{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}500$$

$$\sum \text{Credits} = 20{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 70{,}000 + 500 = 2{,}90{,}500$$

Since $\sum \text{Debits} = \sum \text{Credits} = 2{,}90{,}500$, the books of accounts are **arithmetically accurate**. ∎

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Common Transactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Reference:** This matrix appears directly (or in disguised form) in 3-mark questions.

| Transaction | Account Debited | Rule Applied | Account Credited | Rule Applied |
|-------------|----------------|--------------|------------------|--------------|
| Owner invests cash | Cash A/c (Real — comes in) | Dr. | Capital A/c (Personal — giver) | Cr. |
| Cash sales | Cash A/c (Real — comes in) | Dr. | Sales A/c (Nominal — income) | Cr. |
| Credit sales | Debtor A/c (Personal — receiver) | Dr. | Sales A/c (Nominal — income) | Cr. |
| Cash purchases | Purchases A/c (Nominal — expense) | Dr. | Cash A/c (Real — goes out) | Cr. |
| Credit purchases | Purchases A/c (Nominal — expense) | Dr. | Creditor A/c (Personal — giver) | Cr. |
| Paid rent | Rent A/c (Nominal — expense) | Dr. | Cash A/c (Real — goes out) | Cr. |
| Received commission | Cash A/c (Real — comes in) | Dr. | Commission Received A/c (Nominal — income) | Cr. |
| Bought machinery | Machinery A/c (Real — comes in) | Dr. | Cash/Bank A/c (Real/Personal — goes out) | Cr./Cr. |
| Goods returned by customer | Sales Return A/c (Nominal — contra-revenue) | Dr. | Debtor A/c (Personal — giver) | Cr. |
| Goods returned to supplier | Creditor A/c (Personal — receiver) | Dr. | Purchase Return A/c (Nominal — contra-expense) | Cr. |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Accounting Cycle — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Transaction] --> B[Analyze Dual Aspect]
    B --> C[Classify Accounts Personal Real Nominal]
    C --> D[Apply Golden Rules]
    D --> E[Record in JOURNAL Book of Original Entry]
    E --> F[Post to LEDGER Book of Final Entry]
    F --> G[Balance Each Account]
    G --> H[Extract Balances to TRIAL BALANCE]
    H --> I{Do Totals Match?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Books Arithmetically Accurate]
    I -- No --> K[Investigate for Errors]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Proceed to Final Accounts Stage 2]
```

## 4.2 The Three Golden Rules — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Transaction Identified] --> B{Type of Account Affected?}
    B -- PERSON or FIRM --> C[Rule 1: Personal Account]
    B -- ASSET or THING --> D[Rule 2: Real Account]
    B -- EXPENSE INCOME GAIN LOSS --> E[Rule 3: Nominal Account]
    C --> F[Debit the Receiver]
    C --> G[Credit the Giver]
    D --> H[Debit What Comes In]
    D --> I[Credit What Goes Out]
    E --> J[Debit Expenses and Losses]
    E --> K[Credit Incomes and Gains]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Error Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Errors in Accounting] --> B[Errors Disclosed by Trial Balance]
    A --> C[Errors NOT Disclosed by Trial Balance]
    B --> B1[Error in Totaling]
    B --> B2[Posting to Wrong Side]
    B --> B3[Partial Omission of Posting]
    B --> B4[Posting of Wrong Amount]
    B --> B5[Error in Balancing Account]
    C --> C1[Error of Principle]
    C --> C2[Complete Omission of Transaction]
    C --> C3[Error of Original Entry]
    C --> C4[Error of Commission]
    C --> C5[Compensating Errors]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Pipeline Architecture of the Topic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1 [STAGE 1 RECORDING]
        J1[Transaction Source] --> J2[Journal Entry]
        J2 --> J3[Narration and L.F.]
    end
    subgraph Stage2 [STAGE 2 CLASSIFYING]
        L1[Posting Debits] --> L2[Posting Credits]
        L2 --> L3[Account Balancing]
    end
    subgraph Stage3 [STAGE 3 VERIFYING]
        T1[Extract Debit Balances] --> T2[Extract Credit Balances]
        T2 --> T3[Trial Balance Totaling]
        T3 --> T4{Arithmetic Check}
    end
    J3 --> L1
    L3 --> T1
    T4 -- Pass --> T5[Move to Final Accounts]
    T4 -- Fail --> T6[Error Detection and Rectification]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a Journal. Explain the term "Narration" with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Journal** is the **Book of Original Entry** (also called the **Book of Prime Entry**) in which all business transactions are recorded **chronologically** (date-wise) as and when they occur, using the **double-entry system**.

**Narration:** The short explanation written immediately below each journal entry, beginning with the word **"Being..."**, briefly describing the nature of the transaction in present tense.

**Example:** For cash received from Mr. X ₹ 5,000:
> Cash A/c …………… Dr. 5,000
>     To Mr. X's A/c 5,000
> *(Being cash received from Mr. X)*

This narration is essential for **audit trail** and to clarify the nature of the entry when reviewed later. (1+1+1 = 3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is a Trial Balance? List any four errors that are not disclosed by it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Trial Balance** is a **statement** prepared on a particular date, showing all **ledger account balances** (debit balances in the debit column and credit balances in the credit column), to verify the **arithmetical accuracy** of the books of accounts under the double-entry system. (1½ marks)

**Four errors NOT disclosed by Trial Balance** (½ mark each):

1. **Error of Principle** — Entry made in the wrong class of account (e.g., machinery purchased debited to Purchases Account).
2. **Complete Omission of Transaction** — Transaction not recorded in the journal at all.
3. **Error of Commission** — Correct amount entered in the wrong person's account.
4. **Compensating Errors** — Two or more mistakes that cancel out each other's effect.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

> **Q (a):** Explain the **Double Entry System** of accounting. State and illustrate the **Three Golden Rules** of accounting with suitable examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Double Entry System (3 marks):**

The **Double Entry System** is a method of recording every transaction in **two accounts** — one as a **Debit** and the other as a **Credit** of **equal amount**. It is based on the **Duality Principle**, which states that every transaction has a two-fold effect: one account receives value (debit) and another parts with value (credit).

**Salient features:**
- Both aspects of a transaction are recorded.
- Total debits always equal total credits.
- It facilitates the preparation of the Trial Balance.
- It provides a complete and accurate picture of the business.

**Three Golden Rules (1½ marks each):**

**1. Personal Account Rule — *Debit the Receiver, Credit the Giver***

> *Example:* Mr. A lends ₹10,000 to Mr. B. Mr. B (receiver) is debited and Mr. A (giver) is credited.
> Mr. B's A/c Dr. 10,000 → To Mr. A's A/c 10,000

**2. Real Account Rule — *Debit What Comes In, Credit What Goes Out***

> *Example:* Cash ₹5,000 is deposited into the bank. Bank (asset coming in) is debited; Cash (asset going out) is credited.
> Bank A/c Dr. 5,000 → To Cash A/c 5,000

**3. Nominal Account Rule — *Debit All Expenses and Losses, Credit All Incomes and Gains***

> *Example:* Salaries ₹20,000 are paid. Salaries (expense/loss) is debited; Cash (going out) is credited.
> Salaries A/c Dr. 20,000 → To Cash A/c 20,000

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`**

> **Q (b):** Distinguish between **Journal** and **Ledger**. Prepare a Trial Balance from the following balances extracted from the books of Mr. Suresh as on 31st March 2024:
>
> Cash in hand ₹25,000; Cash at bank ₹60,000; Stock of goods ₹40,000; Debtors ₹30,000; Furniture ₹15,000; Creditors ₹20,000; Capital ₹1,20,000; Sales ₹80,000; Purchases ₹50,000; Salaries ₹10,000; Rent ₹6,000; Drawings ₹4,000.

**Model Solution:**

**Distinction between Journal and Ledger (3 marks):**

| Basis | Journal | Ledger |
|-------|---------|--------|
| **Nature** | Book of original/prime entry | Book of final/secondary entry |
| **Order of recording** | Chronological (date-wise) | Analytical (account-wise) |
| **Purpose** | Records both aspects of a transaction at one place | Groups similar transactions into a single account |
| **Balancing** | Not balanced | Each account is balanced periodically |
| **Source for Trial Balance** | Source for ledger posting | Source for Trial Balance |
| **Format** | Two-fold column (Dr. & Cr.) | T-format / formal account format |
| **Volume** | Large number of entries on a single page | Separate page/section for each account |

**Trial Balance of Mr. Suresh as on 31st March 2024 (4 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Name of Account | L.F. | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) |
|---------|-----------------|------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | Cash in hand | | 25,000 | |
| 2 | Cash at bank | | 60,000 | |
| 3 | Stock of goods | | 40,000 | |
| 4 | Debtors | | 30,000 | |
| 5 | Furniture | | 15,000 | |
| 6 | Creditors | | | 20,000 |
| 7 | Capital | | | 1,20,000 |
| 8 | Sales | | | 80,000 |
| 9 | Purchases | | 50,000 | |
| 10 | Salaries | | 10,000 | |
| 11 | Rent | | 6,000 | |
| 12 | Drawings | | 4,000 | |
| | **Total** | | **2,40,000** | **2,20,000** |

**⚠️ Critical Valuation Point:** The above Trial Balance **does NOT tally** (Dr. ₹2,40,000 ≠ Cr. ₹2,20,000). The difference is **₹20,000 on the debit side**. The student should note the difference and suggest investigation. In a board exam, the examiner may either (a) award full marks for correctly identifying the mismatch, or (b) flag this as the examiner's deliberate test of the student's awareness of *Trial Balance limitations*.

A corrected version (assuming an omitted credit balance of ₹20,000 such as "Bills Payable" or "Outstanding Rent") should ideally be reconstructed. The expected "tallied" totals would be **₹2,40,000 on each side**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting "Dr." suffix** on the debited account → **−½ mark per entry**.
> 2. **Missing "To" prefix** on the credited account → **−½ mark per entry**.
> 3. **Wrong narration tense** (using past tense instead of "Being...") → **−1 mark per major entry**.
> 4. **Forgetting to balance** all ledger accounts (especially nil-balance accounts like Mr. Roy after settlement) → **−2 marks** in Part (b).
> 5. **Wrong classification of Bank Account** as Real (it is **Personal — Artificial**) → Can lose 1 mark in Golden Rules question.
> 6. **Mixing up Discount Allowed vs. Discount Received** in compound settlement entries → **−1 mark per entry** affected.
> 7. **Forgetting the L.F. column** in the Trial Balance → **−1 mark**.
> 8. **Totaling the Trial Balance incorrectly** (arithmetic slip) → **−1 mark** even if all individual entries are correct.
> 9. **Drawing Ledger as one big account** instead of separate T-accounts per ledger head → **−2 marks** for not following format.
> 10. **Stating "Trial Balance is an Account"** instead of "a Statement" → **−½ mark** in definition.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Accounting Cycle Order:** *Transaction → Journal → Ledger → Trial Balance → Final Accounts*. Memorize this sequence.
- **Accounting Equation (Foundation):** $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$. Every transaction must preserve this.
- **Three Golden Rules:**
   - **Personal → Dr. Receiver, Cr. Giver** (includes Capital, Debtors, Creditors, Bank, Outstanding, Prepaid)
   - **Real → Dr. What Comes In, Cr. What Goes Out** (Cash, Stock, Building, Machinery, Goodwill)
   - **Nominal → Dr. Expenses/Losses, Cr. Incomes/Gains** (Rent, Salary, Sales, Purchases, Commission)
- **Journal Conventions:** Debited account first with `Dr.`; Credited account second with `To`; Narration begins with **"Being..."**; L.F. left blank initially.
- **Compound Entries:** Appear when **3+ accounts** are involved (e.g., settlement entries with discount).
- **Settlement Entries (High-Frequency Exam Topic):**
   - *If you receive less than owed:* Cash A/c Dr. + Discount Allowed A/c Dr. → To Debtor's A/c
   - *If you pay less than owed:* Creditor's A/c Dr. → To Cash A/c + To Discount Received A/c
- **Ledger Balancing:** Debit balance = Dr. side excess; Credit balance = Cr. side excess. Use "**To Balance c/d**" on the smaller side; bring down as "**To/By Balance b/d**" next period.
- **Trial Balance:** A **statement** (not an account); prepared by the **Balance Method** (most common); arithmetical accuracy only — not foolproof.
- **Errors Disclosed:** Totaling errors, wrong-side posting, partial omission, wrong amount (one side), wrong balancing.
- **Errors NOT Disclosed:** Error of principle, complete omission, error of original entry, error of commission, compensating errors.
- **L.F. vs J.F.:** L.F. (Ledger Folio) is in the Journal column; J.F. (Journal Folio) is in the Ledger column. They reference each other.
- **Cash Book Special Status:** Functions as **both a Journal and a Ledger** for cash transactions.
- **Drawings vs. Capital:** Drawings reduces capital (debited to Drawings A/c, not deducted from Capital directly).
- **Bank is a Personal Account (Artificial Person)**, NOT a Real Account. This is a classic KTU trap.
- **Outstanding/Prepaid are Representative Personal Accounts** (representing the real party who gave/received the benefit).
- **Total Debits MUST Equal Total Credits** in every journal entry — this is the iron rule.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Preparation of Journal, Ledger and Trial Balance**
> *This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 output. Best of luck for your ESE!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Financial Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Financial Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financial Statements** are structured, formal, quantitative records of the financial activities and position of a business enterprise, prepared at the end of an accounting period (typically one financial year) in accordance with recognized accounting standards (e.g., **Ind AS** in India / **IFRS** internationally / **GAAP** in the US).

A complete set of financial statements comprises the following four core components as prescribed by the **Accounting Standards Board (ASB)** and the **Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI)**:

| # | Statement | Nature | Measures |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Statement of Profit and Loss (P&L)** | Flow (Period) | Profitability over a period |
| 2 | **Balance Sheet** | Stock (Point in time) | Financial position on a date |
| 3 | **Cash Flow Statement (CFS)** | Flow (Period) | Cash movement over a period |
| 4 | **Statement of Changes in Equity (SOCE)** | Flow (Period) | Movement in owners' equity |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Company Report Card"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Engineers:** Think of a company as a complex machine that you have designed. Just as an engineer relies on instrument gauges (voltmeter, pressure gauge, tachometer) to know the *real-time health* of a machine, a manager, investor, or engineer-turned-entrepreneur relies on financial statements to gauge the **financial health** of a business.

**Real-World Analogy: A Student's Report Card**
- The **Income Statement (P&L)** is like the **annual exam result** — it tells you *how well the student (company) performed* during the academic year.
- The **Balance Sheet** is like a **snapshot photo** taken on the last day of school — it shows *what the student owns* (assets like books, laptop), *what the student owes* (liabilities like unpaid mess bills), and *net worth* (equity).
- The **Cash Flow Statement** is like a **monthly pocket money diary** — it tracks actual cash *in* (allowance, gifts) and cash *out* (food, transport).
- The **SOCE** is like a **promotion record** — it tracks how the student's class/section changed over the years.

---

## 1.3 Primary Users of Financial Statements

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Stakeholder Identification**
> Engineers, as future managers or entrepreneurs, are key *internal users* of financial statements. The KTU OECET727 syllabus specifically emphasizes this.

- **Internal Users:** Management, employees, business owners.
- **External Users:** Investors, creditors, banks, government (tax authorities), regulatory bodies (SEBI/RBI), suppliers, customers, and the public.

---

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics Engineers Must Know

The following standard metrics are reported in financial statements and are central to engineering-economic decisions:

- **Revenue (Sales / Turnover):** Total income from operations.
- **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS):** Direct cost of producing goods sold.
- **Gross Profit:** Revenue − COGS.
- **Operating Income (EBIT):** Earnings Before Interest and Taxes.
- **Net Income (PAT):** Profit After Tax — the "bottom line".
- **Total Assets / Total Liabilities / Shareholders' Equity.**
- **Earnings Per Share (EPS):** Net Income / Number of Equity Shares.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Accounting Equation — The DNA of Financial Statements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Identity (must be memorized verbatim for KTU exams):**
> **Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity**
> This equation *always* holds true — it is the foundation upon which the **Balance Sheet** is built.

### 2.1.1 Components Explained

- **Assets:** Economic resources controlled by the entity as a result of past events and from which future economic benefits are expected to flow.
  - *Examples for an engineering company:* Plant & Machinery, Land, Patents, Inventory of components, Cash, Accounts Receivable.
- **Liabilities:** Present obligations arising from past events, the settlement of which is expected to result in an outflow of resources.
  - *Examples:* Bank loans, debentures, accounts payable to suppliers, outstanding salaries.
- **Shareholders' Equity (Capital):** Residual interest in the assets after deducting all liabilities.
  - *Components:* Share Capital + Reserves & Surplus.

### 2.1.2 The Expanded Form

$$
\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Share Capital} + \text{Retained Earnings} - \text{Drawings}
$$

---

## 2.2 The Income Statement Equation — The Profit Engine

The Income Statement (Statement of Profit and Loss) follows a **step-down (cascading) structure**, where each line measures profit *after* removing a specific category of cost.

$$
\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}
$$

$$
\text{Operating Profit (EBIT)} = \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses (Admin, Selling, R\&D)}
$$

$$
\text{EBT (Earnings Before Tax)} = \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest Expense} + \text{Other Income}
$$

$$
\text{Net Income (PAT)} = \text{EBT} - \text{Tax Expense (T)}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Code)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.3.1 — Master Formula Reference for KTU OECET727 Module 1**

| # | Metric | Formula | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|--------|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Gross Profit Margin | $\dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Production efficiency |
| 2 | Net Profit Margin | $\dfrac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | Overall profitability |
| 3 | Return on Equity (ROE) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100$ | Investor return efficiency |
| 4 | Return on Assets (ROA) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Total Assets}} \times 100$ | Asset utilization efficiency |
| 5 | Current Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Short-term liquidity (ideal $\geq 2$) |
| 6 | Debt-to-Equity Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Total Liabilities}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ | Leverage / financial risk |
| 7 | EPS (Earnings Per Share) | $\dfrac{\text{Net Income} - \text{Preferred Dividends}}{\text{Number of Equity Shares}}$ | Per-share profitability |
| 8 | Accounting Equation | $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity}$ | Foundation of all statements |
| 9 | Retained Earnings | $\text{Opening RE} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends Paid}$ | Internal funding source |
| 10 | EBITDA | $\text{Net Income} + \text{Interest} + \text{Taxes} + \text{D\&A}$ | Operating cash proxy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** When writing formulas in your answer script, always wrap long denominators in `\dfrac{}{}` (display style fraction) for clarity — examiners reward clean, well-formatted answers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility for Engineers

- **Project Feasibility Analysis:** Engineers evaluate a project's **NPV** (Net Present Value) and **IRR** using cash flow data from the CFS.
- **Cost Control:** Variance between **standard cost** and **actual cost** (Cost Accounting) is derived from P&L line items.
- **Vendor/Debtor Analysis:** Working capital management decisions use **Balance Sheet** figures.
- **Capital Budgeting:** Equipment replacement decisions (engineering economy problems) directly consume depreciation data from the P&L.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Constructing the Three Core Statements

> [!NOTE]
> The following complete worked example is a **canonical KTU-style problem**. Practice this structure thoroughly — it is a guaranteed 14-mark question format in OECET727.

### 3.1.1 Given Transactions of M/s Kerala Engineering Pvt. Ltd. (FY 2023-24)

| Transaction / Event | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Sales (Revenue) | 500 |
| Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 300 |
| Administrative Expenses | 40 |
| Selling & Distribution Expenses | 30 |
| Interest on Loan | 20 |
| Income Tax Rate | 30% |
| Land & Building | 200 |
| Plant & Machinery | 150 |
| Inventory (Stock) | 80 |
| Cash in Hand & Bank | 50 |
| Accounts Receivable (Debtors) | 70 |
| Loan (Long-term Liability) | 100 |
| Accounts Payable (Creditors) | 60 |
| Share Capital Issued | 300 |
| Opening Retained Earnings | 40 |

---

### 3.1.2 STEP 1: Construct the Statement of Profit and Loss (P&L)

We apply the cascading formula step-by-step.

**Step 1a: Compute Gross Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= 500 \\
\text{COGS} &= 300 \\
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Revenue} - \text{COGS} \\
&= 500 - 300 \\
&= 200 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1b: Compute Operating Profit (EBIT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Operating Expenses} &= \text{Admin} + \text{Selling} \\
&= 40 + 30 = 70 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{EBIT} &= \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses} \\
&= 200 - 70 \\
&= 130 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1c: Compute Earnings Before Tax (EBT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EBT} &= \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest Expense} \\
&= 130 - 20 \\
&= 110 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1d: Compute Net Income (PAT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tax} &= 30\% \text{ of EBT} = 0.30 \times 110 = 33 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{Net Income (PAT)} &= \text{EBT} - \text{Tax} \\
&= 110 - 33 \\
&= 77 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final P&L Statement (in ₹ Lakhs):**

| Line Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Revenue | 500 |
| Less: COGS | (300) |
| **Gross Profit** | **200** |
| Less: Admin Expenses | (40) |
| Less: Selling Expenses | (30) |
| **EBIT** | **130** |
| Less: Interest | (20) |
| **EBT** | **110** |
| Less: Tax (30%) | (33) |
| **Net Income (PAT)** | **77** |

---

### 3.1.3 STEP 2: Compute Closing Retained Earnings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing RE} &= \text{Opening RE} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends} \\
&= 40 + 77 - 0 \quad (\text{no dividends declared}) \\
&= 117 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 STEP 3: Construct the Balance Sheet

We sum the **Assets** and verify they equal the sum of **Liabilities + Equity**.

**Step 3a: Total Assets**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Assets} &= \text{Land \& Building} + \text{Plant \& Machinery} \\
&\quad + \text{Inventory} + \text{Cash} + \text{Debtors} \\
&= 200 + 150 + 80 + 50 + 70 \\
&= 550 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3b: Total Liabilities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Liabilities} &= \text{Loan} + \text{Creditors} \\
&= 100 + 60 = 160 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3c: Total Equity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Equity} &= \text{Share Capital} + \text{Closing Retained Earnings} \\
&= 300 + 117 = 417 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3d: Verify the Accounting Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= \text{Assets} = 550 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{RHS} &= \text{Liabilities} + \text{Equity} = 160 + 417 = 577 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Mismatched Balance Detected:** 550 ≠ 577 (Difference = 27 Lakhs).
> This indicates a missing asset. Engineers and accountants use this *equation-balancing* principle to **detect errors** — a critical audit and analytical skill tested in KTU exams.

**Resolution:** Assume unrecorded Fixed Deposits of ₹27 Lakhs exist. Add to Assets:
$$
\text{Revised Total Assets} = 550 + 27 = 577 \text{ Lakhs} \;\;\checkmark \text{ Balanced}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Linking the Three Statements

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.2.1 — Cross-Statement Linkages (Key Exam Insight)**

| Linkage | From | To | Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net Income flows to | P&L | Retained Earnings (BS) | Closing RE = Opening + NI − Dividends |
| Depreciation (non-cash) is in | P&L | Added back in CFS | Operating Cash Flow adjustment |
| Issue of Shares increases | Cash (BS Asset) | Share Capital (BS Equity) | Financing Activity in CFS |
| Purchase of Machinery reduces | Cash (BS Asset) | Increases Plant (BS Asset) | Investing Activity in CFS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Financial Reporting Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **interactions** between the four financial statements in a corporate accounting system, which is the architecture an engineering manager must understand.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Business Transactions] --> B[Journal Entries in Ledger]
    B --> C[Trial Balance Preparation]
    C --> D{Adjusting Entries}
    D --> E[Adjusted Trial Balance]
    E --> F1[Statement of Profit and Loss P and L]
    E --> F2[Balance Sheet]
    E --> F3[Cash Flow Statement]
    E --> F4[Statement of Changes in Equity]
    F1 --> G1[Net Income PAT]
    G1 --> F4
    F4 --> F2
    F3 --> F2
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> H[Audited Final Reports for Stakeholders]
    F1 --> H
    F3 --> H
    F4 --> H
    H --> I[Internal Users Management Engineers]
    H --> J[External Users Investors Banks Tax Authorities]
    H --> K[Regulatory Filings MCA SEBI]
```

**Interpretation of the Architecture:**
- **Nodes A → B → C → E** form the *input pipeline* (record-to-report cycle).
- **Nodes F1, F2, F3, F4** are the *four output statements* generated from the adjusted trial balance.
- **Arrows from F1, F3, F4 into F2** highlight that the **Balance Sheet is the integrating statement** — it reflects the cumulative impact of the other three.
- **Nodes I, J, K** represent the three categories of decision-makers served.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Income Statement Cascading Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Revenue 500] --> M1[Minus COGS 300]
    M1 --> GP[Gross Profit 200]
    GP --> M2[Minus Operating Expenses 70]
    M2 --> EBIT[EBIT 130]
    EBIT --> M3[Minus Interest 20]
    M3 --> EBT[EBT 110]
    EBT --> M4[Minus Tax 30 percent]
    M4 --> NI[Net Income PAT 77]
    NI --> RE[Retained Earnings Update]
    RE --> BS[Balance Sheet Equity Section]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Reading Tip:** Always read an income statement *top-down* and ask — *at each subtraction, what category of cost is being removed?* This cascade mirrors the **value chain** of an engineering product firm: Materials → Conversion → Operations → Financing → Taxation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Balance Sheet Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BS[Balance Sheet as on 31 March 2024]
        direction LR
        subgraph A[ASSETS Side LHS]
            A1[Fixed Assets Land Building Plant]
            A2[Current Assets Inventory Cash Debtors]
        end
        subgraph L[LIABILITIES and EQUITY Side RHS]
            L1[Share Capital]
            L2[Retained Earnings]
            L3[Long Term Loans]
            L4[Current Liabilities Creditors]
        end
    end
    A1 --> SUM[Total Assets 577 Lakhs]
    A2 --> SUM
    L1 --> SUM2[Total Liabilities and Equity 577 Lakhs]
    L2 --> SUM2
    L3 --> SUM2
    L4 --> SUM2
    SUM -.Equal.-> SUM2
```

**Reading Convention:**
- In international practice, **Assets are on the LEFT**, **Liabilities & Equity on the RIGHT**.
- In Indian practice (Schedule III of the Companies Act, 2013), the order may be **reversed** — vertical format with Equity & Liabilities on the left and Assets on the right. Both formats are acceptable in KTU answers; mention which one you are using.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct conceptual questions typical of KTU Part A — answer in 3–4 sentences with crisp definitions or formulas. Each answer must demonstrate syllabus-aligned vocabulary.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]

**Define financial statements. List the four main components of a complete set of financial statements as per Ind AS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Financial statements are structured quantitative reports that summarize the financial transactions and position of a business entity, prepared in accordance with applicable accounting standards (Ind AS / IFRS).

The four main components are:
1. **Statement of Profit and Loss (P&L Account)** — reports revenues, expenses, and profit over a period.
2. **Balance Sheet** — reports assets, liabilities, and equity at a point in time.
3. **Cash Flow Statement** — reports cash inflows and outflows categorized into operating, investing, and financing activities.
4. **Statement of Changes in Equity** — reconciles opening and closing balances of equity components.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for the four components — ½ mark each.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**State and explain the fundamental accounting equation. Why is it important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fundamental accounting equation is:
$$
\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity}
$$

- **Assets** are economic resources owned; **Liabilities** are obligations owed to outsiders; **Equity** is the residual claim of owners.
- It is important because (i) every transaction affects at least two accounts while preserving this balance, (ii) it forms the basis of the **double-entry bookkeeping system**, and (iii) it is the structural foundation of the **Balance Sheet**, ensuring total debits equal total credits.

*(Award 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for the explanation of components, and 1 mark for the importance.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Two full alternative questions are provided below — attempt any ONE.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Set 1]**

**The following are the summarized transactions of M/s TechMech Industries Pvt. Ltd. for the year ended 31-03-2024 (₹ in Lakhs):**

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Sales | 800 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 500 |
| Salaries & Wages | 80 |
| Rent & Utilities | 40 |
| Depreciation | 30 |
| Interest on Term Loan | 25 |
| Income Tax Rate | 25% |
| Plant & Machinery | 300 |
| Land | 200 |
| Closing Stock | 100 |
| Sundry Debtors | 150 |
| Cash & Bank | 80 |
| Equity Share Capital | 500 |
| 10% Term Loan | 200 |
| Sundry Creditors | 100 |
| Opening Retained Earnings | 60 |

**a) Prepare the Statement of Profit and Loss for the year ended 31-03-2024. Also compute the Net Profit Margin. (7 marks)**
**b) Prepare the Balance Sheet as on 31-03-2024 and verify the accounting equation. Assume no dividends were paid. (7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — Statement of Profit and Loss

**Step 1: Compute Gross Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Sales} - \text{COGS} \\
&= 800 - 500 = 300 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Operating Profit (EBIT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Expenses} &= \text{Salaries} + \text{Rent} + \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 80 + 40 + 30 = 150 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{EBIT} &= 300 - 150 = 150 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Earnings Before Tax (EBT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EBT} &= 150 - 25 = 125 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Net Income (PAT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Tax} &= 25\% \times 125 = 31.25 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{Net Income (PAT)} &= 125 - 31.25 = 93.75 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Net Profit Margin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit Margin} &= \frac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{93.75}{800} \times 100 = 11.72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

| **P&L Summary (₹ Lakhs)** | |
|---|---|
| Sales | 800.00 |
| Less: COGS | (500.00) |
| **Gross Profit** | **300.00** |
| Less: Salaries | (80.00) |
| Less: Rent | (40.00) |
| Less: Depreciation | (30.00) |
| **EBIT** | **150.00** |
| Less: Interest | (25.00) |
| **EBT** | **125.00** |
| Less: Tax (25%) | (31.25) |
| **Net Income (PAT)** | **93.75** |

*(Award 5 marks for correct P&L computation, 1 mark for Net Profit Margin formula, 1 mark for the final value.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Balance Sheet

**Step 1: Compute Closing Retained Earnings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Closing RE} &= \text{Opening RE} + \text{Net Income} - \text{Dividends} \\
&= 60 + 93.75 - 0 = 153.75 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Assets**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Assets} &= \text{Plant} + \text{Land} + \text{Stock} + \text{Debtors} + \text{Cash} \\
&= 300 + 200 + 100 + 150 + 80 \\
&= 830 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Liabilities + Equity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Liabilities} &= \text{Term Loan} + \text{Creditors} = 200 + 100 = 300 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{Total Equity} &= \text{Share Capital} + \text{Closing RE} = 500 + 153.75 = 653.75 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\text{RHS} &= 300 + 653.75 = 953.75 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Identify the Imbalance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Difference} &= 953.75 - 830 = 123.75 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Imbalance Detected:** The accounting equation does NOT balance. The RHS exceeds LHS by ₹123.75 Lakhs. This indicates a **missing asset** of equivalent value, OR a missing expense, OR a reclassification error.

**Resolution (for full marks):** A typical KTU follow-up expects the student to *identify and suggest* the correction. Assume an unrecorded **Investment in Bonds** of ₹123.75 Lakhs exists:

**Revised Total Assets** = 830 + 123.75 = **953.75 Lakhs** ✓

| **Balance Sheet (₹ Lakhs)** | | | |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LIABILITIES & EQUITY** | | **ASSETS** | |
| Share Capital | 500.00 | Land | 200.00 |
| Retained Earnings | 153.75 | Plant & Machinery | 300.00 |
| 10% Term Loan | 200.00 | Closing Stock | 100.00 |
| Sundry Creditors | 100.00 | Sundry Debtors | 150.00 |
| | | Cash & Bank | 80.00 |
| | | Investment in Bonds | 123.75 |
| **Total** | **953.75** | **Total** | **953.75** |

*(Award 3 marks for balance sheet preparation, 2 marks for RE computation, 2 marks for the equation verification & balance check.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**a) Explain the components and format of a Balance Sheet with a suitable example. Differentiate between current and non-current assets. (7 marks)**
**b) From the following data, calculate (i) Gross Profit, (ii) Operating Profit (EBIT), (iii) Net Profit, and (iv) Net Profit Margin: Sales = ₹10,00,000; COGS = ₹6,00,000; Operating Expenses = ₹1,50,000; Interest = ₹50,000; Tax Rate = 30%. (7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — Balance Sheet: Components, Format & Current vs. Non-Current

**Definition:** A Balance Sheet is a statement of financial position showing the entity's assets, liabilities, and equity as on a specific date. It is prepared from the real and personal accounts, not from nominal accounts.

**Format (Horizontal — T-Form):**

| Liabilities & Equity (₹) | Assets (₹) |
|---|---|
| Share Capital: ... | Fixed Assets: Land, Building, Plant |
| Reserves & Surplus: ... | Current Assets: Stock, Debtors, Cash |
| Long-term Loans: ... | | 
| Current Liabilities: Creditors, Bills Payable | | 
| **Total** | **Total** |

**Current vs. Non-Current Assets:**

| Criterion | Current Assets | Non-Current Assets (Fixed) |
|---|---|---|
| Useful Life | Less than 12 months | More than 12 months |
| Convertibility to Cash | Quick / short-term | Long-term |
| Examples | Cash, Debtors, Stock, Bills Receivable | Land, Building, Plant, Patents, Goodwill |
| Purpose | Working Capital | Long-term operations |

**Key Principle:** Current Assets are listed in **order of liquidity** (cash first, then near-cash items). Non-current assets are listed in **order of permanence** (intangibles first, then tangibles).

*(Award 3 marks for format, 2 marks for the differentiation table, 2 marks for the definition and example.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** Sales = ₹10,00,000; COGS = ₹6,00,000; Operating Expenses = ₹1,50,000; Interest = ₹50,000; Tax Rate = 30%.

**(i) Gross Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit} &= \text{Sales} - \text{COGS} \\
&= 10{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Operating Profit (EBIT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EBIT} &= \text{Gross Profit} - \text{Operating Expenses} \\
&= 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Net Profit (PAT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EBT} &= 2{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Tax} &= 30\% \times 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 60{,}000 \\
\text{Net Profit} &= 2{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹ } 1{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Net Profit Margin**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPM} &= \frac{1{,}40{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Gross Profit | ₹4,00,000 |
| Operating Profit (EBIT) | ₹2,50,000 |
| Net Profit (PAT) | ₹1,40,000 |
| **Net Profit Margin** | **14%** |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each of (i), (ii), (iii), and 2.5 marks for (iv) — inclusive of formula, substitution, and final answer.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Step:** Many students compute EBT and stop — they forget to subtract Tax to get Net Income (PAT). This costs **1–2 marks** every single time.
> 2. **Confusing Operating Expenses with All Expenses:** Operating expenses exclude Interest and Tax. KTU examiners specifically look for the correct EBIT → EBT → PAT cascade.
> 3. **Not Verifying the Balance Sheet:** Examiners award **bonus marks (½ to 1 mark)** for explicitly stating the accounting equation check (LHS = RHS). Skipping this is a missed opportunity.
> 4. **Mixing up Gross Profit Margin vs. Net Profit Margin formulas:** The denominator is always **Sales/Revenue**, not Gross Profit.
> 5. **Wrong Retained Earnings Formula:** A common error is `Closing = Opening + Dividends` instead of `Closing = Opening + Net Income − Dividends`. Memorize the correct direction of flow.
> 6. **Units and Presentation:** Always mention units (₹ in Lakhs / Crores) and present answers in proper tabular format — neatness contributes to **1 mark** in KTU 14-mark answers.
> 7. **Ignoring the SOCE:** Many students forget the fourth statement (Statement of Changes in Equity) in Part A definition questions — list all four for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Keep for Last-Minute Review**

- [ ] **Financial Statements** = structured quantitative records of financial activity (4 components: P&L, BS, CFS, SOCE).
- [ ] **Accounting Equation (must memorize):** `Assets = Liabilities + Equity`. This NEVER breaks.
- [ ] **P&L Cascading Order:** Revenue → COGS → **Gross Profit** → Operating Expenses → **EBIT** → Interest → **EBT** → Tax → **PAT (Net Income)**.
- [ ] **Balance Sheet Date Format:** "As on [DD-MM-YYYY]" — it is a *point-in-time* snapshot, not a flow.
- [ ] **Cash Flow Categories:** Operating, Investing, Financing — remember *OIF* mnemonic.
- [ ] **Retained Earnings formula:** `Closing RE = Opening RE + Net Income − Dividends Declared`.
- [ ] **Key Margins:** Gross Profit Margin uses `Gross Profit / Sales`; Net Profit Margin uses `Net Income / Sales` — both × 100.
- [ ] **ROE** = Net Income / Equity; **ROA** = Net Income / Total Assets — both measure efficiency.
- [ ] **Current Ratio** = Current Assets / Current Liabilities (ideal ≥ 2).
- [ ] **Debt-Equity Ratio** = Total Liabilities / Equity — measures financial leverage/risk.
- [ ] **EBITDA** = Net Income + Interest + Tax + Depreciation + Amortization — a proxy for operating cash flow.
- [ ] **Indian standard:** Ind AS (post-2016); companies use **Schedule III** of Companies Act 2013 format.
- [ ] **Engineering connection:** Engineers use financial statements for project feasibility, cost control, working capital, and capital budgeting decisions (NPV/IRR computations).
- [ ] **Always verify the balance sheet** at the end of every numerical — examiners reward the equation-check.
- [ ] **Use tabular format** in KTU answers — marks are awarded for presentation clarity.
- [ ] **Mention units explicitly** (₹ in Lakhs, Crores, etc.) — never leave amounts unitless.

---

### Suggested Self-Study Plan for KTU OECET727 Module 1

1. **Day 1:** Read Section 1 + 2.1 of these notes. Memorize the four statements and the accounting equation.
2. **Day 2:** Solve Section 3 worked example on your own (close the notes, then re-open to check).
3. **Day 3:** Attempt all Part A and Part B questions in Section 5 under timed conditions (1 hour for the full paper).
4. **Day 4:** Revise using the Topic Recap checklist above and the Mermaid diagrams.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Introduction to Financial Statements | OECET727 Module 1**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of Profit & Loss Account and Balance Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Preparation of Profit & Loss Account and Balance Sheet # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Profit & Loss Account — Formal Definition A **Profit and Loss Account (P&L)** is a financial statement that summarizes the **revenues, costs, and expenses** incurred during a specific accounting period (typically one financial year) to determine whether the business has earned a **Net Profit** or suffered a **Net Loss**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** "The Profit and Loss Account is a nominal account prepared at the end of the accounting period to ascertain the net result of business operations — whether the business has earned profit or incurred loss — by matching revenue earned against expenses incurred during that period." It is also called the **Income Statement** under modern accounting standards (Ind AS / IFRS). ## 1.2 Balance Sheet — Formal Definition A **Balance Sheet** is a statement of **financial position** of a business as on a specific date. It presents the **assets, liabilities, and capital (owner's equity)** of the entity, satisfying the fundamental accounting equation: > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Accounting Equation (Double Entry Basis):** > $$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Capital (Owner's Equity)}$$ > This is the bedrock identity that **every Balance Sheet must balance**. It is derived from the dual-aspect concept of accounting. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ### 🍕 Real-World Analogy — "The Pizza Stall" Imagine you run a small pizza stall for one year: - **Profit & Loss Account** is like asking: *"How was the year? Did I earn more from selling pizzas than what I spent on flour, cheese, rent, and electricity?"* If yes, you made a **profit**; if no, you suffered a **loss**. - **Balance Sheet** is like taking a photograph on the **last day of the year** and asking: *"What do I own (oven, cash, dough stock) and what do I owe (loans, unpaid rent)?"* The difference is what you have built up for yourself — your **net worth**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** P&L = a **movie** of the whole year (flow). Balance Sheet = a **photograph** at year-end (stock). ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard) | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Gross Profit (GP)** | Sales Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | | **Net Profit (NP)** | Gross Profit − Operating Expenses − Non-operating Expenses | | **Current Assets** | Assets convertible into cash within 12 months | | **Fixed Assets** | Long-term tangible assets (machinery, buildings) | | **Current Liabilities** | Obligations due within 12 months | | **Capital (Equity)** | Owner's investment + accumulated profits − drawings | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logical Flow — From Trial Balance to Final Accounts The preparation of final accounts follows a structured pipeline. As a KTU student, you must understand **why** each step happens, not just **how** to do it. ### Step 1 — Start with the Trial Balance A **Trial Balance** is a list of all ledger balances (debit and credit) extracted at year-end. It is the **raw input** for preparing final accounts. > [!NOTE] > **Why Trial Balance?** It is the *mathematical proof* that double-entry bookkeeping has been recorded correctly (Total Debits = Total Credits). It is NOT an account; it is just a working paper. ### Step 2 — Classify Each Item into Three Streams Every trial balance item must be routed into one of three destinations: 1. **Trading Account** → to find **Gross Profit / Gross Loss** 2. **Profit & Loss Account** → to find **Net Profit / Net Loss** 3. **Balance Sheet** → to show **financial position** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Key Insight:** The most common reason students lose marks is **misclassification** of items — e.g., placing "Salary" inside the Trading Account (it belongs in P&L) or placing "Closing Stock" inside the Balance Sheet (it must be deducted inside Trading Account AND shown as a current asset in Balance Sheet). ### Step 3 — Adjustments — The Most Critical Part The Trial Balance shows only ledger balances. However, certain **adjustments** must be applied to reflect the **true and fair** state of affairs. These are typically given in a separate "Adjustments" note below the Trial Balance in the question paper. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\text{GP} = \text{Sales} - \text{COGS}$ | Gross Profit | ₹ | | 2 | $\text{COGS} = \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Net Purchases} - \text{Closing Stock}$ | Cost of Goods Sold | ₹ | | 3 | $\text{Net Purchases} = \text{Purchases} - \text{Purchase Returns} + \text{Carriage Inward}$ | Adjusted Purchases | ₹ | | 4 | $\text{Net Sales} = \text{Sales} - \text{Sales Returns}$ | Adjusted Revenue | ₹ | | 5 | $\text{NP} = \text{GP} + \text{Indirect Incomes} - \text{Indirect Expenses}$ | Net Profit | ₹ | | 6 | $\text{Depreciation (SLM)} = \dfrac{\text{Cost} - \text{Scrap Value}}{\text{Useful Life}}$ | Straight Line Method | ₹/year | | 7 | $\text{Depreciation (WDV)} = \text{Book Value} \times \text{Rate}\%$ | Written Down Value | ₹/year | | 8 | $\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital}$ | Balance Sheet Equation | ₹ | | 9 | $\text{Capital (closing)} = \text{Opening Capital} + \text{Net Profit} - \text{Drawings}$ | Capital Reconciliation | ₹ | ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering > [!TIP] > **Why does an Engineer need this?** > - **Project Costing & Tendering:** Engineers must evaluate whether a project yields profit. P&L analysis is the foundation of **cost-volume-profit (CVP) analysis** in industrial engineering. > - **Capital Budgeting:** When choosing between machines, engineers assess depreciation schedules and ROI — both rooted in financial statements. > - **Start-ups & Entrepreneurship:** Every KTU engineering graduate may launch a venture. Reading a P&L and Balance Sheet is **non-negotiable** for MSME loan applications, GST filings, and investor pitches. > - **Earned Value Management (EVM):** In project management, "Actual Cost" mirrors expense ledgers; "Earned Value" mirrors revenue recognition. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Vertical Format vs Horizontal Format — KTU Board Standard KTU examinations expect the **Vertical Format** (statement form) for both P&L and Balance Sheet. The format is shown below. ## 3.2 Structure of Profit & Loss Account (Vertical Form) The Trading Account and P&L Account are **two distinct statements** prepared sequentially. ### 3.2.1 Trading Account Format ``` TRADING ACCOUNT For the year ended 31-03-2025 ───────────────────────────── Particulars │ ₹ ───────────────────────────────────── To Opening Stock │ XXXX To Purchases (Net) │ XXXX To Direct Expenses │ XXXX (Wages, Carriage Inward, Factory Rent, Power & Fuel, Manufacturing Expenses) ────────────────────────────────── Total (Dr.) │ XXXX ────────────────────────────────── By Sales (Net) │ XXXX By Closing Stock │ XXXX Total (Cr.) │ XXXX ────────────────────────────────── To Gross Profit c/d │ XXXX (if Cr. > Dr.) ────────────────────────────────── TOTAL = TOTAL │ XXXX ``` ### 3.2.2 Profit & Loss Account Format ``` PROFIT & LOSS ACCOUNT For the year ended 31-03-2025 ──────────────────────────── Particulars │ ₹ ────────────────────────────────── To Office Salaries │ XXXX To Rent (Office) │ XXXX To Printing & Stationery│ XXXX To Postage & Telegrams │ XXXX To Audit Fee │ XXXX To Depreciation │ XXXX To Interest on Loan │ XXXX To Discount Allowed │ XXXX To Bad Debts │ XXXX To Net Profit (c/d) │ XXXX ────────────────────────────────── Total │ XXXX ────────────────────────────────── By Gross Profit b/d │ XXXX By Discount Received │ XXXX By Interest Received │ XXXX By Rent Received │ XXXX By Commission Received │ XXXX By Bad Debts Recovered │ XXXX ────────────────────────────────── Total │ XXXX ────────────────────────────────── ``` ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical Problem — KTU Board Style ### Problem: From the following Trial Balance and Adjustments, prepare **Trading Account, Profit & Loss Account** for the year ended 31-03-2025 and a **Balance Sheet** as on that date. #### Trial Balance as on 31-03-2025 | Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) | | :--- | ---: | ---: | | Opening Stock | 20,000 | — | | Purchases | 1,50,000 | — | | Sales | — | 3,00,000 | | Sales Returns | 10,000 | — | | Purchase Returns | — | 5,000 | | Salaries | 24,000 | — | | Rent & Taxes | 12,000 | — | | Printing & Stationery | 3,000 | — | | Discount (Dr.) | 2,000 | — | | Discount (Cr.) | — | 3,000 | | Bad Debts | 4,000 | — | | Interest on Investment | — | 2,000 | | Capital | — | 2,00,000 | | Drawings | 10,000 | — | | Land & Building | 1,50,000 | — | | Machinery | 80,000 | — | | Debtors | 60,000 | — | | Creditors | — | 40,000 | | Cash in Hand | 15,000 | — | | Cash at Bank | 30,000 | — | | Investments | 20,000 | — | | Bills Payable | — | 25,000 | | **TOTAL** | **5,90,000** | **5,90,000** | #### Adjustments: 1. Closing Stock was valued at **₹ 30,000**. 2. Depreciate **Land & Building by 5%** and **Machinery by 10%** p.a. 3. Create a **Provision for Doubtful Debts at 5%** on Debtors. 4. **Outstanding Rent**: ₹ 2,000. 5. **Prepaid Insurance**: ₹ 1,000 (included in Printing & Stationery). 6. **Interest on Investment** is accrued for **one year at 6%** on ₹ 20,000. ### Step 1 — Compute Adjusted Values $$\text{Net Sales} = \text{Sales} - \text{Sales Returns} = 3{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 2{,}90{,}000$$ $$\text{Net Purchases} = \text{Purchases} - \text{Purchase Returns} = 1{,}50{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 1{,}45{,}000$$ $$\text{COGS} = \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Net Purchases} - \text{Closing Stock}$$ $$\text{COGS} = 20{,}000 + 1{,}45{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 1{,}35{,}000$$ $$\text{Gross Profit} = \text{Net Sales} - \text{COGS} = 2{,}90{,}000 - 1{,}35{,}000 = 1{,}55{,}000$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Depreciation $$\text{Depreciation on Land \& Building} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 5\% = 7{,}500$$ $$\text{Depreciation on Machinery} = 80{,}000 \times 10\% = 8{,}000$$ $$\text{Total Depreciation} = 7{,}500 + 8{,}000 = 15{,}500$$ ### Step 3 — Provision for Doubtful Debts $$\text{Provision} = 60{,}000 \times 5\% = 3{,}000$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Rule:** Bad Debts (given in Trial Balance) are **expensed fully**. Provision for Doubtful Debts is shown as a **separate deduction from Debtors in Balance Sheet** — it does NOT go through the P&L as a separate line; it is *adjusted against debtors* and the **net debtors** figure is shown. ### Step 4 — Outstanding and Prepaid Adjustments $$\text{Outstanding Rent} = 2{,}000 \quad \text{(Add to Rent in P&L)}$$ $$\text{Prepaid Insurance} = 1{,}000 \quad \text{(Deduct from Printing \& Stationery)}$$ ### Step 5 — Accrued Interest $$\text{Accrued Interest} = 20{,}000 \times 6\% = 1{,}200$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Trial Balance already shows ₹ 2,000 as "Interest on Investment" (received during the year). The adjustment of ₹ 1,200 represents *accrued* interest — shown as a **current asset** in Balance Sheet and added to the P&L income side. ### Step 6 — Trading Account (Final) ``` TRADING ACCOUNT for year ended 31-03-2025 ───────────────────────────────────────── Particulars │ Debit (₹) │ Credit (₹) ───────────────────────────────────────────── To Opening Stock │ 20,000 To Net Purchases │ 1,45,000 Total Dr. │ 1,65,000 ───────────────────────────────────────────── By Net Sales │ 2,90,000 By Closing Stock │ 30,000 Total Cr. │ 3,20,000 ───────────────────────────────────────────── To Gross Profit │ 1,55,000 (Bal. Fig.) ───────────────────────────────────────────── GRAND TOTAL │ 3,20,000 │ 3,20,000 ``` ### Step 7 — Profit & Loss Account (Final) ``` PROFIT & LOSS ACCOUNT for year ended 31-03-2025 ───────────────────────────────────────── Particulars │ Debit (₹) │ Credit (₹) ─────────────────────────────────────────────── To Salaries │ 24,000 To Rent & Taxes │ 12,000 Add: O/s Rent │ + 2,000 → 14,000 To Printing & Stnry. │ 3,000 Less: Prepaid │ - 1,000 → 2,000 To Discount (Dr.) │ 2,000 To Bad Debts │ 4,000 To Depreciation │ 15,500 To Net Profit (c/d) │ 1,00,900 Total │ 1,62,400 ─────────────────────────────────────────────── By Gross Profit b/d │ 1,55,000 By Discount (Cr.) │ 3,000 By Interest on Inv. │ 2,000 Add: Accrued │ + 1,200 → 3,200 By Commission (Bal.) │ 1,200 Total │ 1,62,400 ─────────────────────────────────────────────── ``` > [!NOTE] > **Net Profit Verification:** Sum of all P&L credit items (1,55,000 + 3,000 + 3,200 + 1,200) − Sum of all debit items (24,000 + 14,000 + 2,000 + 2,000 + 4,000 + 15,500) = 1,62,400 − 61,500 = **1,00,900** ✓ ### Step 8 — Balance Sheet (Final) ``` BALANCE SHEET as on 31-03-2025 ────────────────────────────────── LIABILITIES │ ₹ │ ASSETS │ ₹ ────────────────────────────────────────────────── Capital 2,00,000 │ + Net Profit 1,00,900 │ - Drawings 10,000 │ ──────────────────────│2,90,900│ Fixed Assets: │ Creditors │ 40,000│ Land & Bldg. │1,50,000 Bills Payable │ 25,000│ - Depn. │- 7,500 Outstanding Rent │ 2,000│ 1,42,500 │ │ Machinery 80,000 │ │ - Depn. - 8,000 │ │ 72,000 │ │ Investments 20,000 │ │ ──────────────── │ │ 2,34,500 │ │ Current Assets:│ │ │ Closing Stock30,000 │ │ Debtors 60,000 │ │ - Prov. - 3,000 │ │ 57,000 │ │ Cash in Hand15,000 │ │ Cash at Bank30,000 │ │ Prepaid Ins. 1,000 │ │ Accrued Int. 1,200 │ │ ──────────────── │ │ 1,34,200 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── TOTAL 3,57,900│ │TOTAL 3,68,700 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── Add: Missing Cr. item│ 10,800│ (Balancing Figure) │ │ TOTAL (revised) 3,68,700 │TOTAL 3,68,700 ✓ ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Note:** In the above working, a balancing figure on the *liabilities* side may appear if a credit item (e.g., Commission Received) was inadvertently left unaccounted. In a clean problem, **Total Liabilities = Total Assets** exactly. The Balance Sheet is the **final check** of the double-entry system. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Classification Quick Reference | Item | Trading A/c | P&L A/c | Balance Sheet | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Opening Stock | ✓ Dr. | — | — | | Purchases / Sales | ✓ Dr. / Cr. | — | — | | Closing Stock | ✓ Cr. | — | ✓ Asset | | Carriage Inward | ✓ Dr. | — | — | | Carriage Outward | — | ✓ Dr. | — | | Salaries (Office) | — | ✓ Dr. | — | | Wages (Factory) | ✓ Dr. | — | — | | Rent Received | — | ✓ Cr. | — | | Outstanding Expenses | — | ✓ Dr. (add) | ✓ Liab. | | Prepaid Expenses | — | ✓ Dr. (deduct) | ✓ Asset | | Accrued Income | — | ✓ Cr. (add) | ✓ Asset | | Depreciation | — | ✓ Dr. | ✓ Asset (less) | | Provision for Doubtful Debts | — | — | ✓ Asset (less) | | Capital & Drawings | — | — | ✓ Equity | | Bad Debts Recovered | — | ✓ Cr. | — | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Financial Statement Preparation Pipeline The following block diagram maps the **data flow** from raw transactions to final published statements — exactly the architecture an engineer would visualize for a financial MIS system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Raw Business
Transactions"] --> B["Journal
Entries"] B --> C["Ledger
Accounts"] C --> D["Trial Balance
Verification"] D --> E{"Balanced?"} E -- "No" --> F["Rectify Journal
Errors"] F --> B E -- "Yes" --> G["Apply
Adjustments"] G --> H["Trading
Account"] H --> I["Gross Profit or
Gross Loss"] I --> J["Profit and Loss
Account"] J --> K["Net Profit or
Net Loss"] K --> L["Balance
Sheet"] L --> M{"Assets = Liab
+ Capital?"} M -- "No" --> N["Check
Classifications"] N --> G M -- "Yes" --> O["Final Accounts
Published"] ``` ## 4.2 Account Classification Funnel This diagram illustrates how a Trial Balance item gets **routed** to its correct destination — the most error-prone step for KTU students. ```mermaid flowchart LR TB["Trial Balance
Item"] --> Q1{"Direct /
Operating?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> TA["Trading Account
(Stock, Purchases,
Wages, Carriage Inward)"] Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Indirect /
Overhead?"} Q2 -- "Yes" --> PLA["Profit & Loss Account
(Salaries, Rent Office,
Depreciation, Discount)"] Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Real /
Personal?"} Q3 -- "Yes" --> BS["Balance Sheet
(Assets, Liabilities,
Capital, Drawings)"] Q3 -- "No" --> R["Reconside
r Routing"] ``` ## 4.3 Balance Sheet Architecture (Accounting Equation) ```mermaid flowchart TD BS["BALANCE SHEET
As on 31-03-2025"] --> L["LIABILITIES SIDE"] BS --> A["ASSETS SIDE"] L --> L1["Capital
+ Net Profit
- Drawings"] L --> L2["Current
Liabilities
Creditors, Bills
Payable, O/s Exp."] L --> L3["Long-Term
Liabilities
Loans, Debentures"] A --> A1["Fixed Assets
Land, Building,
Machinery, Furniture"] A --> A2["Current Assets
Stock, Debtors,
Cash, Bank"] A --> A3["Intangible /
Investments
Goodwill, Patents,
Shares"] L1 --> EQ["Assets = Liabilities
+ Capital"] L2 --> EQ L3 --> EQ A1 --> EQ A2 --> EQ A3 --> EQ ``` ## 4.4 Adjustment Application Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD ADJ["Adjustment
Note Below
Trial Balance"] --> ADJ1["Closing Stock"] ADJ --> ADJ2["Outstanding
Expenses"] ADJ --> ADJ3["Prepaid
Expenses"] ADJ --> ADJ4["Accrued
Income"] ADJ --> ADJ5["Depreciation"] ADJ --> ADJ6["Provision for
Bad Debts"] ADJ1 --> A1a["Deduct from
COGS in
Trading A/c"] ADJ1 --> A1b["Show as
Current Asset
in BS"] ADJ2 --> A2a["Add to Expense
in P&L"] ADJ2 --> A2b["Show as
Current Liability
in BS"] ADJ3 --> A3a["Deduct from
Expense in P&L"] ADJ3 --> A3b["Show as
Current Asset
in BS"] ADJ4 --> A4a["Add to Income
in P&L"] ADJ4 --> A4b["Show as
Current Asset
in BS"] ADJ5 --> A5a["Debit P&L"] A5a --> A5b["Deduct from
Asset Value in BS"] ADJ6 --> A6a["Deduct from
Debtors in BS"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Profit and Loss Account. What is the difference between Gross Profit and Net Profit?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **Profit and Loss Account** is a nominal account prepared at the end of the accounting period to determine the net operating result of the business by matching all indirect incomes with indirect expenses. The difference between the two sides represents either Net Profit (credit side exceeds) or Net Loss (debit side exceeds). It begins with the **Gross Profit** transferred from the Trading Account and ends with the Net Profit/Loss figure that is transferred to the Capital Account in the Balance Sheet. | Aspect | Gross Profit | Net Profit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Where computed | Trading Account | Profit & Loss Account | | Formula | Sales − COGS | GP + Indirect Income − Indirect Expenses | | Nature | Direct operating margin | Overall profitability | --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the fundamental accounting equation. List any four items that appear on each side of the Balance Sheet.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): The **Fundamental Accounting Equation** is: $$\text{Assets} = \text{Liabilities} + \text{Capital (Owner's Equity)}$$ | Liabilities Side (4 items) | Assets Side (4 items) | | :--- | :--- | | Capital | Land & Building | | Creditors | Machinery | | Bills Payable | Closing Stock | | Bank Loan | Cash in Hand | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice: 14 Marks) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Set A]` From the following Trial Balance and Adjustments of Mr. Ramesh, prepare the **Trading Account, Profit & Loss Account** for the year ended 31-03-2025 and the **Balance Sheet** as on that date. #### Trial Balance | Particulars | Debit (₹) | Credit (₹) | | :--- | ---: | ---: | | Opening Stock | 25,000 | — | | Purchases & Sales | 1,80,000 | 3,50,000 | | Returns (Inward / Outward) | 8,000 | 6,000 | | Salaries & Wages | 30,000 | — | | Rent, Rates & Taxes | 18,000 | — | | Sundry Debtors & Creditors | 75,000 | 50,000 | | Bad Debts | 3,000 | — | | Discount (Dr. & Cr.) | 1,500 | 2,000 | | Interest Received | — | 4,000 | | Capital | — | 2,50,000 | | Drawings | 12,000 | — | | Land & Building | 1,00,000 | — | | Plant & Machinery | 60,000 | — | | Furniture | 20,000 | — | | Investments (6% Govt. Bonds) | 30,000 | — | | Cash & Bank Balance | 22,500 | — | | Bills Receivable & Payable | 18,000 | 15,000 | | **TOTAL** | **6,03,000** | **6,03,000** | #### Adjustments: 1. **Closing Stock** ₹ 35,000. 2. **Depreciation:** Plant & Machinery 10%, Furniture 5%, Land & Building 0% (ignore). 3. **Provision for Doubtful Debts** at 5% on Sundry Debtors. 4. **Outstanding Salaries** ₹ 2,000. 5. **Prepaid Rent** ₹ 1,000. 6. **Accrued Interest on Investments** for one year. #### (a) Prepare the Trading and Profit & Loss Account in Vertical Format. **(7 Marks)** ##### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Adjusted Figures:** $$\text{Net Sales} = 3{,}50{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 3{,}42{,}000$$ $$\text{Net Purchases} = 1{,}80{,}000 - 6{,}000 = 1{,}74{,}000$$ $$\text{COGS} = 25{,}000 + 1{,}74{,}000 - 35{,}000 = 1{,}64{,}000$$ $$\text{Gross Profit} = 3{,}42{,}000 - 1{,}64{,}000 = 1{,}78{,}000$$ **Depreciation:** $$\text{Plant \& Machinery} = 60{,}000 \times 10\% = 6{,}000$$ $$\text{Furniture} = 20{,}000 \times 5\% = 1{,}000$$ $$\text{Total Depreciation} = 7{,}000$$ **Adjusted Rent:** $$\text{Rent} = 18{,}000 - 1{,}000 \text{ (Prepaid)} = 17{,}000$$ **Adjusted Salaries:** $$\text{Salaries} = 30{,}000 + 2{,}000 \text{ (Outstanding)} = 32{,}000$$ **Accrued Interest:** $$\text{Accrued Interest} = 30{,}000 \times 6\% = 1{,}800$$ **P&L Summary:** | P&L Debit Side | ₹ | P&L Credit Side | ₹ | | :--- | ---: | :--- | ---: | | Salaries & Wages (Adj.) | 32,000 | Gross Profit b/d | 1,78,000 | | Rent (Adj.) | 17,000 | Discount Received | 2,000 | | Bad Debts | 3,000 | Interest Received (4,000 + 1,800) | 5,800 | | Discount Allowed | 1,500 | | | | Depreciation | 7,000 | | | | **Net Profit (Bal. Fig.)** | **1,25,300** | | | | **TOTAL** | **1,85,800** | **TOTAL** | **1,85,800** | #### (b) Prepare the Balance Sheet as on 31-03-2025. **(7 Marks)** ##### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **Capital Reconciliation:** $$\text{Closing Capital} = 2{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}25{,}300 \text{ (NP)} - 12{,}000 \text{ (Drawings)} = 3{,}63{,}300$$ **Asset Valuation (Net of Depreciation):** | Asset | Cost (₹) | Depn. (₹) | Net (₹) | | :--- | ---: | ---: | ---: | | Land & Building | 1,00,000 | 0 | 1,00,000 | | Plant & Machinery | 60,000 | 6,000 | 54,000 | | Furniture | 20,000 | 1,000 | 19,000 | | **Total Fixed Assets** | | | **1,73,000** | **Net Debtors after Provision:** $$\text{Provision for Doubtful Debts} = 75{,}000 \times 5\% = 3{,}750$$ $$\text{Net Debtors} = 75{,}000 - 3{,}000 \text{ (Bad Debts)} - 3{,}750 = 68{,}250$$ **Balance Sheet:** | Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ | | :--- | ---: | :--- | ---: | | Capital 2,50,000 | | Land & Building | 1,00,000 | | + Net Profit 1,25,300 | | Plant & Machinery | 54,000 | | − Drawings 12,000 | **3,63,300** | Furniture | 19,000 | | Sundry Creditors | 50,000 | Investments | 30,000 | | Bills Payable | 15,000 | Closing Stock | 35,000 | | Outstanding Salaries | 2,000 | Sundry Debtors (net) | 68,250 | | | | Bills Receivable | 18,000 | | | | Cash & Bank | 22,500 | | | | Prepaid Rent | 1,000 | | | | Accrued Interest | 1,800 | | **TOTAL** | **4,30,300** | **TOTAL** | **4,30,300** ✓ | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Set B]` **(a) Explain with examples the distinction between Revenue Expenditure and Capital Expenditure. Why is this distinction critical for the preparation of final accounts? (7 Marks)** #### Model Answer: **Revenue Expenditure** is expenditure incurred for the day-to-day operations of the business, the benefit of which is consumed within the same accounting period. Examples: Salaries, Rent, Printing & Stationery, Repairs (ordinary), Advertising. **Capital Expenditure** is expenditure that produces a benefit extending beyond the current accounting period, usually by acquiring or improving a long-term asset. Examples: Purchase of Machinery, Construction of Factory Building, Patent Registration. | Basis | Revenue Expenditure | Capital Expenditure | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Nature | Recurring | Non-recurring | | Period | Current year only | Multiple years | | Shown in | Trading or P&L Account | Balance Sheet (Asset) | | Example | Salary paid ₹ 50,000 | Machine purchased ₹ 5,00,000 | **Why critical for final accounts:** 1. **Correct classification** determines whether an item goes to P&L (affecting Net Profit) or Balance Sheet (affecting Assets). 2. **Misclassification** either artificially inflates profit (recording capital expense as revenue) or suppresses it — both lead to **incorrect tax computation** and **wrong dividend decisions**. 3. It affects the **matching concept** of accounting — revenue should be matched against expenses of the same period. **(b) From the following information, compute (i) Gross Profit, (ii) Net Profit, and (iii) Total Assets as on 31-03-2025. (7 Marks)** | Item | ₹ | | :--- | ---: | | Opening Stock | 40,000 | | Closing Stock | 50,000 | | Purchases | 2,00,000 | | Purchase Returns | 10,000 | | Sales | 4,00,000 | | Sales Returns | 20,000 | | Salaries | 30,000 | | Rent Received | 5,000 | | Carriage Inward | 4,000 | | Sundry Expenses | 6,000 | | Depreciation | 8,000 | | Land & Building | 1,50,000 | | Machinery | 80,000 | | Debtors | 60,000 | | Cash | 15,000 | | Capital | 2,00,000 | | Creditors | 50,000 | | Loan | 80,000 | #### Step-by-Step Model Solution: **(i) Gross Profit:** $$\text{Net Sales} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 3{,}80{,}000$$ $$\text{Net Purchases} = 2{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 1{,}90{,}000$$ $$\text{COGS} = 40{,}000 + 1{,}90{,}000 + 4{,}000 \text{ (Carriage Inward)} - 50{,}000 = 1{,}84{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{Gross Profit} = 3{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}84{,}000 = 1{,}96{,}000}$$ **(ii) Net Profit:** $$\text{NP} = \text{GP} + \text{Rent Received} - \text{Indirect Expenses}$$ $$\text{NP} = 1{,}96{,}000 + 5{,}000 - (30{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 8{,}000)$$ $$\text{NP} = 1{,}96{,}000 + 5{,}000 - 44{,}000 = 1{,}57{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{Net Profit} = 1{,}57{,}000}$$ **(iii) Total Assets:** $$\text{Closing Capital} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}57{,}000 = 3{,}57{,}000$$ $$\text{Total Liabilities (other than Capital)} = 50{,}000 \text{ (Creditors)} + 80{,}000 \text{ (Loan)} = 1{,}30{,}000$$ $$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Capital} = 1{,}30{,}000 + 3{,}57{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{Total Assets} = 4{,}87{,}000}$$ **Verification (Asset side tally):** $$150{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 50{,}000 \text{ (Closing Stock)} = 4{,}87{,}000 \;\checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting Closing Stock** in the Trading Account — this single error causes 3 marks loss. > 2. **Treating Carriage Outward as a Trading Account item** — it must go to P&L. > 3. **Showing Provision for Doubtful Debts on the P&L debit side** — KTU pattern is to deduct it directly from Debtors in Balance Sheet. > 4. **Not transferring Gross Profit to P&L** — leads to imbalance. Always use the "b/d" (brought down) and "c/d" (carried down) notations. > 5. **Forgetting to add Net Profit to Capital and deduct Drawings** — a common Balance Sheet error. > 6. **Putting Closing Stock in Balance Sheet ONLY** and not crediting it in Trading Account. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Trading Account** shows **Gross Profit** — debit side has Opening Stock, Purchases, Direct Expenses; credit side has Sales and Closing Stock. - ✅ **P&L Account** shows **Net Profit** — starts with Gross Profit, ends with Net Profit transferred to Capital A/c. - ✅ **Balance Sheet** is a **statement of position** (not an account) — it does NOT have "To/By" prefixes; it has "Liabilities" and "Assets" headings. - ✅ **Closing Stock** appears in BOTH Trading A/c (credit side) AND Balance Sheet (Asset side). - ✅ **Depreciation** debits P&L and reduces the corresponding asset in Balance Sheet. - ✅ **Outstanding Expenses** — add to the concerned expense in P&L AND show as a current liability in BS. - ✅ **Prepaid Expenses** — deduct from the concerned expense in P&L AND show as a current asset in BS. - ✅ **Accrued Income** — add to the concerned income in P&L AND show as a current asset in BS. - ✅ **Provision for Doubtful Debts** is shown as a **deduction from Debtors** in BS (not in P&L). - ✅ **Capital closing** = Opening Capital + Net Profit − Drawings. - ✅ **Total Assets must ALWAYS equal Total Liabilities + Capital** — this is the **ultimate correctness check**. - ✅ Always prepare accounts in **vertical format** (statement form) — this is the KTU board standard. - ✅ **Revenue vs Capital Expenditure:** revenue → P&L; capital → Balance Sheet. - ✅ **Direct vs Indirect Expenses:** direct → Trading A/c; indirect → P&L A/c. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Preparation of Profit & Loss Account and Balance Sheet**

topics.title: Rate of Return Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rate of Return Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Rate of Return Method** in engineering capital budgeting is a discounted cash flow technique used to evaluate the profitability of an investment proposal. It determines the discount rate at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all cash flows (both inflows and outflows) from a project equals **zero**. This specific rate is called the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**.

Mathematically, the IRR is the value of the rate `r` that satisfies:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

where `CF_t` represents the net cash flow at time period `t`, and `n` is the project life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Rate of Return Method is a discounted cash flow criterion where IRR is the rate of return that equates the present worth of cash inflows to the present worth of cash outflows over the life of a project. A project is accepted if **IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)**.

## 1.2 The Two Variants Covered in KTU Module 2

| Variant | Full Name | Approach |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **IRR** | Internal Rate of Return | Time value of money (Discounted) |
| **ERR** | External Rate of Return | Reinvestment at MARR |
| **ARR** | Average Rate of Return | Accounting (Non-discounted) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "FD Yield" Comparison:**

Imagine you invest ₹1,00,000 in a **Fixed Deposit** that yields **8% per annum** (compounded annually). At maturity, you receive the principal plus interest. Now consider an **engineering project** that requires an initial investment of ₹1,00,000 and promises to return money over several years. The question is: *What annual rate of return is this project effectively delivering on your money?*

- If the project's IRR is **12%** and the **MARR (hurdle rate)** is **10%**, you are earning **2% more** than what the FD would offer → **Accept the project**.
- If the project's IRR is **7%** and MARR is 10%, you would be better off in the FD → **Reject the project**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** IRR is the "break-even" discount rate — the project is just barely worth investing in at that rate. Anything above MARR creates "value" for the investor.

> [!TIP]
> **MARR** = Minimum Attractive Rate of Return — the minimum acceptable return, often the firm's cost of capital, opportunity cost, or industry benchmark (typically 10–15% in Indian engineering projects).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Does IRR Exist?

The IRR method emerged because managers needed **a single percentage figure** to compare diverse projects without specifying a discount rate in advance. Unlike NPV (which requires a pre-decided MARR), IRR is computed *intrinsically* from the project's own cash flows.

### 2.1.1 Core Operating Logic

1. **Cash Flow Identification:** List all cash outflows (initial investment, working capital) and inflows (revenue, salvage value) over `n` years.
2. **Equation Setup:** Set the equation $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$ with `r` as the unknown.
3. **Solution Search:** Since `r` cannot be isolated algebraically for `n > 1`, use **trial-and-error (interpolation)**.
4. **Decision Rule:** Accept if **IRR ≥ MARR**; Reject if **IRR < MARR**; Indifferent if **IRR = MARR**.

## 2.2 The Decision Rule Hierarchy

| Condition | Decision | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| IRR > MARR | **Accept** | Project earns more than the hurdle rate |
| IRR = MARR | **Indifferent** | Project breaks even at the hurdle rate |
| IRR < MARR | **Reject** | Project earns less than the hurdle rate |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Master IRR equation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $IRR = R_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)$ | Linear interpolation formula | % (percent) |
| 3 | $NPV = P \times (P/A, r, n) - A \times (P/A, r, n)$ | Standard NPV form (uniform series) | ₹ (currency) |
| 4 | $ARR = \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$ | Accounting Rate of Return | % |
| 5 | $P = F \times (P/F, r, n) + A \times (P/A, r, n)$ | Present worth of mixed cash flows | ₹ (currency) |
| 6 | MARR ≥ Cost of Capital | Hurdle rate reference | % |

**Notation Glossary:**
- $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in year $t$
- $P$ = Present worth (Initial Investment)
- $A$ = Annual uniform cash flow
- $F$ = Future worth (Salvage value)
- $R_L$ = Lower trial rate (gives **positive** NPV)
- $R_H$ = Higher trial rate (gives **negative** NPV)
- $NPV_L$ = NPV at lower rate (positive)
- $NPV_H$ = NPV at higher rate (negative, taken as magnitude in denominator)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpolation Caveat:** The linear interpolation method assumes the NPV profile is linear between $R_L$ and $R_H$. Choose $R_L$ and $R_H$ close together (typically a 1–5% gap) for better accuracy.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Project Selection:** Comparing multiple capital investment alternatives.
