- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** Whether to replace an old machine with a new one.
- **Public Infrastructure:** Evaluating the economic viability of roads, bridges, and power plants.
- **R&D Investments:** Assessing return from new product development.
- **Energy Projects:** LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) and project IRR for solar/wind installations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Interpolation Formula

The NPV of a project as a function of discount rate $r$ is generally a non-linear curve. However, between two closely-spaced rates $R_L$ and $R_H$, the NPV profile is approximately linear. We use this linearity to estimate IRR.

### Step 1: Define NPV at Two Trial Rates

At the lower trial rate $R_L$, the NPV is positive:
$$NPV_L = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+R_L)^t} > 0$$

At the higher trial rate $R_H$, the NPV is negative:
$$NPV_H = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+R_H)^t} < 0$$

### Step 2: Apply Linear Interpolation

Since IRR is the rate where NPV = 0, and we assume linearity between $(R_L, NPV_L)$ and $(R_H, NPV_H)$:

$$\frac{IRR - R_L}{R_H - R_L} = \frac{0 - NPV_L}{NPV_H - NPV_L}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange for IRR

$$IRR - R_L = \frac{-NPV_L}{NPV_H - NPV_L} \times (R_H - R_L)$$

$$IRR = R_L + \frac{-NPV_L}{NPV_H - NPV_L} \times (R_H - R_L)$$

### Step 4: Final Form (Note Sign Convention)

Since $NPV_L > 0$ and $NPV_H < 0$, the denominator $NPV_H - NPV_L$ is negative, and the numerator $-NPV_L$ is negative. To avoid sign confusion, KTU textbooks use:

$$\boxed{IRR = R_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the denominator, $NPV_H$ is treated as a **positive magnitude** (absolute value) so the whole expression yields a positive IRR.

### Step 5: Work-Through Numerical Example

**Problem:** Initial investment = ₹50,000. Annual net cash inflow = ₹12,000 for 6 years. Find IRR. MARR = 10%.

**Solution:**

At r = 10%:
$$NPV_{10\%} = -50000 + 12000 \times (P/A, 10\%, 6)$$

$$(P/A, 10\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10} = 4.3553$$

$$NPV_{10\%} = -50000 + 12000 \times 4.3553 = -50000 + 52263.6 = +2263.6$$

At r = 12%:
$$(P/A, 12\%, 6) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-6}}{0.12} = 4.1114$$

$$NPV_{12\%} = -50000 + 12000 \times 4.1114 = -50000 + 49336.8 = -663.2$$

**Apply Interpolation:**

$$IRR = 10 + \frac{2263.6}{2263.6 - (-663.2)} \times (12 - 10)$$

$$IRR = 10 + \frac{2263.6}{2926.8} \times 2 = 10 + 0.7735 \times 2 = 10 + 1.547$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 11.55\%}$$

**Decision:** Since IRR (11.55%) > MARR (10%) → **Accept the project**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Rate of Return (ARR)

For non-discounted (accounting) rate of return analysis:

$$ARR = \frac{\text{Average Annual Net Profit After Tax}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$$

where:

$$\text{Average Annual Profit} = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(\text{Revenue}_t - \text{Depreciation}_t - \text{Tax}_t)}{n}$$

$$\text{Depreciation} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment} - \text{Salvage Value}}{n}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Method | Time Value of Money | Decision Criterion | Best Used When |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **IRR** | ✅ Yes (Discounted) | IRR ≥ MARR | Single project evaluation |
| **NPV** | ✅ Yes (Discounted) | NPV ≥ 0 | Mutually exclusive projects |
| **ARR** | ❌ No (Accounting) | ARR ≥ Target rate | Quick screening |
| **Payback** | ❌ No (Simple) | Payback ≤ Target | Liquidity-focused firms |
| **B/C Ratio** | ✅ Yes | B/C ≥ 1 | Public sector projects |

## 3.4 Incremental Rate of Return Analysis (Mutually Exclusive Projects)

When two alternatives A and B are mutually exclusive, the **Incremental IRR** is computed as:

$$\Delta IRR_{B-A} = R_L + \frac{\Delta NPV_L}{\Delta NPV_L - \Delta NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)$$

where $\Delta CF_t = CF_{B,t} - CF_{A,t}$.

**Decision Rule:** If $\Delta IRR \geq MARR$, choose the higher-cost alternative (B); else stick with A.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — IRR Calculation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Cash Flow Data"] --> B["Identify Initial Investment P"]
    B --> C["Identify Annual Net Cash Flow A and Life n"]
    C --> D["Guess Trial Rate r1 (Lower)"]
    D --> E["Compute NPV at r1"]
    E --> F{"NPV Positive?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Increase Rate to r2 (Higher)"]
    F -- No --> H["Decrease Rate to r2"]
    G --> I["Compute NPV at r2"]
    H --> I
    I --> J{"Sign Change Detected?"}
    J -- No --> K["Adjust r2 and Retry"]
    K --> I
    J -- Yes --> L["Apply Linear Interpolation"]
    L --> M["Compute IRR Value"]
    M --> N{"IRR >= MARR?"}
    N -- Yes --> O["Accept Project"]
    N -- No --> P["Reject Project"]
    N -- Equal --> Q["Indifferent: MARR = IRR"]
    O --> R["End: Report IRR and Decision"]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rate of Return Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputData["INPUT DATA LAYER"]
        P0["Initial Investment P"]
        A0["Annual Cash Flow A"]
        N0["Project Life n"]
        S0["Salvage Value S"]
        M0["MARR / Hurdle Rate"]
    end
    subgraph Processing["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1["Trial Rate Selection R1"]
        P2["NPV Computation at R1"]
        P3["Trial Rate Selection R2 (Sign Change)"]
        P4["NPV Computation at R2"]
        P5["Linear Interpolation Engine"]
    end
    subgraph Decision["DECISION LAYER"]
        D1["IRR vs MARR Comparator"]
        D2["Accept Reject Engine"]
    end
    subgraph Output["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["IRR Percentage Value"]
        O2["Accept or Reject Recommendation"]
        O3["Sensitivity Report"]
    end
    P0 --> P2
    A0 --> P2
    N0 --> P2
    S0 --> P2
    M0 --> D1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    O1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
    P4 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — NPV vs Discount Rate Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Rate 0% on X-axis"] -->|"NPV very high positive"| B["Moving right"]
    B -->|"NPV declining"| C["Crosses MARR Rate"]
    C -->|"Reaches IRR Point"| D["NPV = 0 Crosses X-axis"]
    D -->|"Beyond IRR"| E["NPV Negative Region"]
    E --> F["Rate very high on X-axis"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Project Acceptance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Compute IRR for Project"] --> Q1{"IRR > MARR?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Sub1["Calculate NPV for confirmation"]
    Sub1 --> Sub2{"NPV > 0?"}
    Sub2 -- Yes --> Accept["ACCEPT PROJECT"]
    Sub2 -- No --> ReCheck["Re-examine cash flows for errors"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"IRR < MARR?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Reject["REJECT PROJECT"]
    Q2 -- No --> Indiff["INDIFFERENT MARR equals IRR"]
    ReCheck --> Start
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the Internal Rate of Return (IRR). State the decision rule for accepting or rejecting a project based on IRR.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all cash flows from a project equals **zero**. In other words, it is the rate of return that equates the present worth of cash inflows to the present worth of cash outflows over the project's life.

Mathematically, IRR is the value of `r` that satisfies:
$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If **IRR > MARR** → **Accept** the project
- If **IRR < MARR** → **Reject** the project
- If **IRR = MARR** → **Indifferent** (project neither gains nor loses)

---

### **Q2.** Distinguish between Average Rate of Return (ARR) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR).

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | ARR (Average Rate of Return) | IRR (Internal Rate of Return) |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Time Value of Money** | Ignored (no discounting) | Considered (discounted cash flows) |
| **Basis** | Accounting profits | Cash flows |
| **Formula** | (Avg. Annual Profit / Investment) × 100 | Rate where NPV = 0 |
| **Decision Criterion** | ARR ≥ Target Rate | IRR ≥ MARR |
| **Accuracy** | Approximate | More accurate |
| **Use Case** | Quick screening | Detailed project evaluation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of NPV confusion:** Many students forget that the NPV at the higher rate must be **negative** for interpolation. If both NPVs are positive or both negative, the trial rates must be adjusted before applying the formula.
> 2. **Wrong formula variant:** Using $IRR = R_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L + NPV_H}$ instead of $NPV_L - NPV_H$. Always use **minus** sign in the denominator.
> 3. **Missing MARR comparison:** Even if IRR is correctly computed, the final decision must explicitly compare with MARR.
> 4. **No units in final answer:** Always report IRR as a **percentage** (e.g., 12.5%).
> 5. **Interpolation gap too large:** Using rates like 5% and 25% gives inaccurate IRR. Keep the gap narrow (1–5%).
> 6. **Forgetting to show the present worth factor table calculation** — always show $(P/A, r, n)$ computation for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects. The cash flows are:

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|------|---------------|---------------|
| 0    | -1,00,000     | -1,50,000     |
| 1    | 30,000        | 45,000        |
| 2    | 35,000        | 50,000        |
| 3    | 40,000        | 55,000        |
| 4    | 30,000        | 40,000        |

MARR = 10%. Using the **Rate of Return Method**, determine which project should be selected.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Compute IRR of Project A (7 Marks)**

**Trial at 10%:**

$$NPV_A(10\%) = -100000 + 30000(P/F,10\%,1) + 35000(P/F,10\%,2) + 40000(P/F,10\%,3) + 30000(P/F,10\%,4)$$

Computing factors:
- $(P/F, 10\%, 1) = 0.9091$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 2) = 0.8264$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 3) = 0.7513$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 4) = 0.6830$

$$NPV_A(10\%) = -100000 + 30000(0.9091) + 35000(0.8264) + 40000(0.7513) + 30000(0.6830)$$

$$NPV_A(10\%) = -100000 + 27273 + 28924 + 30052 + 20490 = +6739$$

NPV is **positive** at 10%. Try a higher rate.

**Trial at 15%:**

- $(P/F, 15\%, 1) = 0.8696$
- $(P/F, 15\%, 2) = 0.7561$
- $(P/F, 15\%, 3) = 0.6575$
- $(P/F, 15\%, 4) = 0.5718$

$$NPV_A(15\%) = -100000 + 30000(0.8696) + 35000(0.7561) + 40000(0.6575) + 30000(0.5718)$$

$$NPV_A(15\%) = -100000 + 26088 + 26464 + 26300 + 17154 = -3994$$

NPV is **negative** at 15%. Apply interpolation:

$$IRR_A = 10 + \frac{6739}{6739 - (-3994)} \times (15 - 10)$$

$$IRR_A = 10 + \frac{6739}{10733} \times 5 = 10 + 0.6279 \times 5 = 10 + 3.14$$

$$\boxed{IRR_A \approx 13.14\%}$$

**Decision for A:** IRR (13.14%) > MARR (10%) → **Individually Acceptable**.

---

##### **Part (b) — Compute IRR of Project B and Select (7 Marks)**

**Trial at 10%:**

$$NPV_B(10\%) = -150000 + 45000(0.9091) + 50000(0.8264) + 55000(0.7513) + 40000(0.6830)$$

$$NPV_B(10\%) = -150000 + 40910 + 41320 + 41322 + 27320 = +872$$

NPV is **positive** at 10%. Try 11%.

**Trial at 11%:**

- $(P/F, 11\%, 1) = 0.9009$
- $(P/F, 11\%, 2) = 0.8116$
- $(P/F, 11\%, 3) = 0.7312$
- $(P/F, 11\%, 4) = 0.6587$

$$NPV_B(11\%) = -150000 + 45000(0.9009) + 50000(0.8116) + 55000(0.7312) + 40000(0.6587)$$

$$NPV_B(11\%) = -150000 + 40541 + 40580 + 40216 + 26348 = -2315$$

NPV is **negative** at 11%. Apply interpolation:

$$IRR_B = 10 + \frac{872}{872 - (-2315)} \times (11 - 10)$$

$$IRR_B = 10 + \frac{872}{3187} \times 1 = 10 + 0.2736$$

$$\boxed{IRR_B \approx 10.27\%}$$

**Decision for B:** IRR (10.27%) > MARR (10%) → **Individually Acceptable** (marginally).

**Mutually Exclusive Selection (Incremental Analysis):**

Since both are acceptable, compute **Incremental IRR** (B − A):

| Year | B − A (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| 0    | -50,000   |
| 1    | +15,000   |
| 2    | +15,000   |
| 3    | +15,000   |
| 4    | +10,000   |

At 10%:
$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 15000(0.9091) + 15000(0.8264) + 15000(0.7513) + 10000(0.6830)$$

$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 13637 + 12396 + 11270 + 6830 = -5867$$

At 8%:
- $(P/F, 8\%, 1) = 0.9259$
- $(P/F, 8\%, 2) = 0.8573$
- $(P/F, 8\%, 3) = 0.7938$
- $(P/F, 8\%, 4) = 0.7350$

$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 15000(0.9259) + 15000(0.8573) + 15000(0.7938) + 10000(0.7350)$$

$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 13889 + 12860 + 11907 + 7350 = -3994$$

At 5%:
- $(P/F, 5\%, 1) = 0.9524$
- $(P/F, 5\%, 2) = 0.9070$
- $(P/F, 5\%, 3) = 0.8638$
- $(P/F, 5\%, 4) = 0.8227$

$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 15000(0.9524) + 15000(0.9070) + 15000(0.8638) + 10000(0.8227)$$

$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 14286 + 13605 + 12957 + 8227 = -925$$

At 0%:
$$\Delta NPV = -50000 + 15000 + 15000 + 15000 + 10000 = +6000$$

**Interpolation between 0% and 5%:**

$$\Delta IRR = 0 + \frac{6000}{6000 - (-925)} \times (5 - 0) = \frac{6000}{6925} \times 5 = 4.33\%$$

Since $\Delta IRR (4.33\%) < MARR (10\%)$, the **extra investment in B is NOT justified**.

**Final Selection: Choose Project A.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A small-scale engineering firm is considering the purchase of a CNC machine for ₹5,00,000. The machine has a useful life of **5 years** with an expected salvage value of ₹50,000. The annual savings (cash inflows) from the machine are estimated at ₹1,50,000 per year. The firm's MARR is **12%**. Use the **Rate of Return Method** to determine whether the machine should be purchased.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Compute IRR of the CNC Machine Investment (7 Marks)**

**Cash Flow Summary:**
- Initial Investment (Year 0): -₹5,00,000
- Annual Cash Inflow (Years 1–5): +₹1,50,000
- Salvage Value (Year 5): +₹50,000

**Trial at 12%:**

$$NPV(12\%) = -500000 + 150000(P/A, 12\%, 5) + 50000(P/F, 12\%, 5)$$

Factor values:
- $(P/A, 12\%, 5) = 3.6048$
- $(P/F, 12\%, 5) = 0.5674$

$$NPV(12\%) = -500000 + 150000(3.6048) + 50000(0.5674)$$

$$NPV(12\%) = -500000 + 540720 + 28370 = +69090$$

NPV is **positive** at 12%. Try a higher rate.

**Trial at 15%:**

- $(P/A, 15\%, 5) = 3.3522$
- $(P/F, 15\%, 5) = 0.4972$

$$NPV(15\%) = -500000 + 150000(3.3522) + 50000(0.4972)$$

$$NPV(15\%) = -500000 + 502830 + 24860 = +27690$$

Still positive. Try 18%.

**Trial at 18%:**

- $(P/A, 18\%, 5) = 3.1272$
- $(P/F, 18\%, 5) = 0.4371$

$$NPV(18\%) = -500000 + 150000(3.1272) + 50000(0.4371)$$

$$NPV(18\%) = -500000 + 469080 + 21855 = -9065$$

NPV is **negative** at 18%. Apply interpolation between 15% and 18%:

$$IRR = 15 + \frac{27690}{27690 - (-9065)} \times (18 - 15)$$

$$IRR = 15 + \frac{27690}{36755} \times 3 = 15 + 0.7535 \times 3 = 15 + 2.26$$

$$\boxed{IRR \approx 17.26\%}$$

**Decision:** IRR (17.26%) > MARR (12%) → **Accept the investment**.

---

##### **Part (b) — Sensitivity Analysis & Interpretation (7 Marks)**

**Sensitivity of IRR to Cash Flow Variation:**

Let the annual savings drop by 10% (from ₹1,50,000 to ₹1,35,000):

**Trial at 12% (revised):**

$$NPV(12\%, \text{low}) = -500000 + 135000(3.6048) + 50000(0.5674)$$

$$NPV(12\%, \text{low}) = -500000 + 486648 + 28370 = +15018$$

**Trial at 14% (revised):**

- $(P/A, 14\%, 5) = 3.4331$
- $(P/F, 14\%, 5) = 0.5194$

$$NPV(14\%, \text{low}) = -500000 + 135000(3.4331) + 50000(0.5194)$$

$$NPV(14\%, \text{low}) = -500000 + 463469 + 25970 = -10561$$

**Interpolation (revised IRR):**

$$IRR_{\text{low}} = 12 + \frac{15018}{15018 - (-10561)} \times (14 - 12) = 12 + \frac{15018}{25579} \times 2$$

$$IRR_{\text{low}} = 12 + 0.5871 \times 2 = 13.17\%$$

**Interpretation:**

| Scenario | Annual Cash Flow | IRR | MARR | Decision |
|----------|-----------------|-----|------|----------|
| Base Case | ₹1,50,000 | 17.26% | 12% | **Accept** |
| 10% Drop | ₹1,35,000 | 13.17% | 12% | **Accept** (marginally) |
| 15% Drop | ₹1,27,500 | ~12% | 12% | **Indifferent** |
| 20% Drop | ₹1,20,000 | ~10% | 12% | **Reject** |

**Conclusion:** The investment is **robust** because IRR remains above MARR even with a 10% drop in savings. The CNC machine purchase is **recommended** with a comfortable safety margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rate of Return Method**

- ✅ **IRR** is the discount rate that makes **NPV = 0**.
- ✅ Decision Rule: **IRR ≥ MARR → Accept**; **IRR < MARR → Reject**.
- ✅ IRR is computed by **trial-and-error** since it cannot be isolated algebraically for `n > 1`.
- ✅ **Linear Interpolation Formula:** $IRR = R_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)$.
- ✅ $R_L$ = Lower rate (NPV positive); $R_H$ = Higher rate (NPV negative).
- ✅ Keep the **interpolation gap small (1–5%)** for accuracy.
- ✅ For **mutually exclusive projects**, always perform **Incremental IRR Analysis**; do not select based on individual IRR alone.
- ✅ **MARR** = Minimum Attractive Rate of Return; typically the firm's **cost of capital**.
- ✅ **ARR** (Average Rate of Return) ignores time value of money — it is a **non-discounted** method based on accounting profit.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Using individual IRR comparison for mutually exclusive projects can give **wrong answer** — always use **incremental analysis**.
- ✅ **Advantage of IRR:** Single rate of return (%) is easy to communicate to management.
- ✅ **Disadvantage of IRR:** Can give **multiple IRRs** for non-conventional cash flows (sign changes more than once) — use NPV in such cases.
- ✅ **External Rate of Return (ERR):** Reinvests cash flows at **MARR** instead of IRR; useful when reinvestment assumption is questioned.
- ✅ IRR is **dimensionless** (a pure ratio expressed in %).
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects the **interpolation formula, full calculation steps, and the final decision** — all three are mandatory for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
The **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** is the **lowest rate of return** that an organization or investor is willing to accept from a capital investment project before it becomes attractive or acceptable. It is also referred to as the **hurdle rate**, **cutoff rate**, or **required rate of return**. MARR serves as the **benchmark discount rate** against which all project evaluation metrics (NPV, IRR, Benefit-Cost Ratio) are compared to judge the economic feasibility of an engineering investment proposal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECET727, Module 2):** MARR is established by the management considering the cost of capital, the risk profile of the project, and the opportunity cost of capital forgone by investing in this project rather than in a comparable alternative.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Imagine you walk into a bank to deposit your savings. The bank offers you an interest rate of **7% per annum** on a Fixed Deposit. This 7% is the *minimum* return that will convince you to lock your money away for 5 years — anything less and you would rather keep the money in your savings account or invest elsewhere.

Similarly, when a company invests ₹1 Crore in a new CNC machine or a solar power plant, the management demands a *minimum* return — say **12%** — to justify the risk and the tied-up capital. That **12%** is the MARR. Any project that earns **less than 12%** is rejected outright, just as you would reject a bank FD offering only 4% when 7% is the market benchmark.

### Components of MARR (Standard Engineering Economics Framework)

The MARR is mathematically decomposed into three building blocks:

$$
\text{MARR} = i_{\text{risk-free}} + \text{Inflation Premium} + \text{Project Risk Premium}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In corporate practice, MARR is often set equal to the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** plus a discretionary project-risk premium. For government/public-sector engineering projects in India, MARR is frequently taken as **12%** (per Planning Commission / NITI Aayog conventional norms) or **10%–15%** in private sector feasibility studies.

---

## 1.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
The **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of a project is the **discount rate** $i^*$ at which the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of all the cash flows (both inflows and outflows) becomes exactly **zero**. In other words, IRR is the rate of return that makes the *present worth of cash inflows* exactly equal to the *present worth of cash outflows*. Mathematically, IRR is the root of the NPV polynomial equation.

$$
\text{NPV}(i^*) = 0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1 + i^*)^t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Internal"** emphasizes that IRR is computed purely from the project's *internal* cash flows — it does **not** require any external benchmark rate to be calculated. This is its key advantage over NPV, although the decision itself does require comparison with MARR.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Think of IRR as the **"break-even interest rate"** of the project. If you borrowed money at exactly the IRR to finance the project and repaid the loan from the project's cash inflows, you would end up with **zero profit and zero loss** — you would exactly break even on a present-value basis.

**Example:** A project requires an initial investment of ₹1,00,000 and yields ₹30,000 per year for 5 years. The IRR turns out to be **15.24%**. Interpretation: *"The project earns 15.24% per year on the invested capital, compounded annually. If you had to pay 15.24% interest on a loan taken to fund this project, the project's cash flows would exactly service the loan."*

---

## 1.3 The Logical Linkage: How MARR and IRR Interact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Decision Rule (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - If $\text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR}$ → **ACCEPT** the project (it earns at least the minimum required return).
> - If $\text{IRR} < \text{MARR}$ → **REJECT** the project (it fails to clear the hurdle).
> - If $\text{IRR} = \text{MARR}$ → Project is at the **break-even** threshold (indifferent acceptance).

This is illustrated schematically in the table below:

| Comparison Scenario | Decision | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| IRR = 18%, MARR = 12% | **ACCEPT** | Project earns 6% extra above the minimum hurdle — financially attractive. |
| IRR = 12%, MARR = 12% | **MARGINAL** | Indifferent — meets the cutoff but offers no safety margin. |
| IRR = 8%, MARR = 12% | **REJECT** | Project destroys value — investor is better off putting money in the next-best alternative. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting the Theoretical Foundation

The economic logic of comparing IRR with MARR stems from the **Net Present Value (NPV)** criterion. Recall that:

$$
\text{NPV}(i) = -P + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

where:
- $P$ = Initial investment (cash outflow at $t=0$)
- $\text{CF}_t$ = Net cash inflow in year $t$
- $i$ = Discount / interest rate
- $n$ = Project life in years

**Step-by-Step Logic of the NPV Profile:**

- **Step 1:** When $i = 0\%$, NPV equals the **undiscounted sum** of all cash flows (simply $\sum \text{CF}_t - P$).
- **Step 2:** As $i$ increases, the present value of future inflows shrinks because of the $(1+i)^t$ denominator.
- **Step 3:** There exists a **unique** discount rate (for conventional cash flows) at which NPV becomes zero — this is the **IRR**.
- **Step 4:** For all $i < \text{IRR}$, NPV > 0 (project is attractive). For all $i > \text{IRR}$, NPV < 0 (project destroys value).
- **Step 5:** The decision rule then reduces to: **Accept the project if MARR < IRR**, because at the MARR, NPV is still positive.

> [!TIP]
> The IRR is essentially the **x-intercept of the NPV-vs-discount-rate curve**. The MARR is a vertical line drawn on the same graph. If the IRR line lies to the right of the MARR line, accept; otherwise reject.

## 2.2 Assumptions and Limitations of IRR

1. **Reinvestment Assumption:** IRR implicitly assumes that all intermediate cash flows are reinvested at the **IRR itself** — a controversial assumption (NPV reinvests at MARR).
2. **Multiple IRR Problem:** For **non-conventional cash flows** (e.g., $-P, +CF, -CF_2, +CF_3$), the NPV equation can have **multiple real roots**, leading to multiple IRRs — making the IRR criterion ambiguous.
3. **Scale Ignorance:** IRR is a **percentage** and ignores the **absolute magnitude** of cash flows. Two projects with the same IRR can have vastly different NPVs.
4. **Mutually Exclusive Problem:** IRR **cannot** be used directly to rank mutually exclusive projects of different sizes or lives. The **Incremental Rate of Return** method is required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary Condition / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NPV Definition | $\text{NPV}(i) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Decision rule: NPV $\geq 0$ → Accept |
| 2 | IRR Definition (root of NPV) | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$ | Find $i^*$ by trial/interpolation |
| 3 | MARR Definition | $\text{MARR} = \text{WACC} + \text{Risk Premium}$ | Set by management policy |
| 4 | IRR Decision Rule | Accept iff $\text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR}$ | Single-project acceptance |
| 5 | Linear Interpolation for IRR | $i^* = i_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}(i_H - i_L)$ | $i_L$ = lower trial rate, $i_H$ = higher trial rate |
| 6 | Incremental IRR (for mutually exclusive) | $\text{IRR}_{\Delta} = i^* \text{ such that } \sum \dfrac{\Delta \text{CF}_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$ | Compare with MARR |
| 7 | Uniform-Series IRR (annuity case) | $i^* \text{ such that } P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i^*)^{-n}}{i^*}$ | $P$ = investment, $A$ = annual return |
| 8 | Modified IRR (MIRR) | $\text{MIRR} = \left( \dfrac{FV_{\text{inflows at MARR}}}{-PV_{\text{outflows}}} \right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Fixes reinvestment problem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When the cash flow is a **uniform annual series** plus an initial investment, the IRR can be obtained by equating the Present Worth of an Annuity factor to the P/A ratio: $P/A = \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Application of MARR & IRR |
|---|---|
| **Civil/Construction** | Evaluating highway, bridge, or building projects against a 12% social discount rate. |
| **Mechanical/Production** | Justifying purchase of CNC machines, robotics, or factory automation lines. |
| **Electrical/Power** | Feasibility of solar/wind farms, transmission line upgrades, substation additions. |
| **Software/IT Services** | ROI of new ERP systems, data centres, or cloud migration initiatives. |
| **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP)** | Determining viability gap funding and concession-period pricing. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The NPV Equation and the IRR Root

We start with the **fundamental capital-recovery identity**. For a project with initial investment $P$ at $t=0$ and net cash inflows $\text{CF}_t$ for $t = 1, 2, \dots, n$, the Net Present Value evaluated at discount rate $i$ is defined as the algebraic sum of the present values of all cash flows:

$$
\text{NPV}(i) = -P + \frac{\text{CF}_1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{\text{CF}_2}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{\text{CF}_3}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{\text{CF}_n}{(1+i)^n}
$$

This can be compactly written using summation notation:

$$
\text{NPV}(i) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

where $\text{CF}_0 = -P$ (initial outflow is taken as a negative cash flow at $t = 0$).

**Setting NPV equal to zero** to define the Internal Rate of Return $i^*$:

$$
\text{NPV}(i^*) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0
$$

**Textual Explanation of the Step:** The IRR is defined as that specific discount rate $i^*$ which exactly balances the present worth of all positive cash flows (inflows) against the present worth of all negative cash flows (outflows). Geometrically, $i^*$ is the unique abscissa at which the NPV profile curve crosses the horizontal axis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Form IRR for the Uniform Annuity Case

A **very common KTU board problem** involves a single initial investment $P$ followed by $n$ equal annual cash flows of magnitude $A$. For this case, the NPV equation simplifies elegantly.

**Step 1 — Write the NPV for an annuity stream:**

$$
\text{NPV}(i) = -P + A \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the bracketed sum as a finite geometric series** with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$. The sum of $n$ such terms is:

$$
S_n = a \cdot \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r} = \frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{1 - \frac{1}{1+i}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$
1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{1+i - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
S_n = \frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}} = \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the NPV equation:**

$$
\text{NPV}(i) = -P + A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

**Step 6 — The IRR $i^*$ is the value of $i$ that makes NPV zero:**

$$
0 = -P + A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i^*)^{-n}}{i^*}
$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to obtain the uniform-series IRR equation:**

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i^*)^{-n}}{i^*}
$$

**Textual Explanation of the Step:** Equation (Step 7) is the workhorse identity of engineering economics. It says that the initial investment $P$ is exactly equal to the present worth of the $n$-year annuity of $A$ when discounted at the IRR $i^*$. Since the equation is **transcendental** in $i^*$, it has no closed-form algebraic solution for $n \geq 3$ and must be solved by **trial-and-error followed by linear interpolation** (or via a financial calculator / Excel's `IRR()` function).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Interpolation Formula for IRR

Because direct algebraic solution is generally impossible, the standard KTU board technique is **linear interpolation** between two trial discount rates $i_L$ (lower) and $i_H$ (higher) such that NPV is positive at $i_L$ and negative at $i_H$.

**Step 1 — Define the two known NPV values:**

$$
\text{NPV}(i_L) = \text{NPV}_L > 0
$$
$$
\text{NPV}(i_H) = \text{NPV}_H < 0
$$

**Step 2 — Assume NPV is approximately linear** between $i_L$ and $i_H$ (this is a first-order approximation that is accurate when $i_L$ and $i_H$ are close).

**Step 3 — Apply the two-point form of a line.** The IRR lies at the point where the line crosses zero:

$$
\frac{i^* - i_L}{\text{NPV}_L - 0} = \frac{i_H - i_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $i^*$:**

$$
i^* = i_L + \frac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \cdot (i_H - i_L)
$$

This is the **canonical IRR interpolation formula** quoted in every engineering-economics textbook and used in every KTU valuation script.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Incremental Rate of Return (ΔIRR) for Mutually Exclusive Projects

When two projects A and B are **mutually exclusive** (you must pick one or the other, not both), comparing their individual IRRs against MARR is **insufficient** because the projects may differ in **scale** (initial investment) and **life**. The correct method is **Incremental Analysis**.

**Step 1 — Order the projects** by increasing initial investment. Assume $P_B > P_A$.

**Step 2 — Form the incremental cash flow stream (B − A):**

$$
\Delta P = P_B - P_A \quad (\text{extra investment required for B})
$$
$$
\Delta \text{CF}_t = \text{CF}_{B,t} - \text{CF}_{A,t} \quad \text{for } t = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the IRR of the incremental cash flow stream** $\Delta \text{IRR}$, by solving:

$$
0 = -\Delta P + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta \text{CF}_t}{(1 + \Delta \text{IRR})^t}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule for incremental analysis:**

- If $\Delta \text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR}$ → the **extra investment in B is justified** → **choose B**.
- If $\Delta \text{IRR} < \text{MARR}$ → the **extra investment in B is NOT justified** → **stick with A**.
- If A and B have the same $P$ (equal scale), then simple IRR comparison suffices.

**Textual Explanation of the Step:** Incremental analysis essentially asks: *"If I have already chosen the cheaper Project A, does it pay an additional dividend of at least MARR to spend the extra money and upgrade to Project B?"* This is the only mathematically rigorous way to rank mutually exclusive alternatives by rate of return.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Single-Project IRR

**Problem:** A precision-engineering firm is considering an automated welding cell costing **₹1,00,000**. The cell is expected to generate net savings of **₹30,000 per year** for **5 years**. The firm's MARR is **10%**. Determine the IRR and decide whether to invest.

**Solution using the uniform-series formula derived in Section 3.2:**

**Trial 1 — at $i_L = 15\%$:** Compute the P/A factor:

$$
\left( \frac{P}{A}, 15\%, 5 \right) = \frac{1 - (1.15)^{-5}}{0.15} = \frac{1 - 0.4972}{0.15} = \frac{0.5028}{0.15} = 3.3522
$$

Present worth of inflows at 15%:

$$
\text{PW}_{\text{inflow}} = 30{,}000 \times 3.3522 = 1{,}00{,}566
$$

NPV at 15%:

$$
\text{NPV}_{15\%} = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}566 = +566
$$

NPV is slightly **positive** at 15% → IRR is just above 15%.

**Trial 2 — at $i_H = 16\%$:**

$$
\left( \frac{P}{A}, 16\%, 5 \right) = \frac{1 - (1.16)^{-5}}{0.16} = \frac{1 - 0.4761}{0.16} = \frac{0.5239}{0.16} = 3.2743
$$

Present worth of inflows at 16%:

$$
\text{PW}_{\text{inflow}} = 30{,}000 \times 3.2743 = 98{,}229
$$

NPV at 16%:

$$
\text{NPV}_{16\%} = -1{,}00{,}000 + 98{,}229 = -1{,}771
$$

NPV is **negative** at 16%. So $i^*$ lies between 15% and 16%.

**Apply the linear interpolation formula (Section 3.3):**

$$
i^* = 15\% + \frac{+566}{+566 - (-1{,}771)} \cdot (16\% - 15\%)
$$

$$
i^* = 15\% + \frac{566}{566 + 1771} \cdot 1\% = 15\% + \frac{566}{2{,}337} \cdot 1\%
$$

$$
i^* = 15\% + 0.2422 \cdot 1\% = 15.24\%
$$

**Decision:** Since **IRR (15.24%) > MARR (10%)**, the automated welding cell **SHOULD BE PURCHASED**. The project earns a 5.24% premium over the hurdle rate.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Incremental Analysis

**Problem (KTU Board Style):** A manufacturer must choose between two mutually exclusive machines:
- **Machine A:** Investment = ₹1,00,000; Net annual revenue = ₹30,000; Life = 5 years.
- **Machine B:** Investment = ₹1,50,000; Net annual revenue = ₹42,000; Life = 5 years.
- **MARR = 10%** (given).

**Step A — Compute individual IRRs** (using interpolation as above):

*Machine A* (already computed): $\text{IRR}_A \approx 15.24\% > $ MARR (A is individually acceptable).

*Machine B:* Test at $i = 12\%$. P/A factor (12%, 5 yr) = 3.6048.

$$
\text{NPV}_{12\%}(B) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 42{,}000 \times 3.6048 = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}51{,}402 = +1{,}402
$$

Test at $i = 13\%$. P/A factor (13%, 5 yr) = 3.5172.

$$
\text{NPV}_{13\%}(B) = -1{,}50{,}000 + 42{,}000 \times 3.5172 = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}47{,}722 = -2{,}278
$$

Interpolate:

$$
\text{IRR}_B = 12\% + \frac{1{,}402}{1{,}402 + 2{,}278} \cdot 1\% = 12\% + 0.381\% = 12.38\%
$$

**Both A and B are individually acceptable (both IRR > MARR = 10%).** But which is *better*?

**Step B — Incremental Analysis (B − A):**

Incremental investment: $\Delta P = 1{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000$
Incremental annual revenue: $\Delta A = 42{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 12{,}000$

**Step C — Find the IRR of the incremental stream.** Test at $i = 6\%$. P/A factor (6%, 5 yr) = 4.2124.

$$
\text{NPV}_{6\%}(\Delta) = -50{,}000 + 12{,}000 \times 4.2124 = -50{,}000 + 50{,}549 = +549
$$

Test at $i = 7\%$. P/A factor (7%, 5 yr) = 4.1002.

$$
\text{NPV}_{7\%}(\Delta) = -50{,}000 + 12{,}000 \times 4.1002 = -50{,}000 + 49{,}202 = -798
$$

Interpolate:

$$
\Delta \text{IRR} = 6\% + \frac{549}{549 + 798} \cdot 1\% = 6\% + 0.41\% = 6.41\%
$$

**Step D — Decision:** Since $\Delta \text{IRR} = 6.41\% < \text{MARR} = 10\%$, the extra ₹50,000 invested in B (over and above A) earns only 6.41% — *below* the hurdle rate. **Therefore, REJECT the upgrade and choose Machine A.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often conclude that since $\text{IRR}_B$ (12.38%) > $\text{IRR}_A$ (15.24%) is *false*, Machine A is automatically better. But the proper criterion is *not* to compare individual IRRs when projects differ in scale. Always use **Incremental IRR vs MARR** for mutually exclusive alternatives.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: MARR–IRR Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Project Cash Flow Stream] --> A1[Compute NPV at i equals zero]
    A1 --> A2[Define NPV function of i]
    A2 --> A3[Find i star where NPV equals zero]
    A3 --> A4[This i star is the IRR]
    A4 --> A5{Is IRR greater than or equal to MARR?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Project ACCEPTED]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Project REJECTED]
    A6 --> A8[Stop: Proceed to Implementation]
    A7 --> A9[Stop: Do Not Invest]

    B0[Management Policy Input] --> B1[Determine WACC from Capital Structure]
    B1 --> B2[Add Project Risk Premium]
    B2 --> B3[Add Inflation Premium]
    B3 --> A0

    C0[Alternative: Mutually Exclusive Project Pair] --> C1[Order by Increasing Investment]
    C1 --> C2[Form Incremental Cash Flow Delta]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Incremental IRR]
    C3 --> C4{Is Delta IRR greater than MARR?}
    C4 -- Yes --> C5[Choose Higher Investment Project]
    C4 -- No --> C6[Choose Lower Investment Project]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The top branch shows the **single-project IRR decision flow**. The left branch (B-series) shows the **upstream determination of MARR** from the firm's cost of capital. The bottom branch (C-series) shows the **incremental analysis pathway** required when alternatives are mutually exclusive.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: NPV Profile and IRR Location

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[NPV positive region] -->|i less than IRR| P2[IRR point NPV equals zero]
    P2 -->|i greater than IRR| P3[NPV negative region]
    P4[Vertical MARR line] --> P5{Is MARR to left of IRR?}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Project ACCEPTED]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Project REJECTED]
    P2 --- P4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow: IRR Computation Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Identify all Cash Flows CF t for t = 0 to n] --> Q2[Guess a trial rate iL]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute NPV at iL]
    Q3 --> Q4{NPV positive at iL?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Guess a higher trial rate iH]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Guess a lower trial rate iL]
    Q5 --> Q7[Compute NPV at iH]
    Q7 --> Q8{NPV negative at iH?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10[Repeat until sign change occurs]
    Q6 --> Q3
    Q10 --> Q5
    Q9 --> Q11[Obtain Approximate IRR]
    Q11 --> Q12[Compare with MARR]
    Q12 --> Q13{Make Accept Reject Decision}
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram precisely mirrors the algorithmic procedure that KTU examiners expect students to *verbally describe* in 7–10 mark descriptive questions on the IRR computation methodology.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR). List any two factors that influence its selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** is the lowest rate of return that a firm or investor is willing to accept from a proposed capital investment project, below which the project will be deemed financially unacceptable. It acts as the benchmark hurdle rate used in conjunction with evaluation methods such as NPV, IRR, and Benefit-Cost Ratio.

**Factors influencing MARR:**
1. **Cost of Capital (WACC):** The weighted average cost of equity, debt, and preference capital of the firm sets the baseline.
2. **Project Risk Premium:** Risky projects (e.g., R&D, new technology) carry a higher premium over the WACC.
3. **Opportunity Cost of Capital:** Return available from the next-best alternative investment.
4. **Inflation:** Expected rise in the general price level erodes the real value of returns.

*(Any two well-explained points = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sessional]`
**State the IRR decision rule. Why is IRR compared with MARR rather than with the cost of capital directly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**IRR Decision Rule:** A project should be **accepted if its IRR is greater than or equal to MARR**, and rejected if IRR is less than MARR.

$$
\text{Accept if } i^* \geq \text{MARR}; \quad \text{Reject if } i^* < \text{MARR}
$$

**Why MARR and not just cost of capital:**
- MARR includes the cost of capital **plus** an explicit **project-risk premium**, which adjusts for the unique risk profile of the engineering project under evaluation.
- A project may have a higher return than the cost of capital yet still be insufficient given the risk involved — MARR captures both dimensions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer ANY ONE FULL QUESTION** from the choice provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A manufacturing company is evaluating two independent projects A and B. The cash flows are:**

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −2,00,000 | −3,00,000 |
| 1 | 60,000 | 1,00,000 |
| 2 | 70,000 | 1,10,000 |
| 3 | 80,000 | 1,20,000 |
| 4 | 90,000 | 1,30,000 |
| 5 | 1,00,000 | 1,00,000 |

**The firm's MARR is 12%.**

**(a) Calculate the IRR of each project using linear interpolation and determine whether each project is acceptable independently. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the projects are mutually exclusive, perform incremental IRR analysis and recommend the optimal choice. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**For Project A:** Use trial-and-error.

**Trial at 18%:** P/A factors must be applied year-wise. Compute PW of inflows:

$$
\text{PW}_{18\%}(A) = \frac{60{,}000}{1.18} + \frac{70{,}000}{1.18^2} + \frac{80{,}000}{1.18^3} + \frac{90{,}000}{1.18^4} + \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.18^5}
$$

$$
= 50{,}847 + 50{,}272 + 48{,}772 + 46{,}624 + 43{,}711 = 2{,}40{,}226
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{18\%}(A) = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}226 = +40{,}226 \quad (\text{positive})
$$

**Trial at 25%:** Compute PW of inflows:

$$
\text{PW}_{25\%}(A) = \frac{60{,}000}{1.25} + \frac{70{,}000}{1.5625} + \frac{80{,}000}{1.9531} + \frac{90{,}000}{2.4414} + \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{3.0518}
$$

$$
= 48{,}000 + 44{,}800 + 40{,}960 + 36{,}864 + 32{,}768 = 2{,}03{,}392
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{25\%}(A) = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}03{,}392 = +3{,}392 \quad (\text{positive})
$$

**Trial at 26%:** Compute PW of inflows:

$$
\text{PW}_{26\%}(A) = \frac{60{,}000}{1.26} + \frac{70{,}000}{1.5876} + \frac{80{,}000}{2.0004} + \frac{90{,}000}{2.5205} + \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{3.1758}
$$

$$
\approx 47{,}619 + 44{,}092 + 39{,}992 + 35{,}707 + 31{,}489 = 1{,}98{,}899
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{26\%}(A) = -2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}98{,}899 = -1{,}101 \quad (\text{negative})
$$

**Interpolate between 25% and 26%:**

$$
\text{IRR}_A = 25\% + \frac{3{,}392}{3{,}392 + 1{,}101} \times 1\% = 25\% + \frac{3{,}392}{4{,}493} \times 1\% \approx 25\% + 0.755\% \approx 25.76\%
$$

**Conclusion for A:** $\text{IRR}_A \approx 25.76\% > \text{MARR} = 12\%$ → **ACCEPT Project A**.

**For Project B:** Similar procedure (omitted here for brevity, but shown in valuation keys).
Expected result: $\text{IRR}_B \approx 22\text{–}24\%$, which is also > MARR → **ACCEPT Project B independently**.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

Since the projects are now **mutually exclusive**, we must perform **incremental analysis** (B − A):

| Year | ΔCF (B − A) (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | −1,00,000 |
| 1 | 40,000 |
| 2 | 40,000 |
| 3 | 40,000 |
| 4 | 40,000 |
| 5 | 0 |

**Trial at 30%:** P/A (30%, 4 yr) = 2.1662.

$$
\text{PW}_{30\%}(\Delta) = 40{,}000 \times 2.1662 = 86{,}648
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{30\%}(\Delta) = -1{,}00{,}000 + 86{,}648 = -13{,}352 \quad (\text{negative})
$$

**Trial at 25%:** P/A (25%, 4 yr) = 2.3616.

$$
\text{PW}_{25\%}(\Delta) = 40{,}000 \times 2.3616 = 94{,}464
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{25\%}(\Delta) = -1{,}00{,}000 + 94{,}464 = -5{,}536 \quad (\text{negative})
$$

**Trial at 20%:** P/A (20%, 4 yr) = 2.5887.

$$
\text{PW}_{20\%}(\Delta) = 40{,}000 \times 2.5887 = 1{,}03{,}548
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{20\%}(\Delta) = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}03{,}548 = +3{,}548 \quad (\text{positive})
$$

**Interpolate between 20% and 25%:**

$$
\Delta \text{IRR} = 20\% + \frac{3{,}548}{3{,}548 + 5{,}536} \times 5\% = 20\% + \frac{3{,}548}{9{,}084} \times 5\% \approx 20\% + 1.95\% = 21.95\%
$$

**Decision:** $\Delta \text{IRR} \approx 21.95\% > \text{MARR} = 12\%$ → the extra ₹1,00,000 invested in B (over A) earns 21.95% — above the hurdle rate. **Therefore, ACCEPT the upgrade: choose Project B.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Explain the concept of Internal Rate of Return (IRR) with a suitable cash-flow diagram. Discuss any four limitations of the IRR method. (7 marks)**

**(b) A solar power installation requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000. Annual net cash inflows are ₹1,20,000 for 6 years. The investor's MARR is 9%. Determine the IRR by linear interpolation and comment on the project's acceptability. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Concept of IRR:**

The Internal Rate of Return (IRR) is the discount rate $i^*$ that makes the Net Present Value (NPV) of a project equal to zero. It is the rate of return earned on the unrecovered balance of an investment, such that the terminal value of the inflows equals the original investment.

**Cash Flow Diagram (described verbally for text):**

$$
\text{Time axis: } t = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
$$
- At $t=0$: downward arrow of magnitude $P$ (initial investment, outflow).
- At $t=1$ to $t=6$: upward arrows of equal magnitude $A$ (annual net inflows).
- IRR is the rate at which the present value of all upward arrows exactly equals the downward arrow at $t=0$.

**Limitations of IRR:**

1. **Multiple IRR problem:** For non-conventional cash flows (sign changes more than once), the NPV equation has multiple real roots, making the IRR ambiguous.
2. **Reinvestment rate assumption:** IRR assumes cash flows are reinvested at the IRR, which is unrealistic; NPV reinvests at MARR.
3. **Scale problem / Mutually exclusive projects:** IRR cannot rank projects of different sizes correctly; incremental analysis is required.
4. **No real solution:** For certain cash-flow patterns, the NPV equation may have no real root (e.g., a project with low early returns and high later returns may have no mathematically real IRR).
5. **Borrowing vs lending confusion:** The IRR equation does not distinguish whether the project is generating or consuming funds at various points — interpretation errors can occur.

*(Any four explained = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:** $P = ₹5{,}00{,}000$; $A = ₹1{,}20{,}000$; $n = 6$ years; MARR = 9%.

**Step 1 — Compute the P/A ratio that must equal the IRR:**

$$
\frac{P}{A} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} = 4.1667
$$

**Step 2 — Find two trial rates** where the P/A factor straddles 4.1667.

**Trial at $i_L = 7\%$:** P/A (7%, 6 yr) = 4.7665

$$
\text{PW}_{7\%} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.7665 = 5{,}71{,}980
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{7\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}71{,}980 = +71{,}980 \quad (\text{positive})
$$

**Trial at $i_H = 12\%$:** P/A (12%, 6 yr) = 4.1114

$$
\text{PW}_{12\%} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4.1114 = 4{,}93{,}368
$$

$$
\text{NPV}_{12\%} = -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}93{,}368 = -6{,}632 \quad (\text{negative})
$$

**Step 3 — Apply linear interpolation:**

$$
\text{IRR} = 7\% + \frac{71{,}980}{71{,}980 + 6{,}632} \times (12\% - 7\%)
$$

$$
\text{IRR} = 7\% + \frac{71{,}980}{78{,}612} \times 5\% = 7\% + 0.9156 \times 5\% = 7\% + 4.58\% = 11.58\%
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:** $\text{IRR} = 11.58\% > \text{MARR} = 9\%$ → **ACCEPT the solar power installation**.

**Interpretation:** The project earns 11.58% per year on the capital deployed, which exceeds the 9% minimum required — it adds ₹71,980 in present-value terms at the 7% lower trial and only marginally falls short at 12%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in MARR–IRR Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compare IRR with MARR (not cost of capital).** A 1–2 mark penalty is typical if the decision rule is mis-stated.
> 2. **Using individual IRRs to compare mutually exclusive projects.** This is the most common conceptual error. Always use **incremental IRR vs MARR**. Marks lost: typically **3–4 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Sign errors in cash flows** when forming incremental streams (B − A): the difference must be computed **year-by-year**, not just summed.
> 4. **Trial rate choice for interpolation:** If $i_L$ and $i_H$ are too far apart, the linear approximation is poor and the answer is marked as approximate. Choose rates that bracket the IRR tightly (within 2–3 percentage points).
> 5. **Forgetting to state the decision explicitly:** Always end with "ACCEPT" or "REJECT" and justify with the IRR-MARR comparison.
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Always express IRR and MARR in the same compounding basis (annual nominal). Mismatch loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MARR** is the **hurdle rate** set by management, used as the benchmark for project acceptance. It is **not derived from project data** — it is an **input** to the analysis.
- **IRR** is the **root of the NPV equation** — it is the **output** of project cash-flow analysis.
- **Decision Rule:** Accept iff $\text{IRR} \geq \text{MARR}$.
- **For mutually exclusive projects** of different scale, **Incremental IRR vs MARR** is mandatory — never compare individual IRRs directly.
- **MARR components:** Risk-free rate + Inflation premium + Project-risk premium ≈ WACC + Risk premium.
- **IRR interpolation formula:** $i^* = i_L + \dfrac{\text{NPV}_L}{\text{NPV}_L - \text{NPV}_H} \times (i_H - i_L)$.
- **Uniform-annuity IRR equation:** $P = A \times \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$.
- **Multiple IRR problem** occurs when cash flows change sign more than once. KTU often tests this as a 3-mark "limitation" question.
- **Reinvestment assumption:** IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR; NPV assumes reinvestment at MARR. MARR is generally lower → NPV is more conservative.
- **MIRR (Modified IRR)** overcomes the reinvestment limitation by using MARR as the reinvestment rate. Mention it for bonus marks in descriptive answers.
- **KTU Standard Practice Rate:** When MARR is not explicitly given, many KTU problems default to **10% or 12%** — always read the problem statement carefully.
- **Always show:** (i) Two trial rates, (ii) NPV at each, (iii) Sign change, (iv) Interpolation, (v) Decision with MARR comparison. This 5-step structure is what the valuation key expects.
- **Common error in incremental analysis:** Forgetting to compute $\Delta P$ correctly — it is $P_{\text{higher}} - P_{\text{lower}}$, taken as a positive outflow.
- **Engineering context recall:** MARR is often called the **"hurdle rate"** in finance journals and the **"cutoff rate"** in older engineering-economics textbooks. All three terms are synonymous for KTU purposes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) Analysis** is a capital budgeting decision-making technique used to economically evaluate and rank **mutually exclusive alternatives** by comparing the *additional* (incremental) investment required to move from a lower-cost option to a higher-cost option, against the *additional* (incremental) benefits generated by that extra investment.

Mathematically, IRoR ($i^*$) is the discount rate at which the **incremental net cash flow** has a Net Present Worth (NPW) equal to zero:

$$\text{NPW}_{\text{increment}} = 0 = -\Delta I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \Delta \text{CF}_t (1+i^*)^{-t}$$

For **Public Projects** (government, infrastructure, social welfare schemes), IRoR analysis is integrated with the **Benefit–Cost (B–C) Ratio** framework because public investments are justified on the basis of *social welfare* (Total Economic Value to society) rather than private profit alone. The decision rule compares the **Incremental B–C Ratio** against a benchmark of **1.0** (or against the social MARR / social discount rate prescribed by the planning commission).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For public projects, the "return" being measured is not pure financial profit — it is the **Incremental Net Social Benefit (NSB)** to the community, discounted at the **Social Discount Rate (SDR)**, typically 8%–12% in Indian public investment appraisal (Planning Commission / NITI Aayog guidelines).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a government engineer deciding between two road projects for Kerala:
- **Alternative A:** A 2-lane coastal highway costing ₹50 Cr.
- **Alternative B:** A 4-lane expressway costing ₹80 Cr.

The intuitive question is **not** "which road is profitable?" (it is a public good). The real question is:

> *"If we already plan to build the 2-lane road, is the EXTRA ₹30 Cr we spend on the 4-lane upgrade worth the EXTRA social benefit (faster travel, fewer accidents, higher trade volume) it produces?"*

That "extra return on the extra rupee" is exactly the **Incremental Rate of Return**. We do not judge each project in isolation; we judge the *upgrade decision*. This is the heart of IRoR analysis for public projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just pick the project with the highest absolute B-C ratio?**
> Because a smaller project can have a *deceptively high* B-C ratio (e.g., 1.5) while the larger project — even with a B-C of 1.2 — may deliver more *total* net social benefit. IRoR correctly channels investment toward the alternative that maximizes *aggregate* public welfare, not the one that just looks "most efficient per rupee."

---

## 1.3 Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** | The lowest acceptable rate; for public projects this is the **Social Discount Rate (SDR)**. |
| **Do-Nothing Alternative (DN)** | The baseline of "no investment" — used to compute individual B-C ratios. |
| **Incremental Cash Flow ($\Delta \text{CF}$)** | Difference in cash flow between the higher-cost and lower-cost alternative. |
| **Net Social Benefit (NSB)** | Total discounted social benefits minus total discounted social costs. |
| **Mutually Exclusive Alternatives** | Selecting one automatically rejects the others (e.g., choose either 2-lane or 4-lane road, not both). |
| **Independent Alternatives** | Each can be chosen or rejected independently (no IRoR needed across them). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Conventional ROR Fails for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives

A common student error is to compute the simple ROR (or B-C ratio) of every alternative and pick the highest. This **fails** for two reasons:

1. **Reinvestment Assumption Problem:** A higher ROR on a smaller base may yield a smaller *absolute* return than a lower ROR on a larger base.
2. **Scale of Investment Problem:** Public projects are lumpy; a small project cannot replicate a large one's total welfare contribution.

Hence, we must perform a **pairwise incremental comparison** in increasing order of capital outlay.

---

## 2.2 The IRoR Decision Logic (Public Project Context)

The methodology has **five mandatory steps**:

**Step 1 — Order the alternatives by increasing Initial Investment ($I_0$).**

**Step 2 — Eliminate the "Do-Nothing" by checking each alternative individually against the social MARR.**
- For a revenue/service project: compute ROR; accept if ROR ≥ MARR.
- For a pure public project (no revenue, only social benefits): compute B–C ratio; accept if B–C ≥ 1.0.

**Step 3 — Compare the lowest-investment viable alternative with the next-higher one using the Incremental B–C Ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{B-A} = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \Delta B_t (1+i)^{-t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \Delta C_t (1+i)^{-t}}$$

where $i$ = social discount rate (SDR), and $\Delta B_t, \Delta C_t$ are incremental benefits and costs of alternative B over A.

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
- If $\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{B-A} \geq 1.0$ → **choose the higher-cost alternative B** (extra rupee produces more than one extra rupee of social benefit).
- If $\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{B-A} < 1.0$ → **retain the lower-cost alternative A**.

**Step 5 — Propagate the Winner** to be compared with the next-higher alternative. Continue until the topmost alternative is reached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent IRoR formulation:** Find the discount rate $i^*$ that makes the incremental NSB = 0:
> $$\text{NSB}_{\text{increment}} = \sum \Delta B_t (1+i^*)^{-t} - \sum \Delta C_t (1+i^*)^{-t} = 0$$
> If $i^* \geq \text{SDR}$, accept the higher-cost option.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{NPW} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \text{CF}_t (1+i)^{-t}$ | Net Present Worth at discount rate $i$ | Currency (₹); must be evaluated at $i = \text{SDR}$ for public projects |
| 2 | $\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{\text{conventional}} = \frac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}}$ | Conventional B-C ratio | Dimensionless; accept if $\geq 1$ |
| 3 | $\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{\text{incremental}} = \frac{\sum \Delta B_t (P/F, i, t)}{\sum \Delta C_t (P/F, i, t)}$ | Incremental B-C ratio between two alternatives | Dimensionless; accept the higher-cost if $\geq 1$ |
| 4 | $\Delta \text{NPW} = 0 \Rightarrow i = i^*$ | IRoR definition equation | Discount rate |
| 5 | $P = A \cdot \left[\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}\right] = A(P/A, i, n)$ | Uniform series present worth factor | Pure number |
| 6 | $\text{ENPV} = (\text{B-C Ratio} - 1) \times \text{PV of Costs}$ | Expected NPV (Marshall's surrogate) | Currency (₹) |
| 7 | $\text{Net Social Benefit} = \sum B_t (P/F, i, t) - \sum C_t (P/F, i, t)$ | Total welfare measure | Currency (₹) |
| 8 | MARR (Public) | **Social Discount Rate (SDR)** | Typically **8% – 12%** in India |
| 9 | MARR (Private) | **Cost of Capital / Opportunity Cost** | Typically **12% – 18%** for Indian firms |
| 10 | Constraint on ranking | Always sort by **increasing $I_0$** before incremental comparison | Mandatory sequencing rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Never use the absolute B-C ratio of two alternatives directly to choose. *Always* perform the **incremental B-C test** after sorting by initial investment. This is the most-frequently-tested concept in Module 2.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Public Works Departments (PWD):** Road, bridge, and irrigation project appraisal in Kerala uses incremental B-C analysis at 8% social discount rate.
- **NITI Aayog / Planning Commission:** Mandates IRoR-based ranking of alternatives in Detailed Project Reports (DPRs).
- **World Bank / ADB Funded Projects:** Mandatory incremental cost-effectiveness analysis for infrastructure loans.
- **Smart City & AMRUT Missions:** Selection between conventional vs. smart-tech alternatives is done on incremental B-C basis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the IRoR Equation

Starting from the **Net Social Benefit (NSB)** identity for an alternative:

$$\text{NSB}(i) = \sum_{t=0}^{n} B_t (1+i)^{-t} - \sum_{t=0}^{n} C_t (1+i)^{-t}$$

For two mutually exclusive alternatives $X$ (lower cost) and $Y$ (higher cost), the **incremental NSB** is:

$$\Delta \text{NSB}(i) = \text{NSB}_Y(i) - \text{NSB}_X(i)$$

$$\Delta \text{NSB}(i) = \left[\sum B_t^Y (1+i)^{-t} - \sum C_t^Y (1+i)^{-t}\right] - \left[\sum B_t^X (1+i)^{-t} - \sum C_t^X (1+i)^{-t}\right]$$

$$\Delta \text{NSB}(i) = \sum (B_t^Y - B_t^X)(1+i)^{-t} - \sum (C_t^Y - C_t^X)(1+i)^{-t}$$

$$\Delta \text{NSB}(i) = \sum \Delta B_t (1+i)^{-t} - \sum \Delta C_t (1+i)^{-t}$$

By definition, the **Incremental Rate of Return ($i^*$)** is the discount rate that sets $\Delta \text{NSB}(i) = 0$:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \Delta B_t (1+i^*)^{-t} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \Delta C_t (1+i^*)^{-t}$$

This can equivalently be written in the **Incremental B–C Ratio** form:

$$\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{\text{inc}} = \frac{\sum \Delta B_t (1+i)^{-t}}{\sum \Delta C_t (1+i)^{-t}} = 1.0 \quad \text{at } i = i^*$$

If we evaluate at the **Social Discount Rate (SDR)** and find $(B/C)_{\text{inc}} \geq 1$, then $i^* \geq \text{SDR}$, and the extra investment in $Y$ is socially justified.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (End-to-End IRoR Analysis of a Public Project)

### Problem Statement
The Kerala Public Works Department is evaluating two mutually exclusive bridge designs across the Periyar River:

| Parameter | Alt A (2-Lane) | Alt B (4-Lane) | Alt C (6-Lane Expressway) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment ($I_0$) | ₹60 Lakh | ₹100 Lakh | ₹160 Lakh |
| Annual Social Benefit ($B_t$) | ₹14 Lakh | ₹24 Lakh | ₹34 Lakh |
| Annual O&M Cost ($C_t$) | ₹3 Lakh | ₹5 Lakh | ₹7 Lakh |
| Useful Life ($n$) | 20 years | 20 years | 20 years |
| Salvage Value (SV) | ₹10 Lakh | ₹15 Lakh | ₹20 Lakh |

**Social Discount Rate (SDR) = 10%** (per NITI Aayog norm for public infrastructure).

**Required:** Use Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) / Incremental B-C analysis to select the best alternative.

---

### Step 1 — Order by Increasing Initial Investment

$$A\ (\text{₹}60\text{L}) \;\longrightarrow\; B\ (\text{₹}100\text{L}) \;\longrightarrow\; C\ (\text{₹}160\text{L})$$

---

### Step 2 — Convert Annual Cash Flows to Present Worth

For uniform annual series over $n = 20$ years at $i = 10\%$, the **Present Worth Factor** is:

$$\left(\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 20\right) = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}}$$

Compute $(1.10)^{20}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^{20} &= 6.7275 \\
\left(\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 20\right) &= \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} = 8.5136
\end{aligned}$$

The **Present Worth of Salvage Value** (received at end of year 20):

$$\left(\frac{P}{F}, 10\%, 20\right) = \frac{1}{6.7275} = 0.1486$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute PV of Benefits and Costs for Each Alternative

**Alternative A:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of Benefits}_A &= 14 \times 8.5136 = ₹119.19 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV of O\&M Costs}_A &= 3 \times 8.5136 = ₹25.54 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV of Salvage}_A &= 10 \times 0.1486 = ₹1.486 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Total PV of Costs}_A &= 60 + 25.54 - 1.486 = ₹84.05 \text{ Lakh} \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_A &= \frac{119.19}{84.05} = 1.418 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept A (vs. Do-Nothing)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative B:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of Benefits}_B &= 24 \times 8.5136 = ₹204.33 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV of O\&M Costs}_B &= 5 \times 8.5136 = ₹42.57 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV of Salvage}_B &= 15 \times 0.1486 = ₹2.229 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Total PV of Costs}_B &= 100 + 42.57 - 2.229 = ₹140.34 \text{ Lakh} \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_B &= \frac{204.33}{140.34} = 1.456 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept B (vs. Do-Nothing)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative C:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of Benefits}_C &= 34 \times 8.5136 = ₹289.46 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV of O\&M Costs}_C &= 7 \times 8.5136 = ₹59.60 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV of Salvage}_C &= 20 \times 0.1486 = ₹2.972 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Total PV of Costs}_C &= 160 + 59.60 - 2.972 = ₹216.63 \text{ Lakh} \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_C &= \frac{289.46}{216.63} = 1.336 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept C (vs. Do-Nothing)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three are individually viable. The IRoR test now decides which one maximizes social welfare.

---

### Step 4 — Incremental Analysis: B vs. A

Compute incremental benefits and costs of B over A:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta B_{B-A} &= 24 - 14 = ₹10 \text{ Lakh/year} \\
\Delta C_{B-A} &= 5 - 3 = ₹2 \text{ Lakh/year} \\
\Delta I_{B-A} &= 100 - 60 = ₹40 \text{ Lakh} \\
\Delta \text{SV}_{B-A} &= 15 - 10 = ₹5 \text{ Lakh (at } t=20\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Present worth of incremental values at 10%:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(\Delta B) &= 10 \times 8.5136 = ₹85.14 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV}(\Delta C \text{ O\&M}) &= 2 \times 8.5136 = ₹17.03 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV}(\Delta \text{SV}) &= 5 \times 0.1486 = ₹0.743 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Total PV of } \Delta \text{Costs} &= 40 + 17.03 - 0.743 = ₹56.29 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Incremental B-C Ratio (B over A):**

$$\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{B-A} = \frac{85.14}{56.29} = 1.512 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; > 1.0$$

✅ **Decision: Choose B (replaces A as the current best).**

---

### Step 5 — Incremental Analysis: C vs. B

Compute incremental values of C over B:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta B_{C-B} &= 34 - 24 = ₹10 \text{ Lakh/year} \\
\Delta C_{C-B} &= 7 - 5 = ₹2 \text{ Lakh/year} \\
\Delta I_{C-B} &= 160 - 100 = ₹60 \text{ Lakh} \\
\Delta \text{SV}_{C-B} &= 20 - 15 = ₹5 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Present worth at 10%:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(\Delta B) &= 10 \times 8.5136 = ₹85.14 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV}(\Delta C \text{ O\&M}) &= 2 \times 8.5136 = ₹17.03 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{PV}(\Delta \text{SV}) &= 5 \times 0.1486 = ₹0.743 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{Total PV of } \Delta \text{Costs} &= 60 + 17.03 - 0.743 = ₹76.29 \text{ Lakh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Incremental B-C Ratio (C over B):**

$$\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{C-B} = \frac{85.14}{76.29} = 1.116 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; > 1.0$$

✅ **Decision: Choose C (replaces B as the current best).**

---

### Step 6 — Final Decision

Since C is the topmost alternative and the incremental test over B passed, **Alternative C (6-Lane Expressway) is the economically optimal choice**.

**Verification using NSB at i = 10%:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NSB}_A &= 119.19 - 84.05 = ₹35.14 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{NSB}_B &= 204.33 - 140.34 = ₹63.99 \text{ Lakh} \\
\text{NSB}_C &= 289.46 - 216.63 = ₹72.83 \text{ Lakh} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Maximum} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The maximum NSB rule and the IRoR rule both confirm **Alternative C**.

---

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix of the Example

| Comparison | $\Delta$ Benefit PV (₹L) | $\Delta$ Cost PV (₹L) | Inc. B/C | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A vs. Do-Nothing | 119.19 | 84.05 | 1.418 | A viable |
| B vs. Do-Nothing | 204.33 | 140.34 | 1.456 | B viable |
| C vs. Do-Nothing | 289.46 | 216.63 | 1.336 | C viable |
| **B vs. A** | 85.14 | 56.29 | **1.512** | **Choose B** |
| **C vs. B** | 85.14 | 76.29 | **1.116** | **Choose C** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of IRoR Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT Mutually Exclusive Public Alternatives A, B, C with Cost Benefit Life data"] --> nodeB["STEP 1 Order alternatives by ascending Initial Investment I0"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 2 Compute Conventional B-C Ratio for each alternative vs Do Nothing"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"STEP 2 Check B-C Ratio of each alternative greater than 1.0?"}
    nodeD -- "No" --> nodeE["Eliminate that alternative"]
    nodeD -- "Yes" --> nodeF["Retain alternative in feasible set"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["STEP 3 Take lowest cost viable alternative as Defender"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 4 Compute Incremental B-C Ratio Higher cost alternative versus Defender"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"STEP 5 Is Incremental B-C Ratio greater than or equal to 1.0?"}
    nodeI -- "No" --> nodeJ["Retain Defender reject higher cost option"]
    nodeI -- "Yes" --> nodeK["Promote higher cost option to new Defender"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL{"More alternatives remaining?"}
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL -- "Yes" --> nodeH
    nodeL -- "No" --> nodeM["STEP 6 Final Defender is the Economically Optimal Public Project"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["OUTPUT Recommended alternative with maximum Net Social Benefit"]
```
