# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR). Why is it preferred over the conventional ROR method for evaluating mutually exclusive public projects?*

**Model Answer:**

Incremental Rate of Return (IRoR) is the discount rate at which the net present worth of the *incremental* cash flow between two mutually exclusive alternatives equals zero. For public projects, this incremental analysis is performed on the basis of social benefits and costs discounted at the **Social Discount Rate (SDR)**, with the decision rule that the higher-cost alternative is selected only if the **Incremental B–C Ratio ≥ 1.0**.

It is preferred over conventional ROR because:
1. Conventional ROR compares each project to a "do-nothing" baseline, which can mislead when projects differ vastly in scale.
2. A smaller project may show a high B-C ratio while delivering less *total* social welfare.
3. IRoR correctly channels scarce public capital toward the alternative that produces the greatest *additional* benefit per *additional* rupee invested, ensuring maximum Net Social Benefit (NSB).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List the steps involved in the IRoR analysis of mutually exclusive public project alternatives. State the decision rule at each incremental comparison.*

**Model Answer:**

**Steps:**
1. **Order** all alternatives by increasing Initial Investment ($I_0$).
2. **Eliminate** any alternative with conventional B-C ratio < 1.0 (compared to "do-nothing").
3. Set the **lowest-cost viable alternative** as the current Defender.
4. Compute the **Incremental B-C Ratio** of the next higher-cost alternative versus the Defender.
5. Apply the **Decision Rule:**
   - If $(B/C)_{\text{inc}} \geq 1.0$ → adopt the higher-cost option as the new Defender.
   - If $(B/C)_{\text{inc}} < 1.0$ → reject the higher-cost option, retain the Defender.
6. **Repeat** Step 4–5 until all alternatives have been tested; the final Defender is the optimal choice.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A State Government is evaluating three mutually exclusive water-supply schemes. The data are:

| Parameter | Alt X (Conventional) | Alt Y (Semi-Automated) | Alt Z (Fully Automated) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost (₹ Crore) | 30 | 55 | 80 |
| Annual Social Benefit (₹ Crore) | 6 | 12 | 17 |
| Annual O&M Cost (₹ Crore) | 1.5 | 2.5 | 3.0 |
| Life (years) | 15 | 15 | 15 |
| Salvage Value (₹ Crore) | 3 | 5 | 7 |

Social Discount Rate (SDR) = 9%. Using IRoR / Incremental B-C analysis, select the best alternative.

#### Part (a) — Compute Conventional B-C Ratios and Perform Incremental Analysis [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Present Worth Factors at 9%, n = 15:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.09)^{15} &= 3.6425 \\
\left(\frac{P}{A}, 9\%, 15\right) &= \frac{3.6425 - 1}{0.09 \times 3.6425} = \frac{2.6425}{0.3278} = 8.0607 \\
\left(\frac{P}{F}, 9\%, 15\right) &= \frac{1}{3.6425} = 0.2745
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — PV of Benefits and Costs (in ₹ Crore):**

**Alternative X:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(B)_X &= 6 \times 8.0607 = 48.36 \\
\text{PV}(C \text{ O\&M})_X &= 1.5 \times 8.0607 = 12.09 \\
\text{PV}(\text{SV})_X &= 3 \times 0.2745 = 0.824 \\
\text{Total PV}(C)_X &= 30 + 12.09 - 0.824 = 41.27 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_X &= \frac{48.36}{41.27} = 1.172 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative Y:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(B)_Y &= 12 \times 8.0607 = 96.73 \\
\text{PV}(C \text{ O\&M})_Y &= 2.5 \times 8.0607 = 20.15 \\
\text{PV}(\text{SV})_Y &= 5 \times 0.2745 = 1.373 \\
\text{Total PV}(C)_Y &= 55 + 20.15 - 1.373 = 73.78 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_Y &= \frac{96.73}{73.78} = 1.311 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative Z:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(B)_Z &= 17 \times 8.0607 = 137.03 \\
\text{PV}(C \text{ O\&M})_Z &= 3.0 \times 8.0607 = 24.18 \\
\text{PV}(\text{SV})_Z &= 7 \times 0.2745 = 1.922 \\
\text{Total PV}(C)_Z &= 80 + 24.18 - 1.922 = 102.26 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_Z &= \frac{137.03}{102.26} = 1.340 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) — Incremental Comparisons and Final Selection [7 Marks]

**Step 3 — Order by Investment:** X (30) → Y (55) → Z (80). Defender = X.

**Incremental Y vs. X:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta B &= 12 - 6 = 6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 6 \times 8.0607 = 48.36 \\
\Delta C_{\text{O\&M}} &= 2.5 - 1.5 = 1.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 1.0 \times 8.0607 = 8.06 \\
\Delta I_0 &= 55 - 30 = 25 \\
\Delta \text{SV} &= 5 - 3 = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 2 \times 0.2745 = 0.549 \\
\text{Total PV of } \Delta C &= 25 + 8.06 - 0.549 = 32.51 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{Y-X} &= \frac{48.36}{32.51} = 1.488 > 1 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

→ **Promote Y as new Defender.**

**Incremental Z vs. Y:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta B &= 17 - 12 = 5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 5 \times 8.0607 = 40.30 \\
\Delta C_{\text{O\&M}} &= 3.0 - 2.5 = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 0.5 \times 8.0607 = 4.03 \\
\Delta I_0 &= 80 - 55 = 25 \\
\Delta \text{SV} &= 7 - 5 = 2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 2 \times 0.2745 = 0.549 \\
\text{Total PV of } \Delta C &= 25 + 4.03 - 0.549 = 28.48 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{Z-Y} &= \frac{40.30}{28.48} = 1.415 > 1 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

→ **Promote Z as new Defender. No more alternatives.**

**Final Recommendation:** **Alternative Z (Fully Automated Scheme)** is the economically optimal public investment.

**Verification via NSB:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NSB}_X &= 48.36 - 41.27 = 7.09 \\
\text{NSB}_Y &= 96.73 - 73.78 = 22.95 \\
\text{NSB}_Z &= 137.03 - 102.26 = 34.77 \;\;\text{(Maximum)} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

A municipality is selecting among three mutually exclusive solid-waste management technologies. The data are:

| Parameter | Alt P (Composting) | Alt Q (Incineration) | Alt R (Bio-Methanation) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment (₹ Lakh) | 40 | 75 | 110 |
| Annual Social Benefit (₹ Lakh) | 9 | 19 | 26 |
| Annual O&M Cost (₹ Lakh) | 2.0 | 4.0 | 5.0 |
| Life (years) | 10 | 10 | 10 |
| Salvage (₹ Lakh) | 5 | 8 | 12 |

Social Discount Rate = 10%. Use IRoR / Incremental B-C analysis to recommend the best technology.

#### Part (a) — Conventional B-C Ratios at 10% [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Factors at 10%, n = 10:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.10)^{10} &= 2.5937 \\
\left(\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 10\right) &= \frac{2.5937 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.5937} = \frac{1.5937}{0.25937} = 6.1446 \\
\left(\frac{P}{F}, 10\%, 10\right) &= \frac{1}{2.5937} = 0.3855
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative P:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(B)_P &= 9 \times 6.1446 = 55.30 \\
\text{PV}(C \text{ O\&M})_P &= 2.0 \times 6.1446 = 12.29 \\
\text{PV}(\text{SV})_P &= 5 \times 0.3855 = 1.928 \\
\text{Total PV}(C)_P &= 40 + 12.29 - 1.928 = 50.36 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_P &= \frac{55.30}{50.36} = 1.098 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative Q:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(B)_Q &= 19 \times 6.1446 = 116.75 \\
\text{PV}(C \text{ O\&M})_Q &= 4.0 \times 6.1446 = 24.58 \\
\text{PV}(\text{SV})_Q &= 8 \times 0.3855 = 3.084 \\
\text{Total PV}(C)_Q &= 75 + 24.58 - 3.084 = 96.50 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_Q &= \frac{116.75}{96.50} = 1.210 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative R:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}(B)_R &= 26 \times 6.1446 = 159.76 \\
\text{PV}(C \text{ O\&M})_R &= 5.0 \times 6.1446 = 30.72 \\
\text{PV}(\text{SV})_R &= 12 \times 0.3855 = 4.626 \\
\text{Total PV}(C)_R &= 110 + 30.72 - 4.626 = 136.09 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_R &= \frac{159.76}{136.09} = 1.174 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Accept}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Part (b) — Incremental IRoR / B-C Analysis [7 Marks]

**Order:** P (40) → Q (75) → R (110). Defender = P.

**Incremental Q vs. P:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta B &= 19 - 9 = 10 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 10 \times 6.1446 = 61.45 \\
\Delta C_{\text{O\&M}} &= 4.0 - 2.0 = 2.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 2.0 \times 6.1446 = 12.29 \\
\Delta I_0 &= 75 - 40 = 35 \\
\Delta \text{SV} &= 8 - 5 = 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 3 \times 0.3855 = 1.157 \\
\text{Total PV of } \Delta C &= 35 + 12.29 - 1.157 = 46.13 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{Q-P} &= \frac{61.45}{46.13} = 1.332 > 1 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

→ **Promote Q as new Defender.**

**Incremental R vs. Q:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta B &= 26 - 19 = 7 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 7 \times 6.1446 = 43.01 \\
\Delta C_{\text{O\&M}} &= 5.0 - 4.0 = 1.0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 1.0 \times 6.1446 = 6.14 \\
\Delta I_0 &= 110 - 75 = 35 \\
\Delta \text{SV} &= 12 - 8 = 4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{PV} = 4 \times 0.3855 = 1.542 \\
\text{Total PV of } \Delta C &= 35 + 6.14 - 1.542 = 39.60 \\
\left(\frac{B}{C}\right)_{R-Q} &= \frac{43.01}{39.60} = 1.086 > 1 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

→ **Promote R as new Defender. No more alternatives.**

**Final Recommendation:** **Alternative R (Bio-Methanation Plant)** is the optimal public project.

**NSB Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NSB}_P &= 55.30 - 50.36 = 4.94 \\
\text{NSB}_Q &= 116.75 - 96.50 = 20.25 \\
\text{NSB}_R &= 159.76 - 136.09 = 23.67 \;\;\text{(Maximum)} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to order by Initial Investment first** — 2 marks lost immediately.
> 2. **Computing only the conventional B-C ratio and picking the highest** — this is the *single most common error*; full 5–7 marks lost.
> 3. **Using the wrong discount rate** — public projects must use the **Social Discount Rate (SDR = 8%–12%)**, not the firm's cost of capital.
> 4. **Forgetting to subtract the salvage value present worth** from the cost side (it is a negative cost, not a benefit).
> 5. **Stopping at the first incremental comparison** — must propagate until the topmost alternative is tested.
> 6. **Forgetting the "Do-Nothing" elimination step** — alternatives with B-C < 1 must be dropped before incremental analysis begins.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** IRoR is the discount rate that makes the **incremental net social benefit** between two alternatives exactly zero.
- **Public Project Context:** Always use the **Social Discount Rate (SDR)**, not the private cost of capital.
- **Five-Step Algorithm:** (1) Order by $I_0$ ascending; (2) Eliminate via conventional B-C < 1; (3) Defender = lowest viable cost; (4) Incremental B-C test on each next alternative; (5) Promote if inc. B-C ≥ 1, else retain.
- **Decision Rule:** Adopt the higher-cost option **only if** $(B/C)_{\text{increment}} \geq 1.0$.
- **Equivalent NSB Rule:** The selected alternative is the one with the **maximum Net Social Benefit (NSB)** at the SDR — both rules give identical results.
- **Salvage Value Treatment:** Discounted PV of salvage is **subtracted** from the cost side (a negative cost, i.e., a credit).
- **Scale Trap:** Never pick the alternative with the highest conventional B-C ratio directly — it is scale-biased.
- **Do-Nothing:** Always include the "do-nothing" option as the baseline; it has $I_0 = 0, B_t = 0, C_t = 0$.
- **Equivalency:** $\text{NPW}(i^*) = 0$ for the increment is mathematically identical to $(B/C)_{\text{increment}} = 1$ at the social discount rate.
- **Indian Norm:** For public sector projects in India, NITI Aayog recommends **SDR = 8% for social sector**, **10% for economic infrastructure**, and **12% for commercial public enterprises**.
- **Independent vs. Mutually Exclusive:** IRoR is **only** for mutually exclusive alternatives; for independent projects, use a simple B-C test against the MARR/SDR.
- **Annual vs. Project-Life Factors:** When in doubt, use $(P/A, i, n)$ for uniform annual series and $(P/F, i, n)$ for single end-of-life values like salvage.
- **Final Cross-Check:** Always verify your IRoR result by computing NSB of all alternatives and confirming the chosen one has the maximum NSB at the SDR.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefit-Cost Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Benefit-Cost Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Benefit-Cost Analysis (BCA)** is a systematic, quantitative financial evaluation technique used in engineering economics to determine the economic viability of a project, investment, or engineering alternative by comparing the **time-adjusted (discounted) value of all expected benefits** against the **time-adjusted value of all associated costs** over the entire life cycle of the project.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, BCA is defined as:

> "A method of economic evaluation in which the present worth of all benefits (revenues, savings, and other positive economic returns) is compared with the present worth of all costs (capital investment, operating expenses, maintenance, and disbenefits) to determine the desirability of an investment alternative, using a pre-specified **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** as the discount rate."

Mathematically, the core identity is:

$$BCR = \frac{\text{Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)}}{\text{Present Worth of Costs (PWC)}}$$

where **BCR** stands for **Benefit-Cost Ratio**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Shopping Value Test"**
> Imagine you walk into a store and see a vacuum cleaner that costs ₹5,000 today. The salesman promises it will save you ₹1,500 per year in housekeeping services for the next 5 years. The question is: *Is it worth buying?*
>
> The naïve thought says: "5 × ₹1,500 = ₹7,500, which is more than ₹5,000, so buy it!" — But this is **WRONG** because money received in the future is *worth less* than money today (the **time value of money**).
>
> **Benefit-Cost Analysis** is the formal, disciplined version of this question. It answers: *After discounting all future savings back to today, do they still exceed the cost?* If yes (BCR > 1), the project is financially attractive.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in BCA

The following standard economic metrics are central to KTU Board Examinations:

- **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return):** The lowest rate of return a project must earn to be considered worthwhile. It represents the **opportunity cost of capital**.
- **BCR (Benefit-Cost Ratio):** The dimensionless ratio of discounted benefits to discounted costs.
- **NPV (Net Present Value):** The arithmetic difference between discounted benefits and discounted costs.
- **PW (Present Worth):** The equivalent value of all cash flows at time zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (a) compute the BCR for a single project, (b) interpret the result using the *Accept/Reject* decision rule, (c) perform **incremental BCR analysis** for mutually exclusive alternatives, and (d) select the most economical alternative from a set of options.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Framework of BCA

The Benefit-Cost Analysis procedure is built on four sequential pillars:

1. **Identify Cash Flows:** Enumerate *all* benefits (revenues, savings, salvage value) and *all* costs (initial investment, O&M, replacements, taxes).
2. **Apply the Time Value of Money:** Discount every future cash flow back to **time zero (present)** using the MARR as the discount rate.
3. **Compute Aggregate Values:** Sum the present worth of all benefits (PWB) and the present worth of all costs (PWC).
4. **Apply the Decision Rule:** Compare the metrics against benchmark thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (Conventional BCR Method):**
> - If **BCR ≥ 1** (equivalently, **NPV ≥ 0**) → **ACCEPT** the project
> - If **BCR < 1** (equivalently, **NPV < 0**) → **REJECT** the project
>
> When two mutually exclusive alternatives are compared, use the **Incremental BCR (ΔBCR)** approach on the *cost increment* vs *benefit increment* — NOT the absolute BCR of each.

## 2.2 Why Discounting is Non-Negotiable

A rupee received 10 years from now is **not** worth a rupee today, because:
- Inflation erodes purchasing power.
- The rupee could have been invested today to earn returns.
- Future outcomes carry uncertainty and risk.

Discounting at the MARR captures all three effects simultaneously.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Expression** | **Unit** |
|------------|-------------|--------------------------|----------|
| P | Present Worth (single sum) | $\displaystyle P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | ₹ |
| F | Future Worth (single sum) | $\displaystyle F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | ₹ |
| A | Annual Worth (uniform series) | $\displaystyle A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | ₹/year |
| PWB | Present Worth of Benefits | $\displaystyle \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | ₹ |
| PWC | Present Worth of Costs | $\displaystyle \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | ₹ |
| NPV | Net Present Value | $\displaystyle NPV = PWB - PWC$ | ₹ |
| BCR | Conventional BCR | $\displaystyle BCR = \dfrac{PWB}{PWC}$ | dimensionless |
| ΔBCR | Incremental BCR | $\displaystyle \Delta BCR = \dfrac{\Delta PWB}{\Delta PWC}$ | dimensionless |
| IRR | Internal Rate of Return | Rate where NPV = 0 | % per year |
| i | Discount Rate (MARR) | Given / Opportunity Cost | % per year |
| n | Project Life | Analysis horizon | years |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL BOARD EXAM PITFALL:** Never use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside LaTeX expressions in your answer sheet to denote absolute value. KTU examiners expect clean, typeset math. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolutely needed.

## 2.4 Conventional BCR vs. Modified BCR

There are two variants of the BCR formula in the KTU syllabus:

**(a) Conventional BCR (most common in KTU papers):**
$$BCR_{conv} = \frac{\text{PW of Benefits}}{\text{PW of Costs}}$$

**(b) Modified BCR (uses Net Benefits in numerator, only initial investment in denominator):**
$$BCR_{mod} = \frac{\text{PW of Benefits} - \text{PW of O\&M Costs}}{\text{Initial Investment}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 2 primarily tests the **Conventional BCR** form. Always confirm from the problem statement whether O&M costs are already inside the "Cost" stream or are netted out.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

BCA is the cornerstone of:
- **Public Sector Project Appraisal** (highways, irrigation, metro rail, power plants)
- **Environmental Engineering Decisions** (cost of pollution control vs. health benefits)
- **Renewable Energy Selection** (solar vs. wind farm evaluation)
- **Industrial Equipment Replacement** (defender vs. challenger analysis)
- **Defense and Infrastructure Procurement** (where monetizing benefits is critical)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From First Principles of Time Value of Money

The foundation of BCA rests on the **Present Worth Factor (P/F, i, n)**, which itself is derived from the simple interest-compounding identity.

**Step 1 — Define the Future-to-Present Relationship**

If a sum *F* is due at the end of *n* years, and the prevailing discount (interest) rate is *i* per year, then by the law of compound interest, the present equivalent *P* is obtained by "discounting" *F*:

$$P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Extend to a Series of Benefits**

Suppose benefits $B_0, B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ occur at the end of years 0, 1, 2, …, n. The present worth of all benefits is:

$$PWB = B_0 + \frac{B_1}{(1+i)} + \frac{B_2}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{B_n}{(1+i)^n}$$

In compact summation form:

$$PWB = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 3 — Extend to a Series of Costs**

Similarly, for costs $C_0, C_1, \ldots, C_n$:

$$PWC = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Benefit-Cost Ratio**

The BCR is defined as the ratio of these two present worths:

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$$

**Step 5 — Decision Logic from the Inequality**

For the project to be *worthwhile*, the discounted benefits must at least equal the discounted costs:

$$PWB \geq PWC$$

Dividing both sides by *PWC* (which is strictly positive):

$$\frac{PWB}{PWC} \geq 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad BCR \geq 1$$

Equivalently, from the definition of NPV:

$$NPV = PWB - PWC \geq 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The BCR ≥ 1 rule and the NPV ≥ 0 rule are **algebraically equivalent** for *single* independent projects. They may, however, give *different rankings* for mutually exclusive alternatives — this is why the **Incremental BCR** method is required.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

### **Example 1 — Single Project Evaluation (Most Common KTU Question Type)**

A municipal corporation is evaluating a small bridge project. The data are:

- Initial investment (at year 0): ₹ **8,00,000**
- Annual toll revenue (years 1 to 10): ₹ **1,50,000**
- Annual maintenance cost (years 1 to 10): ₹ **30,000**
- Salvage value at year 10: ₹ **50,000**
- MARR: **10% per annum**

Determine whether the project is economically justified using BCR.

**Step 1 — Identify Cash Flow Streams**

Costs: Initial investment = ₹ 8,00,000 (year 0); Annual O&M = ₹ 30,000 (years 1–10)
Benefits: Annual toll = ₹ 1,50,000 (years 1–10); Salvage = ₹ 50,000 (year 10)

**Step 2 — Compute PW of Benefits (PWB)**

The annual toll is a uniform series. Use the P/A factor at i = 10%, n = 10.

From standard interest tables:

$$\frac{P}{A}, 10\%, 10 = 6.1446$$

The salvage value is a single future sum at year 10. Use the P/F factor:

$$\frac{P}{F}, 10\%, 10 = 0.3855$$

Therefore:

$$PWB = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 6.1446 \; + \; 50{,}000 \times 0.3855$$

$$PWB = 9{,}21{,}690 \; + \; 19{,}275 \; = \; 9{,}40{,}965$$

**Step 3 — Compute PW of Costs (PWC)**

Initial investment is already at present (year 0), so its PW is ₹ 8,00,000.

The O&M cost is a uniform annual outflow:

$$PWC = 8{,}00{,}000 \; + \; 30{,}000 \times 6.1446$$

$$PWC = 8{,}00{,}000 \; + \; 1{,}84{,}338 \; = \; 9{,}84{,}338$$

**Step 4 — Compute the BCR**

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC} = \frac{9{,}40{,}965}{9{,}84{,}338} = 0.956$$

**Step 5 — Apply Decision Rule**

Since BCR = 0.956 < 1.0, the project is **NOT** economically justified at MARR = 10%. The NPV is negative:

$$NPV = 9{,}40{,}965 - 9{,}84{,}338 = -43{,}373 \text{ ₹}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for students:** A BCR of 0.956 means the project recovers only ~95.6 paise for every rupee invested (in present-value terms). The shortfall is ₹ 43,373 in present-day money.

---

### **Example 2 — Incremental BCR for Two Mutually Exclusive Alternatives**

A factory can install either **Machine A** or **Machine B** (both have a 6-year life, MARR = 12%):

| Item | Machine A | Machine B |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| First Cost | ₹ 1,00,000 | ₹ 1,60,000 |
| Annual Net Revenue | ₹ 40,000 | ₹ 60,000 |
| Salvage Value | ₹ 10,000 | ₹ 15,000 |

Choose the better machine using **Incremental BCR**.

**Step 1 — Establish the "Defender"**

The machine with the **lower first cost** is the defender. Hence, Machine A is the defender, Machine B is the challenger.

**Step 2 — Compute BCR of the Defender (Machine A) using Net Cash Flow Approach**

Net annual revenue already accounts for O&M. So we treat Revenue as Benefit and First Cost + (–Salvage) as Cost.

For Machine A:

PWB (A) = 40,000 × (P/A, 12%, 6) + 10,000 × (P/F, 12%, 6)

From tables: (P/A, 12%, 6) = 4.1114; (P/F, 12%, 6) = 0.5066

PWB (A) = 40,000 × 4.1114 + 10,000 × 0.5066 = 1,64,456 + 5,066 = **1,69,522**

PWC (A) = 1,00,000

BCR (A) = 1,69,522 / 1,00,000 = **1.695** (Defender is independently viable.)

**Step 3 — Compute Incremental Cash Flows (B – A)**

- ΔFirst Cost = 1,60,000 − 1,00,000 = ₹ 60,000
- ΔAnnual Revenue = 60,000 − 40,000 = ₹ 20,000
- ΔSalvage = 15,000 − 10,000 = ₹ 5,000

**Step 4 — Compute ΔBCR**

ΔPWB = 20,000 × 4.1114 + 5,000 × 0.5066 = 82,228 + 2,533 = **84,761**

ΔPWC = 60,000

$$\Delta BCR = \frac{84{,}761}{60{,}000} = 1.413$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule for Incremental BCR**

Since ΔBCR = 1.413 > 1.0, the **extra investment in Machine B is justified** over Machine A.

**Conclusion:** Choose **Machine B**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often compute BCR for each machine *individually* and pick the highest. This is **WRONG** for mutually exclusive alternatives. Always use **incremental BCR** on the (Challenger – Defender) cash flows.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The BCA Decision Process — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Project Scope and Life Cycle n] --> B[Identify All Cash Flows: Benefits and Costs]
    B --> C[Select the Discount Rate equal to MARR]
    C --> D[Compute Present Worth of Benefits PWB]
    C --> E[Compute Present Worth of Costs PWC]
    D --> F[Calculate BCR equals PWB divided by PWC]
    E --> F
    F --> G{BCR greater than or equal to 1.0}
    G -- Yes --> H[Calculate NPV equals PWB minus PWC]
    G -- No  --> I[Reject Project and Stop]
    H --> J{NPV greater than or equal to 0}
    J -- Yes --> K[Accept Project]
    J -- No  --> L[Recheck Cash Flow Estimates and Try MARR Adjustment]
    L --> M[If Still Negative: Reject]
    K --> N[End of Single Project Analysis]
    M --> N
    I --> N
```

## 4.2 Incremental BCR Decision Matrix for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: List All Alternatives] --> S2[Rank by Increasing First Cost]
    S2 --> S3[Identify Defender: Lowest First Cost]
    S3 --> S4[Compute BCR of Defender]
    S4 --> D1{Defender BCR greater than or equal to 1}
    D1 -- No --> D2[Drop Defender and Pick Next Lowest Cost as New Defender]
    D2 --> S4
    D1 -- Yes --> D5[Identify Next Challenger]
    D5 --> D6[Compute Incremental Cash Flows: Challenger minus Defender]
    D6 --> D7[Compute Delta BCR]
    D7 --> D8{Delta BCR greater than or equal to 1}
    D8 -- Yes --> D9[Accept Challenger: New Defender]
    D9 --> D10{More Alternatives Remaining}
    D10 -- Yes --> D5
    D10 -- No  --> D11[Final Defender is the Selected Best Alternative]
    D8 -- No --> D12[Reject Challenger: Keep Current Defender]
    D12 --> D10
```

## 4.3 Cash-Flow Timeline Visualization (Schematic)

The timeline below shows the typical structure of cash flows in a BCA problem. Brackets above the axis are **benefits** and brackets below the axis are **costs**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0: -Initial Cost] --> Y1[Year 1: +Revenue, -OM Cost]
    Y1 --> Y2[Year 2: +Revenue, -OM Cost]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3: +Revenue, -OM Cost]
    Y3 --> Yn[Year n: +Revenue, -OM Cost, +Salvage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows in the timeline represent the **direction of discounted value flow** back to "Year 0" — the present moment, where the BCR comparison is performed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR). State the decision rule for accepting or rejecting a project based on the conventional BCR method.**

**Model Answer:**
The Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) is the ratio of the present worth of all benefits (PWB) to the present worth of all costs (PWC), both computed at the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR).

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If BCR ≥ 1.0 (equivalently, NPV ≥ 0) → **Accept** the project
- If BCR < 1.0 (equivalently, NPV < 0) → **Reject** the project

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for decision rule)*

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Why is incremental BCR analysis required when comparing two mutually exclusive alternatives? Why is direct comparison of individual BCRs incorrect?**

**Model Answer:**
For two mutually exclusive alternatives, the project with the higher individual BCR is *not necessarily* the most economical, because BCR is a **relative** measure, not an absolute measure of profitability.

The correct procedure is:
1. Rank alternatives by increasing first cost.
2. Compute the BCR of the lowest-cost alternative (defender).
3. Compute the **Incremental BCR (ΔBCR)** = (PWB of Challenger − PWB of Defender) / (PWC of Challenger − PWC of Defender).
4. If ΔBCR ≥ 1, the extra investment in the challenger is justified; otherwise, retain the defender.

This is required because a small, low-cost project may have a high BCR but contribute very little in *absolute* terms, while a larger project may have a slightly lower BCR but generate far greater net benefits.

*(3 marks: 1 for the flaw in direct comparison, 1 for the correct incremental method, 1 for the decision rule on ΔBCR)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B.A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A state public works department is evaluating a flyover construction project with the following data:

- Initial Construction Cost: **₹ 50,00,000**
- Annual Maintenance Cost: **₹ 2,00,000**
- Annual Toll Collection (Revenue): **₹ 12,00,000**
- Expected Life: **20 years**
- Salvage Value at end of year 20: **₹ 5,00,000**
- MARR: **8% per annum**

**(a)** Compute the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) for the project. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Should the project be accepted? Justify with NPV and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B.A**

**Given Data (Restated):**
- First Cost (year 0) = ₹ 50,00,000
- Annual Revenue = ₹ 12,00,000
- Annual O&M Cost = ₹ 2,00,000
- Salvage = ₹ 5,00,000 (year 20)
- n = 20 years
- i = 8%

**Interest Factors at 8%, n = 20:**
- (P/A, 8%, 20) = **9.8181**
- (P/F, 8%, 20) = **0.2145**

---

**(a) Compute BCR — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)**

Benefits = Annual toll + Salvage value (these are positive cash flows)

$$PWB = 12{,}00{,}000 \times (P/A, 8\%, 20) + 5{,}00{,}000 \times (P/F, 8\%, 20)$$

$$PWB = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 9.8181 + 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2145$$

$$PWB = 1{,}17{,}81{,}720 + 1{,}07{,}250 = 1{,}18{,}88{,}970$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth of Costs (PWC)**

Costs = First cost + Annual O&M (these are negative cash flows)

$$PWC = 50{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times (P/A, 8\%, 20)$$

$$PWC = 50{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \times 9.8181$$

$$PWC = 50{,}00{,}000 + 19{,}63{,}620 = 69{,}63{,}620$$

**Step 3 — Compute BCR**

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC} = \frac{1{,}18{,}88{,}970}{69{,}63{,}620} = 1.707$$

---

**(b) Decision and NPV Interpretation — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute NPV**

$$NPV = PWB - PWC = 1{,}18{,}88{,}970 - 69{,}63{,}620 = 49{,}25{,}350$$

**Step 2 — Decision**

Since BCR = 1.707 > 1.0, and equivalently NPV = ₹ 49,25,350 > 0, the project is **ACCEPTED**.

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The BCR of 1.707 means that for every ₹ 1 invested (in present value), the project returns ₹ 1.707 in discounted benefits — a surplus of ₹ 0.707 per rupee. The NPV of ₹ 49,25,350 represents the **net wealth creation** for the public at MARR = 8%. The project is highly economically justified.

---

### **Question B.B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A manufacturing firm must choose between two machines, **X** and **Y**, with mutually exclusive service lives. MARR = 12%.

| Parameter | Machine X | Machine Y |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Initial Cost | ₹ 2,00,000 | ₹ 3,20,000 |
| Annual Operating Cost | ₹ 50,000 | ₹ 30,000 |
| Annual Revenue | ₹ 1,10,000 | ₹ 1,50,000 |
| Salvage Value | ₹ 20,000 | ₹ 30,000 |
| Life | 5 years | 5 years |

**(a)** Compute the BCR for each machine. Identify the defender. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Perform incremental BCR analysis. State which machine should be selected and why. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B.B**

**Interest Factors at 12%, n = 5:**
- (P/A, 12%, 5) = **3.6048**
- (P/F, 12%, 5) = **0.5674**

**Net Annual Cash Flow (Revenue − Operating Cost):**
- Machine X: 1,10,000 − 50,000 = **₹ 60,000** per year
- Machine Y: 1,50,000 − 30,000 = **₹ 1,20,000** per year

---

**(a) BCR of Each Machine — 7 marks**

**Machine X (Likely Defender — Lower First Cost):**

PWB (X) = 60,000 × 3.6048 + 20,000 × 0.5674
PWB (X) = 2,16,288 + 11,348 = **2,27,636**

PWC (X) = **2,00,000**

BCR (X) = 2,27,636 / 2,00,000 = **1.138**

**Machine Y:**

PWB (Y) = 1,20,000 × 3.6048 + 30,000 × 0.5674
PWB (Y) = 4,32,576 + 17,022 = **4,49,598**

PWC (Y) = **3,20,000**

BCR (Y) = 4,49,598 / 3,20,000 = **1.405**

**Defender:** Machine X (lower first cost of ₹ 2,00,000).

---

**(b) Incremental BCR Analysis (Y − X) — 7 marks**

**Incremental Cash Flows:**

- ΔFirst Cost = 3,20,000 − 2,00,000 = ₹ 1,20,000
- ΔNet Annual Revenue = 1,20,000 − 60,000 = ₹ 60,000
- ΔSalvage = 30,000 − 20,000 = ₹ 10,000

**Compute ΔPWB and ΔPWC:**

ΔPWB = 60,000 × 3.6048 + 10,000 × 0.5674
ΔPWB = 2,16,288 + 5,674 = **2,21,962**

ΔPWC = **1,20,000**

**Incremental BCR:**

$$\Delta BCR = \frac{2{,}21{,}962}{1{,}20{,}000} = 1.850$$

**Decision:** Since ΔBCR = 1.850 > 1.0, the **additional investment of ₹ 1,20,000 in Machine Y is fully justified** over Machine X.

**Selected Alternative: Machine Y**

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although Machine Y has a slightly lower BCR/₹ of first cost (1.405 vs 1.138, the second ratio is irrelevant for choice), the *extra* ₹ 1,20,000 invested in Y yields ₹ 1.85 in present benefits per rupee — well above MARR. This is a classic case where direct BCR comparison misleads students.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in BCA Problems (as per KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert the MARR to a decimal** — Using 10 instead of 0.10 destroys every calculation downstream. *(−2 marks typical)*
> 2. **Confusing "Annual Revenue" with "Net Annual Cash Flow"** — If the problem gives Revenue and O&M separately, you MUST net them out *or* treat them in the correct PWB/PWC bucket. *(−2 to −3 marks)*
> 3. **Direct BCR comparison for mutually exclusive alternatives** — This is a guaranteed loss of 4–5 marks. Always use **Incremental BCR**.
> 4. **Treating salvage value as a "cost" or omitting it entirely** — Salvage is a positive benefit at year n; discount it back using the (P/F) factor.
> 5. **Writing the answer without showing factor values** — KTU examiners award partial marks for *correct factor selection*. Always write the factor name (e.g., "P/A, 12%, 5") and the value used.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for BCA (Module 2):**
>
> - **Definition:** BCR = PWB / PWC at MARR.
> - **Decision Rule:** Accept if BCR ≥ 1, Reject if BCR < 1.
> - **Equivalence:** BCR ≥ 1 ⇔ NPV ≥ 0 for a single independent project.
> - **NPV Formula:** NPV = PWB − PWC.
> - **Time Value Factors to Master:** (P/F, i, n), (P/A, i, n), (F/P, i, n), (A/P, i, n), (F/A, i, n), (A/F, i, n).
> - **Mutually Exclusive Alternatives:** Always use **Incremental BCR** on (Challenger − Defender). Defender = lower first cost. Drop defender if BCR < 1, then re-rank.
> - **Modified BCR:** Numerator = PW of benefits − PW of O&M; Denominator = First cost only.
> - **Conventional BCR:** Numerator = PW of all benefits (incl. salvage); Denominator = PW of all costs (incl. O&M).
> - **Salvage Value:** Always a **benefit** at year n, discounted using (P/F, i, n).
> - **Initial Cost:** Already at time zero; do **not** discount it.
> - **Common Pitfall:** Direct BCR comparison is **invalid** for mutually exclusive alternatives — use ΔBCR.
> - **Key Equivalence Rule:** BCR and NPV give the **same** accept/reject decision for *independent* projects, but **different rankings** for mutually exclusive ones.
> - **Formula Cheat (Uniform Series to Present):** $A \rightarrow P$ uses factor $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$.
> - **Formula Cheat (Single Future to Present):** $F \rightarrow P$ uses factor $(1+i)^{-n}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breakeven Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Breakeven Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breakeven Analysis (BEA)** is a fundamental managerial accounting and cost-volume-profit (CVP) analytical tool that determines the precise point at which **Total Revenue (TR)** equals **Total Cost (TC)** — meaning the business makes neither a profit nor a loss. The output volume at this point is termed the **Breakeven Point (BEP)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Breakeven Analysis is the systematic study of the inter-relationship between the following five key variables:

- **Selling Price per Unit (SP)** — the price at which a single unit of product is sold
- **Variable Cost per Unit (VC)** — costs that vary directly with output
- **Total Fixed Cost (FC)** — costs that remain constant irrespective of output
- **Output Volume (Q)** — quantity produced and sold
- **Profit (P)** — the resulting financial surplus

Mathematically, the breakeven condition is expressed as:

$$TR = TC \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad SP \times Q = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tea Stall" Intuition:** Imagine a small tea stall owner. Every day, he must pay a fixed rent of ₹500 for his stall (this is his **Fixed Cost**, paid even if he sells zero cups of tea). For every cup of tea he brews, he spends ₹10 on milk, sugar, tea leaves, and a cup (this is his **Variable Cost**). He sells each cup for ₹30 (**Selling Price**). So, every cup gives him a **Contribution of ₹20** (₹30 − ₹10) towards paying the rent. He needs to sell **25 cups** (₹500 ÷ ₹20) to just cover the rent. Below 25 cups, he is in **loss**; above 25 cups, he earns **profit**. The 25th cup is his **Breakeven Point**.

This simple analogy helps us visualize the central idea: BEP is the moment when the accumulated **contribution from each unit sold** exactly recovers the entire burden of **fixed costs**.

### 1.3 Why Breakeven Analysis Matters in Engineering Ventures

For a B.Tech student, BEA is critical because:

- **Product Pricing Decisions:** Helps engineers price new products when launching startups.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Useful in industrial engineering for outsourcing choices.
- **Capacity Planning:** Helps determine the minimum production volume for a new plant.
- **Risk Assessment:** Quantifies the safety margin before losses begin.
- **Cost Control:** Identifies how fixed and variable cost structures affect profitability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost Behavior Classification

A foundational prerequisite to BEA is the proper classification of costs. KTU 2024 Scheme strictly distinguishes costs into three behavior categories:

| Cost Type | Behavior | Examples | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Cost (FC)** | Constant in total; varies per unit inversely with output | Rent, Salary, Insurance, Depreciation | $FC$ |
| **Variable Cost (VC)** | Varies in total proportionally with output; constant per unit | Raw materials, Direct labor, Packaging | $VC$ |
| **Semi-Variable Cost** | Has both fixed and variable components | Telephone bill, Electricity (partly) | $SVC$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A cost is classified as fixed or variable **with respect to a given output range and time period**. Fixed costs become variable in the long run.

### 2.2 Core Concept: Contribution Margin

The single most important concept in BEA is **Contribution**. It is the amount remaining from sales revenue after deducting variable costs, available to contribute towards fixed costs and profit.

$$C = S - V$$

Where:
- $C$ = Contribution per unit (in ₹)
- $S$ = Selling Price per unit (in ₹)
- $V$ = Variable Cost per unit (in ₹)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** When you sell one unit, you first recover its variable cost. Whatever is left is a "contribution" — first to fixed costs, and once fixed costs are fully recovered, every additional unit's contribution flows directly to **profit**.

### 2.3 Profit Volume (P/V) Ratio

The **P/V Ratio** (also called Contribution Margin Ratio) is a powerful indicator showing the rate at which contribution is generated per rupee of sales.

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{C}{S} \times 100$$

A higher P/V ratio means greater profitability and a lower breakeven point. **P/V ratio is the most diagnostic single metric in CVP analysis.**

### 2.4 Margin of Safety (MoS)

The **Margin of Safety** is the difference between actual/anticipated sales and breakeven sales. It measures the cushion of safety — how much sales can drop before losses begin.

$$\text{MoS (in ₹)} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$$

$$\text{MoS (in units)} = \text{Actual Sales Units} - \text{BEP Units}$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a board-exam goldmine. Memorize it thoroughly — almost every BEP numerical in KTU papers uses one of these formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Revenue | $TR = S \times Q$ | ₹ |
| 2 | Total Cost | $TC = FC + (V \times Q)$ | ₹ |
| 3 | Total Contribution | $TC_c = C \times Q = (S - V) \times Q$ | ₹ |
| 4 | Profit | $P = TR - TC = (C \times Q) - FC$ | ₹ |
| 5 | Contribution per unit | $C = S - V$ | ₹ |
| 6 | P/V Ratio | $\frac{C}{S} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | **BEP in Units** | $\frac{FC}{C} \;=\; \frac{FC}{S - V}$ | units |
| 8 | **BEP in ₹ (Sales Value)** | $\frac{FC}{\text{P/V Ratio}} \;=\; \frac{FC \times S}{C}$ | ₹ |
| 9 | Margin of Safety (Amount) | Actual Sales $-$ BEP Sales | ₹ |
| 10 | Margin of Safety (Ratio) | $\frac{\text{MoS Amount}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Desired Profit Sales (₹) | $\frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | ₹ |
| 12 | Desired Profit Units | $\frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{C}$ | units |
| 13 | Sales to Earn Specific Profit | $\frac{FC + P}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | ₹ |

### 2.6 Assumptions of Breakeven Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"State the assumptions of BEA."* The seven classical assumptions are:

1. **Costs are linear:** Total cost and total revenue are straight lines (constant SP, constant VC per unit, constant FC).
2. **Fixed costs remain constant** throughout the relevant range.
3. **Variable cost per unit is constant** — proportional to output.
4. **Selling price per unit is constant** — no bulk discounts.
5. **All units produced are sold** — no inventory buildup (no change in stock).
6. **Single product or constant sales mix** — for multi-product cases, the sales mix is stable.
7. **No change in operating efficiency**, technology, or price levels during the period.

### 2.7 Limitations of Breakeven Analysis

- Ignores **time value of money**.
- Unrealistic assumption of **linear cost-revenue relationship**.
- Difficult to **classify semi-variable costs** into fixed and variable components.
- Becomes **complex for multi-product firms**.
- Based on **historical data**; not useful for futuristic radical changes.
- Ignores **external factors** like competition, market dynamics, taxation changes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Breakeven Point in Units (BEP_Q)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most fundamental derivation** in BEA. KTU examiners expect students to memorize and reproduce this derivation verbatim.

**Starting Premise:** At breakeven, Total Revenue equals Total Cost.

$$\text{At BEP:} \quad TR = TC$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the linear equations for TR and TC.

$$S \times Q = FC + (V \times Q)$$

**Step 2:** Bring all $Q$ terms to one side of the equation.

$$S \times Q - V \times Q = FC$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $Q$ on the left-hand side.

$$Q \times (S - V) = FC$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $(S - V) = C$ (Contribution per unit).

$$Q \times C = FC$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $Q$ to obtain the breakeven quantity.

$$\boxed{\;Q_{\text{BEP}} = \frac{FC}{C} = \frac{FC}{S - V}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Breakeven Point in Sales Rupees (BEP_₹)

**Step 1:** Multiply BEP in units by selling price per unit.

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = BEP_Q \times S = \frac{FC}{C} \times S$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $C = S - V$ to get the explicit form.

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC \times S}{S - V}$$

**Step 3:** Divide numerator and denominator by $S$.

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC}{\frac{S - V}{S}} = \frac{FC}{1 - \frac{V}{S}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\frac{S - V}{S} = \frac{C}{S} = \text{P/V Ratio}$.

$$\boxed{\;BEP_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{FC \times 100}{\text{P/V Ratio \%}}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Profit Formula

**Step 1:** Profit is defined as TR minus TC.

$$P = TR - TC = (S \times Q) - \big[FC + (V \times Q)\big]$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.

$$P = (S \times Q) - (V \times Q) - FC = (S - V) \times Q - FC$$

**Step 3:** Use the contribution notation.

$$\boxed{\;P = (C \times Q) - FC\;}$$

### 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a model problem structured exactly as a 7-mark KTU Part B sub-question.**

**Problem:** A company manufactures a single product with the following data:
- Selling Price per unit (S) = ₹50
- Variable Cost per unit (V) = ₹30
- Total Fixed Costs (FC) = ₹4,00,000
- Actual Sales = 20,000 units

**Required:** Calculate (a) BEP in units, (b) BEP in ₹, (c) P/V Ratio, (d) Margin of Safety in ₹ and %, (e) Profit at actual sales.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Contribution per unit.**

$$C = S - V = 50 - 30 = ₹20 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2: BEP in Units.**

$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{C} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20} = 20{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: BEP in Sales Rupees.**

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = BEP_Q \times S = 20{,}000 \times 50 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4: P/V Ratio.**

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{C}{S} \times 100 = \frac{20}{50} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 5: Margin of Safety.**

$$\text{Actual Sales} = 20{,}000 \times 50 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{MoS (₹)} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales} = 10{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = ₹0$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{0}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 0\%$$

**Step 6: Profit at actual sales.**

$$P = (C \times Q) - FC = (20 \times 20{,}000) - 4{,}00{,}000$$

$$P = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = ₹0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The company is operating *exactly at breakeven*. It must increase sales beyond 20,000 units to start generating profit. Notice the elegant relationship: BEP_units = 20,000, Actual Sales = 20,000, hence MoS = 0 and Profit = 0.

### 3.5 Reverse Calculation Problem (Inverse Working)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 frequently tests "reverse BEA" — given BEP and one parameter, find the missing variable.**

**Problem:** A firm's BEP is at ₹6,00,000 sales. Variable costs are 60% of sales. Find the Fixed Cost.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find P/V Ratio from the given V/S ratio.

$$\text{Since } \frac{V}{S} = 0.60, \quad \frac{C}{S} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$$

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = 40\%$$

**Step 2:** Apply the BEP formula rearranged for FC.

$$FC = BEP_{\text{₹}} \times \text{P/V Ratio} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹2{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Cross-verify using P/V % form.

$$FC = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 \times 40}{100} = ₹2{,}40{,}000$$

### 3.6 Multi-Step Problem: Computing Sales for a Target Profit

**Problem:** Using the data from the first example (S=₹50, V=₹30, FC=₹4,00,000), find the sales required (in units and in ₹) to earn a profit of ₹2,00,000.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Sales Units for Target Profit.**

$$Q_{\text{target}} = \frac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{C} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{20} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{20} = 30{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: Sales ₹ for Target Profit.**

$$\text{Sales}_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC + P}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{0.40} = ₹15{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Verification via P × Q.**

$$TR = 30{,}000 \times 50 = ₹15{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$

$$TC = 4{,}00{,}000 + (30 \times 30{,}000) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 = ₹13{,}00{,}000$$

$$P = TR - TC = 15{,}00{,}000 - 13{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the five CVP variables interact in the analytical flow of Breakeven Analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["INPUT PARAMETERS"]:::input
    node2["Fixed Cost FC"]:::fixed
    node3["Variable Cost V per unit"]:::variable
    node4["Selling Price S per unit"]:::price
    node5["Output Volume Q"]:::output
    node6["CORE COMPUTATION ENGINE"]:::engine
    node7["Contribution per unit C = S minus V"]:::corecalc
    node8["Total Cost TC = FC plus V into Q"]:::corecalc
    node9["Total Revenue TR = S into Q"]:::corecalc
    node10["BEP QUANTITY = FC divided by C"]:::bep
    node11["BEP IN RUPEES = FC divided by P V Ratio"]:::bep
    node12["P V RATIO = C divided by S times 100"]:::ratio
    node13["MARGIN OF SAFETY"]:::mos
    node14["PROFIT OUTPUT"]:::profit
    node15["DECISION SUPPORT"]:::decision
    node16["Loss Zone Q less than BEP"]:::loss
    node17["Breakeven Zone Q equals BEP"]:::breakeven
    node18["Profit Zone Q greater than BEP"]:::profit

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    node2 --> node6
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
    node7 --> node10
    node7 --> node12
    node9 --> node11
    node9 --> node13
    node10 --> node15
    node11 --> node15
    node13 --> node15
    node12 --> node15
    node5 --> node16
    node5 --> node17
    node5 --> node18

    classDef input fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef fixed fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef variable fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef price fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef output fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#D81B60,stroke-width:2px,color:#880E4F
    classDef engine fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,stroke-width:3px,color:#006064
    classDef corecalc fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#F57F17
    classDef bep fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef ratio fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#5E35B1,stroke-width:2px,color:#311B92
    classDef mos fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef profit fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00796B,stroke-width:2px,color:#004D40
    classDef decision fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#E64A19,stroke-width:3px,color:#BF360C
    classDef loss fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#E53935,stroke-width:2px,color:#B71C1C
    classDef breakeven fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#FBC02D,stroke-width:2px,color:#F57F17
    classDef profit2 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
```

### 4.2 The Breakeven Chart — Conceptual Layout

The classical **Breakeven Chart** plots Total Revenue, Total Cost, and Fixed Cost on the y-axis against Output Volume on the x-axis. The following flowchart represents the geometric interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisOrigin["ORIGIN 0,0"]:::origin
    xAxis["X AXIS: Output Volume Q in units"]:::axisx
    yAxis["Y AXIS: Cost and Revenue in rupees"]:::axisy
    fcLine["FIXED COST LINE: Horizontal at FC"]:::fixedline
    tcLine["TOTAL COST LINE: Starts at FC, slope V"]:::tcline
    trLine["TOTAL REVENUE LINE: Starts at origin, slope S"]:::trline
    bepPoint["BREAKEVEN POINT"]:::bepoint
    lossZone["LOSS ZONE: Below TR line, Left of BEP"]:::losszone
    profitZone["PROFIT ZONE: Above TR line, Right of BEP"]:::profitzone
    angDemand["ANGLE theta greater than angle phi indicates profitability"]:::angle

    axisOrigin --> xAxis
    axisOrigin --> yAxis
    fcLine --- tcLine
    fcLine --- trLine
    tcLine --- bepPoint
    trLine --- bepPoint
    bepPoint --- lossZone
    bepPoint --- profitZone
    trLine --- angDemand

    classDef origin fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:2px,color:#212121
    classDef axisx fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef axisy fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,stroke-width:2px,color:#33691E
    classDef fixedline fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Operational Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["GIVEN: Output Q, SP S, VC V, FC"]:::startnode
    computeC["Compute Contribution C = S minus V"]:::step1
    computeBEP["Compute BEP in units = FC divided by C"]:::step2
    compareQ["Compare Q with BEP"]:::decision
    case1["CASE 1: Q less than BEP"]:::case1
    case2["CASE 2: Q equals BEP"]:::case2
    case3["CASE 3: Q greater than BEP"]:::case3
    result1["OUTPUT: LOSS equal to C into BEP minus Q minus FC magnitude"]:::result1
    result2["OUTPUT: NO PROFIT NO LOSS, TR equals TC"]:::result2
    result3["OUTPUT: PROFIT equal to C into Q minus FC"]:::result3
    action1["ACTION: Reduce FC, Increase SP, Reduce V"]:::action1
    action2["ACTION: Maintain current operations"]:::action2
    action3["ACTION: Scale up production, Increase MoS"]:::action3

    start --> computeC
    computeC --> computeBEP
    computeBEP --> compareQ
    compareQ --> case1
    compareQ --> case2
    compareQ --> case3
    case1 --> result1
    case2 --> result2
    case3 --> result3
    result1 --> action1
    result2 --> action2
    result3 --> action3

    classDef startnode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579B

    classDef result2 fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#F57F17
```
