<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Breakeven Point. Explain the concept of P/V Ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breakeven Point (BEP):** It is the level of output or sales at which total revenue equals total cost, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss**. Mathematically, it is the point where **Contribution = Fixed Cost**.

**P/V Ratio (Profit-Volume Ratio):** It is the ratio of contribution to sales, expressed as a percentage. It indicates the rate at which profit increases with a change in sales volume.

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{C}{S} \times 100$$

A higher P/V ratio implies greater profitability per rupee of sales and a lower breakeven point.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Margin of Safety? Why is it important for a business?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin of Safety (MoS):** It is the difference between **actual or expected sales** and **breakeven sales**. It represents the amount by which sales can decline before the business incurs a loss.

$$\text{MoS} = \text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}$$

**Importance:**
- **Indicates risk:** A higher MoS implies lower business risk; a lower MoS signals vulnerability.
- **Guides planning:** Helps management set realistic sales targets and budgeting decisions.
- **Triggers corrective action:** A low or negative MoS warns management of impending losses.
- **Useful in capacity expansion:** Helps assess whether current sales comfortably exceed the BEP.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

#### Question A:
A manufacturing company produces a single product with the following annual data:
- Selling Price per unit (S) = ₹100
- Variable Cost per unit (V) = ₹60
- Total Fixed Costs (FC) = ₹8,00,000

**(a)** Calculate:
- (i) Breakeven point in units
- (ii) Breakeven point in sales rupees
- (iii) P/V Ratio
- (iv) Profit when sales are 25,000 units

**(b)** If the company desires a profit of ₹4,00,000:
- (i) Find the required sales in units and in rupees
- (ii) Find the Margin of Safety if the actual sales are 30,000 units
- (iii) What will be the new BEP if Fixed Costs increase by 25%?

---

### 5.3 Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)

**Given:** S = ₹100, V = ₹60, FC = ₹8,00,000

**Step 1: Compute Contribution per unit.**

$$C = S - V = 100 - 60 = ₹40 \text{ per unit}$$

**(a)(i) BEP in Units.**

$$BEP_Q = \frac{FC}{C} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{40} = 20{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**(a)(ii) BEP in Sales Rupees.**

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = BEP_Q \times S = 20{,}000 \times 100 = ₹20{,}00{,}000$$

**(a)(iii) P/V Ratio.**

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = \frac{C}{S} \times 100 = \frac{40}{100} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**(a)(iv) Profit at Q = 25,000 units.**

$$P = (C \times Q) - FC = (40 \times 25{,}000) - 8{,}00{,}000$$

$$P = 10{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$$

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)

**(b)(i) Sales units and rupees for profit = ₹4,00,000.**

$$Q_{\text{target}} = \frac{FC + P_{\text{target}}}{C} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{40} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{40} = 30{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Sales}_{\text{₹}} = 30{,}000 \times 100 = ₹30{,}00{,}000$$

**(b)(ii) Margin of Safety at Q = 30,000 units.**

$$\text{Actual Sales} = 30{,}000 \times 100 = ₹30{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{BEP Sales} = ₹20{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{MoS} = 30{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

**(b)(iii) New BEP if FC increases by 25%.**

$$\text{New FC} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.25 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{New BEP}_Q = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{40} = 25{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{New BEP}_{\text{₹}} = 25{,}000 \times 100 = ₹25{,}00{,}000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 25% rise in fixed costs increases BEP by 5,000 units. The company must now sell 25,000 units (instead of 20,000) to break even.

---

### 5.4 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

#### Question B:
A company provides the following data for the upcoming year:
- Total Fixed Costs (FC) = ₹5,00,000
- Variable Cost Ratio = 70% of sales
- Expected Sales = ₹25,00,000

**(a)** Calculate:
- (i) Breakeven point in sales rupees
- (ii) P/V Ratio and Contribution per unit (if total units sold = 25,000)
- (iii) Expected Profit
- (iv) Margin of Safety ratio

**(b)** Answer the following:
- (i) If the company wants to **double its profit**, what should be the new sales volume in rupees?
- (ii) How many additional units must be sold to achieve the doubled profit (assuming 25,000 units correspond to ₹25,00,000 sales)?
- (iii) If management reduces the selling price by 10% and variable cost remains 70% of new sales, what is the new P/V ratio and new BEP in ₹?

---

### 5.5 Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)

**Given:** FC = ₹5,00,000, V/S = 0.70, Expected Sales = ₹25,00,000

**Step 1: P/V Ratio.**

$$\text{P/V Ratio} = 1 - \frac{V}{S} = 1 - 0.70 = 0.30 \text{ or } 30\%$$

**(a)(i) BEP in Sales Rupees.**

$$BEP_{\text{₹}} = \frac{FC}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.30} = ₹16{,}66{,}667$$

**(a)(ii) Contribution per unit (assuming 25,000 units).**

$$\text{Total Contribution} = \text{P/V Ratio} \times \text{Sales} = 0.30 \times 25{,}00{,}000 = ₹7{,}50{,}000$$

$$C = \frac{\text{Total Contribution}}{Q} = \frac{7{,}50{,}000}{25{,}000} = ₹30 \text{ per unit}$$

$$\text{Selling Price per unit} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{25{,}000} = ₹100$$

**Verification:** V/S = 0.70 → V = ₹70, C = ₹30 ✓

**(a)(iii) Expected Profit.**

$$P = \text{Contribution} - FC = 7{,}50{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$$

**(a)(iv) Margin of Safety Ratio.**

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BEP Sales}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$$

$$\text{MoS Ratio} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}66{,}667}{25{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{8{,}33{,}333}{25{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)

**(b)(i) Sales to Double the Profit.**

$$\text{Original Profit} = ₹2{,}50{,}000, \quad \text{Doubled Profit} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Required Sales} = \frac{FC + \text{New Profit}}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000}{0.30} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{0.30} = ₹33{,}33{,}333$$

**(b)(ii) Additional Units Required.**

$$\text{Original Sales Units} = 25{,}000$$

$$\text{New Sales} = ₹33{,}33{,}333, \quad \text{SP per unit} = ₹100$$

$$\text{New Sales Units} = \frac{33{,}33{,}333}{100} = 33{,}333 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Additional Units} = 33{,}333 - 25{,}000 = 8{,}333 \text{ units}$$

**(b)(iii) New P/V Ratio and BEP after 10% price reduction.**

$$\text{New SP} = 100 \times 0.90 = ₹90$$

$$\text{New V} = 0.70 \times 90 = ₹63$$

$$\text{New C} = 90 - 63 = ₹27$$

$$\text{New P/V Ratio} = \frac{27}{90} \times 100 = 30\%$$

$$\text{New BEP}_{\text{₹}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.30} = ₹16{,}66{,}667$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counterintuitive Insight:** A 10% price reduction *kept the P/V ratio unchanged* at 30% because the V/S ratio remained 70% of the new (lower) sales. Hence BEP in rupees is the same. However, BEP in **units** changes — the new BEP units = 5,00,000 / 27 ≈ **18,519 units** (versus 16,667 earlier). So the firm must sell more *units* to break even.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing BEP units with BEP ₹:** Always re-read the question. "BEP in units" uses FC/C; "BEP in rupees" uses FC/(P/V Ratio). Mixing them costs full 7 marks.
> 2. **Using total contribution vs per-unit contribution:** When asked for P/V ratio, the answer is a percentage. When asked for contribution, the unit (per unit or total) must be specified.
> 3. **Forgetting the "+ Desired Profit":** A frequent slip in target-profit problems is to compute $\frac{FC}{C}$ instead of $\frac{FC + P}{C}$.
> 4. **Computing MoS in ₹ vs MoS Ratio:** Both are valid but distinct quantities. KTU expects both when asked.
> 5. **Neglecting to interpret the result:** A 7-mark answer without a concluding sentence ("Thus the company must sell X units to break even") is marked down by 1 mark.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Day Saver**

- **BEP Definition:** The output where **TR = TC**; equivalently, where **Contribution = Fixed Cost**.
- **BEP Units Formula:** $\dfrac{FC}{S - V}$ — never forget that contribution is per unit.
- **BEP ₹ Formula:** $\dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ — P/V ratio must be expressed as a decimal when FC is in ₹.
- **P/V Ratio = (C / S) × 100 = (1 − V/S) × 100.** Both forms are commonly tested.
- **Margin of Safety** is a **safety cushion**: the higher the MoS, the safer the business.
- **Profit Equation:** $P = (C \times Q) - FC$. This is the workhorse equation for almost all BEP problems.
- **Target Sales Formula:** $\dfrac{FC + \text{Desired Profit}}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ — must include profit in the numerator.
- **Assumptions:** Linear cost functions, constant SP, constant VC per unit, no inventory changes, single product (or constant mix).
- **Limitations:** Ignores time value of money, semi-variable cost complexity, multi-product scenarios, and external market forces.
- **Highest-Marks Memory Hook:** *"Contribution first recovers FC; thereafter, every rupee of contribution is profit."* This single sentence captures the philosophy of BEA.
- **Common Numerical Trap:** KTU often gives **VC as a percentage of sales** — convert it to rupees using total expected sales first, then derive per-unit values.
- **Always state units** in your final answer (units, ₹, or %) — examiners deduct marks for ambiguous answers.
- **Show derivation, not just formula:** A 7-mark question expects the formula, substitution, and the numerical result. Skipping any step risks 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Cost # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fixed Cost (FC) **Fixed Cost (FC)** is defined as the cost that **remains constant in total** irrespective of the level of production or activity volume, within a relevant range, and over a specified time period. Fixed costs do not vary with the number of units produced — they are incurred even when output is zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > "Fixed costs are costs that do not vary with the level of output in the short run, such as rent, salaries of permanent staff, depreciation, and insurance premiums." ### Common Examples of Fixed Costs - **Factory rent** (e.g., ₹5,00,000 per month) - **Salaries of permanent employees** - **Depreciation** on plant and machinery (Straight Line Method) - **Insurance premiums** - **Property tax** - **Loan interest** on capital ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Your House Rent** > Imagine you rent a house for ₹20,000 per month. Whether you stay at home for 30 days or go on vacation for 28 days, the rent is still ₹20,000. The rent does not depend on how much you "use" the house. This is exactly how fixed costs behave in a factory — whether you produce 100 units or 1000 units, the factory rent remains the same. ## 1.2 Variable Cost (VC) **Variable Cost (VC)** is defined as the cost that **varies directly and proportionately** with the level of production or activity. When output is zero, variable cost is zero; when output doubles, variable cost doubles. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** > "Variable costs are costs that change in direct proportion to the volume of production, such as raw materials, direct labour (piece-rate), and packaging costs." ### Common Examples of Variable Costs - **Raw materials** (e.g., steel, plastic, fabric) - **Direct labour** (piece-rate wages) - **Power and electricity** (production-related) - **Packaging costs** - **Sales commission** (as % of sales) - **Freight outwards** (transport of finished goods) ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Groceries for a Party** > If you host a party, you buy ₹100 worth of snacks per guest. For 10 guests, you spend ₹1,000. For 20 guests, you spend ₹2,000. If no guest comes (output = 0), you spend ₹0. The cost moves *in lock-step* with the number of guests — this is a variable cost. ## 1.3 The Total Cost Framework The **Total Cost (TC)** is the sum of Fixed Cost and Variable Cost: $$TC = FC + TVC$$ Where: - **FC** = Total Fixed Cost (constant) - **TVC** = Total Variable Cost (varies with output) - **TVC** = $v \times Q$, where $v$ is variable cost per unit and $Q$ is quantity produced. --- ## 1.4 Summary Snapshot | Cost Type | Behaviour with Output | Example | At Output = 0 | |-----------|----------------------|---------|---------------| | **Fixed Cost** | Constant in total | Factory Rent | Incurred | | **Variable Cost** | Proportional to output | Raw Material | Zero | | **Total Cost** | Sum of FC + VC | Combined | Equal to FC | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Behavioural Analysis of Costs The behaviour of cost can be analysed along three dimensions: 1. **Total Cost Behaviour** (how the rupee amount changes with output) 2. **Per Unit / Average Cost Behaviour** (how cost per unit changes with output) 3. **Behaviour Outside Relevant Range** ### 2.1.1 Total Fixed Cost (TFC) — Behaviour - **Stays constant** for all output levels within the relevant range. - On a graph, TFC is a **horizontal straight line parallel to the X-axis**. - At output zero, TFC is still present. ### 2.1.2 Total Variable Cost (TVC) — Behaviour - **Starts from origin (0,0)**. - Rises **linearly** if variable cost per unit is constant. - Slope of the TVC line = Variable cost per unit ($v$). ### 2.1.3 Total Cost (TC) — Behaviour - **Parallel to TVC line** because TC = FC + VC. - **Vertical intercept** of TC line = Fixed Cost. - **Slope** of TC line = Variable cost per unit. ### 2.1.4 Average Cost Behaviour (Why and How) > [!NOTE] > **Why does Average Fixed Cost (AFC) fall as output rises?** > Because the *same* fixed cost is now being *spread over more units*. Mathematically, $AFC = \dfrac{FC}{Q}$, so as $Q \uparrow$, $AFC \downarrow$. **Average Variable Cost (AVC):** Remains *constant* per unit (since $v$ is constant per unit). It stays at the variable cost rate. **Average Total Cost (ATC):** Since ATC = AFC + AVC, and AFC falls while AVC stays constant, ATC **continuously falls** as output increases. ## 2.2 Why This Matters in Engineering Decision-Making For an engineer managing a project, understanding cost behaviour helps in: - **Cost estimation** for new projects - **Make-or-buy decisions** - **Break-even analysis** (covered later in Module 2) - **Pricing decisions** (variable costing vs absorption costing) - **Production planning** to optimise capacity utilisation ## 2.3 Assumptions Behind the Linear Cost Model > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU-2024 linear model assumes: > 1. Variable cost per unit ($v$) is **constant** (linear variable cost). > 2. Fixed cost remains **unchanged within the relevant range**. > 3. **Productivity** of variable inputs is constant. > 4. Output is the **only cost driver**. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $TC = FC + TVC$ | Total Cost equation | ₹ | | 2 | $TVC = v \times Q$ | Total Variable Cost | ₹ | | 3 | $TC = FC + v \times Q$ | Total Cost in terms of output | ₹ | | 4 | $AFC = \dfrac{FC}{Q}$ | Average Fixed Cost | ₹/unit | | 5 | $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q} = v$ | Average Variable Cost | ₹/unit | | 6 | $ATC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$ | Average Total Cost | ₹/unit | | 7 | $ATC = AFC + AVC$ | ATC decomposition | ₹/unit | | 8 | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | Marginal Cost (≈ $v$ for linear model) | ₹/unit | | 9 | $FC = TC - TVC$ | Reverse calculation of FC | ₹ | | 10 | $v = \dfrac{TVC}{Q}$ | Variable cost per unit | ₹/unit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Note (KTU Board Exam):** > - $FC$ or $TFC$ = Total Fixed Cost > - $VC$ or $TVC$ = Total Variable Cost > - $v$ or $VC_{pu}$ = Variable cost **per unit** > - $Q$ or $N$ = Quantity / Units produced > - **Do not confuse** $VC$ (total) with $v$ (per unit). KTU examiners deduct marks for this. ## 2.5 Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs — A Brief Introduction In real engineering projects, some costs are partly fixed and partly variable. These are called **Semi-Variable Costs** or **Mixed Costs**. > [!NOTE] > **Example:** A telephone bill has a fixed monthly rental of ₹500 (fixed) + ₹2 per minute of usage (variable). Total bill = ₹500 + ₹2 × (minutes used). This is a semi-variable cost. **Formula for Mixed Cost:** $$TC = FC + v \times Q + MX$$ Where $MX$ is the additional mixed cost element. In KTU problems, mixed costs are usually segregated using the **High-Low Method** (covered in Module 2 extensions). --- ## 2.6 Step Fixed Costs (Conceptual Mention) Some costs are fixed only for a *limited* range of output, then they "step up" to a new fixed level. These are **Step Fixed Costs** or **Step Costs**. **Example:** Hiring one supervisor (₹30,000/month) for 0–100 units, two supervisors for 101–200 units, and so on. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Cost Behaviour Equations ### 3.1.1 Deriving the Total Cost Equation Let us start with the basic principle: **Step 1: Define Fixed Cost (FC)** A cost is fixed if it does not depend on quantity. So we can write: $$FC = \text{constant } k$$ where $k$ is a non-negative real number ($k \geq 0$). **Step 2: Define Variable Cost (VC) per unit** Variable cost is proportional to output. If $v$ is the variable cost per unit, then for producing $Q$ units: $$TVC = v \times Q$$ This follows from the proportionality assumption: if producing 1 unit costs $v$ in variable inputs, then producing $Q$ units costs $v \times Q$. **Step 3: Combine to get Total Cost** By definition of total cost: $$TC = FC + TVC$$ **Step 4: Substitute and simplify** $$TC = FC + v \times Q$$ This is the **fundamental linear cost equation** that KTU expects you to memorise and apply. ### 3.1.2 Deriving Average Cost Equations **Step 1: Average Fixed Cost (AFC)** Average cost = Total cost ÷ Number of units $$AFC = \dfrac{FC}{Q}$$ **Step 2: Average Variable Cost (AVC)** $$AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q} = \dfrac{v \times Q}{Q} = v$$ This is an important result: **AVC equals the variable cost per unit**, and it is **independent of output** in the linear model. **Step 3: Average Total Cost (ATC)** $$ATC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = \dfrac{FC + v \times Q}{Q} = \dfrac{FC}{Q} + v = AFC + AVC$$ **Step 4: Marginal Cost (MC) — In the Linear Model** Marginal cost is the additional cost of producing one more unit. $$MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q} = \dfrac{TC_{n+1} - TC_{n}}{1}$$ For the linear model: $$TC_n = FC + v \times n$$ $$TC_{n+1} = FC + v \times (n+1)$$ Subtracting: $$MC = TC_{n+1} - TC_n = v$$ So in the linear model, **MC = AVC = v** (a constant). This is a key KTU result. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstration **Problem:** A small-scale manufacturing unit has the following cost data: - Fixed Cost per month = ₹40,000 - Variable cost per unit = ₹120 - Current production = 500 units **Find:** (a) Total Cost (b) AFC (c) AVC (d) ATC (e) Total cost if production rises to 800 units **Solution:** **Step (a): Total Cost at Q = 500** $$TC = FC + v \times Q = 40000 + 120 \times 500$$ $$TC = 40000 + 60000 = 100000$$ So TC = **₹1,00,000** **Step (b): Average Fixed Cost at Q = 500** $$AFC = \dfrac{FC}{Q} = \dfrac{40000}{500} = 80$$ So AFC = **₹80 per unit** **Step (c): Average Variable Cost at Q = 500** $$AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q} = \dfrac{120 \times 500}{500} = 120$$ So AVC = **₹120 per unit** (equals $v$, as expected) **Step (d): Average Total Cost at Q = 500** $$ATC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = \dfrac{100000}{500} = 200$$ So ATC = **₹200 per unit** **Step (e): Total Cost at Q = 800** $$TC = 40000 + 120 \times 800 = 40000 + 96000 = 136000$$ So TC at 800 units = **₹1,36,000** **Inference:** The fixed cost of ₹40,000 remains the same, but total cost rose from ₹1,00,000 to ₹1,36,000 (an increase of ₹36,000) — entirely due to the variable component rising from ₹60,000 to ₹96,000. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table for Problem-Solving | Given | Find | Formula to Use | Result | |-------|------|----------------|--------| | FC, v, Q | TC | $TC = FC + vQ$ | Total Cost | | FC, v, Q | AFC | $AFC = FC/Q$ | Average Fixed Cost | | FC, v, Q | AVC | $AVC = v$ | Average Variable Cost | | FC, v, Q | ATC | $ATC = (FC + vQ)/Q$ | Average Total Cost | | TC, Q | FC | $FC = TC - vQ$ | Fixed Cost | | TVC, Q | v | $v = TVC/Q$ | Per unit VC | | TC at 2 levels of Q, Q1, Q2 | v | $v = (TC_2 - TC_1)/(Q_2 - Q_1)$ | Variable cost per unit | | v, Q1, Q2, TC1, TC2 | FC | $FC = TC_1 - vQ_1$ | Fixed Cost | ## 3.4 Verification of Cost Behaviour Properties Let us verify that **AFC falls as Q rises**: $$AFC = \dfrac{FC}{Q}$$ Take derivative with respect to $Q$: $$\dfrac{d(AFC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{FC}{Q^2} < 0$$ Since $FC > 0$ and $Q^2 > 0$, the derivative is **negative**, confirming that AFC strictly decreases as Q increases. This explains the *rectangular hyperbola* shape of the AFC curve. > [!NOTE] > **Insight for KTU Exam:** Even if you forget the formula, the *concept* — "spreading fixed cost over more units lowers the per-unit fixed cost" — earns full marks in conceptual questions. ## 3.5 Segregating Mixed Costs — High-Low Method (Supplementary) If a cost has both fixed and variable components, KTU often asks you to separate them. Use: $$v = \dfrac{C_{high} - C_{low}}{Q_{high} - Q_{low}}$$ $$FC = C_{high} - v \times Q_{high}$$ Where $C_{high}$ and $C_{low}$ are total costs at highest and lowest activity levels. **Example:** At 200 units, total cost = ₹5,000. At 600 units, total cost = ₹9,000. $$v = \dfrac{9000 - 5000}{600 - 200} = \dfrac{4000}{400} = 10 \text{ ₹/unit}$$ $$FC = 9000 - 10 \times 600 = 9000 - 6000 = 3000$$ So Fixed Cost = ₹3,000 and Variable cost per unit = ₹10. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cost Behaviour Graph (Mermaid Flowchart Representation) The following flowchart represents the **logical cost behaviour model** as taught in the KTU syllabus. Each block maps a specific cost concept to its typical graphical behaviour. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Cost TC] --> B[Fixed Cost FC] A --> C[Variable Cost VC] B --> D[Constant in total
Horizontal Line] B --> E[AFC falls as Q rises
Rectangular Hyperbola] C --> F[Linear with Q
Starts from origin] C --> G[AVC constant per unit
Horizontal line at v] F --> H[TC line parallel to VC
Intercept = FC] E --> I[ATC = AFC + AVC
Falls as Q rises] G --> I I --> J[MC equals v
Constant in linear model] D --> K[Cost Behaviour Summary] H --> K I --> K J --> K ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Cost Classification This diagram represents the **classification of costs** into fixed, variable, and semi-variable categories, mapped to the KTU-2024 syllabus framework. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Total Cost TC] --> nodeB[Cost Classification] nodeB --> nodeC[Fixed Cost FC] nodeB --> nodeD[Variable Cost VC] nodeB --> nodeE[Semi-Variable Cost] nodeC --> nodeF[Committed FC
e.g. Depreciation] nodeC --> nodeG[Discretionary FC
e.g. Advertising] nodeD --> nodeH[Direct VC
e.g. Raw Material] nodeD --> nodeI[Indirect VC
e.g. Sales Commission] nodeE --> nodeJ[Step Fixed
e.g. Supervisor Salary] nodeE --> nodeK[Mixed Cost
e.g. Telephone Bill] nodeJ --> nodeL[High-Low Method
or Scattergraph] nodeK --> nodeL ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Cost Decision Pipeline for Engineers ```mermaid flowchart TD step1[Identify Cost Items] --> step2[Classify FC vs VC] step2 --> step3[Apply Total Cost Equation
TC = FC plus vQ] step3 --> step4[Compute Average Costs
AFC, AVC, ATC] step4 --> step5[Use in Decisions
Pricing, Break-Even, Make vs Buy] step5 --> step6[Output: Engineering
Financial Decision] ``` ## 4.4 Cost Behaviour Behavioural Summary Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OutputAxis [X Axis: Output Q in units] O[Origin Q equals 0] end subgraph CostAxis [Y Axis: Cost in Rupees] C0[Zero] end Q0[Q equals 0] --> V0[VC equals 0] Q0 --> F1[FC equals k constant] Q0 --> T1[TC equals k equals FC] V0 --> L1[VC Line through origin
Slope equals v] F1 --> L2[FC Horizontal Line
at height k] T1 --> L3[TC Line parallel to VC
Intercept equals k] L1 --> R1[Relationship: TC minus FC equals VC] L2 --> R2[Relationship: FC is invariant] L3 --> R3[Relationship: TC equals FC plus vQ] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:** > - The **first flowchart** shows how Total Cost is decomposed and the typical graph shapes. > - The **second diagram** shows the classification of costs (this is what KTU expects in 14-mark answers when the question asks "Classify costs with examples"). > - The **third diagram** is a decision pipeline — useful for application questions. > - The **fourth diagram** is a schematic of cost behaviour in the cost-output plane. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between fixed cost and variable cost with two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** **Fixed Cost:** A cost that does not change in total with the level of output in the short run, within a relevant range. - Example 1: **Factory rent** of ₹2,00,000 per month — remains the same whether 100 or 1000 units are produced. - Example 2: **Depreciation** of machinery on straight-line basis — a fixed amount charged every year. **Variable Cost:** A cost that varies in direct proportion with the level of output. When output is zero, variable cost is zero. - Example 1: **Raw material cost** — if 1 unit consumes ₹50 of material, then 100 units consume ₹5,000 and 200 units consume ₹10,000. - Example 2: **Piece-rate direct labour wages** — workers are paid per unit produced. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Why does Average Fixed Cost (AFC) fall as output increases, even though Total Fixed Cost (TFC) remains constant?** **Model Answer:** Average Fixed Cost is calculated as: $$AFC = \dfrac{TFC}{Q}$$ Since TFC is a constant and $Q$ is the denominator, as $Q$ increases, the value of the fraction decreases. Economically, this means the same fixed cost is being **spread (apportioned) over a larger number of units**, so the fixed cost per unit keeps falling. Mathematically, $\dfrac{d(AFC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{TFC}{Q^2} < 0$, confirming the downward slope. For example, a fixed cost of ₹10,000 spread over 100 units = ₹100/unit, but spread over 500 units = ₹20/unit. Thus AFC keeps falling — though it never reaches zero. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]` **Q (a) [7 marks]:** A manufacturing company has a fixed cost of ₹80,000 per month. The variable cost per unit is ₹200. Compute the Total Cost, Average Fixed Cost, Average Variable Cost, and Average Total Cost for production levels of 200, 400, 600, and 800 units. Present the results in a table and comment on the trend of AFC and ATC. **Q (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the behaviour of Total Fixed Cost, Total Variable Cost, Total Cost, Average Fixed Cost, and Average Variable Cost with the help of suitable diagrams. #### Model Solution for (a): **Given:** $FC = ₹80,000$, $v = ₹200$ per unit Using the formulas: - $TC = FC + v \times Q$ - $AFC = FC/Q$ - $AVC = v = ₹200$ - $ATC = TC/Q$ **Step 1: For Q = 200 units** $$TC = 80000 + 200 \times 200 = 80000 + 40000 = 120000$$ $$AFC = \dfrac{80000}{200} = 400$$ $$AVC = 200$$ $$ATC = \dfrac{120000}{200} = 600$$ **Step 2: For Q = 400 units** $$TC = 80000 + 200 \times 400 = 80000 + 80000 = 160000$$ $$AFC = \dfrac{80000}{400} = 200$$ $$AVC = 200$$ $$ATC = \dfrac{160000}{400} = 400$$ **Step 3: For Q = 600 units** $$TC = 80000 + 200 \times 600 = 80000 + 120000 = 200000$$ $$AFC = \dfrac{80000}{600} = 133.33$$ $$AVC = 200$$ $$ATC = \dfrac{200000}{600} = 333.33$$ **Step 4: For Q = 800 units** $$TC = 80000 + 200 \times 800 = 80000 + 160000 = 240000$$ $$AFC = \dfrac{80000}{800} = 100$$ $$AVC = 200$$ $$ATC = \dfrac{240000}{800} = 300$$ **Tabulated Result:** | Q (units) | TC (₹) | AFC (₹/unit) | AVC (₹/unit) | ATC (₹/unit) | |-----------|--------|--------------|--------------|--------------| | 200 | 1,20,000 | 400.00 | 200 | 600.00 | | 400 | 1,60,000 | 200.00 | 200 | 400.00 | | 600 | 2,00,000 | 133.33 | 200 | 333.33 | | 800 | 2,40,000 | 100.00 | 200 | 300.00 | **Comment on Trends:** - **AFC falls** as output rises — from ₹400 to ₹100 — because fixed cost is spread over more units. - **AVC remains constant** at ₹200 — by definition, variable cost per unit is fixed in the linear model. - **ATC falls** as output rises — from ₹600 to ₹300 — because the falling AFC pulls ATC down, while AVC is constant. - This is the **economies of scale** effect visible in the cost structure. #### Model Solution for (b): **Total Fixed Cost (TFC) Behaviour:** - Remains **constant** at all output levels within the relevant range. - On a graph, TFC is a **horizontal straight line** parallel to the X-axis at height = FC. - At output = 0, TFC = FC (cost is still incurred). **Total Variable Cost (TVC) Behaviour:** - **Starts from the origin (0, 0)**. - Rises **linearly** with output — straight line through origin with slope $v$. - At output = 0, TVC = 0. **Total Cost (TC) Behaviour:** - $TC = FC + VC$, so TC is a **straight line parallel to the TVC line**. - Its **vertical intercept** = FC (where Q = 0). - Its **slope** = $v$. **Average Fixed Cost (AFC) Behaviour:** - $AFC = FC/Q$ → falls continuously as Q rises. - Graphically, this is a **rectangular hyperbola** — steeply falling at first, then flattening asymptotically toward zero. **Average Variable Cost (AVC) Behaviour:** - $AVC = v$ (constant per unit). - Graphically, a **horizontal line** at height $v$. **Schematic of Cost Curves:** ``` Cost │ 800│ │ TC (slope = v, intercept = FC) 600│ ╱ │ ╱ 400│ ╱ ─── AVC (horizontal at v) │ ╱ ─── 200│ ╱ ─── │ ╱ ─── 0│___________╱_________AFC falls as hyperbola___________ 0 200 400 600 800 Q (units) ``` **Key Insight:** The vertical distance between TC and TVC = FC (constant). The vertical distance between ATC and AVC = AFC (falling). --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]` **Q (a) [7 marks]:** The total cost of a firm at output levels of 500 units and 700 units is ₹3,50,000 and ₹4,50,000 respectively. The variable cost per unit is constant. Find: (i) Variable cost per unit, (ii) Fixed cost, (iii) Total cost at 1000 units. **Q (b) [7 marks]:** What are semi-variable costs? Give two examples and explain the High-Low method of segregating them into fixed and variable components. #### Model Solution for (a): **Given:** - At $Q_1 = 500$ units, $TC_1 = ₹3,50,000$ - At $Q_2 = 700$ units, $TC_2 = ₹4,50,000$ - Variable cost per unit is constant. **Step 1: Set up the equations using $TC = FC + vQ$** For $Q_1 = 500$: $$FC + 500v = 350000 \quad \text{...(1)}$$ For $Q_2 = 700$: $$FC + 700v = 450000 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 2: Subtract equation (1) from equation (2)** $$(FC + 700v) - (FC + 500v) = 450000 - 350000$$ $$200v = 100000$$ $$v = \dfrac{100000}{200} = 500$$ **Step 3: Variable cost per unit (i)** $$\boxed{v = ₹500 \text{ per unit}}$$ **Step 4: Substitute back in equation (1) to find FC** $$FC = 350000 - 500 \times 500 = 350000 - 250000$$ $$\boxed{FC = ₹1,00,000}$$ **Step 5: Total cost at 1000 units (iii)** $$TC_{1000} = FC + v \times 1000 = 100000 + 500 \times 1000$$ $$TC_{1000} = 100000 + 500000 = 600000$$ $$\boxed{TC_{1000} = ₹6,00,000}$$ **Verification using equation (2):** $$FC + 700 \times 500 = 100000 + 350000 = 450000 \checkmark$$ #### Model Solution for (b): **Semi-Variable Costs:** Semi-variable (or mixed) costs are those costs that contain **both a fixed component and a variable component**. They vary with output, but not in direct proportion. A portion remains constant while the rest changes with activity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Standard):** "A semi-variable cost is a cost that has a fixed minimum and a variable portion that changes with the level of activity." **Two Examples:** 1. **Telephone Bill:** A fixed monthly rental of ₹500 + variable charge of ₹2 per minute. Total bill = ₹500 + ₹2 × (minutes used). The ₹500 is fixed; ₹2 per minute is variable. 2. **Electricity Bill (Commercial):** A fixed service charge of ₹300 per month + energy charge of ₹8 per unit consumed. Total = ₹300 + ₹8 × (units). **High-Low Method of Segregation:** The High-Low method is a simple technique to separate a semi-variable (mixed) cost into its fixed and variable components using data from the **highest and lowest** activity periods. **Formulas:** $$v = \dfrac{C_{high} - C_{low}}{Q_{high} - Q_{low}}$$ $$FC = C_{high} - (v \times Q_{high})$$ **Where:** - $C_{high}$ = Total mixed cost at highest activity level - $C_{low}$ = Total mixed cost at lowest activity level - $Q_{high}$ = Highest activity quantity - $Q_{low}$ = Lowest activity quantity - $v$ = Variable cost per unit - $FC$ = Fixed cost component **Procedure (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Collect** total cost data for at least two periods (or more). 2. **Identify** the period with the highest activity ($Q_{high}$) and the period with the lowest activity ($Q_{low}$). 3. **Note** the corresponding costs $C_{high}$ and $C_{low}$. 4. **Apply** the High-Low formula above to compute $v$ and $FC$. 5. **Verify** by plugging into $TC = FC + vQ$ for an intermediate period. **Illustration:** Suppose a maintenance department's total cost is ₹18,000 at the highest activity month of 1500 machine hours and ₹10,000 at the lowest activity month of 500 machine hours. Then: $$v = \dfrac{18000 - 10000}{1500 - 500} = \dfrac{8000}{1000} = 8 \text{ ₹/hour}$$ $$FC = 18000 - 8 \times 1500 = 18000 - 12000 = 6000 \text{ ₹}$$ So total cost = ₹6,000 + ₹8 × (machine hours). **Limitations of High-Low Method:** - It uses only two data points (high and low), ignoring intermediate data. - It assumes linear cost behaviour. - Extreme or unusual data points can distort the result. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Total VC with VC per unit:** A common error is writing $VC = 200$ when the question states $VC$ per unit is ₹200. KTU deducts 1–2 marks for unit confusion. > 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** If the question gives output in thousands, students forget to scale $Q$ before substituting in $TC = FC + vQ$. > 3. **Not stating assumptions:** KTU awards marks for stating "assuming linear cost behaviour" in long answers. > 4. **Skipping the table/working note:** In 7-mark computation questions, only the final answer earns partial marks. Always show the table of values and intermediate steps. > 5. **Using the wrong formula for MC:** Marginal Cost in a linear model is $v$ (constant), not $TC/Q$. Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks. > 6. **Drawing graphs without axes labels:** In diagram-based questions, KTU deducts marks if axes are not labelled clearly with "Cost (₹)" and "Output (units)". --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fixed and Variable Cost** - ✅ **Fixed Cost (FC):** Constant in total; does not depend on output. Examples: rent, salary, depreciation, insurance. - ✅ **Variable Cost (VC):** Varies in proportion to output; zero at zero output. Examples: raw material, piece-rate wages, packing. - ✅ **Total Cost:** $TC = FC + TVC = FC + vQ$ - ✅ **Total Variable Cost:** $TVC = v \times Q$ - ✅ **Average Fixed Cost:** $AFC = FC / Q$ — falls as Q rises. - ✅ **Average Variable Cost:** $AVC = v$ — constant in linear model. - ✅ **Average Total Cost:** $ATC = AFC + AVC$ — falls as Q rises. - ✅ **Marginal Cost (Linear Model):** $MC = v$ — constant. - ✅ **At Output = 0:** TC = FC, TVC = 0. - ✅ **Graph of TFC:** Horizontal line at height FC. - ✅ **Graph of TVC:** Straight line from origin with slope $v$. - ✅ **Graph of TC:** Parallel to TVC; vertical intercept = FC. - ✅ **Graph of AFC:** Rectangular hyperbola, falling. - ✅ **Mixed (Semi-Variable) Costs:** Have both fixed and variable elements; segregated using High-Low Method. - ✅ **Step Costs:** Fixed over a small range, then jump to a new fixed level. - ✅ **Assumption:** Linear model valid only within the **relevant range** of output. - ✅ **Engineering Relevance:** Cost behaviour drives pricing, break-even, and make-or-buy decisions.

topics.title: Total Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Cost (TC)** is defined as the aggregate monetary value of all economic resources (factors of production) expended by a firm or project to produce a given level of output (Q) over a specific period. In the context of **Engineering Economics** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, Total Cost is the sum of **Total Fixed Cost (TFC)** and **Total Variable Cost (TVC)**.

Mathematically, the foundational identity is:

$$TC(Q) = TFC + TVC(Q)$$

Where:
- $TC(Q)$ = Total Cost as a function of output quantity $Q$
- $TFC$ = Total Fixed Cost (constant, independent of $Q$)
- $TVC(Q)$ = Total Variable Cost (a function of $Q$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Specifics:** The Finance for Engineering course treats Total Cost not merely as an accounting number, but as a **decision-making tool**. Engineers use TC curves to perform break-even analysis, make-or-buy decisions, capacity planning, and cost-volume-profit (CVP) modelling.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

### The "Lemonade Stand" Analogy 🍋

Imagine a 10-year-old child running a lemonade stand in Kerala during summer vacation.

| Cost Type | Lemonade Stand Example | Behaviour |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Fixed Cost** | The stand table, the jug, the signboard — bought ONCE at the start | Stays the same whether you sell **1 glass or 1,000 glasses** |
| **Variable Cost** | Lemons, sugar, ice, water for **each extra glass** | **Increases** with every additional glass sold |
| **Total Cost** | What the child has spent in total by end of day | = Fixed setup cost + (Cost per glass × Glasses sold) |

**Plain English Explanation:**
- On Day 1, the child spends ₹500 on the stand and signboard (this is the **Fixed Cost** — it never goes away).
- Each glass of lemonade costs ₹10 in ingredients (lemons, sugar, ice).
- If the child sells **20 glasses**, the **Total Cost** = ₹500 (fixed) + (20 × ₹10 = ₹200) = **₹700**.

This is exactly how a manufacturing firm, a software company, or a civil engineering contractor calculates their **Total Cost**. The principle is universal.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Engineering Context)

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, the following **standard units and metrics** are mandatory to remember:
> - **Currency Unit:** Indian Rupee (₹), often expressed in **Lakhs (L)** or **Crores (Cr)** for industrial projects. **1 Lakh = ₹1,00,000** and **1 Crore = ₹1,00,00,000**.
> - **Output Unit (Q):** Units produced (nos.), kilograms (kg), cubic metres (m³), or service hours.
> - **Time Period:** Typically **per month** or **per annum (p.a.)**.
> - **Depreciation:** A non-cash fixed cost computed via **Straight Line Method (SLM)** or **Written Down Value (WDV)** method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Total Cost

Total Cost is a **composite construct** with multiple layers. A KTU student must understand each layer because valuation questions frequently ask for the classification.

### 2.1.1 By Behaviour (Volume Sensitivity)

1. **Total Fixed Cost (TFC) — "The Rent Problem"**
   - Remains **constant** in total amount regardless of output level, within the *relevant range*.
   - Examples: Factory rent, salaried staff, insurance premiums, depreciation, property tax.
   - **Key Insight:** Per unit, fixed cost *falls* as output rises (spreading effect).

2. **Total Variable Cost (TVC) — "The Raw Material"**
   - Changes **directly and proportionately** (in total) with output.
   - Examples: Raw materials, direct labour (piece-rate), electricity for machinery, packaging.
   - **Key Insight:** If you produce zero, TVC = 0.

3. **Total Semi-Variable Cost (TSVC) — "The Hybrid"**
   - Contains both a fixed component and a variable component.
   - Example: Electricity bill has a fixed minimum charge + variable usage charge.
   - Formula: $TSVC = \text{Fixed Component} + (\text{Variable Rate} \times Q)$

### 2.1.2 By Traceability (Cost Accounting View)

1. **Direct Cost** — Traceable to a specific product/project (e.g., steel for a bridge).
2. **Indirect Cost (Overhead)** — Shared across products (e.g., factory supervisor's salary).

### 2.1.3 By Relevance to Decision

1. **Sunk Cost** — Already incurred, irreversible (do NOT include in future decisions).
2. **Opportunity Cost** — Value of the next best alternative foregone.
3. **Incremental Cost** — Additional cost of producing one more unit.
4. **Book Cost** — Accounting depreciation (non-cash).
5. **Cash Cost** — Actual cash outflow.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Logical Decomposition

### Step 1: Why does TFC remain constant?
Because fixed costs are tied to **time-based contracts** (annual rent, monthly salary) or **capacity-based assets** (machinery, buildings) that do not fluctuate with short-term production. A factory paying ₹50,000 monthly rent pays ₹50,000 whether it produces 0 or 10,000 units.

### Step 2: Why does TVC vary with Q?
Because variable costs require **physical inputs per unit**. Producing one more unit requires more raw material, more machine-hours, more direct labour. This is the principle of *proportionality* in the short run.

### Step 3: How do we combine them?
Through the **additivity principle** — because fixed and variable costs represent independent resource pools, the total cost is their arithmetic sum. This is the cornerstone of all engineering economic models.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU board exam numericals. Memorize every row.

| # | Cost Metric | Formula | Unit | Behaviour |
|---|-------------|---------|------|-----------|
| 1 | **Total Fixed Cost** | $TFC$ | ₹ | Constant w.r.t. $Q$ |
| 2 | **Total Variable Cost** | $TVC = v \cdot Q$ | ₹ | Linear in $Q$ |
| 3 | **Total Cost** | $TC = TFC + TVC$ | ₹ | Linear in $Q$ (linear TC model) |
| 4 | **Average Fixed Cost** | $AFC = \dfrac{TFC}{Q}$ | ₹/unit | Hyperbolic — decreases as $Q$ rises |
| 5 | **Average Variable Cost** | $AVC = \dfrac{TVC}{Q} = v$ | ₹/unit | Constant (if linear model) |
| 6 | **Average Cost (Unit Cost)** | $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q} = AFC + AVC$ | ₹/unit | U-shaped in realistic models |
| 7 | **Marginal Cost** | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | ₹/unit | Cost of one additional unit |
| 8 | **Total Revenue** | $TR = P \cdot Q$ | ₹ | Linear in $Q$ |
| 9 | **Profit / Loss** | $\pi = TR - TC$ | ₹ | Positive → profit, Negative → loss |
| 10 | **Break-Even Quantity** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{TFC}{P - v}$ | units | Where $\pi = 0$ |

**Where:**
- $v$ = variable cost per unit (₹/unit)
- $P$ = selling price per unit (₹/unit)
- $Q$ = quantity produced/sold (units)
- $\Delta$ = change operator (delta)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Total Cost |
|-------------------|---------------------------|
| **Civil Engineering** | Estimating total project cost (Bridges, Buildings, Roads) using BOQ (Bill of Quantities) |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Determining cost of manufacturing a component in a CNC shop |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Calculating Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE) for a power plant |
| **Computer Science / IT** | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for software/hardware over its lifecycle |
| **Project Management** | Earned Value Management — comparing budgeted cost vs actual cost |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** concept is heavily tested in KTU's Module 2, as it bridges the engineering and finance domains perfectly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost Identity from First Principles

### Given:
A firm produces output $Q$ in a given period. The total expenditure is composed of:
- A fixed obligation $F$ (in ₹) that is independent of $Q$.
- A variable consumption of resources at rate $v$ (₹ per unit of $Q$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Express Total Variable Cost.

The variable cost is proportional to the quantity produced. If each unit costs $v$ rupees in variable inputs, then for $Q$ units:

$$TVC = v \cdot Q$$

**Step 2:** Express Total Fixed Cost.

Fixed cost is a contractual or capacity-based expense that does not depend on $Q$:

$$TFC = F \quad \text{(a constant)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the additivity principle.

By the fundamental accounting identity (which the KTU board examiners expect you to state explicitly):

$$TC = TFC + TVC$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions.

$$TC(Q) = F + v \cdot Q$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation.

- When $Q = 0$, then $TC = F$. (No variable cost is incurred if no production occurs.)
- The slope of the TC line w.r.t. $Q$ is $v$, which is the **Marginal Cost** in the linear model.

$$\frac{dTC}{dQ} = v = MC$$

This confirms that in a linear TC model, **Marginal Cost equals Average Variable Cost equals the variable cost per unit**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Cost and Its Components

### Step 1: Divide TC by Q.

$$AC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{TFC + TVC}{Q}$$

**Step 2:** Split the fraction.

$$AC = \frac{TFC}{Q} + \frac{TVC}{Q}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the components.

$$AC = AFC + AVC$$

**Step 4:** Substitute expressions.

$$AC(Q) = \frac{F}{Q} + v$$

**Step 5:** Behavioural analysis.

- As $Q \to 0$, the term $\frac{F}{Q} \to \infty$ (the cost per unit explodes).
- As $Q \to \infty$, the term $\frac{F}{Q} \to 0$, so $AC \to v$.
- This is why **economies of scale** exist — large production runs dilute the fixed cost burden.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Break-Even Quantity (BEP)

The break-even point is where Total Revenue equals Total Cost (zero profit, zero loss).

### Step 1: Set up the profit equation.

$$\pi(Q) = TR - TC = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute TR and TC.

$$P \cdot Q - (F + v \cdot Q) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Factor out Q.

$$Q(P - v) - F = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for Q.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** The denominator $(P - v)$ is called the **Contribution Margin per unit**. For the BEP to exist (i.e., be positive and finite), we **must** have $P > v$. If $P \le v$, the firm can never break even and must shut down.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Example (Solved Step-by-Step)

### **Problem Statement:**

> A small-scale engineering unit in Kochi manufactures precision bearings. The following cost data is provided:
> - Monthly factory rent: **₹40,000**
> - Monthly depreciation on machinery: **₹15,000**
> - Permanent supervisor salary: **₹25,000**
> - Variable cost per bearing: **₹120** (raw material + direct labour)
> - Selling price per bearing: **₹200**
>
> **Calculate for a production of 500 bearings in a month:**
> 1. Total Fixed Cost (TFC)
> 2. Total Variable Cost (TVC)
> 3. Total Cost (TC)
> 4. Average Cost (AC)
> 5. Profit / Loss
> 6. Break-Even Quantity (BEP)

### **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify TFC components.**

The fixed costs are all those that do NOT depend on the 500 bearings:

$$TFC = \text{Rent} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{Supervisor Salary}$$

$$TFC = 40{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2: Compute TVC for Q = 500 units.**

$$TVC = v \cdot Q = 120 \times 500 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Cost.**

$$TC = TFC + TVC = 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4: Compute Average Cost per unit.**

$$AC = \frac{TC}{Q} = \frac{1{,}40{,}000}{500} = 280 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 5: Compute Total Revenue and Profit.**

$$TR = P \cdot Q = 200 \times 500 = 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\pi = TR - TC = 1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}40{,}000 = -40{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

Since $\pi < 0$, the firm incurs a **loss of ₹40,000** at this production level.

**Step 6: Compute Break-Even Quantity.**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{TFC}{P - v} = \frac{80{,}000}{200 - 120} = \frac{80{,}000}{80} = 1{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 7: Verification of BEP.**

At $Q = 1{,}000$:

$$TR = 200 \times 1{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$TC = 80{,}000 + (120 \times 1{,}000) = 80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

$$\pi = 2{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 0 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark$$

The BEP is confirmed. The firm must produce **at least 1,000 bearings per month** to avoid losses.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Linear vs. Realistic (Non-Linear) TC Models

| Feature | Linear TC Model | Realistic (Non-Linear) Model |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| TC Equation | $TC = F + vQ$ | $TC = F + aQ + bQ^2$ (where $b > 0$ for diminishing returns) |
| Shape of TC curve | Straight line | Parabolic (curved) |
| MC | Constant ($= v$) | Increasing with $Q$ ($MC = a + 2bQ$) |
| AC | Monotonically decreasing (approaches $v$) | U-shaped (initially decreases, then increases) |
| Use in KTU | Module 2 basic problems | Advanced CVP / Optimization problems |
| Why important | Easy to calculate, common in textbook KTU | Reflects diminishing marginal returns in real factories |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
