## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **"Distinguish between long-term and short-term financing. Give two examples of each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Long-Term Financing | Short-Term Financing |
|---|---|---|
| **Tenure** | More than 1 year (typically 3–20 years) | Up to 1 year |
| **Purpose** | Acquire fixed assets, expansion, R&D, long-term growth | Working capital, inventory, wages, day-to-day operations |
| **Cost** | Lower per annum for debt, but higher for equity (8%–20%) | Lower nominal rate, but high annualized cost (6%–18%) |
| **Risk** | Lower interest rate risk, but long-term commitment | Refinancing risk, rollover risk |
| **Sources** | Equity shares, debentures, term loans, venture capital | Trade credit, overdraft, cash credit, commercial paper |
| **Flexibility** | Less flexible (lock-in period) | Highly flexible (can be paid off early) |

**Examples:**
- **Long-term:** Issue of 10-year debentures; equity IPO.
- **Short-term:** Bank overdraft; trade credit from suppliers.

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **"What is Venture Capital? Mention any two features."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Venture Capital (VC) is a form of **private equity financing** provided by specialized investment funds to **early-stage, high-growth, high-risk startups** in exchange for an equity stake, with active involvement in management.

**Two Features:**
1. **High Risk-High Return:** VC investors accept high failure risk in exchange for outsized returns from the few "home run" investments.
2. **Equity Investment:** VC takes ownership stake (typically 20%–40%) rather than lending money, aligning investor and founder interests.
3. *(Any one of the following may also be given:)* Active mentorship, staged funding (rounds), 5–7 year exit horizon via IPO or acquisition.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Most common mistake:** Students mix up **pre-tax and post-tax** cost of debt. Always multiply interest by $(1 - t)$ in WACC computations.
> - **Second pitfall:** Students forget the **growth rate $g$** in Gordon's model. Without $g$, you are computing dividend yield, not cost of equity.
> - **Third pitfall:** In WACC, students sometimes add costs in **percentages** and weights in **decimals** incorrectly. The cleanest method: convert everything to decimals, compute, then multiply by 100.
> - **Fourth pitfall:** Cost of trade credit problems — students forget to **annualize** the periodic cost. Always state "annualized" clearly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) (7 Marks):** A company is financed by the following capital structure:
- **Equity Share Capital:** ₹5,00,000 (10,000 shares of ₹50 each)
- **8% Preference Share Capital:** ₹2,00,000
- **6% Debentures:** ₹3,00,000

The equity shares are currently trading at ₹80 per share, and the company is expected to pay a dividend of ₹6 per share next year with a growth rate of 5% per annum. Debentures are redeemable at par after 10 years, issued at par. Tax rate = **30%**.

**Compute the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Cost of Equity ($K_e$) using Gordon's Model:**

$$K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \dfrac{6}{80} + 0.05$$

$$K_e = 0.075 + 0.05 = 0.125 = 12.5\%$$

**Step 2: Compute Cost of Preference Shares ($K_p$):**

Annual preference dividend per share = 8% of ₹100 (assuming ₹100 face value) = ₹8
Wait, the problem states "₹2,00,000" preference capital. Let us assume face value ₹100, so 2,000 shares.

Dividend per share = 8% of ₹100 = ₹8 (assuming full preference value). Cost of preference = ₹8 / ₹100 = 8%.

$$K_p = \dfrac{8\% \text{ of face value}}{\text{Issue Price}} = \dfrac{8}{100} = 0.08 = 8\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Cost of Debentures ($K_d$):**

Since issued at par and redeemed at par, the only cost is the after-tax interest.

$$K_d = \dfrac{I (1 - t)}{NP} = \dfrac{6\% \times (1 - 0.30)}{1.00} = 6 \times 0.70 = 4.2\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Weights:**

Total Capital = ₹5,00,000 + ₹2,00,000 + ₹3,00,000 = ₹10,00,000

$$W_e = \dfrac{5,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.50$$

$$W_p = \dfrac{2,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.20$$

$$W_d = \dfrac{3,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.30$$

**Verification:** $0.50 + 0.20 + 0.30 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 5: Compute WACC:**

$$WACC = (W_e \times K_e) + (W_p \times K_p) + (W_d \times K_d)$$

$$WACC = (0.50 \times 12.5) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 4.2)$$

$$WACC = 6.25 + 1.60 + 1.26 = 9.11\%$$

**Final Answer:** **WACC = 9.11%**

> **Interpretation:** The firm must earn at least 9.11% on its investments to satisfy all capital providers.

---

**(b) (7 Marks):** A supplier offers credit terms of "**2/10 Net 30**". A firm is considering whether to take the cash discount. Compute the cost of forgoing the discount. Should the firm forgo the discount if it can earn 15% on short-term investments?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Variables:**
- Discount rate $d$ = 2% = 0.02
- Discount period $P$ = 10 days
- Credit period $C$ = 30 days
- Effective credit extension = $C - P = 30 - 10 = 20$ days

**Step 2: Periodic Cost of Forgoing Discount:**

$$r_{period} = \dfrac{d}{1 - d} = \dfrac{0.02}{1 - 0.02} = \dfrac{0.02}{0.98} = 0.020408$$

**Step 3: Annualize Using Compounding:**

Number of 20-day periods in 360 days:
$$N = \dfrac{360}{20} = 18$$

**Step 4: Apply Compounding Formula:**

$$K_{tc} = (1 + 0.020408)^{18} - 1$$

$$K_{tc} = (1.020408)^{18} - 1$$

Calculating $(1.020408)^{18}$:
$$(1.020408)^2 = 1.04123$$
$$(1.04123)^3 = 1.1283$$ (i.e., 9th power)
$$(1.1283)^2 = 1.4330$$ (i.e., 18th power)

$$K_{tc} = 1.4330 - 1 = 0.4330 = 43.30\%$$

**Final Answer:** **Cost of forgoing discount = 43.30% per annum**

**Decision:** Since the firm can earn only **15%** on short-term investments, which is **less than 43.30%**, the firm should **TAKE THE DISCOUNT** and pay on Day 10. Forgoing the discount is equivalent to borrowing at an exorbitant 43.30% annualized rate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) (7 Marks):** Explain the **Matching Principle** of financing. How does it help in determining the working capital strategy of a firm?

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Matching Principle** (also called the **Hedging Approach** or **Risk-Return Trade-off Approach**) states that the **maturity of the source of finance should match the maturity of the asset being financed**. In other words:

> - **Long-term assets** (fixed assets, permanent current assets) → funded by **long-term sources**.
> - **Short-term (temporary) assets** (seasonal inventory, temporary receivables) → funded by **short-term sources**.

**Three Working Capital Strategies:**

1. **Hedging / Matching Approach:** Perfect matching — long-term assets by long-term sources, short-term assets by short-term sources. *Lowest risk, moderate cost.*

2. **Conservative Approach:** All assets (including temporary) funded by long-term sources. *Lowest risk, highest cost (since long-term rates are costlier).*

3. **Aggressive Approach:** All assets (including permanent) funded by short-term sources. *Highest risk, lowest cost (in good times), but exposes firm to rollover risk.*

**Illustration (Numerical):**

Suppose a firm has:
- Fixed Assets: ₹5,00,000 (permanent)
- Permanent Current Assets: ₹2,00,000
- Temporary Current Assets: ₹3,00,000 (seasonal)

**Under Matching Approach:**
- Long-term financing = ₹5,00,000 + ₹2,00,000 = ₹7,00,000
- Short-term financing = ₹3,00,000

**Under Conservative Approach:**
- Long-term financing = ₹5,00,000 + ₹2,00,000 + ₹3,00,000 = ₹10,00,000
- Short-term financing = ₹0

**Under Aggressive Approach:**
- Long-term financing = ₹5,00,000 (only fixed)
- Short-term financing = ₹2,00,000 + ₹3,00,000 = ₹5,00,000

**Conclusion:** The matching principle balances **risk and cost** optimally. Conservative strategies sacrifice return for safety; aggressive strategies maximize return at the cost of liquidity risk.

---

**(b) (7 Marks):** A firm has the following capital structure:
- **Equity:** ₹6,00,000, expected return $K_e$ = 14%
- **Debt:** ₹4,00,000, before-tax cost = 10%, tax rate = 25%

**Compute the WACC. Also, explain the effect of debt on WACC.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Capital:**
$$E + D = 6,00,000 + 4,00,000 = ₹10,00,000$$

**Step 2: Weights:**
$$W_e = \dfrac{6,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.60$$

$$W_d = \dfrac{4,00,000}{10,00,000} = 0.40$$

**Step 3: After-tax Cost of Debt:**
$$K_d (\text{after tax}) = 10\% \times (1 - 0.25) = 10 \times 0.75 = 7.5\%$$

**Step 4: Compute WACC:**
$$WACC = (W_e \times K_e) + (W_d \times K_d)$$

$$WACC = (0.60 \times 14) + (0.40 \times 7.5)$$

$$WACC = 8.4 + 3.0 = 11.4\%$$

**Final Answer:** **WACC = 11.4%**

**Effect of Debt on WACC:**

> **Debt is generally CHEAPER than equity** because:
> 1. **Tax Shield:** Interest on debt is tax-deductible, reducing the effective cost (10% becomes 7.5% after tax). Dividend on equity is paid out of post-tax profit.
> 2. **Lower Risk to Providers:** Debt holders have priority in liquidation and fixed interest, so they demand lower returns than equity holders.
> 3. **No Ownership Dilution:** Debt does not dilute promoter ownership.

**However, increasing debt beyond a threshold increases WACC** because:
- **Financial Risk** rises with higher debt → equity holders demand higher returns.
- **Bankruptcy Risk** increases.
- **Credit Rating** may drop, raising the cost of new debt.

**Conclusion:** There exists an **Optimal Capital Structure** where WACC is minimized — this is the cornerstone of Modigliani-Miller theory (with taxes) and the Trade-off Theory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Long-Term and Short-Term Financing**

- ☐ **Long-term financing** = period > 1 year. Sources: equity, preference, debentures, term loans, venture capital, retained earnings, IPO.
- ☐ **Short-term financing** = period ≤ 1 year. Sources: trade credit, bank overdraft, cash credit, commercial paper, factoring, short-term loans.
- ☐ **Equity** is the costliest (12%–20%) but most flexible; **debt** is cheaper post-tax due to interest tax shield.
- ☐ **Gordon's Model:** $K_e = D_1 / P_0 + g$. Always include growth rate $g$.
- ☐ **Cost of Debt (post-tax):** $K_d = I(1-t) / NP$. Always multiply by $(1-t)$.
- ☐ **WACC** = weighted average of all costs; weights MUST sum to 1.0.
- ☐ **Trade credit cost** is highly annualized; "2/10 Net 30" can be 36%–44% p.a. if discount is forgone.
- ☐ **Matching Principle:** long-term assets → long-term sources; short-term assets → short-term sources.
- ☐ **Conservative strategy** (more long-term) = lower risk, higher cost. **Aggressive strategy** (more short-term) = higher risk, lower cost.
- ☐ **Venture Capital** is for high-growth startups; equity stake; 5–7 year exit.
- ☐ **Commercial Paper** is for highly rated firms; minimum ₹5 Lakhs; 15 days–1 year; discount instrument.
- ☐ **Factoring** converts receivables into immediate cash; types: recourse/non-recourse.
- ☐ **Cash Credit** interest is charged only on **utilized amount**, not the sanctioned limit.
- ☐ **Optimal Capital Structure** minimizes WACC and maximizes firm value — the fundamental goal of financing decisions.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Long-Term and Short-Term Financing**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financing of PPP Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Financing of PPP Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public-Private Partnership (PPP)**: A long-term contractual arrangement between a **public agency** (Government/Statutory Body) and a **private sector entity** for the creation/management of public infrastructure or services, where the **private partner** assumes significant financial, technical, and operational risk in exchange for user charges, government payments, or a combination of both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Finance**: A specialized financing structure where **debt and equity** are raised for a **single, capital-intensive project**, with repayment made **exclusively from the project's own cash flows**. The lenders look primarily to the **project's assets and revenues** as collateral, not the general credit of the sponsors. This is the **cornerstone of PPP financing**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Building Intuition)

**Think of PPP as a "Joint Family Enterprise" for building public assets:**

Imagine a traditional Indian family wanting to build a big commercial complex.

- The **Elder (Government)** owns the **prime land** in the city centre and provides the **legal right** (Concession Agreement) to develop it.
- The **Younger, Wealthier Sibling (Private Company)** brings the **construction money (debt + equity)** and the **business expertise** to run the complex.
- The complex (Highway, Airport, Metro) serves **third parties — the public (users)** who pay rent/toll/fare.
- The earnings are first used to **repay loans**, then to pay **dividends to the sibling**, and a **share goes back to the elder (Concession Fee / Revenue Share)**.
- After **20–30 years**, the complex is **handed back** to the family elder.

In this analogy:
- The "**Elder's land**" = **Right-of-Way / Public Asset**
- The "**Younger's money**" = **Project Finance (Debt + Equity)**
- The "**Complex**" = **Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)**
- The "**Rent collected**" = **Project Cash Flows / User Charges**
- The "**Hand-back**" = **Concession End / Transfer to Government**

> [!TIP]
> **One-line intuition**: *PPP = "Public sector provides the land & rules, private sector provides the money & management, and both share the cake of revenues for a defined period."*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in PPP Financing

The following **standard financial benchmarks** govern PPP financing decisions:

- **Debt-Equity Ratio (DER)**: Typically maintained between **70:30 and 80:20** for Indian infrastructure PPPs.
- **Minimum DSCR (Debt Service Coverage Ratio)**: Lender-mandated floor of **1.20x to 1.50x**.
- **Concession Period**: Generally **20 to 30 years** for highways, **30 to 40 years** for ports/power.
- **Viability Gap Funding (VGF) cap**: Up to **20% of Total Project Cost** (under Government of India Scheme, 2006 — used as a reference metric).
- **Project IRR (Pre-tax)**: Typically **12% to 18%** for infrastructure projects in India.
- **Equity IRR**: Generally targeted at **15% to 22%** to attract private investors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of PPP Project Finance

PPP project finance is built on a **ring-fenced structure** that legally isolates project risks from the sponsors. The theoretical framework has six pillars:

### Pillar 1: The Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)
- A **separate legal company** is created solely for the project.
- All contracts (loan agreements, construction contracts, O&M contracts) are signed **in the name of SPV**.
- This is the **"Single Borrower"** for the lenders.
- Protects sponsors — their liability is **limited to their equity contribution** (Non-Recourse / Limited Recourse finance).

### Pillar 2: The Concession Agreement
- A **concession agreement** between the **Government (Grantor)** and the **SPV (Concessionaire)**.
- Grants the SPV the right to **design, build, finance, operate, and transfer (DBFOT)** the asset.
- Defines **concession period, performance standards, user charges, and termination clauses**.

### Pillar 3: Debt Financing (Senior Debt)
- Largest component (typically **70–80%** of capital).
- Sources: **Commercial banks, Infrastructure Debt Funds (IDFs), Bond markets, Multilateral agencies (World Bank, ADB, IFC)**.
- **Repayment priority**: Senior debt is paid **before equity holders** receive any return ("**cash flow waterfall**").

### Pillar 4: Equity Financing
- **Sponsor equity** (the private company that won the bid).
- May include **co-investors** (pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, infrastructure funds).
- **Subordinated** to debt — last to receive cash, but receives **residual profits**.

### Pillar 5: Government Support Mechanisms
- **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** — one-time capital grant to make the project financially viable.
- **Annuity Payments / Shadow Tolls** — government pays the SPV fixed periodic sums (used in **BOT-Annuity** model).
- **Guarantees** — sovereign guarantee against political/regulatory risks.

### Pillar 6: User Charges (Revenue Streams)
- **Direct user charges**: Tolls (highways), tariffs (power/water), fares (metro).
- **Indirect revenue**: Advertising, real estate development rights (e.g., metro station naming rights).

---

## 2.2 Models of PPP Financing

| **PPP Model** | **Full Form** | **Asset Ownership During Concession** | **Who Bears Demand Risk?** | **Revenue Source** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **BOT (Toll)** | Build-Operate-Transfer | SPV | **Private (High Risk)** | User tolls |
| **BOT (Annuity)** | Build-Operate-Transfer | SPV | **Government (Low Risk)** | Government annuity payments |
| **BOOT** | Build-Own-Operate-Transfer | SPV | **Private** | User charges |
| **DBFOT** | Design-Build-Finance-Operate-Transfer | SPV | **Private** | User charges + VGF |
| **DBOOT** | Design-Build-Own-Operate-Transfer | SPV | **Private** | User charges |
| **HAM** | Hybrid Annuity Model | Government | **Shared (50:50)** | 40% Govt + 60% User |
| **O&M Contract** | Operate-Maintain | Government | **Government** | Government fees |
| **BOO** | Build-Own-Operate (No transfer) | Private (perpetual) | **Private** | User charges |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examination-grade essentials for Module 3.** Memorize the formula, the unit, and the typical threshold values.

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Typical Threshold / Unit** | **Significance in PPP** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Debt-Equity Ratio (DER)** | $DER = \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}$ | **70:30 to 80:20** | Capital structure benchmark |
| 2 | **DSCR (Year-t)** | $DSCR_t = \dfrac{\text{Net Operating Cash Flow}_t}{\text{Debt Service}_t}$ | **Min 1.20x – 1.50x** | Lender's safety ratio |
| 3 | **Annualized Debt Service (Equal Installment)** | $EMI = P \cdot \dfrac{r(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | P = Principal; r = interest rate; n = years | Equal yearly loan repayment |
| 4 | **Loan Life Coverage Ratio (LLCR)** | $LLCR = \dfrac{\text{NPV of CFADS over loan life}}{\text{Outstanding Debt}}$ | **> 1.0x always** | Long-term solvency check |
| 5 | **Debt Life Coverage Ratio (DLCR)** | $DLCR_t = \dfrac{\text{PV of CFADS}_t}{\text{Debt Service}_t}$ | **> 1.0x each year** | Year-by-year solvency |
| 6 | **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** | $VGF = \min(\text{Project Cost} - \text{PV of Revenues},\ 0.20 \times \text{Project Cost})$ | **Cap: 20% of TPC** | Government grant |
| 7 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | **NPV > 0 → Accept** | Project feasibility |
| 8 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | **Solve iteratively** | Discount rate that makes NPV = 0 |
| 9 | **Project IRR (Post-tax)** | $PIRR: \text{solve NPV} = 0 \text{ at project level}$ | **12% – 18% (India Infra)** | Return on total investment |
| 10 | **Equity IRR (Cash-on-Cash)** | $EIRR: \text{equity inflows/outflows only}$ | **15% – 22%** | Return to private sponsor |
| 11 | **Payback Period** | $PB = \text{Years before cumulative CF} \geq \text{Initial Investment}$ | **Years** | Risk timing indicator |
| 12 | **Average DSCR (ADSCR)** | $ADSCR = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} DSCR_t}{n}$ | **> 1.5x preferred** | Average debt safety |
| 13 | **Cumulative DSCR** | $\sum DSCR = \dfrac{\sum CFADS}{\sum Debt Service}$ | **> 1.5x typical** | Total debt coverage |
| 14 | **Project Leverage** | $\text{Leverage} = \dfrac{\text{Debt}}{\text{Total Project Cost}}$ | **60% – 80%** | Debt proportion |
| 15 | **Concession Fee** | $CF = \% \text{ of Gross Revenue (predefined in CA)}$ | **1% – 5% of revenue** | Government revenue share |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safeguard Applied:** Vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` syntax to prevent breaking markdown table rendering.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does a Kerala B.Tech engineer need to know PPP Financing?**
> - The **Kochi Metro Rail Ltd. (KMRL)** is financed as a **PPP-cum-SPV project** combining JICA debt, GoI grant, and state equity.
> - The **Cochin International Airport** expansion used a **public-cooperative-private model** (a variant of PPP).
> - **Smart City Mission (Kochi)** uses PPP for water supply, solid waste, and urban mobility.
> - **Kerala State Road Project** under KRDCL uses **HAM (Hybrid Annuity Model)** extensively.
> - In the **Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board (KIIFB)**, PPP concepts of **blended finance** are applied for state-funded projects.

Understanding PPP financing is **not optional** — it is the **dominant delivery model** for India's ₹100+ lakh crore infrastructure pipeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: DSCR and Debt-Equity Ratio for a Highway BOT Project

### Problem Statement (KTU Exam Style)

> A private consortium is implementing a **4-lane highway BOT (Toll) project** in Kerala with the following financial parameters:
>
> - **Total Project Cost (TPC)** = ₹1,000 crore
> - **Debt component** = ₹700 crore (Senior term loan)
> - **Equity component** = ₹300 crore
> - **Loan terms**: 12% p.a. interest, **15-year** repayment with equal annual installments
> - **Concession Period** = 20 years (5 years construction + 15 years tolling)
> - **Year 1 (Operations)** Net Operating Cash Flow After Tax (NOCFAT) = ₹150 crore
>
> **Calculate**:
> (a) Debt-Equity Ratio
> (b) Annual Debt Service (Equal Installment)
> (c) DSCR in Year 1
> (d) Comment on bankability

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a) — Debt-Equity Ratio

$$
DER = \dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
DER = \dfrac{700}{300} = 2.33
$$

Expressed as a ratio:

$$
\boxed{DER = 2.33 : 1 \quad \text{(or equivalently, 70:30)}}
$$

**Interpretation**: 70% of the project is funded by debt, 30% by equity. This is within the **acceptable range of 70:30 to 80:20** for Indian highway PPPs.

---

#### Part (b) — Annual Debt Service (Equal Installment)

The Equal Monthly Installment (EMI) formula, adapted for annual installments, is:

$$
EMI = P \cdot \dfrac{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}
$$

Where:
- $P = 700$ crore (loan principal)
- $r = 0.12$ (annual interest rate, **12%**)
- $n = 15$ (loan tenure in years)

**Step 1**: Compute $(1+r)^n$

$$
(1.12)^{15}
$$

Using iterative multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^1 &= 1.1200 \\
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^4 &= (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^8 &= (1.5735)^2 = 2.4760 \\
(1.12)^{15} &= (1.12)^8 \times (1.12)^4 \times (1.12)^2 \times (1.12)^1 \\
&= 2.4760 \times 1.5735 \times 1.2544 \times 1.1200 \\
&= 5.4736
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Compute the numerator

$$
r \cdot (1+r)^n = 0.12 \times 5.4736 = 0.65683
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the denominator

$$
(1+r)^n - 1 = 5.4736 - 1 = 4.4736
$$

**Step 4**: Compute the annuity factor

$$
\dfrac{0.65683}{4.4736} = 0.14683
$$

**Step 5**: Compute the annual installment

$$
EMI = 700 \times 0.14683 = \boxed{102.78 \text{ crore per year}}
$$

---

#### Part (c) — DSCR in Year 1

$$
DSCR_1 = \dfrac{\text{Net Operating Cash Flow Year 1}}{\text{Annual Debt Service}}
$$

$$
DSCR_1 = \dfrac{150}{102.78} = \boxed{1.46}
$$

---

#### Part (d) — Bankability Comment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bankability Assessment**:
>
> - DSCR of **1.46x** is **acceptable** to most Indian lenders (typical lender threshold = **1.20x – 1.50x**).
> - DER of **2.33:1 (70:30)** is **within the standard range** for highway BOT projects.
> - **Adequate safety margin** is present — even a 32% drop in toll revenue would not cause a default.
> - **Conclusion**: The project is **bankable**, and financial closure is **feasible** under standard PPP norms.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Viability Gap Funding (VGF) Calculation

### Problem Statement

> A water supply PPP project in a Kerala municipality has:
>
> - **Total Project Cost** = ₹500 crore
> - **PV of expected revenue (30-year operations, 12% discount)** = ₹380 crore
> - **Government's VGF cap** = 20% of TPC
>
> **Calculate** the actual VGF admissible and the private equity required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the financial gap**

$$
\text{Financial Gap} = \text{TPC} - \text{PV of Revenues}
$$

$$
\text{Financial Gap} = 500 - 380 = 120 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the VGF cap**

$$
\text{VGF Cap} = 0.20 \times \text{TPC} = 0.20 \times 500 = 100 \text{ crore}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the minimum rule**

$$
VGF_{admissible} = \min(\text{Financial Gap},\ \text{VGF Cap})
$$

$$
VGF_{admissible} = \min(120,\ 100) = \boxed{100 \text{ crore}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Insight for Exam**: The Financial Gap (₹120 Cr) is **higher** than the VGF Cap (₹100 Cr). Therefore, the **government grants the maximum cap (₹100 Cr)**, and the **remaining ₹20 Cr must be raised as private equity** (because the project is not viable without it).

**Step 4: Derive the private equity requirement**

Assume **debt = 70% of TPC** (₹350 Cr) and **VGF = ₹100 Cr**:

$$
\text{Private Equity} = \text{TPC} - \text{Debt} - \text{VGF}
$$

$$
\text{Private Equity} = 500 - 350 - 100 = \boxed{50 \text{ crore}}
$$

**Verification of Capital Structure**:

$$
\dfrac{\text{Debt} = 350}{\text{Total Non-Grant} = 400} = 87.5\%
$$

Since the government provides ₹100 Cr grant, the effective **DER for the private side** is:

$$
DER_{private} = \dfrac{350}{50} = 7:1
$$

This is a **highly leveraged but typical structure** for VGF-funded PPP projects.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: NPV & Project IRR of a 5-Year Mini-PPP

### Problem Statement

> A small solar-power BOT project has the following cash flows:
>
> | Year | Cash Flow (₹ Crore) |
> |:---:|:---:|
> | 0 | -200 (Investment) |
> | 1 | 50 |
> | 2 | 70 |
> | 3 | 90 |
> | 4 | 100 |
> | 5 | 80 |
>
> **Compute NPV at r = 10%** and verify the bankability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the NPV formula**

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 2: Discount each cash flow at 10%**

| Year | CF (₹ Cr) | Discount Factor $(1.10)^{-t}$ | PV (₹ Cr) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | -200 | 1.0000 | -200.00 |
| 1 | 50 | 0.9091 | 45.45 |
| 2 | 70 | 0.8264 | 57.85 |
| 3 | 90 | 0.7513 | 67.62 |
| 4 | 100 | 0.6830 | 68.30 |
| 5 | 80 | 0.6209 | 49.67 |

**Step 3: Sum the present values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= -200.00 + 45.45 + 57.85 + 67.62 + 68.30 + 49.67 \\
&= 88.89 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{NPV = +₹88.89 \text{ crore (Positive → Project is bankable)}}
$$

**Step 4: Project IRR — Conceptual Approximation**

The IRR is the rate $r^*$ at which NPV = 0. Since NPV at 10% is +₹88.89 Cr, IRR > 10%.

Trial: At r = 25%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{25\%} &= -200 + \dfrac{50}{1.25} + \dfrac{70}{1.5625} + \dfrac{90}{1.9531} + \dfrac{100}{2.4414} + \dfrac{80}{3.0518} \\
&= -200 + 40.00 + 44.80 + 46.08 + 40.96 + 26.21 \\
&= -1.95 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since NPV at 10% = +88.89 and NPV at 25% = -1.95, IRR lies between 10% and 25%.

**Linear interpolation**:

$$
IRR \approx 10\% + \dfrac{88.89}{88.89 - (-1.95)} \times (25\% - 10\%)
$$

$$
IRR \approx 10\% + \dfrac{88.89}{90.84} \times 15\% \approx 10\% + 14.68\% = 24.68\%
$$

$$
\boxed{IRR \approx 24.7\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip**: A Project IRR of **24.7%** is **well above the cost of capital (10%)** and the hurdle rate for infrastructure (12–18%). The project is **highly attractive** and **bankable** under any PPP finance framework.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Ratio Matters

| **Ratio** | **Lender's Concern** | **Equity Investor's Concern** | **Government's Concern** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **DSCR > 1.2x** | Is debt service safe? | Constrains dividend payouts | Will the SPV survive? |
| **LLCR > 1.0x** | Is the loan fully recoverable? | Predicts refinancing risk | Will handback be smooth? |
| **DER 70:30** | Is the leverage manageable? | Maximizes equity return (leverage effect) | Is private skin in the game enough? |
| **VGF Cap 20%** | Reduces debt size | Dilutes equity but improves IRR | Fiscal prudence check |
| **Equity IRR > 15%** | Signals private appetite | The **return benchmark** | Attracts quality bidders |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: PPP Project Finance Architecture

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the **complete flow of funds, contracts, and risk allocation** in a typical PPP project (e.g., a highway BOT project).

```mermaid
graph TD
    Gov[GOVERNMENT GRANTOR] -->|Concession Agreement| SPV[SPECIAL PURPOSE VEHICLE SPV]
    Gov -->|VGF Grant Subsidy| SPV
    PS[PRIVATE SPONSOR] -->|Sponsor Equity Capital| SPV
    PE[PENSION FUNDS SOVEREIGN WEALTH] -->|Co Investor Equity| SPV
    CB[COMMERCIAL BANKS NBFC] -->|Senior Term Loan Debt| SPV
    BM[BOND MARKET IDF] -->|Long Term Bonds| SPV
    MA[MULTILATERAL AGENCIES] -->|Sub Debt| SPV
    SPV -->|EPC Contract| EC[EPC CONTRACTOR]
    EC -->|Constructed Highway| SPV
    SPV -->|O and M Contract| OM[OPERATIONS AND MAINTENANCE FIRM]
    USR[END USERS PUBLIC] -->|Toll Fare Payments| SPV
    SPV -->|Debt Principal Interest Repayment| CB
    SPV -->|Debt Principal Interest Repayment| BM
    SPV -->|Debt Service to Sub Debt| MA
    SPV -->|Dividend Distribution| PS
    SPV -->|Dividend Distribution| PE
    SPV -->|Concession Fee Revenue Share| Gov
    SPV -->|Income Tax Royalty| Gov
    SPV -->|Concession Period 20 to 30 Yrs| HB[HAND BACK TO GOVERNMENT]
```

**Key Insights from the Diagram:**

1. **Triple Money Flow into SPV**: Equity (sponsor + co-investor) + Debt (banks + bonds + multilaterals) + Government Support (VGF).
2. **Single Money Flow Out of Users**: User charges (toll/fare/tariff) are the **primary revenue source**.
3. **Cash Flow Waterfall**: First Debt Service → Then Government Obligations → Then Equity Dividends.
4. **Hand-back Loop**: At concession end, the asset reverts to the Government for continued public use.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Lifecycle of PPP Project Finance

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STAGE 1 PROJECT IDENTIFICATION] --> B[STAGE 2 FEASIBILITY STUDY AND DPR]
    B --> C[STAGE 3 GOVERNMENT APPROVAL AND TENDER]
    C --> D[STAGE 4 BIDDING BY PRIVATE CONSORTIA]
    D --> E[STAGE 5 AWARD AND CONCESSION SIGNING]
    E --> F[STAGE 6 FINANCIAL CLOSURE DEBT PLUS EQUITY LOCKED]
    F --> G[STAGE 7 CONSTRUCTION PHASE 2 to 5 YRS]
    G --> H[STAGE 8 OPERATIONS PHASE 15 to 30 YRS TOLL COLLECTION]
    H --> I[STAGE 9 CONCESSION END AND HAND BACK]
    I --> J[STAGE 10 POST CONCESSION O and M BY GOVERNMENT]
```

**Mapping Finance Activities to Lifecycle Stages:**

| **Stage** | **Finance Activity** |
|:---|:---|
| 1–2 | Pre-feasibility, traffic studies, demand forecasting |
| 3 | Budgetary Quote, PPPAC clearance, VGF sanction |
| 4 | Bid preparation — financial modeling by bidders |
| 5 | Signing of **Concession Agreement** (legally binding) |
| 6 | **Financial Closure** — Loan agreements signed, equity locked in |
| 7 | **Construction drawdowns** from debt, milestone-based payments |
| 8 | **Revenue collection** → **debt service** → **equity dividend** |
| 9 | Final debt repayment, terminal value, asset reverts |
| 10 | Public asset under full government control |

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Risk Allocation Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[CONSTRUCTION RISK] -->|Private| SPV_R[SPV PRIVATE]
    R2[OPERATIONAL RISK] -->|Private| SPV_R
    R3[FINANCIAL RISK] -->|Private| SPV_R
    R4[DEMAND RISK TOLL] -->|Private| SPV_R
    R5[POLITICAL RISK] -->|Government| GOV_R[GOVT GRANTOR]
    R6[REGULATORY RISK] -->|Government| GOV_R
    R7[FORCE MAJEURE] -->|Shared 50 50| SPV_R
    R7 -->|Shared 50 50| GOV_R
    R8[LAND ACQUISITION RISK] -->|Government| GOV_R
    R9[ENVIRONMENTAL RISK] -->|Shared| SPV_R
    R9 -->|Shared| GOV_R
    R10[INFLATION RISK] -->|Indexed User Charges| SPV_R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Allocation Principle** (KTU Board Examiner Favourite):
> *"Allocate each risk to the party best able to manage it."*
> - **Private**: Construction, Operations, Finance, Demand (in toll projects)
> - **Government**: Land, Political, Regulatory
> - **Shared**: Force majeure, Environmental, Inflation

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Define Public-Private Partnership (PPP). List any four characteristics of PPP projects.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
PPP is a long-term contractual arrangement between a public sector entity (Government/Statutory Body) and a private sector entity for the creation, operation, and management of public infrastructure and services, where the private partner assumes significant financial, technical, and operational risk in return for user charges, government payments, or a combination.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 Marks):**

1. **Long-term Concession Period** (typically 20–30 years).
2. **Risk Sharing** between public and private partners based on risk-management capability.
3. **Private Financing** of public assets through a **Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)**.
4. **Performance-Based Payments** linked to service delivery standards.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **What is a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV) in PPP project finance? State any two of its advantages.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
A **Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV)** is a separate legal entity (often a company) created exclusively to **own, build, finance, and operate** a single infrastructure project. All contracts (loan, construction, O&M, concession) are executed **in the name of the SPV**, making it the **single point of accountability** to lenders and the government.

**Two Advantages (1.5 Marks):**

1. **Risk Isolation** — Sponsors' liability is **limited to their equity contribution** (Non-Recourse Finance), protecting the parent companies from project-level risks.
2. **Easier Financing** — Lenders advance loans **against project cash flows** rather than sponsor creditworthiness, enabling **higher leverage (70:30)** and ring-fenced debt servicing.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note**: In the End-Semester Examination, Module-3 questions (14 marks) offer an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

> **(a) Explain the various sources of finance available for PPP projects. Discuss the role of equity, debt, and government support mechanisms in detail.** **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) A BOT highway project has the following data: Total Cost = ₹800 Cr, Debt = ₹560 Cr, Equity = ₹240 Cr, Loan Rate = 11% p.a., Loan Tenure = 12 years (equal annual installments). Expected Year-1 toll revenue (after O&M and tax) = ₹130 Cr. Calculate the DSCR for Year 1 and comment on the project's bankability.** **(7 Marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Sources of PPP Finance (7 Marks)

The three primary sources of finance for PPP projects are:

**1. Debt Finance (4–5 Marks for this section)**

- **Largest source** — typically 70–80% of project cost.
- **Senior Debt** has first claim on cash flows.
- Sources include:
  - **Commercial Bank Loans** (syndicated term loans from Indian banks, e.g., SBI, HDFC, ICICI).
  - **Infrastructure Debt Funds (IDFs)** — pooled funds investing in infra projects.
  - **Bond Markets** — long-tenure tax-free bonds (e.g., NHAI tax-free bonds).
  - **Multilateral Agencies** — World Bank, ADB, IFC, AIIB, JICA.
  - **External Commercial Borrowings (ECBs)** — for foreign currency loans.
- **Repayment**: Through project cash flows over a **predetermined schedule** (typically 10–18 years).

**2. Equity Finance (1.5 Marks)**

- Typically **20–30%** of project cost.
- Sourced from:
  - **Sponsor equity** (the private bidder's own funds).
  - **Private Equity / Infrastructure Funds** (e.g., GIC, Brookfield, Macquarie).
  - **Pension Funds and Sovereign Wealth Funds** (long-duration capital).
  - **Public Listing** of the SPV (rare, mostly in operating stage).
- **Last to be paid** but earns **residual profits**.

**3. Government Support Mechanisms (1 Mark)**

- **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** — one-time capital grant (up to 20% of TPC).
- **Annuity Payments** — fixed periodic payments (BOT-Annuity).
- **Sovereign Guarantees** — against political/regulatory risk.
- **Tax Holidays** — accelerated depreciation, customs duty exemptions.

---

##### Part (b) — DSCR Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $P = 560$ Crore
- $r = 0.11$
- $n = 12$ years
- $NOCFAT_1 = 130$ Crore

**Step 1: Compute $(1.11)^{12}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.11)^2 &= 1.2321 \\
(1.11)^4 &= (1.2321)^2 = 1.5181 \\
(1.11)^8 &= (1.5181)^2 = 2.3046 \\
(1.11)^{12} &= (1.11)^8 \times (1.11)^4 \\
&= 2.3046 \times 1.5181 = 3.4985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the annuity factor**

$$
AF = \dfrac{r \cdot (1+r)^n}{(1+r)^n - 1} = \dfrac{0.11 \times 3.4985}{3.4985 - 1} = \dfrac{0.3848}{2.4985} = 0.15405
$$

**Step 3: Compute Annual Debt Service (EMI)**

$$
EMI = 560 \times 0.15405 = \boxed{86.27 \text{ Crore per year}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute DSCR**

$$
DSCR_1 = \dfrac{130}{86.27} = \boxed{1.51}
$$

**Step 5: Bankability Comment (1 Mark)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bankability**: A DSCR of **1.51x** is **well above** the typical lender minimum of **1.20x**. The project has a **comfortable debt-servicing buffer** (i.e., toll revenue can drop by 34% before default). The project is **bankable** under standard PPP financing norms.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a) What is Viability Gap Funding (VGF)? Explain its purpose, eligibility, and the formula used to compute the admissible VGF with a suitable illustration.** **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b) Discuss the various PPP models (BOT, BOOT, DBFOT, HAM, Annuity) with emphasis on risk allocation and revenue source. State two real-world examples of PPP projects in Kerala.** **(7 Marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Viability Gap Funding (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Viability Gap Funding (VGF) is a **one-time capital grant** provided by the Government (Central and/or State) to make a **commercially unviable but socially desirable** infrastructure project financially attractive to private bidders. It bridges the gap between the **Total Project Cost (TPC)** and the **Present Value of expected revenues**.

**Purpose (1.5 Marks):**
- To **enhance project viability** without distorting user charges.
- To **attract private capital** into socially important but commercially weak sectors (water supply, solid waste, urban transport, health, education).
- To **reduce the debt burden** on the SPV and improve DSCR.

**Eligibility Criteria (1.5 Marks):**
The project must be:
1. Implemented through **PPP mode** with private sector participation.
2. Falling under defined **infrastructure sub-sectors** (roads, ports, airports, water, power, urban infrastructure).
3. Sponsored by a **Central/State Ministry, statutory entity, or local body**.
4. Recommended by the **Empowered Committee / PPP Appraisal Committee**.

**Formula (1 Mark) and Illustration (1 Mark):**

$$
VGF_{admissible} = \min(\text{Financial Gap},\ 0.20 \times \text{Total Project Cost})
$$

Where:

$$
\text{Financial Gap} = \text{Total Project Cost} - \text{PV of Revenues}
$$

**Illustration**: If a water supply PPP has TPC = ₹500 Cr, PV of Revenues = ₹380 Cr, then:
- Financial Gap = ₹120 Cr
- VGF Cap = 20% × 500 = ₹100 Cr
- **Admissible VGF = min(120, 100) = ₹100 Cr**
- Remaining ₹20 Cr must be raised as private equity.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip**: Always state the **min() function explicitly** — examiners reward this technical precision.

---

##### Part (b) — PPP Models and Kerala Examples (7 Marks)

| **Model** | **Risk Bearer (Demand)** | **Revenue Source** | **Best For** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **BOT (Toll)** | Private | User tolls | High-traffic highways |
| **BOT (Annuity)** | Government | Govt. fixed annuity | Low-traffic roads |
| **DBFOT** | Private | User fees + VGF | Expressways, ports |
| **BOOT** | Private | User fees | Power, airports |
| **HAM** | Shared (50:50) | 40% Govt + 60% User | Hybrid road projects |
| **O&M** | Government | Govt. fees | Mature assets |

**Risk Allocation Principle (2 Marks):**
- The party that **can best control a risk** should bear it.
- Construction risk → **Private** (because private contractor manages construction).
- Demand/traffic risk → **Private** in toll projects, **Government** in annuity projects.
- Political/regulatory/land risk → **Government**.
- Force majeure → **Shared**.

**Two Kerala Real-World Examples (2 Marks):**

1. **Kochi Metro Rail Limited (KMRL)** — A **Joint Venture PPP** with equity from GoI (₹1,000+ Cr), GoK, and external borrowing from **JICA** (Japan International Cooperation Agency). Revenue from fare collection, advertising, and station development. Demand risk is partially absorbed by the government through a **Pass-Through Assistance** mechanism.

2. **Kerala State Road Project under KRDCL** — Implemented under the **Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM)**, where 40% of the project cost is paid by the government as construction-linked payments, and 60% is paid as annuity over the operations period, with toll collection risks shared between the government and the concessionaire.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
>
> Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Confusing BOT with DBFOT** — DBFOT includes **Design** as a private responsibility; BOT often does not. Be explicit.
> 2. **Forgetting to cap VGF at 20%** — Many students apply VGF = full financial gap, ignoring the **statutory cap**. Always apply the **min() rule**.
> 3. **Writing "Government provides funds" without naming the mechanism** — Be specific: VGF, Annuity, Tax Holiday, Sovereign Guarantee, etc.
> 4. **Incorrect DSCR formula order** — DSCR = **CFADS in numerator, Debt Service in denominator**. Reversing this gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Ignoring the loan amortization period** vs. **concession period** — they are usually **different** (loan: 10–15 years; concession: 20–30 years).
> 6. **Writing NPV calculation without showing the discount factor** step-by-step** — Examiners want to see the **table of present values**, not just the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist** (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)

- ✅ **PPP** = Long-term contract between Public + Private sector for infrastructure delivery.
- ✅ **SPV** = Single-purpose legal entity; ring-fences project risk from sponsors.
- ✅ **Project Finance** = Non-Recourse / Limited-Recourse finance where lenders rely on **project cash flows**, not sponsor's balance sheet.
- ✅ **Standard Capital Structure** = Debt : Equity = **70:30 to 80:20** for Indian infrastructure PPPs.
- ✅ **DSCR Formula** = $\dfrac{\text{Net Operating Cash Flow}}{\text{Debt Service}}$ — **MUST be > 1.20x** (lender minimum); preferred **> 1.50x**.
- ✅ **VGF Cap** = **20% of Total Project Cost** — always apply $\min(\text{Gap}, \text{Cap})$.
- ✅ **LLCR** = NPV of CFADS over loan life ÷ Outstanding debt — should be **> 1.0x at all times**.
- ✅ **Project IRR** in Indian infra PPPs = typically **12%–18%**; **Equity IRR** = **15%–22%**.
- ✅ **BOT (Toll)** = Private bears demand risk; **BOT (Annuity)** = Government bears demand risk.
- ✅ **HAM (Hybrid Annuity Model)** = Government pays 40% during construction + 60% as annuity; risk is shared.
- ✅ **Risk Allocation Rule** = Allocate each risk to the party **best able to manage it**.
- ✅ **Concession Period** = 20–30 years (highways, ports); 30–40 years (power, water).
- ✅ **Financial Closure** = The single most important milestone — when all loan agreements are signed and equity is committed.
- ✅ **Cash Flow Waterfall** = Senior Debt Service → Subordinated Debt → O&M Reserve → Tax → Equity Dividend.
- ✅ **Kerala Context** = Kochi Metro (JV-PPP with JICA), KRDCL roads (HAM), Cochin Airport (Cooperative PPP).

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme)**:
> - For **3-mark questions**, write the **definition** (1.5 marks) + **two well-explained points** (1.5 marks).
> - For **14-mark questions**, structure as **(a) Theory (7 marks)** + **(b) Numerical (7 marks)** OR **(a) Model comparison (7 marks)** + **(b) Application (7 marks)**.
> - Always **state assumptions** before calculations (e.g., "Assuming equal annual installments...").
> - Always **end numerical answers with a bankability comment** — this is the **discriminating factor** between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.
> - **Use Indian examples** (NHAI, Adani, GMR, KMRL) wherever possible — Kerala-context answers earn extra appreciation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Project Finance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sources of Project Finance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sources of Project Finance** refer to the diverse set of financial channels, instruments, markets, and institutions from which a firm raises capital to fund the planning, execution, and operation of a specific capital project. These sources are classified by **tenure** (short-term, medium-term, long-term), **ownership claim** (debt vs. equity), and **origin** (internal accruals, domestic markets, international agencies).

In the KTU 2024 framework, project finance is treated as a **"structured financing"** discipline where the risk-return profile of the project — not the sponsor's balance sheet — dictates the optimal mix of funding sources. The capital structure decision is a **trade-off between cost of capital and financial risk**.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Project Finance

| Pillar | Nature | Claim on Assets | Voting Rights |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equity Capital** | Ownership | Residual | Yes |
| **Debt Capital** | Borrowing | Fixed Charge | No |
| **Hybrid / Mezzanine** | Quasi-Equity | Subordinated | Limited / Convertible |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Startup" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you want to open a premium restaurant in Kochi.
> - **Your own savings (₹10 lakh)** = *Equity / Retained Earnings*. You are the owner; you bear all the profit and loss.
> - **Bank loan (₹20 lakh)** = *Term Loan / Debt*. The bank does not share your profit but wants fixed interest and principal back.
> - **Uncle's contribution (₹5 lakh)** = *Preference Capital*. He gets a fixed "dividend" each year, paid before you take any profit.
> - **Vendor supplying furniture on 60-day credit** = *Trade Credit*. Short-term, interest-free working capital.
> - **Government MSME subsidy** = *External / Institutional Source*.

Just as a restaurant owner blends these to optimise risk, an engineering project promoter blends equity, debt, and grants to minimise the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

- Large infrastructure projects (roads, ports, power plants) routinely require **₹500 Cr – ₹50,000 Cr** outlays — far beyond internal accruals.
- Engineers managing **BOT / PPP / EPC contracts** must understand which source suits which phase (construction vs. operation).
- Wrong source selection (e.g., short-term loan funding a 20-year asset) creates **maturity mismatch** and bankruptcy risk.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Sources

```
SOURCES OF PROJECT FINANCE
│
├── A. INTERNAL SOURCES (Owned Funds)
│   ├── 1. Equity Share Capital (Ordinary Shares)
│   ├── 2. Preference Share Capital
│   ├── 3. Retained Earnings (Ploughed-back Profits)
│   └── 4. Depreciation Provision (Internal Cash Generation)
│
├── B. EXTERNAL — DEBT SOURCES
│   ├── 1. Term Loans (Banks / Financial Institutions)
│   ├── 2. Debentures / Bonds (Public + Private Placement)
│   ├── 3. Working Capital Loans (CC, OD, Cash Credit)
│   ├── 4. Trade Credit & Bills Payable
│   └── 5. Leasing & Hire-Purchase
│
├── C. EXTERNAL — EQUITY SOURCES
│   ├── 1. Public Issue (IPO / FPO)
│   ├── 2. Rights Issue
│   ├── 3. Venture Capital / Private Equity
│   ├── 4. Angel Investors
│   └── 5. Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)
│
├── D. HYBRID / MEZZANINE
│   ├── 1. Convertible Debentures
│   ├── 2. Convertible Preference Shares
│   └── 3. Optionally Fully Convertible Debentures
│
└── E. SPECIALISED / INSTITUTIONAL
    ├── 1. Government Grants & Subsidies
    ├── 2. Multilateral Agencies (World Bank, ADB, NDB, AIIB)
    ├── 3. Development Finance Institutions (SIDBI, NABARD, IFCI)
    ├── 4. Export Credit Agencies (ECGC, Exim Bank)
    └── 5. Infrastructure Debt Funds (IDF-Mutual Funds)
```

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **high-yield equations** repeatedly asked in KTU End Semester Examinations for cost-of-capital and capital-structure problems.

### Table 2.2 — Master Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost of Equity — Dividend Growth Model (DGM) | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = expected dividend, $P_0$ = market price, $g$ = growth rate |
| 2 | Cost of Equity — CAPM | $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $\beta$ = beta, $R_m$ = market return |
| 3 | Cost of Preference Capital | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ | $D_p$ = preference dividend |
| 4 | Cost of Irredeemable Debt (Pre-tax) | $K_d = \dfrac{I}{NP}$ | $I$ = annual interest, $NP$ = net proceeds |
| 5 | Cost of Redeemable Debt | $K_d = \dfrac{I + \dfrac{(F-NP)}{n}}{\dfrac{(F+NP)}{2}}$ | $F$ = face value, $n$ = years to redemption |
| 6 | After-tax Cost of Debt | $K_{d(\text{net})} = K_d (1 - t)$ | $t$ = corporate tax rate |
| 7 | Cost of Retained Earnings | $K_r = K_e (1 - \text{brokerage tax rate})$ | Opportunity cost to shareholders |
| 8 | Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) | $WACC = w_e K_e + w_p K_p + w_d K_{d(\text{net})}$ | $w_i$ = weight of each component |
| 9 | Debt-Equity Ratio | $D/E$ | Total debt ÷ Total equity |
| 10 | Interest Coverage Ratio (DSCR) | $\dfrac{EBIT}{\text{Interest}}$ | Safety margin for lenders |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember for KTU valuation:** Every numerical in cost-of-capital questions must end with a statement such as *"Hence, the WACC of the project is 12.45 %."* Omitting the conclusion costs 1 mark.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Source

- **Equity** is the **risk capital** — costly but flexible; no compulsory payment.
- **Debt** is the **cheapest source** (because of tax shield) but rigid — fixed commitment.
- **Retained earnings** avoid **flotation costs** and **signalling dilution**.
- **Preference shares** sit between debt and equity — fixed dividend, no security, no tax shield.
- **Venture capital** brings **smart money** — capital plus managerial expertise.
- **Multilateral agencies** fund projects with **positive externalities** (highways, renewable energy).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Project Finance Mix |
|---|---|
| Highways / BOT | Equity 20–30 % + NHAI Grant 20–40 % + Bank Debt 40–50 % |
| Thermal Power | Equity 25 % + IDF/NCD 25 % + Term Loan 50 % |
| IT Start-ups | Founders' Equity + Angel + VC + Convertible Notes |
| Metro Rail | Sovereign Guarantee + JICA/World Bank Loan + State Equity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the WACC Formula

The WACC is a **weighted average** of the component costs, weighted by their proportion in the total capital employed.

### Step 1 — Total Capital

$$
C_{\text{total}} = E + D + P
$$

where $E$ = market value of equity, $D$ = market value of debt, $P$ = market value of preference capital.

### Step 2 — Weights

$$
w_e = \dfrac{E}{C_{\text{total}}}, \quad w_d = \dfrac{D}{C_{\text{total}}}, \quad w_p = \dfrac{P}{C_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Each weight represents the *relative claim* each financing source has on the firm's cash flows.

### Step 3 — Component Costs (with tax adjustment)

Debt is tax-deductible; therefore, only the **after-tax cost of debt** is loaded into WACC. Equity and preference dividends are paid out of **post-tax profit** and are not tax-shielded.

### Step 4 — Final WACC Equation

$$
\boxed{WACC = w_e K_e + w_p K_p + w_d K_d(1 - t)}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiplying $K_d$ by $(1 - t)$ reduces the effective cost of debt because interest is allowed as a deduction under the Income Tax Act, 1961.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Modelled after KTU Pattern):**
> A new infrastructure project is financed as follows:
> - **Equity Share Capital:** ₹ 6,00,000 (1,00,000 shares @ ₹ 6 each, fully paid)
> - **9 % Preference Shares:** ₹ 2,00,000
> - **8 % Debentures:** ₹ 4,00,000
> - **Expected Equity Dividend:** 12 % per annum
> - **Corporate Tax Rate:** 30 %
> - **Internal Rate of Return of the project:** 14 %
> - **Floatation cost on equity:** 5 %
>
> **Calculate WACC and advise whether the project should be accepted.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

#### Step 1: Cost of Equity (with floatation cost adjustment)

Equity shares are issued at face value ₹ 6. Floatation cost = 5 % of ₹ 6 = ₹ 0.30.
Therefore, **Net Proceeds per share** = $6 - 0.30 = 5.70$ ₹.

Expected dividend per share = 12 % of ₹ 6 = ₹ 0.72.

$$
K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{NP} = \dfrac{0.72}{5.70} = 0.12632 = 12.63\%
$$

#### Step 2: Cost of Preference Capital

Preference dividend $D_p$ = 9 % of ₹ 100 (assumed face value) = ₹ 9.
Net proceeds = ₹ 100 (assume no floatation).

$$
K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0} = \dfrac{9}{100} = 9.00\%
$$

#### Step 3: Cost of Debt (after tax)

$$
K_d = \dfrac{I}{NP} = \dfrac{8\% \text{ of } 100}{100} = 8.00\%
$$

$$
K_{d(\text{net})} = K_d (1 - t) = 8.00 \times (1 - 0.30) = 5.60\%
$$

#### Step 4: Compute Weights

Total Capital $C = 6{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000$ ₹.

$$
w_e = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.50
$$

$$
w_p = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.1667
$$

$$
w_d = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.3333
$$

#### Step 5: Compute WACC

$$
\boxed{WACC = (0.50 \times 12.63) + (0.1667 \times 9.00) + (0.3333 \times 5.60)}
$$

$$
WACC = 6.3150 + 1.5003 + 1.8665
$$

$$
\boxed{WACC = 9.68\%}
$$

#### Step 6: Decision Rule

**Decision Criterion:** Accept the project if **IRR > WACC**.

Here, IRR = 14 % > WACC = 9.68 %.
The project generates a **positive NPV** and adds value to the firm.

> **Conclusion:** The project is **financially viable and should be accepted** because the expected return of 14 % comfortably exceeds the weighted average cost of capital of 9.68 %.

---

## 3.3 Cost of Capital Component Analysis Matrix

| Source | Risk to Investor | Cost to Firm | Tax Shield? | Control Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ordinary Equity | High (residual claim) | High (12–18 %) | No | Dilutes promoter control |
| Preference Capital | Medium | Medium (8–12 %) | No | None |
| Retained Earnings | Same as equity | Moderate (no floatation) | No | None |
| Term Loan | Low (secured) | Low (8–12 % pre-tax) | Yes | Usually restrictive covenants |
| Debentures | Low (secured) | Low (7–11 %) | Yes | Charge on assets |
| Convertible Deb. | Medium | Lowest initially | Yes (while debt) | Dilution on conversion |
| Trade Credit | Very low | Free (0 %) | N/A | No |
| Venture Capital | Very high | 20–35 % IRR target | No | Active board seat |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Finance Requirement]:::root
    A --> B[Short Term Sources]:::st
    A --> C[Medium Term Sources]:::mt
    A --> D[Long Term Sources]:::lt

    B --> B1[Trade Credit]
    B --> B2[Bills Payable]
    B --> B3[Bank Overdraft]
    B --> B4[Working Capital Loan]

    C --> C1[Term Loans 1 to 5 Years]
    C --> C2[Leasing]
    C --> C3[Hire Purchase]
    C --> C4[Public Deposits]

    D --> D1[Internal Accruals]:::internal
    D --> D2[Equity Capital]:::equity
    D --> D3[Preference Capital]:::pref
    D --> D4[Debt Capital]:::debt
    D --> D5[Hybrid Instruments]:::hybrid
    D --> D6[Institutional Sources]:::inst

    D1 --> D1a[Retained Earnings]
    D1 --> D1b[Depreciation Provision]

    D2 --> D2a[Ordinary Shares]
    D2 --> D2b[Rights Issue]
    D2 --> D2c[Venture Capital]
    D2 --> D2d[IPO and FPO]

    D3 --> D3a[Cumulative Pref Shares]
    D3 --> D3b[Participating Pref Shares]

    D4 --> D4a[Debentures]
    D4 --> D4b[Bonds]
    D4 --> D5c[Term Loans from FIs]

    D5 --> D5a[Convertible Debentures]
    D5 --> D5b[Convertible Pref Shares]

    D6 --> D6a[World Bank]
    D6 --> D6b[Asian Development Bank]
    D6 --> D6c[IFC and SIDBI]
    D6 --> D6d[Government Subsidies]

    classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px
    classDef st fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b
    classDef mt fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#2563eb
    classDef lt fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#16a34a

```

## 4.2 Capital Structure Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Strategic Objective]:::root --> RA[Risk Appetite]
    SP --> CO[Cost Minimisation]
    SP --> FL[Financial Flexibility]

    RA --> DS[Determine Debt Equity Ratio]
    CO --> WACC[Minimise WACC]
    FL --> MS[Maturity Matching]

    DS --> ALLOC[Allocate Sources]
    WACC --> ALLOC
    MS --> ALLOC

    ALLOC --> EQ[Equity Component]
    ALLOC --> DT[Debt Component]
    ALLOC --> HY[Hybrid Component]

    EQ --> CB1[Promoter Contribution min 26 percent for listed cos]
    DT --> CB2[Debt Equity capped by DSCR and covenants]
    HY --> CB3[Convertibles for upside investors]

    CB1 --> PRJ[Final Project Capital Structure]
    CB2 --> PRJ
    CB3 --> PRJ

    classDef root fill:#0f172a,color:#fff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Funding Life Cycle of an Engineering Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Concept and Feasibility]:::p1
    P2[Phase 2: Detailed Engineering]:::p2
    P3[Phase 3: Construction]:::p3
    P4[Phase 4: Commissioning]:::p4
    P5[Phase 5: Operations and Refinancing]:::p5

    P1 --> F1[Owners Equity and Angel Funding]
    P2 --> F2[Private Placement and Term Loan Commitment]
    P3 --> F3[Debenture Issue and Working Capital]
    P4 --> F4[IDC Financing and Bridge Loans]
    P5 --> F5[Refinancing and Take-out Finance]

    F1 --> P2
    F2 --> P3
    F3 --> P4
    F4 --> P5
    F5 --> P5

    classDef p2 fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b

    classDef p4 fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#16a34a
```
