# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Pattern Note (2024 Scheme):** OECET727 follows a *two-part* structure: **Part A (3 × 3 = 9 marks)** and **Part B (4 × 14 = 56 marks)** with internal choice within Module groups. Below are *Board-Standard* questions calibrated to Module 4 of Construction Economics.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "Construction Economics" and briefly state its scope in the context of a Civil Engineering project.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Construction Economics is the application of economic theory and financial principles to the planning, design, construction, operation, maintenance, and disposal of built facilities, with the objective of *maximising value over the entire life cycle* rather than minimising first cost alone.

**Scope (1.5 marks):** It encompasses (i) **cost estimation and control** of materials, labour, and plant, (ii) **whole-life costing and depreciation analysis**, (iii) **project feasibility appraisal** using NPV, IRR, BCR, and Pay-Back Period, (iv) **risk and uncertainty modelling** in infrastructure investments, and (v) **economic evaluation of design alternatives** such as RCC vs. steel, flexible vs. rigid pavement, or conventional vs. green building materials.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award **1.5 marks** for a *crisp definition* and **1.5 marks** for *at least 3 distinct scope items*. Avoid giving marks for generic statements such as "it deals with money".

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between "First Cost" and "Life-Cycle Cost" in construction. Why is the latter preferred in modern project evaluation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | First Cost | Life-Cycle Cost (LCC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Capital expenditure incurred *at the time of construction* | Sum of *all* costs from inception to disposal, discounted to present |
| **Time Horizon** | $t = 0$ (one-time outflow) | $t = 0$ to $n$ (operating life) |
| **Components** | Land, materials, labour, plant | First cost + O&M + Maintenance + Disposal − Salvage |
| **Decision View** | Short-term / Build-cost focus | Long-term / Total-ownership focus |
| **Formula** | $P$ | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}$ |

**Why LCC is preferred (1 mark):** Modern project evaluation emphasises **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** because (a) **maintenance and energy costs typically exceed 3–5 times the first cost** over a 30-year building life, (b) it incorporates the **Time Value of Money**, and (c) it aligns with **sustainability goals (SDG-9, SDG-11)** by exposing hidden recurring environmental costs.

> **Examiner's Note:** Students often *only write the difference* and forget to justify LCC preference. Missing the 1-mark justification deducts up to 33% of total marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Convention (2024 Scheme):** *Either (a) and (b) from Question A, **OR** (a) and (b) from Question B*. The two choices are *parallel in marks* but cover *different cognitive levels* (typically: Choice A = Computational/Analytical, Choice B = Conceptual/Design-based).

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**
**(a) A construction firm has an annual fixed cost of ₹18,00,000. The variable cost per square metre built is ₹1,500, and the firm charges ₹2,400 per square metre. Find:**
  **(i) Break-Even Point in units (m²) and in sales (₹).**
  **(ii) Profit earned when the firm constructs 5,000 m² in a year.**
  **(iii) Margin of Safety if the firm's actual sales are ₹1.5 crore.**
  **(iv) Comment on the firm's P/V Ratio and what it signifies. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define depreciation. Explain any two methods of depreciation with a suitable numerical example. Discuss the suitability of each method for a heavy construction equipment. (7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — Break-Even Analysis (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $FC = \text{₹}18{,}00{,}000$
- $VC = \text{₹}1{,}500 \text{ per m}^2$
- $P = \text{₹}2{,}400 \text{ per m}^2$ (contract rate)
- Contribution per unit $C_{u} = P - VC = 2400 - 1500 = \text{₹}900 \text{ per m}^2$

**(i) BEP in units and value (3 marks):**

$$
BEP_{Q} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{900} = 2000 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
BEP_{S} = BEP_{Q} \times P = 2000 \times 2400 = \text{₹}48{,}00{,}000
$$

**(ii) Profit at 5,000 m² (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Profit} = C_{u} \cdot Q - FC = 900 \times 5000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}27{,}00{,}000
$$

**(iii) Margin of Safety at ₹1.5 crore sales (1.5 marks):**

Actual sales in units $= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 / 2400 = 6250 \text{ m}^2$

$$
MoS(\%) = \dfrac{6250 - 2000}{6250} \times 100 = \dfrac{4250}{6250} \times 100 = 68\%
$$

**(iv) P/V Ratio and Significance (0.5 mark):**

$$
P/V\ Ratio = \dfrac{C_{u}}{P} \times 100 = \dfrac{900}{2400} \times 100 = 37.5\%
$$

A P/V Ratio of **37.5%** is **healthy** for a construction firm. It means that for every ₹100 of sales, ₹37.50 is available to absorb fixed costs and generate profit. The firm has a **high margin of safety (68%)** and is **less sensitive to volume fluctuations**.

---

### Part (b) — Depreciation (7 marks)

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Depreciation is the **systematic and rational allocation of the depreciable cost (acquisition cost minus salvage value) of a tangible asset over its useful economic life**, reflecting wear-and-tear, obsolescence, and passage of time. It is a *non-cash* expense but a *tax-deductible* item.

**Method 1: Straight Line Method (SLM) (2.5 marks):**

A tower crane costs ₹40,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹4,00,000 and a useful life of 8 years.

$$
\text{Annual Depreciation} = \dfrac{P - S}{n} = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000}{8} = \dfrac{36{,}00{,}000}{8} = \text{₹}4{,}50{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

*Book value at end of year 5:* $40{,}00{,}000 - (5 \times 4{,}50{,}000) = \text{₹}17{,}50{,}000$

**Suitability:** Ideal for **buildings, roads, and structures** where wear-and-tear is *uniform* over the asset's life and **obsolescence is low**.

**Method 2: Written Down Value / Diminishing Balance Method (WDV) (2.5 marks):**

Assume depreciation rate $d = 25\%$ per year on the same ₹40 lakh crane with negligible salvage.

Year 1: $V_1 = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = 30{,}00{,}000$; $D_1 = 40{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 10{,}00{,}000$
Year 2: $V_2 = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = 22{,}50{,}000$; $D_2 = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 7{,}50{,}000$
Year 3: $V_3 = 22{,}50{,}000 \times 0.75 = 16{,}87{,}500$; $D_3 = 16{,}87{,}500 \times 0.25 = 4{,}21{,}875$

**Suitability:** Preferred for **heavy construction equipment** (excavators, batching plants, transit mixers) because (a) it recognises the **higher maintenance cost in later years** by charging higher depreciation in early years, (b) it matches the **declining service potential** of machinery, and (c) it provides a **steady repair-and-depreciation combined cost** over the life of the asset.

**Comparison Table (0.5 mark):**

| Feature | SLM | WDV |
|---|---|---|
| Depreciation per year | Constant | Decreasing |
| Book value at end of life | Salvage value | Not exactly zero |
| Best for | Buildings | Plant and machinery |

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**(a) Explain the concept of "Time Value of Money" with reference to construction projects. Derive the relationship between the six standard compound interest factors. (7 marks)**

**(b) Two machines, A and B, are being considered for a construction site. Machine A costs ₹6,00,000 with annual O&M of ₹50,000 and a 6-year life. Machine B costs ₹9,00,000 with annual O&M of ₹40,000 and an 8-year life. Both have negligible salvage. Using the Equivalent Annual Cost method at 10% interest, recommend the suitable machine. (7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — Time Value of Money & Factor Derivation (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**
The **Time Value of Money (TVM)** states that **a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee receivable in the future**, because of (i) the **earning capacity** of money (it can be invested), (ii) **inflation** erosion of purchasing power, and (iii) the **risk premium** associated with future uncertainty.

In construction projects, TVM governs every comparison between alternatives because the **initial capital cost, periodic O&M, and the salvage value occur at different points in time** and cannot be directly added or compared without first converting them to a **common time basis** (present, future, or annual).

**Six Standard Factors (5 marks — full derivation expected):**

| Factor | Symbol | Formula | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{n}$ | Future value of $P$ |
| Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Present value of $F$ |
| Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | Future value of $A$ series |
| Uniform Series Sinking Fund | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Annual deposit for future $F$ |
| Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Present value of $A$ series |
| Capital Recovery | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | Annualised $P$ recovery |

**Key Relationship:**
$$
(P/A, i, n) = (P/F, i, n) \times (F/A, i, n)
$$
$$
(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n) + i
$$

> **Memory Anchor (Board Trick):** The "A/P" factor is the **largest** of all six because it carries the *interest charge on the unrecovered principal*.

---

### Part (b) — Equivalent Annual Cost Comparison (7 marks)

**Given:**

| Parameter | Machine A | Machine B |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost $P$ | ₹6,00,000 | ₹9,00,000 |
| Annual O&M $A_{O\&M}$ | ₹50,000 | ₹40,000 |
| Life $n$ | 6 years | 8 years |
| Salvage $S$ | 0 | 0 |
| Interest $i$ | 10% | 10% |

Since lives are **unequal (6 ≠ 8)**, PW comparison would be flawed; **EAC method is the correct approach**.

**Machine A — EAC Calculation:**

Step 1: Annualised capital cost using $(A/P, 10\%, 6)$:

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{6}}{(1.10)^{6} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.7716}{0.7716} = \dfrac{0.17716}{0.7716} \approx 0.22961
$$

$$
EAC_{P,A} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.22961 = \text{₹}1{,}37{,}766
$$

Step 2: Add O&M (salvage = 0, so no subtraction):

$$
EAC_{A} = 1{,}37{,}766 + 50{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}87{,}766
$$

**Machine B — EAC Calculation:**

Step 1: $(A/P, 10\%, 8)$:

$$
(A/P, 10\%, 8) = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{8}}{(1.10)^{8} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 2.14359}{1.14359} = \dfrac{0.21436}{1.14359} \approx 0.18744
$$

$$
EAC_{P,B} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 0.18744 = \text{₹}1{,}68{,}696
$$

Step 2: Add O&M:

$$
EAC_{B} = 1{,}68{,}696 + 40{,}000 = \text{₹}2{,}08{,}696
$$

**Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Machine | EAC | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| A | ₹1,87,766 | **1 (Lower)** |
| B | ₹2,08,696 | 2 |

> **Recommendation (1 mark):** **Machine A should be selected** because its Equivalent Annual Cost (₹1,87,766) is **lower than that of Machine B (₹2,08,696)** by ₹20,930 per year. The higher capital cost of B is *not justified* by its marginally lower O&M, even after accounting for its longer life via the EAC method.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **EAC vs. PW Mix-up:** When lives are unequal, *PW comparison is invalid unless a repeatability assumption is explicitly stated*. Many students blindly use PW and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Salvage Treatment:** If the salvage is non-zero, it MUST be subtracted using the $(A/F, i, n)$ factor. Forgetting it gives inflated EAC.
> 3. **Factor Notation:** Writing $(A/P)$ when you mean $(P/A)$ is a **common transcription error** that confuses the entire solution. Always cross-check using the *dimension* (yearly vs. one-time).
> 4. **O&M Inflation:** O&M is given in *current (year-0) rupees*. If escalation is given, escalate geometrically first, *then* annualise.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Re-read it 30 minutes before the exam.**

### One-Line Definitions (Quick Recall)
- **Construction Economics** = Application of economic principles across the *entire life cycle* of a built asset.
- **First Cost** = One-time capital outlay at $t = 0$.
- **Life-Cycle Cost** = Present worth of all costs from $t = 0$ to $t = n$ (including disposal).
- **Time Value of Money** = "A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow."
- **Sunk Cost** = Irrecoverable past cost (irrelevant for decision-making).
- **Opportunity Cost** = Value of the foregone next-best alternative.
- **Depreciation** = Systematic allocation of asset cost over useful life.
- **Break-Even Point** = Output at which Total Revenue = Total Cost.
- **P/V Ratio** = (Contribution / Sales) × 100.
- **Margin of Safety** = Cushion between actual sales and BEP sales.
- **EAC** = Equivalent Annual Cost (for unequal-life comparison).
- **NPV** = Discounted sum of (Benefits − Costs).
- **IRR** = Discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
- **BCR** = Discounted Benefits ÷ Discounted Costs.

### Important Things to Remember

- **Six factors and their inverses are reciprocal pairs**: $(P/F)(F/P) = 1$, $(P/A)(A/P) = 1$, $(A/F)(F/A) = 1$.
- **Add/subtract only at the same time reference**; use the appropriate factor to move between Present, Future, and Annual.
- **BEP formula** assumes the firm has *only one product* (or constant product mix) and that *variable cost per unit is constant* (linear CVP).
- **EAC is the only valid method** for comparing alternatives with **unequal lives** (unless repeatability is assumed).
- **SLM** → constant annual charge → buildings, roads.
- **WDV** → high early charge → machinery, equipment.
- **Sinking Fund** → incorporates time value into depreciation → used in *public sector* projects.
- **Kerala-context insight:** Keralite construction projects often face **monsoon delays** and **labour cost escalation**; always include a 5–8% escalation in O&M when solving KTU numericals.
- **Discount rate convention:** Use the **bank rate + 2–3% risk premium** as a rule-of-thumb MARR for civil projects.
- **Sign convention:** Cash *outflows* (costs) are *negative*; cash *inflows* (revenue, salvage) are *positive*.
- **NEP-2020 alignment:** Modern KTU 2024 questions blend **economics + sustainability** — always consider environmental externalities in benefit-cost analysis.

> **Final Word of Motivation:** Construction Economics is the *bridge* between an *engineer's technical mind* and a *manager's financial mind*. Mastering it transforms you from a "doer" into a "decision-maker" — the exact skill industry 4.0 demands from B.Tech graduates in Kerala and beyond. All the best for your KTU examination!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Value of Money
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Time Value of Money

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the foundational financial principle which states that **a unit of currency (e.g., one Rupee / one Dollar) available at the present time is worth more than the identical unit of currency received at a future date**, because of its *earning potential*, *risk profile*, and *inflation exposure*.

In the formal language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, TVM is the financial concept that establishes equivalence between cash flows occurring at *different points in time* through the mechanism of **discounting** (backward shift) and **compounding** (forward shift). The core engine behind this transformation is the **interest rate** (denoted $i$ or $r$), which is the *time-price of money*.

### Mathematical Statement of TVM

The TVM principle can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Value Today} \;>\; \text{Same Value at a Future Date}
$$

Or, more formally, the **present value** of a future cash flow $FV$ receivable after $n$ periods at a discount rate $i$ is:

$$
PV = \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n}
$$

If $i > 0$ and $n > 0$, then $PV < FV$, mathematically confirming the TVM principle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Fresh Apple vs. Week-Old Apple" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a vendor gives you two options:
> - **Option A:** Receive **1 fresh, juicy apple today**.
> - **Option B:** Receive **1 apple (of the same variety) one week later**.
>
> Logically, you would choose **Option A**, because:
> 1. The fresh apple gives you *immediate satisfaction* (consumption today).
> 2. The fresh apple can be *consumed now OR traded/sold now* for some value.
> 3. There is a *risk* that the apple may rot, be stolen, or be unavailable in a week.
> 4. A week's wait has a *psychological cost* (time preference).
>
> In finance, money behaves exactly like that apple. **₹100 today can be invested, spent, or saved, whereas ₹100 a year from now is "less fresh"** — it has lost opportunity, incurred risk, and possibly lost purchasing power to inflation.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of TVM

The Time Value of Money rests on three universal financial realities. **All three must be memorized** as they form the *Board Examiner's favourite* set of marks:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Why Money Has Time Value:**
>
> 1. **Opportunity Cost (Earning Potential):** Money in hand can be *invested* (FDs, stocks, business, bonds) to generate returns. Idle future money misses this earning potential.
>
> 2. **Inflation (Purchasing Power Erosion):** General price levels rise over time. **₹100 today buys more goods and services than ₹100 will buy 5 years later.** The historical average Indian inflation rate hovers around **5%–6% per annum**.
>
> 3. **Risk & Uncertainty (Default / Volatility):** Future payments carry *uncertainty*. The borrower may default, the currency may be devalued, or an emergency may arise. Lenders demand a *risk premium* for this uncertainty.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — KTU Board Vocabulary

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Present Value** | $PV$ | Today's worth of a future cash flow (also called *Present Worth*) |
| **Future Value** | $FV$ | The value of a present cash flow at a specified future date |
| **Interest Rate** | $i$ or $r$ | The percentage charge for the use of money per period |
| **Number of Periods** | $n$ or $t$ | The time horizon (in years, months, etc.) |
| **Compounding** | — | Forward-shifting cash flow by multiplying by $(1+i)^n$ |
| **Discounting** | — | Backward-shifting cash flow by dividing by $(1+i)^n$ |
| **Annuity** | $A$ | A series of equal cash flows at regular intervals |
| **Perpetuity** | — | An annuity that continues forever ($n \to \infty$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Branches of TVM: Compounding & Discounting

TVM mathematics is built on **two inverse operations** that move money through time:

> [!NOTE]
> **Compounding** is the process of calculating the **future value** of a present amount by *growing* it forward at the interest rate. The "engine" is **multiplication by $(1+i)^n$**.
>
> **Discounting** is the reverse process — calculating the **present value** of a future amount by *shrinking* it backward. The "engine" is **division by $(1+i)^n$**.

A student must internalise: **Compounding = Forward gear ; Discounting = Reverse gear.**

## 2.2 The Interest Spectrum: Simple vs. Compound

### (a) Simple Interest (SI)

Interest is computed **only on the original principal** every period. The principal never grows.

$$
\text{Simple Interest} = P \times r \times t
$$

$$
\text{Total Amount} \; A = P + SI = P(1 + rt)
$$

### (b) Compound Interest (CI)

Interest is computed on the **principal + accumulated interest** of previous periods. This produces **interest-on-interest**, the so-called "**eighth wonder of the world**" attributed to Albert Einstein.

$$
\text{Future Value} \; FV = P(1+i)^n
$$

$$
\text{Compound Interest} = FV - P = P\left[(1+i)^n - 1\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In all TVM problems, *unless explicitly stated*, you must use **Compound Interest**. Simple interest is a rare exception (used in some banking treasury bills and certain contractual loans).

## 2.3 Single Cash Flow TVM Formulas

The following table contains the **complete single-cash-flow formula set** that the KTU Board expects you to memorize verbatim:

| # | Operation | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Future Value of a Lump Sum** | $FV = PV(1+i)^n$ | $PV$ = Present Value, $i$ = rate per period, $n$ = number of periods |
| 2 | **Present Value of a Lump Sum** | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+i)^n}$ | Equivalent single-payment discount formula |
| 3 | **Simple Interest Future Value** | $FV = P(1 + i \cdot n)$ | Linear growth (no compounding) |
| 4 | **Doubling Time (Rule of 72)** | $n \approx \dfrac{72}{i(\%)}$ | Quick approximation: years to double an investment |
| 5 | **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** | $EAR = \left(1 + \dfrac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$ | $m$ = compounding frequency per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value signs in this notes set are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve the markdown table syntax. For example, **discount factor** $= \vert (1+i)^{-n} \vert$.

## 2.4 Annuity Formulas — The Most Tested Section

An **annuity** is a stream of **equal cash flows** occurring at the end of each period for a *finite* number of periods. The two canonical types are:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Ordinary Annuity (Annuity-Ordinary):** Payments occur at the **END** of each period. This is the *default* assumption in KTU problems.
> - **Annuity Due (Annuity-Due):** Payments occur at the **BEGINNING** of each period. Multiply the ordinary annuity result by $(1+i)$.

### KTU Annuity Master Formula Table

| Annuity Type | Future Value ($FV$) | Present Value ($PV$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordinary Annuity** | $FV = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | $PV = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ |
| **Annuity Due** | $FV_{due} = FV_{ord} \cdot (1+i)$ | $PV_{due} = PV_{ord} \cdot (1+i)$ |
| **Perpetuity** ($n \to \infty$) | — | $PV = \dfrac{A}{i}$ |
| **Growing Perpetuity** ($g$ = growth) | — | $PV = \dfrac{A}{i - g}$ |

Here, $A$ is the **equal periodic payment** (also called *Annuity Amount*).

## 2.5 The Six Functional Factors of Time Value

Engineering finance uses **six standard compound-interest factors**. Memorise the *names* — they frequently appear in KTU theory questions:

| Factor # | Standard Name | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F/P** | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(1+i)^n$ | Find $FV$ given $PV$ |
| **P/F** | Single Payment Present Worth | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | Find $PV$ given $FV$ |
| **F/A** | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | Find $FV$ of an annuity |
| **P/A** | Uniform Series Present Worth | $\dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ | Find $PV$ of an annuity |
| **A/F** | Sinking Fund Factor | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Find $A$ given $FV$ |
| **A/P** | Capital Recovery Factor | $\dfrac{i}{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}$ | Find $A$ given $PV$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Reciprocal Rule:** Notice that $F/P = 1/(P/F)$, $F/A = 1/(A/F)$, and $P/A = 1/(A/P)$. This **reciprocal property** is a free lifeline in numerical problems — if you forget one, flip its reciprocal partner.

## 2.6 Engineering & Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does an engineering student need TVM?**
>
> 1. **Capital Budgeting of Engineering Projects:** Engineers evaluate whether a new plant, machinery, or R&D project is *financially viable* using **NPV**, **IRR**, and **Payback Period** — all built on TVM.
> 2. **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** TVM helps decide whether to keep an old machine (with high maintenance) or buy a new one.
> 3. **Loan Amortization:** EMIs (Equated Monthly Instalments) on home/car loans are pure annuity mathematics.
> 4. **Depreciation & Salvage Value Calculations:** Future salvage values must be discounted to present.
> 5. **Bid/Tender Evaluation:** Comparing multi-year contracts requires bringing all cash flows to a single point in time.
> 6. **Personal Financial Planning:** Retirement planning, SIPs, FDs — all depend on TVM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Future Value of a Single Cash Flow (Compounding)

**Starting Premise:** A principal $P$ is invested for $n$ periods at a rate $i$ per period, with interest added at the end of each period.

**Year-by-year buildup:**

| Period | Opening Balance | Interest Earned | Closing Balance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P$ | $P \cdot i$ | $P + Pi = P(1+i)$ |
| 2 | $P(1+i)$ | $P(1+i)\cdot i$ | $P(1+i)^2$ |
| 3 | $P(1+i)^2$ | $P(1+i)^2 \cdot i$ | $P(1+i)^3$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $n$ | $P(1+i)^{n-1}$ | $P(1+i)^{n-1}\cdot i$ | $P(1+i)^n$ |

**Formal derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{End of Year 1:} \quad & FV_1 = P + Pi = P(1+i) \\
\text{End of Year 2:} \quad & FV_2 = FV_1 + FV_1 \cdot i = P(1+i) \cdot (1+i) = P(1+i)^2 \\
\text{End of Year 3:} \quad & FV_3 = FV_2(1+i) = P(1+i)^3 \\
\vdots \quad & \\
\text{End of Year } n: \quad & FV_n = P(1+i)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusively:**

$$
\boxed{\,FV = P(1+i)^n\,}
$$

This is the **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor** $(F/P, i, n)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Present Value of a Single Cash Flow (Discounting)

**Starting Premise:** We wish to find the present equivalent of a future amount $FV$ receivable in $n$ periods at rate $i$.

We simply algebraically invert the compounding formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FV &= PV(1+i)^n \\
\text{Divide both sides by } (1+i)^n: \quad \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} &= PV \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad PV &= \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} = FV \cdot (1+i)^{-n}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,PV = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}\,}
$$

The factor $(1+i)^{-n}$ is called the **discount factor** or **present worth factor** $(P/F, i, n)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity

**Starting Premise:** A cash flow of $A$ is deposited at the **end** of each of $n$ periods. The first deposit earns interest for $(n-1)$ periods, the second for $(n-2)$ periods, …, the $n$-th deposit earns **no interest** (it is deposited on the last day).

**Mathematical Setup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FV} &= A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0} \\
\text{FV} &= A\left[(1+i)^{n-1} + (1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + (1+i) + 1\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is a Geometric Progression (G.P.)** with:
- First term: $a = 1$
- Common ratio: $r = (1+i)$
- Number of terms: $n$

**Sum of G.P. formula:** $S = a \cdot \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$

**Applying the G.P. formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FV &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1} \\
FV &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}\,}
$$

This is the **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor** $(F/A, i, n)$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity

**Approach:** We take the Future Value of an annuity and *discount* it back by $n$ periods.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} \\
PV &= \frac{A \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}\right]}{(1+i)^n} \\
PV &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} \\
PV &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Simplifying the numerator** by factoring out $(1+i)^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{i(1+i)^n} \\
PV &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}\,}
$$

This is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor** $(P/A, i, n)$.

## 3.5 Derivation: Perpetuity

A perpetuity is an annuity that lasts **forever** ($n \to \infty$). As $n$ approaches infinity, $(1+i)^{-n}$ approaches 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{perpetuity}} &= \lim_{n \to \infty} A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \\
&= A \cdot \frac{1 - 0}{i} \\
&= \frac{A}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{perpetuity}} = \frac{A}{i}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Perpetuity Example:** A *perpetual bond* (also called a *consol bond*) issued by the British Government. The bond never matures; it pays a fixed coupon forever. Its present value is simply the annual coupon divided by the discount rate.

## 3.6 Worked-Out Numerical Examples (KTU Board Style)

### Example 1: Single Cash Flow — Future Value

**Problem:** An engineer invests **₹1,00,000** in a fixed deposit yielding **9% per annum** compounded annually. What is the maturity value after **6 years**?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
i &= 0.09 \\
n &= 6 \\
F &= P(1+i)^n \\
F &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.09)^6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-wise evaluation of $(1.09)^6$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.09)^1 &= 1.0900 \\
(1.09)^2 &= 1.1881 \\
(1.09)^3 &= 1.2950 \\
(1.09)^4 &= 1.4116 \\
(1.09)^5 &= 1.5386 \\
(1.09)^6 &= 1.6771
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.6771 = \text{₹}1{,}67{,}710\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The investment grew by ₹67,710, of which the original principal of ₹1,00,000 and compound interest of ₹67,710 form the maturity value.

### Example 2: Single Cash Flow — Present Value

**Problem:** A contractor promises to pay **₹5,00,000** after **8 years**. If the discount rate is **12% per annum**, what is its present worth?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FV &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\
i &= 0.12 \\
n &= 8 \\
PV &= \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-wise evaluation of $(1.12)^8$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.12)^2 &= 1.2544 \\
(1.12)^4 &= (1.2544)^2 = 1.5735 \\
(1.12)^8 &= (1.5735)^2 = 2.4760
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2.4760} \\
PV &= 2{,}01{,}938.61
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,PV \approx \text{₹}2{,}01{,}939\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Receiving ₹5,00,000 in 8 years is economically equivalent to receiving ₹2,01,939 *today*, assuming a 12% opportunity cost of capital.

### Example 3: Annuity — Future Value of SIP

**Problem:** An engineer invests **₹20,000 at the end of every year** in a mutual fund SIP for **10 years** at an expected return of **14% per annum**. Find the future value of the SIP at the end of 10 years.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 20{,}000 \\
i &= 0.14 \\
n &= 10 \\
F &= A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $(1.14)^{10}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.14)^2 &= 1.2996 \\
(1.14)^4 &= 1.6890 \\
(1.14)^5 &= 1.9254 \\
(1.14)^{10} &= (1.9254)^2 = 3.7072
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 20{,}000 \times \frac{3.7072 - 1}{0.14} \\
F &= 20{,}000 \times \frac{2.7072}{0.14} \\
F &= 20{,}000 \times 19.3371 \\
F &= 3{,}86{,}742
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F \approx \text{₹}3{,}86{,}742\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The total investment by the engineer was $20{,}000 \times 10 = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$. The wealth created through compounding is approximately ₹1,86,742 — a powerful demonstration of "interest on interest."

### Example 4: Annuity — Present Value (Loan EMI Equivalent)

**Problem:** A home loan requires the borrower to pay **₹25,000 at the end of every month** for **20 years (240 months)**. If the interest rate is **10% per annum compounded monthly**, what is the maximum loan amount that can be sanctioned?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 25{,}000 \\
i_{\text{monthly}} &= \frac{0.10}{12} = 0.008333 \\
n &= 240 \\
P &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute $(1.008333)^{240}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.008333)^{12} &\approx 1.10471 \quad \text{(Effective annual rate approx.)} \\
(1.008333)^{240} &= (1.10471)^{20}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-wise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.10471)^2 &= 1.22038 \\
(1.10471)^4 &= 1.48933 \\
(1.10471)^5 &= 1.64531 \\
(1.10471)^{10} &= 2.70704 \\
(1.10471)^{20} &= 7.32808
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 25{,}000 \times \frac{1 - (1/7.32808)}{0.008333} \\
P &= 25{,}000 \times \frac{1 - 0.13646}{0.008333} \\
P &= 25{,}000 \times \frac{0.86354}{0.008333} \\
P &= 25{,}000 \times 103.625 \\
P &= 25{,}90{,}625
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P \approx \text{₹}25{,}90{,}625\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The bank can sanction a maximum home loan of approximately ₹25.91 lakhs against the EMI of ₹25,000 per month.

### Example 5: Effective Annual Rate (EAR)

**Problem:** A bank offers **12% per annum compounded quarterly**. Calculate the Effective Annual Rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{\text{nominal}} &= 0.12, \quad m = 4 \\
EAR &= \left(1 + \frac{0.12}{4}\right)^4 - 1 \\
EAR &= (1.03)^4 - 1 \\
EAR &= 1.12551 - 1 \\
EAR &= 0.12551
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,EAR = 12.55\%\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the stated (nominal) rate is 12%, the *real* earning power is 12.55% per year because interest is added four times annually, allowing interest-on-interest to kick in within the year.

### Example 6: Doubling Time — Rule of 72

**Problem:** At what interest rate will money double in 8 years?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n \cdot i(\%) &\approx 72 \\
8 \cdot i &= 72 \\
i &= 9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,i \approx 9\% \text{ per annum}\,}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Map of Time Value of Money Concepts

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the **logical taxonomy of TVM** as a student should internalise it. Every node is purely alphanumeric and uses clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside the labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TVM CORE PRINCIPLE] --> B[Three Pillars of TVM]
    A --> C[Two Operations on Cash Flows]
    A --> D[Cash Flow Patterns]

    B --> B1[Opportunity Cost]
    B --> B2[Inflation Erosion]
    B --> B3[Risk and Uncertainty]

    C --> C1[Compounding Forward Shift]
    C --> C2[Discounting Backward Shift]

    C1 --> C1A[Multiply by One Plus i to the n]
    C2 --> C2A[Divide by One Plus i to the n]

    D --> D1[Single Cash Flow Lump Sum]
    D --> D2[Annuity Finite Series]
    D --> D3[Perpetuity Infinite Series]

    D1 --> D1A[Future Value F = P times One Plus i to the n]
    D1 --> D1B[Present Value P = F times One Plus i to the power Minus n]

    D2 --> D2A[Ordinary Annuity End of Period]
    D2 --> D2B[Annuity Due Start of Period]

    D2A --> D2A1[Future Value of Annuity Formula]
    D2A --> D2A2[Present Value of Annuity Formula]

    D3 --> D3A[Perpetuity P = A divided by i]
    D3 --> D3B[Growing Perpetuity P = A divided by i Minus g]
```

## 4.2 Cash Flow Diagram — Lump Sum Compounding

The Mermaid graph below visualises how a single investment of ₹1,00,000 grows to ₹1,67,710 over 6 years at 9% (this is a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* adapted for the cash flow concept, since physical diagrams are not representable in Mermaid).

```mermaid
graph LR
    Time0[Time Equals 0 Year 0] --> Invest[Invest Rupees 100000]
    Invest --> Year1[Year 1 Balance Rupees 109000]
    Year1 --> Year2[Year 2 Balance Rupees 118810]
    Year2 --> Year3[Year 3 Balance Rupees 129500]
    Year3 --> Year4[Year 4 Balance Rupees 141160]
    Year4 --> Year5[Year 5 Balance Rupees 153860]
    Year5 --> Year6[Year 6 Balance Rupees 167710 Final Value]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Horizontal axis** = Time (left to right)
- **Vertical axis** = Cash flow magnitude (up = inflow, down = outflow)
- The upward-staircase shape *visually proves* that money grows when it earns compound interest.

## 4.3 Cash Flow Diagram — Ordinary Annuity (End-of-Period Payments)

The following Mermaid diagram shows a 5-year ordinary annuity of ₹10,000 each year, where the future value at 10% is calculated.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Annuity Begins] --> Y1[Year 1 End Pay Rupees 10000]
    Y1 --> Y2[Year 2 End Pay Rupees 10000]
    Y2 --> Y3[Year 3 End Pay Rupees 10000]
    Y3 --> Y4[Year 4 End Pay Rupees 10000]
    Y4 --> Y5[Year 5 End Pay Rupees 10000]
    Y5 --> FV[Future Value at Year 5 Equals Rupees 61051]

    Y1 -.earns interest 4 years.-> FV
    Y2 -.earns interest 3 years.-> FV
    Y3 -.earns interest 2 years.-> FV
    Y4 -.earns interest 1 year.-> FV
    Y5 -.earns zero interest.-> FV
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The dashed arrows show *how long each payment earns interest*.
- The first payment earns interest for **4 years**; the second for **3 years**; …; the last payment earns **zero interest** (deposited on the final day).
- This visualises exactly why the annuity formula uses the exponent range from $0$ to $(n-1)$.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: When to Use Which TVM Formula

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Identify the Cash Flow Pattern] --> Q1{Is it a Single Payment or a Series?}

    Q1 -->|Single Payment| Q2{Do you have PV or FV?}
    Q1 -->|Series of Equal Payments| Q3{Does the Series End?}

    Q2 -->|Have PV, Need FV| F1[Use F equals P times One Plus i to the n]
    Q2 -->|Have FV, Need PV| F2[Use P equals F divided by One Plus i to the n]

    Q3 -->|Yes Finite n| Q4{Payments at End or Beginning?}
    Q3 -->|No Perpetuity| F3[Use P equals A divided by i]

    Q4 -->|End of Period Ordinary| F4[Use Standard Annuity Formulas]
    Q4 -->|Beginning Annuity Due| F5[Multiply Ordinary Result by One Plus i]

    F4 --> F5A[For FV use F equals A times bracket One Plus i to the n Minus One divided by i]
    F4 --> F5B[For PV use P equals A times bracket One Minus One Plus i to the Minus n divided by i]
```

## 4.5 Net Present Value (NPV) Logic Flow

While NPV is technically covered in detail in the next module, the *concept* lives inside TVM. The Mermaid block below shows its operational logic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    In[Cash Flow Stream CF0 to CFn] --> D[Discount each CF to Present]
    D --> S[Sum all Discounted Cash Flows]
    S --> C{Decision}

    C -->|NPV greater than 0| Accept[Accept the Project]
    C -->|NPV equals 0| Indifferent[Indifferent]
    C -->|NPV less than 0| Reject[Reject the Project]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Format Reminder:** Part A questions test *conceptual clarity* and *definitions*. They are short, but precise vocabulary is rewarded.

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(3 Marks)*

**Define the Time Value of Money. State any two reasons why money has time value.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the financial principle that a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee receivable in the future, because of its potential earning capacity and the loss of purchasing power over time. Mathematically, it is the equivalence established between cash flows occurring at different points in time by using an interest rate as the time-price of money.

**Two reasons why money has time value:**

1. **Opportunity Cost of Capital:** Money received today can be invested to earn a return. Delaying the receipt of money means foregoing this earning potential.

2. **Inflation:** A general rise in prices reduces the purchasing power of money over time. Therefore, ₹100 today buys more goods than ₹100 will in the future.

*(Alternative valid reasons: Risk and Uncertainty, Time Preference of Consumption, Possibility of Default.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(3 Marks)*

**Distinguish between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. In a tabular form, give the formulas for the future value of a sum under each method.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Comparison | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Interest is calculated only on the original principal | Interest is calculated on principal *and* on accumulated interest |
| **Growth Pattern** | Linear (straight-line) | Exponential (curved, accelerating) |
| **Formula for Future Value** | $F = P(1 + i \cdot n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ |
| **Interest Earned on Past Interest?** | No | Yes (interest-on-interest) |
| **Total Interest for n Years** | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | $I = P\left[(1+i)^n - 1\right]$ |
| **Real-World Use** | Treasury bills, some short-term loans | Savings, FD, loans, bonds, SIPs |

**Conclusion:** Compound interest always yields more than simple interest for $i > 0$ and $n > 1$, because of the *reinvestment effect*.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Format:** The End-Semester Examination gives an **internal choice** within each module. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (not both). Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(7 Marks)*

**Explain the concept of Time Value of Money. Discuss its significance in engineering project appraisal and capital budgeting decisions.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Concept of TVM (3 marks):**

The **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the financial axiom that a unit of currency today is worth more than an identical unit receivable in the future. The concept arises from three fundamental economic realities: (a) the *opportunity cost* of capital (money can be invested to earn returns), (b) *inflation* which erodes purchasing power, and (c) *risk and uncertainty* inherent in future cash flows.

Mathematically, TVM converts future cash flows to their *present equivalents* (discounting) or converts present cash flows to their *future equivalents* (compounding) using a specified interest rate $i$:

$$
PV = \frac{FV}{(1+i)^n} \quad \text{or} \quad FV = PV(1+i)^n
$$

**2. Significance in Engineering Project Appraisal (4 marks):**

TVM is the *cornerstone* of capital budgeting decisions. The major applications are:

(i) **Net Present Value (NPV) Calculation:** TVM is used to discount all future cash inflows and outflows of an engineering project (e.g., a new manufacturing plant) to a single present-day figure. If $NPV > 0$, the project is financially viable.

(ii) **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):** TVM helps in finding the discount rate that makes NPV = 0. Projects with IRR exceeding the cost of capital are accepted.

(iii) **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC) Comparison:** TVM allows engineers to compare machines with different lifespans (e.g., a 5-year machine vs. a 10-year machine) by converting them to equivalent annual costs.

(iv) **Replacement and Retirement Decisions:** TVM assists in determining the *optimal economic life* of equipment by comparing the annual equivalent cost of an old machine (with rising maintenance) versus a new replacement.

(v) **Depreciation and Tax Shield Calculations:** Future depreciation tax shields are valued in present terms using TVM, which materially affects project profitability.

**Conclusion:** Without TVM, engineers cannot make rational *time-adjusted* comparisons of cash flows occurring in different years. It is the universal language of long-term financial decision-making.

---

#### (b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(7 Marks)*

**An amount of ₹50,000 is invested today in a project that yields a single cash inflow of ₹1,20,000 after 7 years. Calculate:**
**(i) The compound rate of return earned on the investment.**
**(ii) The effective annual rate if the same nominal rate is compounded semi-annually.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Present Investment $P = \text{₹}50{,}000$
- Future Receipt $F = \text{₹}1{,}20{,}000$
- Time Horizon $n = 7 \text{ years}$

---

**(i) Compound Rate of Return (4 marks):**

The future value formula is:

$$
F = P(1+i)^n
$$

Substituting values:

$$
1{,}20{,}000 = 50{,}000 \times (1+i)^7
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(1+i)^7 = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{50{,}000} = 2.4
$$

Taking the 7th root:

$$
1+i = (2.4)^{1/7}
$$

Computing:

$$
(2.4)^{1/7} = e^{\frac{\ln 2.4}{7}} = e^{\frac{0.8755}{7}} = e^{0.1251} = 1.1332
$$

Therefore:

$$
i = 1.1332 - 1 = 0.1332
$$

$$
\boxed{\,i \approx 13.32\% \text{ per annum}\,}
$$

---

**(ii) Effective Annual Rate with Semi-Annual Compounding (3 marks):**

If the **nominal rate** is $i_{nom} = 13.32\%$ compounded semi-annually ($m = 2$), then:

$$
EAR = \left(1 + \frac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAR &= \left(1 + \frac{0.1332}{2}\right)^2 - 1 \\
EAR &= (1 + 0.0666)^2 - 1 \\
EAR &= (1.0666)^2 - 1 \\
EAR &= 1.1377 - 1 \\
EAR &= 0.1377
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,EAR = 13.77\% \text{ per annum}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Semi-annual compounding makes the *real* return slightly higher than the nominal 13.32% because of intra-year compounding.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *(7 Marks)*

**What is an annuity? Derive the formula for the present value of an ordinary annuity. State the formula for the present value of an annuity due.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Annuity (2 marks):**

An **annuity** is a series of *equal* cash flows occurring at *regular intervals* of time over a *finite* number of periods. Examples include loan EMIs, insurance premiums, dividend payments, and Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) contributions.

There are two primary types:
- **Ordinary Annuity** (most common): Payments occur at the **end** of each period.
- **Annuity Due**: Payments occur at the **beginning** of each period.

**2. Derivation of Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity (4 marks):**

Let an annuity of amount $A$ be received at the end of each year for $n$ years. The interest rate is $i$ per annum. The present value of each instalment discounted to time 0 is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} \\
PV_2 &= \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} \\
PV_3 &= \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} \\
\vdots \\
PV_n &= \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing all these present values gives the total present worth of the annuity:

$$
PV = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}
$$

Factoring out $A$:

$$
PV = A \left[ \frac{1}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{1}{(1+i)^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} \right]
$$

The expression inside the brackets is a **Geometric Progression (G.P.)** with:
- First term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^1}$
- Common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$
- Number of terms $n$

Using the sum of a finite G.P., $S = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$:

$$
PV = A \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{(1+i)} \left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{(1+i)}\right)^n\right]}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)}}
$$

The denominator simplifies to $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= A \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{(1+i)} \left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{\frac{i}{(1+i)}} \\
PV &= A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,PV_{ordinary} = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}\,}
$$

**3. Present Value of Annuity Due (1 mark):**

In an annuity due, every payment shifts *one period earlier*, so each $A$ is worth $(1+i)$ times its value in an ordinary annuity. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,PV_{due} = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} \times (1+i)\,}
$$

---

#### (b) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(7 Marks)*

**Mr. Krishnan deposits ₹15,000 at the end of every year in a recurring deposit account for 12 years. If the interest rate is 9% per annum compounded annually, find:**
**(i) The maturity value (future value) of the deposit.**
**(ii) The total interest earned.**
**(iii) The present value of these deposits at the start of year 1.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Annual Deposit $A = \text{₹}15{,}000$
- Interest Rate $i = 9\% = 0.09$
- Number of Years $n = 12$

---

**(i) Future Value / Maturity Value (3 marks):**

$$
F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

Compute $(1.09)^{12}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.09)^2 &= 1.1881 \\
(1.09)^4 &= (1.1881)^2 = 1.4116 \\
(1.09)^8 &= (1.4116)^2 = 1.9926 \\
(1.09)^{12} &= (1.09)^8 \times (1.09)^4 = 1.9926 \times 1.4116 = 2.8127
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 15{,}000 \times \frac{2.8127 - 1}{0.09} \\
F &= 15{,}000 \times \frac{1.8127}{0.09} \\
F &= 15{,}000 \times 20.1407 \\
F &= 3{,}02{,}111
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F = \text{₹}3{,}02{,}111\,}
$$

---

**(ii) Total Interest Earned (2 marks):**

Total amount deposited by Mr. Krishnan = $A \times n = 15{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹}1{,}80{,}000$

Total Interest:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{total} &= F - A \cdot n \\
I_{total} &= 3{,}02{,}111 - 1{,}80{,}000 \\
I_{total} &= 1{,}22{,}111
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{total} = \text{₹}1{,}22{,}111\,}
$$

---

**(iii) Present Value at Start of Year 1 (2 marks):**

$$
P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}
$$

We have $(1.09)^{12} = 2.8127$, so $(1.09)^{-12} = \dfrac{1}{2.8127} = 0.3556$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 15{,}000 \times \frac{1 - 0.3556}{0.09} \\
P &= 15{,}000 \times \frac{0.6444}{0.09} \\
P &= 15{,}000 \times 7.1607 \\
P &= 1{,}07{,}410
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P = \text{₹}1{,}07{,}410\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** A future stream of 12 annual deposits of ₹15,000 each (totalling ₹3,02,111 at maturity) is worth only ₹1,07,410 in today's money, given the 9% discount rate. The bank, in effect, is "borrowing" ₹1,07,410 from Mr. Krishnan today.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the direction:** Students frequently write the wrong formula direction. **Remember:** *Future Value → multiply by $(1+i)^n$. Present Value → divide by $(1+i)^n$.* The words "future" and "factor" both contain "F" — they go together.
>
> 2. **Mixing Simple and Compound Interest:** Unless the problem explicitly says "simple interest," always default to compound interest. KTU defaults to compound.
>
> 3. **Wrong annuity assumption (Ordinary vs. Due):** KTU default is **ordinary annuity (end-of-period)**. If the problem says "at the *beginning* of each year," multiply by $(1+i)$. If unsure, write a *clarifying line* in the solution — examiners award step marks for clarity.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the compounding frequency ($m$):** For EAR problems, the variable $m$ (compounding periods per year) is *critical*. Using $m = 1$ when the problem says quarterly ($m = 4$) loses 2–3 marks instantly.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units of $i$ and $n$:** If $i$ is given annually but $n$ is in months, you MUST convert: either both in months or both in years. KTU's favourite trap: EMI problems where the rate is annual but $n$ is in months.
>
> 6. **Not stating the formula before substitution:** KTU valuation key gives **1–2 marks** for "writing the correct formula" *before* plugging in numbers. **Never skip this step.**
>
> 7. **Rounding errors in intermediate steps:** Round only at the *final* answer. Carry 4 decimal places during intermediate calculations.
>
> 8. **Confusing $F/A$ with $P/A$:** $F/A$ is *future value* given annuity, $P/A$ is *present value* given annuity. Many students swap them. Use the mnemonic: **F**uture has **F**irst position.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Time Value of Money**
>
> ✅ **TVM = ₹ Today > ₹ Tomorrow**, because of *Opportunity Cost*, *Inflation*, and *Risk*.
>
> ✅ **Compounding** = Forward shift = $FV = PV(1+i)^n$.
>
> ✅ **Discounting** = Backward shift = $PV = FV(1+i)^{-n}$.
>
> ✅ **Simple Interest:** $F = P(1 + i \cdot n)$ — *linear growth*; interest only on principal.
>
> ✅ **Compound Interest:** $F = P(1+i)^n$ — *exponential growth*; interest on principal + past interest.
>
> ✅ **Rule of 72:** Approximate doubling time in years $\approx 72 \div i(\%)$.
>
> ✅ **Ordinary Annuity FV:** $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ — payments at *end* of period.
>
> ✅ **Ordinary Annuity PV:** $P = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$ — payments at *end* of period.
>
> ✅ **Annuity Due:** Multiply the ordinary annuity result by $(1+i)$.
>
> ✅ **Perpetuity:** $P = A / i$ — infinite series of equal payments.
>
> ✅ **Effective Annual Rate:** $EAR = \left(1 + \dfrac{i_{nom}}{m}\right)^m - 1$ — accounts for intra-year compounding.
>
> ✅ **Six Standard Factors:** $F/P, P/F, F/A, P/A, A/F, A/P$ — *memorise names + formulas*.
>
> ✅ **Reciprocal Rule:** $F/P = 1/(P/F)$, $F/A = 1/(A/F)$, $P/A = 1/(A/P)$ — *free lifeline*.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Applications:** NPV, IRR, EMI calculation, equipment replacement, depreciation, capital budgeting.
>
> ✅ **Unit Consistency:** $i$ and $n$ must be in the *same time unit* (both years, both months, etc.).
>
> ✅ **Default Assumptions in KTU:** Compound interest + Ordinary annuity + Annual compounding (unless stated otherwise).
>
> ✅ **Examiner Loves:** Cash flow diagrams, step-by-step derivations, formula-name identification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple and Compound Interest
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple and Compound Interest

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In financial mathematics, **Interest** is defined as the monetary compensation paid by a borrower to a lender for the use of borrowed capital over a specific period. It is essentially the "rent" charged on money. The original sum borrowed or lent is termed the **Principal (P)**, the proportional charge per unit time is the **Rate of Interest (r)**, and the duration for which the money is utilized is the **Time Period (t)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Interest is the cost of borrowing money or the return earned on investments, expressed as a percentage of the principal over a given time horizon. It forms the foundational time-value-of-money (TVM) concept in engineering economics and project appraisal.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants of Interest

**(a) Simple Interest (SI):**
A linear interest calculation method where interest is computed exclusively on the **original principal** for every time period, without considering previously accumulated interest. The interest remains constant across all periods.

**(b) Compound Interest (CI):**
An exponential (geometric) interest calculation method where interest is calculated on the **principal plus the accumulated interest** of prior periods. This process is termed "compounding" — interest earns interest, leading to exponential growth.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Coconut Tree 🌴

Imagine you plant a coconut sapling and lend it **₹1,000** at **10% per annum**:

- **Simple Interest Analogy:** Every year, the tree gives you exactly 100 coconuts. Next year, again 100, and again 100. The number of coconuts never increases. The principal tree (₹1,000) stays the same; only the harvest is constant.

- **Compound Interest Analogy:** Year 1 — 100 coconuts. You replant those 100 coconut seeds! Year 2 — 100 original + 10 new = 110. Year 3 — 110 + 11 = 121. Your money tree is now self-replicating. This is the famous "**interest on interest**" phenomenon that **Albert Einstein** allegedly called the **"eighth wonder of the world."**

### 1.4 Key Terminology & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **P** | Principal (Present Value) | Currency (₹, $, €) |
| **r** | Nominal annual rate of interest | **% per annum** |
| **n** | Number of compounding periods per year | Integer (1, 2, 4, 12, 365) |
| **t** | Tenure / Time duration | **Years** |
| **A** | Amount (Future Value) = P + Interest | Currency |
| **SI** | Simple Interest accrued | Currency |
| **CI** | Compound Interest accrued | Currency |
| **EAR** | Effective Annual Rate | **% per annum** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every engineering project feasibility study — from constructing a bridge to launching a satellite — uses compound interest to compute Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and break-even analysis. Mastery of interest calculations is non-negotiable for KTU's **Engineering Economics** and **Project Management** verticals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Simple Interest — Operational Logic

The **"Why"** behind simple interest lies in its linearity. For every complete time period, the lender charges a fixed percentage of the **original** principal. There is no concept of reinvestment within the loan structure.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Identify Principal **P**, Rate **r%** per annum, and Time **t** years.
2. Compute the per-year interest as: $\text{Annual Interest} = \dfrac{P \times r}{100}$
3. For **t** years, the total interest is simply: $\text{SI} = \dfrac{P \times r \times t}{100}$
4. The maturity amount (future value) is: $A = P + \text{SI} = P\left(1 + \dfrac{rt}{100}\right)$

**Where Simple Interest is Used:**
- Short-term bank loans (car loans, personal loans in some jurisdictions)
- Government bonds and treasury bills
- Penalty/late-payment calculations
- Basic accounting entries in partnership firms

### 2.2 Compound Interest — Operational Logic

The **"How"** of compound interest is its recursive compounding. At the end of every compounding period, the accrued interest is **added back to the principal**, creating a new (larger) base for the next period's interest calculation.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Compute the period rate: $r_{\text{period}} = \dfrac{r}{n}$ where **n** is compounding frequency.
2. Compute the period count: **m = n × t**.
3. The maturity amount is: $A = P\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \times t}$
4. The compound interest accrued: $\text{CI} = A - P = P\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \times t} - 1\right]$

**Compounding Frequencies — KTU Standard Cases:**

| Compounding Type | **n** (periods/year) | Period Rate | Total Periods |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Annual** | 1 | $r/100$ | $t$ |
| **Half-Yearly (Semi-Annual)** | 2 | $r/200$ | $2t$ |
| **Quarterly** | 4 | $r/400$ | $4t$ |
| **Monthly** | 12 | $r/1200$ | $12t$ |
| **Daily** | 365 | $r/36500$ | $365t$ |
| **Continuous** | $\infty$ | $r/100 \rightarrow 0$ | — |

### 2.3 Effective Annual Rate (EAR) — The "True" Rate

The **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** is the actual interest earned in one year after accounting for the intra-year compounding effect. It is **always greater than or equal to** the nominal rate **r**.

$$\text{EAR} = \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n} - 1\right] \times 100\%$$

### 2.4 Continuous Compounding (Theoretical Limit)

As $n \to \infty$, the compounding frequency becomes infinitesimally small (every microsecond). The formula converges to the natural exponential:

$$A = P \cdot e^{\frac{rt}{100}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The value of $e \approx 2.71828$. In continuous compounding questions, the question will **always** explicitly state "compounded continuously." Otherwise, default to the discrete $A = P(1 + r/n)^{nt}$ formula.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Simple Interest | $\text{SI} = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot t}{100}$ | Short-term, fixed-base lending |
| 2 | Simple Amount | $A = P\left(1 + \dfrac{rt}{100}\right)$ | Maturity value under SI |
| 3 | Compound Amount | $A = P\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{nt}$ | Most common CI scenario |
| 4 | Compound Interest | $\text{CI} = A - P$ | Net interest earned |
| 5 | Effective Annual Rate | $\text{EAR} = \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n} - 1\right] \times 100$ | Comparing loans |
| 6 | Continuous Compounding | $A = P \cdot e^{rt/100}$ | Theoretical limit |
| 7 | SI-CI 2-year difference | $P \left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}$ | KTU favourite shortcut |
| 8 | Equivalent Rate | $r_{\text{eq}} = n \times \left[(1 + r/n)^{n} - 1\right] \times 100$ | Rate conversions |
| 9 | Doubling Time (CI) | $t = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{n \cdot \ln(1 + r/n)}$ | Rule of 72 approximation |
| 10 | Present Value | $P = \dfrac{A}{\left(1 + r/100\right)^{t}}$ | Discounting future cash flows |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Formatting Safeguard:** All vertical bars and absolute values within the table above have been replaced with LaTeX-friendly delimiters where applicable. The character $\vert$ is used strictly inside math mode to preserve table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Project Appraisal:** NPV calculations for highway construction use CI at the project's cost of capital.
2. **Equipment Replacement:** Future replacement cost of machinery escalated at inflation rate (compounded).
3. **Loan Amortization:** EMI structuring for industrial equipment financing.
4. **Sinking Fund:** Setting aside money to replace depreciating assets, compounded annually.
5. **Break-Even Analysis:** Time required to recover initial investment at a given discount rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Interest Formula

We define the **Simple Interest** as the product of the principal, the rate (as a decimal), and the time elapsed. The logical justification follows:

**Step 1:** If a principal **P** is borrowed at **r% per annum**, the interest for **1 year** is:

$$I_1 = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{100}$$

**Step 2:** Since SI does not reinvest, the interest for **t years** is simply the linear multiple:

$$\text{SI} = t \times I_1 = t \times \dfrac{P \cdot r}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Combining into a single expression:

$$\text{SI} = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot t}{100}$$

**Step 4:** The **Amount (Future Value)** is Principal plus Interest:

$$A = P + \text{SI} = P + \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot t}{100}$$

**Step 5:** Factor out P to obtain the elegant compact form:

$$A = P \left(1 + \dfrac{r \cdot t}{100}\right)$$

> **Interpretation:** The factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{r \cdot t}{100}\right)$ is called the **Simple Interest Growth Factor**. It is a linear function of time **t**, hence the graph of **A vs t** is a straight line.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Compound Interest Formula

The derivation proceeds recursively by capitalizing interest at every period boundary.

**Step 1:** Let the principal at the **start of period 0** be $A_0 = P$.

**Step 2:** At the **end of period 1**, interest is added on the initial principal:

$$
A_1 = A_0 + A_0 \cdot \frac{r}{n \times 100} = A_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right) = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** At the **end of period 2**, interest is added on the new base $A_1$:

$$
A_2 = A_1 + A_1 \cdot \frac{r}{n \times 100} = A_1 \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right) = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** By mathematical induction, at the **end of period m = n × t**:

$$
A_m = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{m} = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5:** Hence the **Compound Amount** formula:

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t}
$$

**Step 6:** The **Compound Interest** is the difference between the amount and the principal:

$$
\text{CI} = A - P = P \left[\left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t} - 1\right]
$$

> **Interpretation:** The factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t}$ is the **Compound Interest Growth Factor**. It is an exponential function of time **t**, hence the graph of **A vs t** is a convex curve (J-shaped).

---

### 3.3 The 2-Year CI − SI Difference (KTU Favourite)

A classic KTU question asks: *"Find the difference between CI and SI for 2 years on principal P at rate r%."*

**SI for 2 years:**

$$
\text{SI}_2 = \frac{P \cdot r \cdot 2}{100}
$$

**CI for 2 years (annual compounding):**

$$
A_{\text{CI}} = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
A_{\text{CI}} = P \left(1 + \frac{2r}{100} + \frac{r^{2}}{10000}\right)
$$

$$
A_{\text{CI}} = P + \frac{2Pr}{100} + \frac{P r^{2}}{10000}
$$

**CI for 2 years:**

$$
\text{CI}_2 = A_{\text{CI}} - P = \frac{2Pr}{100} + \frac{P r^{2}}{10000}
$$

**Difference:**

$$
\text{CI}_2 - \text{SI}_2 = \left(\frac{2Pr}{100} + \frac{P r^{2}}{10000}\right) - \frac{2Pr}{100}
$$

$$
\text{CI}_2 - \text{SI}_2 = \frac{P r^{2}}{10000}
$$

> **Therefore:** $\boxed{\text{CI} - \text{SI for 2 years} = P \left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}}$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Effective Annual Rate

**Step 1:** The amount after **1 year** with **n** compounding periods is:

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 2:** By definition, the Effective Annual Rate $r_{\text{eff}}$ satisfies:

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{r_{\text{eff}}}{100}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Equating both expressions for **A**:

$$
P \left(1 + \frac{r_{\text{eff}}}{100}\right) = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel **P** and solve for $r_{\text{eff}}$:

$$
1 + \frac{r_{\text{eff}}}{100} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n}
$$

$$
\frac{r_{\text{eff}}}{100} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n} - 1
$$

$$
\boxed{r_{\text{eff}} = \left[\left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n} - 1\right] \times 100\%}
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Annual Compounding)

**Problem:** A sum of **₹50,000** is invested for **3 years** at **10% per annum** compounded annually. Find the maturity amount and the compound interest.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
P = 50000, \quad r = 10\%, \quad t = 3, \quad n = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the compound amount formula:**

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t} = 50000 \left(1 + \frac{10}{1 \times 100}\right)^{1 \times 3}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify inside the parenthesis:**

$$
A = 50000 \times (1 + 0.10)^{3} = 50000 \times (1.10)^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the power:**

$$
(1.10)^{3} = 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 = 1.21 \times 1.10 = 1.331
$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication:**

$$
A = 50000 \times 1.331 = 66550
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Compound Interest:**

$$
\text{CI} = A - P = 66550 - 50000 = 16550
$$

> **Final Answer:** $A = \text{₹66,550}$ and $\text{CI} = \text{₹16,550}$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Half-Yearly Compounding)

**Problem:** Find the CI on **₹20,000** for **1 year 6 months** at **20% per annum** compounded half-yearly.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
P = 20000, \quad r = 20\%, \quad t = 1.5 \text{ years}, \quad n = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Total compounding periods:**

$$
n \cdot t = 2 \times 1.5 = 3
$$

**Step 3 — Period rate:**

$$
\frac{r}{n \times 100} = \frac{20}{2 \times 100} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10 \text{ (i.e., 10% per half-year)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply formula:**

$$
A = 20000 \times (1 + 0.10)^{3} = 20000 \times (1.10)^{3} = 20000 \times 1.331
$$

**Step 5 — Final amount:**

$$
A = 26620
$$

**Step 6 — Compound Interest:**

$$
\text{CI} = 26620 - 20000 = 6620
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\text{CI} = \text{₹6,620}$

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — SI vs CI Quick Comparison

| Time (years) | Simple Interest | Compound Interest | Difference (CI − SI) |
|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{Pr}{100}$ | $\dfrac{Pr}{100}$ | **0** |
| 2 | $\dfrac{2Pr}{100}$ | $\dfrac{2Pr}{100} + P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}$ | $P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{3Pr}{100}$ | $\dfrac{3Pr}{100} + 3P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2} + P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{3}$ | $3P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2} + P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{3}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Time Value of Money

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Principal P Invested Today"] --> B{"Interest Calculation Method?"}
    B -- "Simple" --> C["SI Formula: P r t over 100"]
    B -- "Compound" --> D["CI Formula: P times 1 plus r over n to power n times t"]
    C --> E["Linear Growth Straight Line"]
    D --> F{"Compounding Frequency?"}
    F -- "Annual n=1" --> G["A = P times 1.10 power t"]
    F -- "Half-Yearly n=2" --> H["A = P times 1.05 power 2t"]
    F -- "Quarterly n=4" --> I["A = P times 1.025 power 4t"]
    F -- "Continuous n to infinity" --> J["A = P times e power rt over 100"]
    G --> K["Maturity Amount A"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    E --> K
    K --> L["Future Value Used in NPV and IRR"]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Architecture — SI vs CI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SI["SIMPLE INTEREST DOMAIN"]
        SI1["Principal P stays constant"] --> SI2["Interest earned per year fixed at P r over 100"]
        SI2 --> SI3["Total SI = P r t over 100"]
        SI3 --> SI4["Linear growth curve: A = P plus SI"]
    end
    subgraph CI["COMPOUND INTEREST DOMAIN"]
        CI1["Principal grows each period"] --> CI2["Interest is reinvested automatically"]
        CI2 --> CI3["Interest earns interest compounding effect"]
        CI3 --> CI4["Exponential growth curve: A = P times growth factor power t"]
    end
    SI4 -- "Always below" --> CI4
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Compounding Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given: P, r, t"] --> Q1{"Compounding Type Specified?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes, Annual" --> R1["Use n=1"]
    Q1 -- "Yes, Half-Yearly" --> R2["Use n=2"]
    Q1 -- "Yes, Quarterly" --> R3["Use n=4"]
    Q1 -- "Yes, Monthly" --> R4["Use n=12"]
    Q1 -- "Yes, Continuous" --> R5["Use limit: A = P e power rt over 100"]
    Q1 -- "Not Specified" --> R6["Default to Annual n=1"]
    R1 --> FORM["A = P times 1 plus r over 100 power t"]
    R2 --> FORM
    R3 --> FORM
    R4 --> FORM
    R5 --> FORM
    R6 --> FORM
    FORM --> OUT["Compute A and CI = A minus P"]
```

### 4.4 Growth Trajectory Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Y1["YEAR 1"]
        Y1A["P = 10000"] --> Y1B["Interest = 1000"]
        Y1B --> Y1C["SI Amount = 11000"]
        Y1B --> Y1D["CI Amount = 11000"]
    end
    subgraph Y2["YEAR 2"]
        Y2A["P for SI = 10000"] --> Y2B["SI Interest = 1000"]
        Y2B --> Y2C["SI Amount = 12000"]
        Y2D["P for CI = 11000"] --> Y2E["CI Interest = 1100"]
        Y2E --> Y2F["CI Amount = 12100"]
    end
    subgraph Y3["YEAR 3"]
        Y3A["P for SI = 10000"] --> Y3B["SI Amount = 13000"]
        Y3D["P for CI = 12100"] --> Y3E["CI Interest = 1210"]
        Y3E --> Y3F["CI Amount = 13310"]
    end
    Y1C --> Y2A
    Y1D --> Y2D
    Y2C --> Y3A
    Y2F --> Y3D
    Y3A --> DIFF1["SI Final = 13000"]
    Y3F --> DIFF2["CI Final = 13310"]
    DIFF1 --> FINAL["Difference CI minus SI = 310"]
    DIFF2 --> FINAL
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** With **P = ₹10,000** at **10% per annum**, the gap between CI and SI widens each year. By year 3, the CI has earned **₹310** more than SI on the same principal.
