## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Differentiate between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. State one real-world example where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Base for interest | Original principal **P** remains constant | Principal **grows** by adding accrued interest |
| Nature of growth | **Linear** with time | **Exponential** with time |
| Total interest for **t** years | $\dfrac{Prt}{100}$ | $P\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{nt} - 1\right]$ |
| Suitable for | Short-term loans, treasury bills | Long-term investments, savings accounts, project NPV |

> **(1 mark)** for correct definition of SI, **(1 mark)** for CI, **(1 mark)** for examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Define the term "Effective Annual Rate" (EAR). If a bank offers 12% compounded quarterly, compute the EAR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Effective Annual Rate (EAR) is the actual interest earned in a year after accounting for the intra-year compounding effect. It is always greater than or equal to the nominal rate.

**Given:** $r = 12\%$, $n = 4$ (quarterly)

**EAR formula (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{EAR} = \left[\left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n} - 1\right] \times 100
$$

**Computation (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{EAR} = \left[\left(1 + \frac{12}{4 \times 100}\right)^{4} - 1\right] \times 100 = \left[(1.03)^{4} - 1\right] \times 100
$$

$$
(1.03)^{4} = 1.12550881 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{EAR} = 12.55\%
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\text{EAR} = \mathbf{12.55\%}$ per annum.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — April 2024, Model Paper A1]**

**(a)** A construction firm borrows **₹5,00,000** from a bank at **12% per annum** simple interest for **4 years**. Calculate:
   (i) The total simple interest payable.
   (ii) The maturity amount.
   (iii) The total amount the firm would have paid under **compound interest (annual compounding)** at the same rate.
   (iv) The savings if the firm had chosen compound interest over simple interest — explain why this is counterintuitive.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sum of money invested at **10% per annum** compounded half-yearly amounts to **₹1,21,000** at the end of **2 years**. Find:
   (i) The original principal.
   (ii) The total compound interest earned.
   (iii) The corresponding simple interest on the same principal for the same period.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Simple vs Compound Comparison

**Given:** $P = 5,00,000$, $r = 12\%$, $t = 4$ years.

**(i) Simple Interest (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{SI} = \frac{P \cdot r \cdot t}{100} = \frac{500000 \times 12 \times 4}{100} = 2,40,000
$$

**(ii) Maturity Amount under SI (1 Mark):**

$$
A_{\text{SI}} = P + \text{SI} = 500000 + 240000 = 7,40,000
$$

**(iii) Amount under CI (annual compounding) (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{\text{CI}} = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^{t} = 500000 \times (1.12)^{4}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{4} = 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 \times 1.12 = 1.57352
$$

$$
A_{\text{CI}} = 500000 \times 1.57352 = 7,86,760
$$

**(iv) Savings on borrowing (3 Marks):**

> **Counterintuitive insight:** Since the firm is the *borrower*, the firm pays *more* under CI than under SI. The difference is $7,86,760 - 7,40,000 = \mathbf{₹46,760}$ extra. From the **bank's perspective**, CI yields higher returns, so the bank always prefers CI. The **borrower** benefits from CI only when *investing*, not when borrowing. This is why long-term industrial loans use reducing-balance or amortized CI methods to lower the effective burden on the borrower.

> **Examiner's Note:** Many students mistakenly claim "CI is always better for the borrower." Clarify whether you are the lender or borrower.

#### Part (b) — Reverse Calculation (Finding Principal)

**Given:** $A = 1,21,000$, $r = 10\%$, $n = 2$, $t = 2$ years, compounded half-yearly.

**(i) Principal (3 Marks):**

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 121000 = P \left(1 + \frac{10}{200}\right)^{2 \times 2}
$$

$$
121000 = P \times (1.05)^{4}
$$

$$
(1.05)^{4} = 1.21550625
$$

$$
P = \frac{121000}{1.21550625} = 99,547.78 \approx 99,548
$$

**(ii) Compound Interest (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{CI} = A - P = 121000 - 99548 = 21,452
$$

**(iii) Simple Interest on the same principal (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{SI} = \frac{P \cdot r \cdot t}{100} = \frac{99548 \times 10 \times 2}{100} = 19,909.6
$$

> **Final Results:** $P \approx \text{₹99,548}$, $\text{CI} = \text{₹21,452}$, $\text{SI} = \text{₹19,909.60}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — April 2024, Model Paper B1]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **continuous compounding**. Derive the formula $A = Pe^{rt/100}$ from the discrete compounding formula by taking the limit as $n \to \infty$. A sum of **₹1,00,000** is invested at **8% per annum** compounded continuously for **5 years**. Find the maturity amount. (Use $e = 2.71828$.)

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sum triples itself in **6 years** under simple interest. In how many years will it **double itself** under compound interest at the same rate (annual compounding)?

**(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Continuous Compounding Derivation & Application

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Starting from the discrete compound amount formula:

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t}
$$

Let $k = \dfrac{n \times 100}{r}$, so $n = \dfrac{k \cdot r}{100}$. Substitute:

$$
A = P \left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)^{k \cdot t \cdot \frac{r}{100}}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $k \to \infty$. Using the famous limit $\lim_{k \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{k}\right)^{k} = e$:

$$
A = P \cdot e^{\frac{r \cdot t}{100}}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**

**Given:** $P = 1,00,000$, $r = 8\%$, $t = 5$ years, continuous compounding.

$$
A = 100000 \times e^{\frac{8 \times 5}{100}} = 100000 \times e^{0.4}
$$

$$
e^{0.4} = 1.49182 \quad \text{(standard value)}
$$

$$
A = 100000 \times 1.49182 = 1,49,182
$$

> **Final Answer:** $A = \text{₹1,49,182}$

#### Part (b) — Tripling and Doubling Time Problem

**Step 1: Find the rate from the SI tripling condition (3 Marks):**

If the sum triples in **6 years** at SI:

$$
A = 3P, \quad \text{SI} = A - P = 2P
$$

$$
\text{SI} = \frac{P \cdot r \cdot 6}{100} = 2P \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{200}{6} = 33.33\%
$$

**Step 2: Doubling time under CI at r = 33.33% (annual compounding) (3 Marks):**

We need $A = 2P$:

$$
2P = P \left(1 + \frac{33.33}{100}\right)^{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 = (1.3333)^{t}
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(2) = t \cdot \ln(1.3333) \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1.3333)} = \frac{0.6931}{0.2877} = 2.409 \text{ years}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $t \approx \mathbf{2.41 \text{ years}}$

> **Sanity Check:** It is intuitive that under CI, the sum doubles *faster* (2.41 years) than under SI would have taken to double (which would be 3 years at 33.33% rate). This confirms the power of compounding.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Time unit mismatch:** If the question says "1 year 6 months," students frequently use **t = 1.5** but forget to adjust **n** correctly. Always re-check: $n \times t$ must equal total compounding periods.
> 2. **Percentage vs decimal confusion:** In the formula, the rate **r** is in **percent** (e.g., 12), not 0.12. The division by 100 in the formula handles the conversion. Mixing these gives wrong answers worth 3-5 marks lost.
> 3. **Half-yearly vs quarterly mix-up:** A 10% rate compounded **half-yearly** means 5% per half-year, not 10% per half-year. Always halve (or quarter, etc.) the rate when adjusting for frequency.
> 4. **Forgetting the base difference in CI vs SI for 2 years:** Students often write $\text{CI} - \text{SI} = \dfrac{Pr^{2}}{100}$ (missing the second zero). The correct form is $P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}$.
> 5. **Not interpreting "find the principal" questions as reverse calculations:** Always isolate **P** by dividing the maturity amount **A** by the growth factor, not by multiplying.
> 6. **Continuous compounding trick:** If the question says "compounded continuously," do **not** apply the $A = P(1+r/n)^{nt}$ formula — use $A = Pe^{rt/100}$ or risk full-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 (Simple and Compound Interest)**

✅ **Interest** is the cost of money over time. Two types: **SI** (linear) and **CI** (exponential).

✅ **Simple Interest Formula:** $\text{SI} = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot t}{100}$ | **Amount:** $A = P\left(1 + \dfrac{rt}{100}\right)$.

✅ **Compound Amount Formula:** $A = P\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n \cdot t}$ | **CI:** $A - P$.

✅ **Compounding Frequencies** to remember: Annual (n=1), Half-Yearly (n=2), Quarterly (n=4), Monthly (n=12), Continuous ($n \to \infty$).

✅ **Continuous Compounding:** $A = P \cdot e^{rt/100}$ where $e \approx 2.71828$.

✅ **Effective Annual Rate:** $\text{EAR} = \left[\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{n \times 100}\right)^{n} - 1\right] \times 100\%$ — always **≥** nominal rate **r**.

✅ **2-Year CI − SI Shortcut:** $\text{CI} - \text{SI} = P \left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}$ — must-know for KTU Part A.

✅ **Rule of 72 (Quick Doubling Estimate):** $t_{\text{double}} \approx \dfrac{72}{r\%}$ for annual compounding.

✅ **Time unit:** Always express **t** in years. For 1 year 6 months → $t = 1.5$.

✅ **Rate unit:** Always express **r** in percent per annum. The formula's division by 100 handles conversion.

✅ **Reverse calculation:** $P = \dfrac{A}{\left(1 + r/(n \cdot 100)\right)^{n \cdot t}}$ — used when "find principal" is asked.

✅ **Engineering context:** Time Value of Money (TVM), NPV, IRR, Break-Even, Equipment Replacement, Sinking Fund all rely on CI.

✅ **Graph signature:** SI produces a **straight line**; CI produces a **convex (J-shaped) curve** on the A-vs-t plane.

✅ **Algebra identity:** $A_{\text{CI}} = P(1 + r/100)^{2} = P + \dfrac{2Pr}{100} + P\left(\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{2}$ for 2 years — memorize the expansion.

✅ **Lender vs Borrower:** Under CI, **lenders gain** more; **borrowers pay** more. Frame your answers accordingly.

✅ **Memorize:** When $n = 1$, the CI formula reduces to the simplest form $A = P(1 + r/100)^{t}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Value Equivalence

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Time Value Equivalence # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Time Value of Money (TVM) / Time Value Equivalence** is the foundational financial principle stating that **a unit of money (₹1, $1, €1) available at the present time is worth more than the same unit of money available at a future date**, because the present money can be invested to earn a return (interest) over time. **Equivalence** is the condition in which two cash flows occurring at different points in time have the **same economic value** when measured at a common point in time (today, future, or any intermediate date) at a specified interest rate. The mathematical machinery that converts cash flows between these different time points is governed by the **Time Value of Money (TVM) Equations** — a set of six standard compound interest factors that form the backbone of engineering economics, project appraisal, loan amortization, and capital budgeting. ### The Six Core Equivalence Symbols (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Name | Engineering Economy Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $(F/P, i, n)$ | Single Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF) | Find Future value given Present value | | $(P/F, i, n)$ | Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF) | Find Present value given Future value | | $(F/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (USCAF) | Find Future value given uniform series A | | $(P/A, i, n)$ | Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF) | Find Present value given uniform series A | | $(A/P, i, n)$ | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | Find Annuity A given Present value P | | $(A/F, i, n)$ | Sinking Fund Factor (SFF) | Find Annuity A given Future value F | Where: - $P$ = Present worth (Present Value, PV) - $F$ = Future worth (Future Value, FV) - $A$ = Uniform end-of-period payment (Annuity) - $i$ = Interest rate per interest period (expressed as decimal, e.g., 10% = 0.10) - $n$ = Number of compounding periods (years, months, etc.) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🍕 The Pizza Analogy — "Why ₹100 Today is NOT the Same as ₹100 Next Year"** Imagine your friend owes you **₹100**. They offer you two choices: - **Option A:** Receive ₹100 **today**. - **Option B:** Receive ₹100 **one year from now**. Which one should you pick? Logically, **Option A (today)** — and here's why: - If you receive ₹100 today, you can deposit it in a bank offering 8% interest per year. - After 1 year, that ₹100 grows to **₹108**. - So ₹100 *today* is equivalent to **₹108 *next year*** at 8% interest. - Equivalently, ₹100 *next year* is equivalent to only **₹92.59 today** at 8% discount. **This is Time Value Equivalence:** It establishes a fair exchange rate between money at different points in time, mediated by the interest rate $i$. > [!TIP] > **Engineer's Intuition:** Money is like a *workhorse*. Idle money (sitting in a wallet) produces nothing. Invested money produces a "foal" (interest) every period. Therefore, money in motion (invested) is always worth more than money at rest. --- ## 1.3 Why Engineering Students Must Master This > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Applications of Time Value Equivalence:** > 1. **Equipment Purchase Decisions** — Should a firm buy a ₹50 lakh CNC machine today, or lease it for ₹5 lakh/year for 12 years? > 2. **Comparing Competing Project Alternatives** — Two bridges with different lifespans and cost streams must be converted to a *common time-equivalent value* (e.g., Annual Worth) for fair comparison. > 3. **Loan EMI Calculations** — A home loan of ₹30 lakh at 9% for 20 years is broken into 240 monthly instalments (EMIs) using the CRF formula. > 4. **Public Infrastructure Bidding** — BOT (Build-Operate-Transfer) toll road contracts use TVM to evaluate 30-year revenue streams against today’s construction cost. > 5. **Depreciation & Replacement Analysis** — Deciding when to replace an old machine with a new, more efficient one. All these decisions reduce to: **"Are these different cash flow streams equivalent in time value terms?"** # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Foundational Concepts Behind Time Value Equivalence ### A. Interest — The "Rent" Paid for Using Money **Interest ($I$)** is the economic cost (or reward) of borrowing (or lending) money over time. It is the engine that drives all TVM calculations. $$I = P \cdot i$$ where $P$ is the principal amount borrowed and $i$ is the interest rate **per period**. ### B. Simple Interest vs. Compound Interest > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A very common mistake students make is using **Simple Interest** where **Compound Interest** is required. All Time Value Equivalence formulas in engineering economics use **COMPOUND INTEREST**, unless the problem explicitly says "simple interest". | Feature | Simple Interest | Compound Interest | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Formula | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | $I_n = P \cdot (1+i)^{n-1} \cdot i$ | | Principal | Stays constant | Grows each period | | Interest Earned | Linear in $n$ | Exponential in $n$ | | Used in TVM? | **NO** (except specific cases) | **YES (Always)** | | Total Amount $F$ | $F = P(1 + i \cdot n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | ### C. Cash Flow Diagrams — The Engineer's Time-Map A **Cash Flow Diagram** is a horizontal time line where: - **Upward arrows (↑)** represent **Receipts / Income / Benefits** (positive cash flow). - **Downward arrows (↓)** represent **Disbursements / Costs / Payments** (negative cash flow). - The **horizontal axis** is time, marked in equal periods (0, 1, 2, …, $n$). - **Time 0 = Today (Present)**, Time $n$ = end of period $n$ (Future). > [!TIP] > **Convention Used in KTU:** By default, all uniform series payments $A$ are assumed to be **end-of-period (EOP)** — this is called an **Ordinary Annuity**. If payments are at the **beginning of each period**, it is an **Annuity Due** (multiply the ordinary annuity factor by $(1+i)$). --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet — The Six Master Factors > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these six factors. Every KTU numerical problem on Time Value Equivalence is solved using ONLY these formulas.** Memorize the *meaning* of each symbol, not just the formula. | # | Factor Notation | Formula | Cash Flow Diagram | Plain English Meaning | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{n}$ | $P$ at $t=0$ → $F$ at $t=n$ | "One ₹ today becomes how many ₹ in future?" | | 2 | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | $F$ at $t=n$ → $P$ at $t=0$ | "One ₹ in future is worth how many ₹ today?" | | 3 | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | $A$ at $t=1\ldots n$ → $F$ at $t=n$ | "Save ₹A each period → How much accumulates at $t=n$?" | | 4 | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | $A$ at $t=1\ldots n$ → $P$ at $t=0$ | "Receive ₹A each period for $n$ periods → Equivalent lump sum today?" | | 5 | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | $P$ at $t=0$ → $A$ at $t=1\ldots n$ | "Borrow ₹P today → Equal yearly instalment to repay?" | | 6 | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | $F$ at $t=n$ → $A$ at $t=1\ldots n$ | "Need ₹F in future → Save how much each period?" | ### Reciprocal & Product Relationships (Time-Saving Tricks!) These identities let you derive any factor from any other — **extremely useful in KTU exams**: | Identity | Why It Works | | :---: | :---: | | $(P/F, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(F/P, i, n)}$ | Single payment reversal | | $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(F/A, i, n)}$ | Uniform series reversal | | $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(P/A, i, n)}$ | Present-to-annuity reversal | | $(F/A, i, n) = (F/P, i, n) \cdot (P/A, i, n)$ | Composite equivalence | | $(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n) + i$ | Capital Recovery = Sinking Fund + Interest on Principal | ### Effective Annual Rate (EAR) — When Compounding is More Frequent Than Annually If interest is quoted at nominal rate $i^{(m)}$ compounded $m$ times per year, the **effective annual rate** is: $$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \dfrac{i^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** A bank may advertise "**12% compounded monthly**". This is NOT 12% per year effective — it's actually $(1 + 0.12/12)^{12} - 1 = 12.683\%$. KTU problems often test this conversion. ### Continuous Compounding (Rare in KTU, but appears in Module End) $$F = P \cdot e^{i \cdot n} \quad \text{where } e \approx 2.71828$$ ### Gradient Cash Flows (Arithmetic Gradient $G$) When cash flows increase by a constant amount $G$ each period (e.g., maintenance costs rising by ₹5,000 every year): - $A_{equiv} = A_1 + G \cdot (A/G, i, n)$ - $(A/G, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n \cdot (A/F, i, n)}{1}$ ### Geometric Gradient (Cash flows grow at constant rate $g$) $$P = A_1 \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{i - g}\right] \quad \text{for } i \neq g$$ If $i = g$, the present worth simplifies to $P = \dfrac{n \cdot A_1}{1+i}$. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Decision Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **How Engineers Use These Formulas in Practice:** > - **Capital Recovery (A/P):** Computes the equivalent annual cost of owning a machine. Decision rule: choose the alternative with the **lowest Annual Equivalent Cost**. > - **Sinking Fund (A/F):** Used by utilities to compute how much they must set aside each year to replace a transformer in 15 years. > - **Present Worth (P/A or P):** Used in **Net Present Value (NPV)** analysis for project acceptance. > - **Future Worth (F/A or F):** Used to forecast the value of a recurring savings plan (e.g., a college fund). > - **Gradient (A/G):** Used to model **escalating O&M (Operation & Maintenance) costs** of infrastructure. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$ **Scenario:** You deposit a principal $P$ in an account earning interest rate $i$ per period, compounded. We want to find the future value $F$ at the end of $n$ periods. **Derivation Logic (Period-by-Period Walkthrough):** | Period $k$ | Opening Balance | Interest Earned This Period | Closing Balance | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | $P$ | $P \cdot i$ | $P + P \cdot i = P(1+i)$ | | 2 | $P(1+i)$ | $P(1+i) \cdot i$ | $P(1+i) + P(1+i) \cdot i = P(1+i)^2$ | | 3 | $P(1+i)^2$ | $P(1+i)^2 \cdot i$ | $P(1+i)^3$ | | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | | $k$ | $P(1+i)^{k-1}$ | $P(1+i)^{k-1} \cdot i$ | $P(1+i)^k$ | | $n$ | $P(1+i)^{n-1}$ | $P(1+i)^{n-1} \cdot i$ | $P(1+i)^n$ | **Mathematical Induction Proof:** Assume the period-$k$ closing balance is $P(1+i)^k$. For period $k+1$: $$\text{Opening Balance}_{k+1} = P(1+i)^k$$ $$\text{Interest}_{k+1} = P(1+i)^k \cdot i$$ $$\text{Closing Balance}_{k+1} = P(1+i)^k + P(1+i)^k \cdot i$$ $$\text{Closing Balance}_{k+1} = P(1+i)^k \cdot (1+i) = P(1+i)^{k+1}$$ Hence by induction, for $k = n$: $$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^n \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = P \cdot (F/P, i, n)}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single Payment Present Worth Factor $(P/F, i, n)$ Starting from $F = P(1+i)^n$, divide both sides by $(1+i)^n$: $$P = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^n} = F(1+i)^{-n}$$ $$\boxed{P = F \cdot (P/F, i, n) = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n}}$$ **Verification with Pizza Analogy:** If $F = ₹108$, $i = 0.08$, $n = 1$: $$P = 108 \cdot (1.08)^{-1} = 108 \cdot 0.9259 = ₹99.99 \approx ₹100 \;\checkmark$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i, n)$ **Scenario:** You deposit $A$ at the **end** of each period for $n$ periods. The first deposit earns interest for $(n-1)$ periods, the second for $(n-2)$, …, the last deposit earns **no** interest. **Sum of geometric series:** $$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}$$ Factor out $A$: $$F = A \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^k$$ The right-hand side is a **finite geometric series** with first term $1$, common ratio $(1+i)$, and $n$ terms. Using the standard geometric series formula: $$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \dfrac{r^{n} - 1}{r - 1}$$ Substituting $r = (1+i)$: $$F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$ $$\boxed{F = A \cdot (F/A, i, n) = A \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}\right]}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$ **Method 1 (Two-Step):** Multiply the F/A formula by $(P/F, i, n)$: $$P = F \cdot (1+i)^{-n} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$$ $$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i \cdot (1+i)^{n}}$$ **Method 2 (Direct Sum):** Discount each $A$ to time 0: $$P = A(1+i)^{-1} + A(1+i)^{-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{-n}$$ $$P = A \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} (1+i)^{-k} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{-1}\left[1 - (1+i)^{-n}\right]}{1 - (1+i)^{-1}}$$ After algebraic simplification, both methods yield: $$\boxed{P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n) = A \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}\right]}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Capital Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$ From the $P/A$ formula, isolate $A$: $$A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$ $$\boxed{A = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)}$$ **Engineering Meaning:** This is the equal annual payment needed to **recover the principal $P$** plus interest over $n$ periods. It is the formula used to calculate **home loan EMIs**. **Numerical Example:** Loan = ₹20,00,000, $i$ = 10% per year, $n$ = 20 years: $$A = 20{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^{20}}{(1.10)^{20} - 1}$$ $$(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$$ $$A = 20{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 6.7275}{6.7275 - 1} = 20{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.67275}{5.7275}$$ $$A = 20{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.11746 = ₹2{,}34{,}920 \text{ per year (EMI equivalent)}$$ --- ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i, n)$ From the $F/A$ formula, isolate $A$: $$A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$ $$\boxed{A = F \cdot (A/F, i, n)}$$ **Engineering Meaning:** Equal annual deposit $A$ to accumulate to future value $F$ at the end of $n$ periods. --- ## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Arithmetic Gradient to Annuity Conversion $(A/G, i, n)$ **Scenario:** Cash flows at end of years 1, 2, 3, …, $n$ are $0, G, 2G, 3G, \ldots, (n-1)G$ (gradient series). Convert to an equivalent uniform annuity. The total series can be rewritten as: $$A_{eq} = G \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} (k-1)(1+i)^{-(n-k+1)}$$ After applying the **arithmetic-geometric series sum formula** and simplifying, we obtain the standard KTU formula: $$\boxed{A_{eq} = G \cdot (A/G, i, n) = G \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}\right]}$$ **Note:** The full equivalent annuity is $A_{total} = A_1 + G(A/G, i, n)$ where $A_1$ is the base cash flow in year 1. --- ## 3.8 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Style) ### Example 1 — Present Worth of a Mixed Cash Flow A small engineering firm is considering an investment. The expected cash flows are: - Year 0: −₹5,00,000 (Investment) - Year 1: +₹2,00,000 - Year 2: +₹2,50,000 - Year 3: +₹3,00,000 Find the **Net Present Worth (NPW)** at $i$ = 10% per year. Decide whether the investment is acceptable. **Solution:** $$NPW = -5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000(P/F, 10\%, 1) + 2{,}50{,}000(P/F, 10\%, 2) + 3{,}00{,}000(P/F, 10\%, 3)$$ Compute each $(P/F, 10\%, k) = (1.10)^{-k}$: $$(P/F, 10\%, 1) = \dfrac{1}{1.10} = 0.9091$$ $$(P/F, 10\%, 2) = \dfrac{1}{1.21} = 0.8264$$ $$(P/F, 10\%, 3) = \dfrac{1}{1.331} = 0.7513$$ $$NPW = -5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000(0.9091) + 2{,}50{,}000(0.8264) + 3{,}00{,}000(0.7513)$$ $$NPW = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}81{,}820 + 2{,}06{,}600 + 2{,}25{,}390$$ $$\boxed{NPW = +₹1{,}13{,}810 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT the investment}}$$ **Decision Rule:** If NPW ≥ 0, the project earns at least the MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) and should be selected. --- ### Example 2 — Capital Recovery (EMI Calculation) A startup borrows ₹10,00,000 from a bank at 12% per annum, repayable in equal annual instalments over 5 years. Calculate the annual instalment and the total interest paid. **Solution:** $$A = P \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 5)$$ $$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^5}{(1.12)^5 - 1}$$ $$(1.12)^5 = 1.7623$$ $$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.12 \cdot 1.7623}{0.7623} = \dfrac{0.2115}{0.7623} = 0.2774$$ $$A = 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.2774 = ₹2{,}77{,}410 \text{ per year}$$ Total paid = $2{,}77{,}410 \times 5 = ₹13{,}87{,}050$ Total interest = $13{,}87{,}050 - 10{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{₹3{,}87{,}050}$ --- ### Example 3 — Sinking Fund Depreciation (Equivalent Annual Cost) A machine costs ₹8,00,000 today. Its salvage value after 10 years is ₹1,00,000. If interest is 8% per year, compute the **sinking fund deposit** and the **depreciation charge per year** (assuming straight-line equivalent). **Solution:** Depreciable amount = $8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = ₹7{,}00{,}000$ **Sinking Fund Factor:** $$(A/F, 8\%, 10) = \dfrac{0.08}{(1.08)^{10} - 1}$$ $$(1.08)^{10} = 2.1589$$ $$(A/F, 8\%, 10) = \dfrac{0.08}{1.1589} = 0.06903$$ $$A_{SF} = 7{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.06903 = ₹48{,}321 \text{ per year}$$ This is the amount that, deposited at 8% interest for 10 years, will accumulate to ₹7,00,000 (the depreciable value). # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Cash Flow Diagram — Ordinary Annuity vs Annuity Due ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph OAN["ORDINARY ANNUITY - Payments at End of Period"] T0A["Time 0"]:::down T1A["Year 1
A received"]:::up T2A["Year 2
A received"]:::up T3A["Year n
A received"]:::up T0A ~~~ T1A ~~~ T2A ~~~ T3A end subgraph ADU["ANNUITY DUE - Payments at Start of Period"] T0B["Time 0
A received"]:::up T1B["Year 1
A received"]:::up T2B["Year 2
A received"]:::up T3B["Year n-1
A received"]:::up T0B ~~~ T1B ~~~ T2B ~~~ T3B end classDef up fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef down fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show the position of payment $A$ on the time axis. In an **ordinary annuity** (the KTU default), the first $A$ occurs at $t=1$; in an **annuity due**, the first $A$ occurs at $t=0$. To convert ordinary annuity factors to annuity due, multiply by $(1+i)$. --- ## 4.2 Equivalence Engine — Block-Level Functional Architecture The diagram below maps how the **six master factors** form a closed equivalence network. Any cash flow at any time can be converted to any other cash flow at any other time by chaining the appropriate factors. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CASH["UNIFIED CASH FLOW DOMAIN"] direction LR P["P
PRESENT
WORTH
at t = 0"]:::nodeP A["A
UNIFORM
SERIES
at t = 1 to n"]:::nodeA F["F
FUTURE
WORTH
at t = n"]:::nodeF end P <-->|"F over P equals one plus i to the n"| F P <-->|"A over P equals i times one plus i to n over one plus i to n minus 1"| A A <-->|"F over A equals one plus i to n minus 1 over i"| F classDef nodeP fill:#cfc,stroke:#060,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef nodeA fill:#ccf,stroke:#006,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef nodeF fill:#fcc,stroke:#600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **How to use this in an exam:** Identify the **known cash flow** and the **unknown cash flow**. Choose the factor whose notation matches this pair. For example, "known $A$, unknown $P$" → use $(P/A, i, n)$. Multiply: $P = A \times (P/A, i, n)$. --- ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which Factor Should I Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"What do you KNOW?"}:::ask Q2{"What do you WANT to find?"}:::ask R1["Use F over P factor
F = P times one plus i to n"]:::result R2["Use P over F factor
P = F times one plus i to the minus n"]:::result R3["Use F over A factor
F = A times F over A formula"]:::result R4["Use P over A factor
P = A times P over A formula"]:::result R5["Use A over P factor
A = P times A over P formula"]:::result R6["Use A over F factor
A = F times A over F formula"]:::result Q1 -->|"P present worth"| P1["Choose P branch"] Q1 -->|"A annuity"| A1["Choose A branch"] Q1 -->|"F future worth"| F1["Choose F branch"] P1 --> Q2 A1 --> Q2 F1 --> Q2 Q2 -->|"Find F"| R1 Q2 -->|"Find A"| R5 Q2 -->|"Find P"| R2 A1 -->|"Find F"| R3 A1 -->|"Find P"| R4 F1 -->|"Find P"| R2 F1 -->|"Find A"| R6 classDef ask fill:#ffe,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#dfd,stroke:#080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Gradient Cash Flow — Schematic Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR T0["t = 0
0"]:::zero T1["t = 1
G"]:::g1 T2["t = 2
2G"]:::g2 T3["t = 3
3G"]:::g3 Tn["t = n
n-1 times G"]:::gn T0 ~~~ T1 ~~~ T2 ~~~ T3 ~~~ Tn classDef zero fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef g1 fill:#fdd,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef g2 fill:#fbb,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef g3 fill:#f99,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gn fill:#f66,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** In an arithmetic gradient, the cash flow in period $k$ is $(k-1) \cdot G$. The equivalent uniform annuity is $A_{eq} = G \cdot (A/G, i, n)$. If there is also a base amount $A_1$ in period 1, the total annuity is $A_1 + A_{eq}$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are concise, definition-based, or formula-statement questions. Answers should be **precise, in bullet form, and include the governing equation**. --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"Define the Time Value of Money. Why is a rupee received today worth more than a rupee received in the future?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the financial principle that the value of a sum of money changes with time due to its earning potential. A rupee received today is worth more than a rupee received in the future because: 1. **Investment Opportunity:** Today's rupee can be invested to earn interest, so $₹1$ today is equivalent to $₹(1+i)^n$ at the end of $n$ periods. 2. **Inflation Erosion:** The purchasing power of money decreases over time due to general price level increases. 3. **Risk & Uncertainty:** Future receipts carry the risk of default or non-receipt; immediate receipt eliminates this risk. 4. **Consumption Preference:** Individuals generally prefer present consumption over future consumption (positive time preference rate). Mathematically: $F = P(1+i)^n > P$ for $i > 0$, confirming the present is always more valuable. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023] **"Distinguish between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. Which one is used in engineering economic analysis and why?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Simple Interest | Compound Interest | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Formula | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | | Principal | Constant over time | Grows period-by-period (interest earns interest) | | Total Interest Growth | Linear with $n$ | Exponential with $n$ | | Final Amount | $F = P(1 + in)$ | $F = P(1+i)^n$ | **Engineering economic analysis uses COMPOUND INTEREST** because: - Real-world financial systems (banks, bonds, loans) charge/credit interest on accumulated balance, not original principal. - It accurately reflects the **"interest-on-interest"** effect (the time value of previously earned interest). - It is the only mathematically consistent method for comparing cash flows across multiple time periods using a single discount rate. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts, typically (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. KTU follows **internal choice** — students answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B. Both are provided below. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Numerical on All Six Factors **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** A construction company is evaluating a small bridge project. The following cash flows are estimated at an interest rate of **12% per year**: - Initial investment at $t=0$: **₹5,00,000** (outflow) - Annual revenue (end of years 1 through 5): **₹1,50,000** per year - Salvage value at end of year 5: **₹80,000** - Annual operating cost (end of years 1 through 5): **₹40,000** per year **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **Net Present Worth (NPW)** of the project. State whether the project is **economically viable** at MARR = 12%. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** Convert the entire cash flow stream into an **equivalent uniform annual worth (AW)** and re-verify your decision. Comment on the equivalence of the two methods. --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Net Present Worth Calculation:** Step 1: Identify the **net uniform annual cash flow** $A$: $$A_{net} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Operating Cost} = 1{,}50{,}000 - 40{,}000 = ₹1{,}10{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ Step 2: Compute the relevant factors at $i = 12\%$, $n = 5$: $$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.12)^5 - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^5}$$ $$(1.12)^5 = 1.7623$$ $$(P/A, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{1.7623 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 1.7623} = \dfrac{0.7623}{0.2115} = 3.6048$$ $$(P/F, 12\%, 5) = (1.12)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.7623} = 0.5674$$ Step 3: Compute Present Worth of each cash flow component: **Present Worth of Net Annuity Revenue (Years 1–5):** $$PW_{annuity} = A_{net} \cdot (P/A, 12\%, 5) = 1{,}10{,}000 \cdot 3.6048 = ₹3{,}96{,}528$$ **Present Worth of Salvage Value (Year 5):** $$PW_{salvage} = S \cdot (P/F, 12\%, 5) = 80{,}000 \cdot 0.5674 = ₹45{,}392$$ **Present Worth of Initial Investment (Year 0):** $$PW_{initial} = -5{,}00{,}000$$ Step 4: Compute **Net Present Worth**: $$NPW = PW_{initial} + PW_{annuity} + PW_{salvage}$$ $$NPW = -5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}96{,}528 + 45{,}392$$ $$\boxed{NPW = -₹58{,}080 \approx -₹58{,}000}$$ **Decision:** Since **NPW < 0**, the project does NOT earn the MARR of 12%. **REJECT the project** on a present worth basis. --- **Part (b) — Equivalent Annual Worth (AW) Verification:** Step 1: Convert all cash flows to end-of-year equivalent annuity. **Convert Initial Investment $P$ to Annual Equivalent using $(A/P, 12\%, 5)$:** $$(A/P, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.12 \cdot 1.7623}{1.7623 - 1} = \dfrac{0.2115}{0.7623} = 0.2774$$ $$A_{initial} = 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.2774 = ₹1{,}38{,}700 \text{ per year (cost equivalent)}$$ **Convert Salvage Value $S$ to Annual Equivalent using $(A/F, 12\%, 5)$:** $$(A/F, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.12}{1.7623 - 1} = \dfrac{0.12}{0.7623} = 0.1574$$ $$A_{salvage} = 80{,}000 \cdot 0.1574 = ₹12{,}592 \text{ per year (revenue equivalent)}$$ Step 2: Compute **Net Annual Worth (AW)**: $$AW = -A_{initial} + A_{net} + A_{salvage}$$ $$AW = -1{,}38{,}700 + 1{,}10{,}000 + 12{,}592$$ $$\boxed{AW = -₹16{,}108 \text{ per year}}$$ **Decision:** Since **AW < 0**, the project is **uneconomical**. **REJECT.** **Equivalence Verification:** $AW$ and $NPW$ should be related by $AW = NPW \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 5)$: $$AW_{theoretical} = -58{,}080 \cdot 0.2774 = -₹16{,}107 \text{ per year} \;\checkmark$$ The small rounding difference (₹16,107 vs ₹16,108) is due to 4-decimal rounding of factors. **Conclusion:** Both methods yield the same decision — **REJECT the project**. Time value equivalence is confirmed. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Loan Amortization & Sinking Fund Problem **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Paper]** An engineering graduate takes an education loan of **₹15,00,000** at an interest rate of **10% per year compounded annually**. The loan must be repaid in **6 equal annual instalments** at the end of each year. **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the **annual instalment (EMI equivalent)** and prepare a **year-wise loan amortization schedule** showing principal repayment and interest component for each year. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the graduate wants to repay the entire loan in a **lump sum at the end of year 6** using a sinking fund that earns **8% per year**, what equal annual deposit must be made into the sinking fund at the end of years 1 through 6? Compare the total out-of-pocket expense under both repayment methods. --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Annual Instalment & Amortization Schedule:** Step 1: Calculate the annual instalment $A$ using the **Capital Recovery Factor**: $$A = P \cdot (A/P, 10\%, 6)$$ $$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.10(1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1}$$ $$(1.10)^6 = 1.7716$$ $$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.10 \cdot 1.7716}{0.7716} = \dfrac{0.17716}{0.7716} = 0.22961$$ $$A = 15{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.22961 = ₹3{,}44{,}415 \text{ per year}$$ Step 2: **Amortization Schedule** (Principal begins at ₹15,00,000): | Year $k$ | Opening Balance (₹) | Interest @ 10% (₹) | Instalment $A$ (₹) | Principal Repaid (₹) | Closing Balance (₹) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 15,00,000 | 1,50,000 | 3,44,415 | 1,94,415 | 13,05,585 | | 2 | 13,05,585 | 1,30,559 | 3,44,415 | 2,13,856 | 10,91,729 | | 3 | 10,91,729 | 1,09,173 | 3,44,415 | 2,35,242 | 8,56,487 | | 4 | 8,56,487 | 85,649 | 3,44,415 | 2,58,766 | 5,97,721 | | 5 | 5,97,721 | 59,772 | 3,44,415 | 2,84,643 | 3,13,078 | | 6 | 3,13,078 | 31,308 | 3,44,415* | 3,13,107 ≈ 3,13,078 | 0 | *Note: Year 6 interest is adjusted by ~₹29 to zero out the balance due to rounding. In practice, the last instalment is slightly lower. **Total Paid under Method 1** = $3{,}44{,}415 \times 6 = ₹20{,}66{,}490$ **Total Interest Paid** = $20{,}66{,}490 - 15{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{₹5{,}66{,}490}$ --- **Part (b) — Sinking Fund Method:** In this method, the graduate only pays the **annual interest on the loan** ($i \cdot P = 10\% \cdot 15{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}50{,}000$) every year, AND simultaneously deposits a sum $D$ into a **sinking fund** earning 8% per year. At the end of year 6, the sinking fund balance must equal the loan principal ₹15,00,000, which is then used to repay the loan in full. Step 1: Compute the **sinking fund deposit** $D$ using $(A/F, 8\%, 6)$: $$(A/F, 8\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.08}{(1.08)^6 - 1}$$ $$(1.08)^6 = 1.5869$$ $$(A/F, 8\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.08}{0.5869} = 0.13632$$ $$D = 15{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.13632 = ₹2{,}04{,}480 \text{ per year}$$ Step 2: Compute the **total annual out-of-pocket expense** for years 1 through 5 (during the sinking fund period): $$\text{Annual Outflow} = \text{Interest on Loan} + \text{Sinking Fund Deposit}$$ $$\text{Annual Outflow} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}04{,}480 = ₹3{,}54{,}480 \text{ per year}$$ **Year 6 Outflow:** Only the interest ₹1,50,000 (sinking fund balance of ₹15,00,000 repays the principal, so no extra deposit is needed; only the interest is paid out of pocket). Step 3: Compute the **total out-of-pocket expense** over 6 years: $$\text{Total Outflow} = (3{,}54{,}480 \times 5) + 1{,}50{,}000$$ $$\text{Total Outflow} = 17{,}72{,}400 + 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹19{,}22{,}400$$ **Comparison of the Two Methods:** | Method | Total Outflow (₹) | Total Interest Paid (₹) | Savings (₹) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1. Direct Amortization at 10% | 20,66,490 | 5,66,490 | — | | 2. Sinking Fund (8%) + Loan Interest (10%) | 19,22,400 | 4,22,400 | 1,44,090 | **Conclusion:** The **Sinking Fund Method saves ₹1,44,090** because the sinking fund earns 8% interest on accumulated deposits, which reduces the effective interest burden. **However**, the sinking fund method requires strict discipline — the borrower must faithfully deposit ₹2,04,480 every year for 6 years into a separate fund. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing ordinary annuity with annuity due** — If the problem says "beginning of each year", you MUST multiply the ordinary annuity factor by $(1+i)$. Forgetting this loses **2–3 marks** per question. > 2. **Mis-identifying $n$** — A common trap: a "5-year loan repaid monthly" means $n = 60$ (months) and $i = $ annual rate / 12. Students often use $n=5$ and lose full marks. > 3. **Ignoring the salvage value in NPW** — Always read the problem statement completely. Salvage $S$ is a Year-$n$ cash inflow, discounted via $(P/F, i, n)$. > 4. **Not writing the formula before substituting numbers** — KTU valuation key demands: **(Formula) → (Substitution) → (Answer)** in that order. Skipping the formula step costs 1 mark. > 5. **Rounding too early** — Round only the final answer. Intermediate steps should carry 5–6 decimal places to avoid compounding error. > 6. **Sign errors in cash flow direction** — Money received is **positive (+)**, money paid is **negative (−)**. Mixing these up flips the sign of NPW and changes the decision. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Use this as a 5-minute pre-exam revision pass:** - **TVM Core Truth:** Money today > Money tomorrow (because of earning potential + inflation + risk). - **Six Master Factors:** $(F/P)$, $(P/F)$, $(F/A)$, $(P/A)$, $(A/P)$, $(A/F)$ — **know all six formulas cold**. - **Cash Flow Direction:** - $P$ lives at $t=0$ - $A$ lives at $t=1 \ldots n$ (ordinary annuity, **end-of-period**) - $F$ lives at $t=n$ - **Reciprocal Pairs:** $(P/F) = 1/(F/P)$ and $(A/F) = 1/(F/A)$ and $(A/P) = 1/(P/A)$. - **Capital Recovery Identity:** $(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n) + i$ — extremely useful for cross-checking. - **Sinking Fund Logic:** Deposit $A$ at the end of each period → grows to $F$ at the end of $n$ periods. - **NPW Decision Rule:** $NPW \geq 0$ → Accept; $NPW < 0$ → Reject. - **AW Decision Rule:** $AW \geq 0$ → Accept; $AW < 0$ → Reject. (NPW and AW always give the **same decision**.) - **Effective vs Nominal Rate:** When compounding is $m$ times per year, $i_{eff} = (1 + i^{(m)}/m)^m - 1$. - **Continuous Compounding:** $F = P \cdot e^{in}$ (rare in KTU, but mentioned in module). - **Arithmetic Gradient:** Cash flows $0, G, 2G, \ldots, (n-1)G$. Convert via $(A/G, i, n)$. - **Geometric Gradient:** Cash flows grow at constant rate $g$. Formula $P = A_1 \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^n}{i - g}\right]$. - **Annuity Due Adjustment:** Multiply the ordinary annuity factor by $(1+i)$. - **Loan Amortization Pattern:** Year-1 interest is highest; principal repayment grows each year; closing balance converges to 0 at year $n$. - **Exam-Answer Format:** State the chosen factor → write formula → substitute values → compute → state answer with units. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use **simple interest** in TVM problems unless explicitly asked. > [!NOTE] > **Final Word:** Time Value Equivalence is the **single most-tested topic** in Engineering Economics across all KTU semesters and competitive exams like GATE (Engineering Sciences). Master these six factors and the cash flow diagram, and you can solve 80% of numerical problems by simply identifying the right factor and substituting. The remaining 20% are creative applications (gradients, multiple alternatives, capitalized cost) — but they all build on this foundation.

topics.title: Cash Flow Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cash Flow Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cash Flow Diagram (CFD):** A Cash Flow Diagram is a **graphical representation of the timing, direction, and magnitude of monetary transactions (receipts and disbursements) occurring at discrete points in time over the planning horizon of an engineering project.** It is a horizontal time-axis (abscissa) schematic where each vertical arrow represents a cash flow, with the **length of the arrow proportional to the magnitude** of the cash flow.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education, the cash flow diagram is the **foundational visual tool** of Module 4 (Engineering Economic Analysis) because it converts a verbal problem statement of an investment, loan, or project into a single, unambiguous picture that any financial analyst can interpret identically.

## 1.2 Components of a Cash Flow Diagram

A cash flow diagram has exactly **three mandatory graphical elements**:

| Element | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Axis | $\longrightarrow$ | Time line, marked in **years, months, or periods** ($t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$) |
| Upward Arrow ($\uparrow$) | Receipt / Income / Benefit | Cash **inflow** (cash received, revenue, salvage value) |
| Downward Arrow ($\downarrow$) | Expenditure / Cost | Cash **outflow** (investment, loan repayment, operating cost) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention (S-P Convention):** The KTU 2024 syllabus follows the standard **S-P convention** where the **point of view of the investor / analyst** is adopted. Cash received is plotted **upward (positive, +)** and cash paid is plotted **downward (negative, −)**. This is identical to the convention used by **Blank & Tarquin** and **Park's Contemporary Engineering Economics**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a young engineer, *Rahul*, who has just joined a company and decides to take a **home loan of ₹30,00,000** for 20 years at a fixed interest rate. Every month, his bank sends him a statement that says: *"Today, we gave you ₹30,00,000"* and *"Today, you paid us back ₹X as EMI."* If Rahul tries to read 240 months of such statements in tabular form, he will be **overwhelmed**. 

A **Cash Flow Diagram is the visual equivalent of that statement**. It compresses the entire 20-year loan history into a single picture: one giant upward arrow at $t=0$ (the loan he received) and 240 small downward arrows (the EMIs he pays). Once the diagram is drawn, the math of *present worth*, *future worth*, and *equivalent annual cost* can be applied almost mechanically.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important in engineering?** Civil engineers size a bridge's life-cycle cost, mechanical engineers compare two pump alternatives over 10 years, and computer engineers evaluate a data centre's cloud-vs-on-premise TCO (Total Cost of Ownership). In *every* case, the **first analytical step is to draw a cash flow diagram** before any formula is touched.

## 1.4 Cash Flow Vocabulary (Standard KTU Glossary)

- **P** $\rightarrow$ Present Worth (a single cash flow occurring at $t=0$).
- **F** $\rightarrow$ Future Worth (a single cash flow occurring at $t=n$).
- **A** $\rightarrow$ Annuity / Uniform Series (an identical cash flow occurring every period, $t=1$ to $t=n$).
- **G** $\rightarrow$ Arithmetic Gradient (a series whose values increase by a constant amount $G$ each period).
- **g** $\rightarrow$ Geometric Gradient (a series whose values increase by a constant percentage each period).
- **i** $\rightarrow$ Interest rate per period (expressed as a decimal, e.g., $i = 0.10$ for 10%).
- **n** $\rightarrow$ Number of compounding periods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Drawing a Cash Flow Diagram

The cash flow diagram is not decorative. It performs **three engineering-economic functions** simultaneously:

1. **Disambiguates the time frame** — distinguishes between $t=0$ (now), intermediate periods, and the planning horizon $t=n$.
2. **Disambiguates the direction of money** — clearly shows whether the analyst is receiving or paying money.
3. **Disambiguates the magnitude** — the length of each arrow is **proportional to the rupee/dollar value**, allowing instant comparison between alternatives.

## 2.2 Rules for Drawing a Cash Flow Diagram (KTU Board Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Golden Rules for Cash Flow Diagrams (Board Examiner's Checklist):**
> 1. The **time axis is a solid horizontal line** divided into equal intervals corresponding to the interest period.
> 2. Arrows are **vertical** and **drawn at the end of each period** (not the beginning), unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 3. **Upward arrow = cash inflow (receipt)**; **downward arrow = cash outflow (payment)**.
> 4. The **length of the arrow must be proportional to the magnitude** of the cash flow.
> 5. **$t=0$ is "now"** (the present moment of decision making) and is placed at the **left end** of the diagram.
> 6. All other time periods are labelled as $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

## 2.3 Classification of Cash Flows

| Type | Notation | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Payment | $P$ or $F$ | One cash flow at a single point in time | Buying equipment today for ₹5,00,000 |
| Uniform Series | $A$ | Equal cash flows every period for $n$ periods | EMI of ₹25,000 per month for 5 years |
| Arithmetic Gradient | $G$ | Series that increases by a constant $G$ each period | Maintenance cost rising by ₹1,000 every year |
| Geometric Gradient | $g$ | Series that increases by a constant percentage $g$ each period | Electricity tariff rising 5% per year |
| Mixed / Irregular | — | A non-uniform combination of the above | Real-world corporate projects |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Core Engineering Economy Factors (Read these in pairs to derive them).** Below is the complete cheat-sheet you will need across all cash-flow-based problems in KTU ESE. Notation: $(X \vert Y, i\%, n)$ means "find $X$ given $Y$."

| # | Factor Name | Symbol | Formula | Use When You Know |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F \vert P, i, n)$ | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | $P$, want $F$ |
| 2 | Single Payment Present Worth | $(P \vert F, i, n)$ | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | $F$, want $P$ |
| 3 | Uniform Series Sinking Fund | $(A \vert F, i, n)$ | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $F$, want $A$ |
| 4 | Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F \vert A, i, n)$ | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | $A$, want $F$ |
| 5 | Capital Recovery | $(A \vert P, i, n)$ | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $P$, want $A$ |
| 6 | Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P \vert A, i, n)$ | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | $A$, want $P$ |

### Gradient Conversion Factor Table

| # | Factor | Symbol | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | Present Worth of Arithmetic Gradient | $(P \vert G, i, n)$ | $P = G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ |
| 8 | Annual Equivalent of Arithmetic Gradient | $(A \vert G, i, n)$ | $A = G \cdot \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ |
| 9 | Present Worth of Geometric Gradient | $(P \vert A_1, g, i, n)$ | $P = A_1 \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left(\frac{1+g}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{i - g}$ (when $i \neq g$) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic:** "**PIG FARM**" — **P**resent worth, **I**nterest, **G**radient / **F**uture, **A**nnuity, **R**ecovery, **M**ixed. The six core factors map directly onto these letters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Civil Engineering | Comparing two bridge designs over a 50-year life cycle |
| Mechanical Engineering | Pump A vs Pump B — purchase cost vs operating cost trade-off |
| Electrical Engineering | Solar PV plant — high $P$, low $A$ vs diesel generator — low $P$, high $A$ |
| Computer Science | Build vs buy decision for a server farm; cloud cost analysis |
| Industrial Engineering | Break-even analysis between manual and automated production |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Single Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F \vert P, i, n)$

**Statement:** A sum of money $P$ invested at the end of period $t=0$ for $n$ periods at an interest rate $i$ per period will accumulate to a future sum $F$ at $t=n$.

**Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation:**

Let $P$ be the principal at $t = 0$. At the end of period 1, the interest earned is $P \cdot i$. The total amount at $t=1$ is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Amount at } t=1 &= P + P \cdot i = P(1+i) \\
\text{Amount at } t=2 &= P(1+i) + P(1+i)\cdot i = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2} \\
\text{Amount at } t=3 &= P(1+i)^{2}(1+i) = P(1+i)^{3} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\text{Amount at } t=n &= P(1+i)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **future worth** is:

$$
F = P(1+i)^{n}
$$

This is the **Single Payment Compound Amount Factor (SPCAF)**, and $(1+i)^{n}$ is the single-payment compound-amount factor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Single Payment Present Worth Factor $(P \vert F, i, n)$

**Statement:** Find the present equivalent of a single future cash flow $F$ occurring at $t = n$.

Starting from the previous result and dividing both sides by $(1+i)^{n}$:

$$
P = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^{n}} = F(1+i)^{-n}
$$

The factor $(1+i)^{-n}$ is the **Single Payment Present Worth Factor (SPPWF)**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Uniform Series Future Worth $(F \vert A, i, n)$

**Statement:** An annuity $A$ deposited at the **end** of each of $n$ periods will accumulate to what future value $F$ at $t = n$?

Consider each $A$ as an individual $P$ and move it forward to $t = n$ using the SPCAF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \ldots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0} \\
F &= A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = 1$ (when $k=0$), common ratio $r = (1+i)$, and $n$ terms. Using the standard formula for a geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^{k} = \dfrac{r^{n}-1}{r-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} \\
F &= A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ is the **Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor (USCAF)**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Uniform Series Present Worth $(P \vert A, i, n)$

Multiply the USCAF result by the SPPWF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F(1+i)^{-n} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i} \cdot (1+i)^{-n} \\
P &= A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Capital Recovery Factor $(A \vert P, i, n)$

Multiplying $P$ by the factor $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ gives the equivalent uniform annual amount $A$:

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}
$$

The fraction $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ is the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**, denoted as $(A/P, i, n)$.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Sinking Fund Factor $(A \vert F, i, n)$

From $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$, solving for $A$:

$$
A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}
$$

The factor $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ is the **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**, denoted as $(A/F, i, n)$.

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Arithmetic Gradient Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** An arithmetic gradient series starts at $0$ at $t=1$, equals $G$ at $t=2$, $2G$ at $t=3$, and equals $(n-1)G$ at $t=n$. It can be decomposed into a **uniform series of $G$** (from $t=2$ to $t=n$) plus a base annuity that we handle separately.

The present worth of the gradient is given by:

$$
P = G \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}
$$

The annual equivalent is:

$$
A = G \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n}-1}\right]
$$

**Analytical Insight:** When $i = 0$, the formula degenerates and the present worth of a simple linear gradient is $P = G \cdot \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$, which is exactly the sum of the first $n-1$ integers.

## 3.8 Numerical Worked Example: Loan Repayment

**Problem:** A mechanical engineer borrows $P = \text{₹}5,00,000$ at $i = 12\%$ per year for $n = 5$ years. Calculate the equivalent uniform annual payment $A$.

**Solution using formulas:**

$$
A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} = 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^{5}}{(1.12)^{5}-1}
$$

Compute $(1.12)^{5} = 1.7623416\ldots$:

$$
A = 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.7623}{1.7623 - 1} = 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{0.21147}{0.76234}
$$

$$
A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.27741 \approx \text{₹}1{,}38{,}705
$$

So the engineer pays approximately **₹1,38,705 per year** for 5 years to clear the loan.

**Verification using present worth:**

$$
P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = 1{,}38{,}705 \times \dfrac{0.7623}{0.12 \times 1.7623} = 1{,}38{,}705 \times 3.6048 \approx \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000 \;\checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Cash Flow Diagram — Notation Map

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The figure below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the cash flow diagram, mapping how each algebraic symbol $P, A, F, G$ is placed on the time axis. This serves as the master schematic you should reproduce for any KTU 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXIS[TIME AXIS t = 0 to n]
        direction LR
        T0["t = 0 NOW"]
        T1["t = 1"]
        T2["t = 2"]
        T3["t = 3"]
        Tn["t = n END"]
    end

    P_ARROW(["P upward arrow at t=0 = Present Worth"]) -.-> T0
    A_ARROW(["A series of equal arrows from t=1 to t=n = Annuity"]) -.-> T1
    A_ARROW -.-> T2
    A_ARROW -.-> T3
    A_ARROW -.-> Tn
    F_ARROW(["F upward arrow at t=n = Future Worth"]) -.-> Tn
    G_ARROW(["G gradient series growing 0, G, 2G ... n-1 times G"]) -.-> T1
    G_ARROW -.-> T2
    G_ARROW -.-> T3
    G_ARROW -.-> Tn

    style T0 fill:#fff5b1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Tn fill:#ffd5b1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P_ARROW fill:#d0f0d0,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style A_ARROW fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    style F_ARROW fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#900,color:#000
    style G_ARROW fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#609,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Cash Flow Diagram for a Loan Problem (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAN["LOAN CASH FLOW DIAGRAM (Investor's View)"]
        direction LR
        t0a["t = 0"]:::timenode
        t1a["t = 1"]:::timenode
        t2a["t = 2"]:::timenode
        tna["t = n"]:::timenode

        PUP(["P up = loan received"]):::inflow
        ADN(["A down each period = EMI paid"]):::outflow
        ADN2(["A down each period = EMI paid"]):::outflow
        ADN3(["A down each period = EMI paid"]):::outflow
    end

    PUP --- t0a
    ADN --- t1a
    ADN2 --- t2a
    ADN3 --- tna

    classDef timenode fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333,color:#000;
    classDef inflow fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000;
    classDef outflow fill:#ea9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000;
```

## 4.3 Cash Flow Diagram for an Equipment Purchase vs Annual Maintenance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EQUIP["EQUIPMENT PURCHASE WITH GRADIENT MAINTENANCE"]
        direction LR
        e0["t = 0"]:::timenode
        e1["t = 1"]:::timenode
        e2["t = 2"]:::timenode
        e3["t = 3"]:::timenode
        en["t = n"]:::timenode

        PDOWN(["P down = Purchase cost"]):::outflow
        M1(["M1 down at t=1 = year 1 maintenance"]):::outflow
        M2(["M2 down at t=2 = M1 + G"]):::outflow
        M3(["M3 down at t=3 = M1 + 2G"]):::outflow
        SV(["SV up at t=n = Salvage value"]):::inflow
    end

    PDOWN --- e0
    M1 --- e1
    M2 --- e2
    M3 --- e3
    SV --- en

    classDef timenode fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333,color:#000;
    classDef inflow fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000;
    classDef outflow fill:#ea9999,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000;
```

## 4.4 Decision-Tree: Which Cash Flow Factor to Apply

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start: Identify the unknowns and knowns"]:::root
    A1["Is there a single cash flow at t=0 or t=n?"]:::q
    A2["Use P-to-F or F-to-P factor (SPCAF / SPPWF)"]:::sol
    A3["Is there a uniform series of equal cash flows?"]:::q
    A4["Use A-to-F, A-to-P, P-to-A or F-to-A (USCAF / USPWF / CRF / SFF)"]:::sol
    A5["Does the series increase by a constant G each period?"]:::q
    A6["Use Arithmetic Gradient Formulas (P/G or A/G)"]:::sol
    A7["Does the series grow by a constant percentage g?"]:::q
    A8["Use Geometric Gradient Formula (valid only if i ≠ g)"]:::sol
    A9["Is the problem a mix of single + uniform + gradient?"]:::q
    A10["Decompose into components, find PW of each, then sum"]:::sol

    A0 --> A1
    A1 -- Yes --> A2
    A1 -- No --> A3
    A3 -- Yes --> A4
    A3 -- No --> A5
    A5 -- Yes --> A6
    A5 -- No --> A7
    A7 -- Yes --> A8
    A7 -- No --> A9
    A9 -- Yes --> A10

    classDef root fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,color:#000;
    classDef q fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#06c,color:#000;
    classDef sol fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000;
```
