<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Cash Flow Diagram. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A Cash Flow Diagram is a graphical representation of the timing, direction, and magnitude of all cash receipts and disbursements over the life of an engineering project. Its main components are:

1. **Horizontal time axis** marked in equal intervals (years/months).
2. **Upward arrows ($\uparrow$)** representing cash inflows (receipts / income).
3. **Downward arrows ($\downarrow$)** representing cash outflows (payments / costs).
4. The **length of each arrow is proportional to the magnitude** of the cash flow.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the symbols P, F, A, and G used in cash flow analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Position on CFD |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Present Worth | Single arrow at $t=0$ |
| $F$ | Future Worth | Single arrow at $t=n$ |
| $A$ | Annuity (Uniform Series) | Equal arrows at $t=1, 2, \ldots, n$ |
| $G$ | Arithmetic Gradient | Series increasing by constant $G$ each period, starting at 0 at $t=1$ |

---

## PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Convention:** Within a single Part B question, internal choice is offered only between distinct OR alternatives. The first 7 marks test diagram-construction and identification; the second 7 marks test numerical conversion between factors.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**a) Draw the Cash Flow Diagram for the following scenario. (7 Marks)**

> An engineering firm buys a CNC machine for **₹8,00,000** at $t=0$. It earns an annual revenue of **₹3,00,000** for 5 years. Annual maintenance cost is **₹50,000** in year 1 and increases by **₹10,000** every year. The machine has a salvage value of **₹1,00,000** at the end of year 5. Assume $i = 10\%$.

**Model Answer:**

The cash flow diagram (analytical schematic) has:

- $t=0$: Downward arrow of ₹8,00,000 (purchase).
- $t=1$ to $t=5$: Upward arrow of ₹3,00,000 each year (revenue).
- $t=1$: Downward arrow of ₹50,000 (year 1 maintenance).
- $t=2$: Downward arrow of ₹60,000 (50,000 + 10,000).
- $t=3$: Downward arrow of ₹70,000.
- $t=4$: Downward arrow of ₹80,000.
- $t=5$: Downward arrow of ₹90,000.
- $t=5$: Upward arrow of ₹1,00,000 (salvage value).

**b) Compute the Net Present Worth of the project at $i = 10\%$. State whether the investment is economically justified. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution):**

Let the present be $t=0$. The interest rate is $i = 0.10$.

**Step 1 — Present Worth of the Purchase Cost:**
$$
P_{\text{purchase}} = -8{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth of the Revenue Annuity (₹3,00,000 per year for 5 years):**
$$
P_{\text{rev}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 5)
$$
$$
(P/A, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.10)^{5}-1}{0.10(1.10)^{5}} = \dfrac{1.61051-1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} = \dfrac{0.61051}{0.161051} = 3.7908
$$
$$
P_{\text{rev}} = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 = \text{₹}11{,}37{,}232
$$

**Step 3 — Present Worth of the Maintenance Gradient:**
The maintenance is ₹50,000 (year 1) + arithmetic gradient $G = 10,000$. Using the equivalent uniform annual worth decomposition:
$$
A_{\text{maint}} = 50{,}000 + 10{,}000 \cdot (A/G, 10\%, 5)
$$
$$
(A/G, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{1}{0.10} - \dfrac{5}{(1.10)^{5}-1} = 10 - \dfrac{5}{0.61051} = 10 - 8.1903 = 1.8097
$$
$$
A_{\text{maint}} = 50{,}000 + 10{,}000 \times 1.8097 = 50{,}000 + 18{,}097 = \text{₹}68{,}097
$$
$$
P_{\text{maint}} = 68{,}097 \times (P/A, 10\%, 5) = 68{,}097 \times 3.7908 = \text{₹}2{,}58{,}103
$$

**Step 4 — Present Worth of the Salvage Value:**
$$
P_{\text{salvage}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 5) = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.10)^{-5}
$$
$$
(1.10)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.61051} = 0.6209
$$
$$
P_{\text{salvage}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6209 = \text{₹}62{,}092
$$

**Step 5 — Net Present Worth:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPW} &= P_{\text{rev}} - P_{\text{purchase}} - P_{\text{maint}} + P_{\text{salvage}} \\
&= 11{,}37{,}232 - 8{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}58{,}103 + 62{,}092 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}41{,}221
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Since $\text{NPW} = +\text{₹}1{,}41{,}221 > 0$, the project is **economically justified** at $i = 10\%$.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]` — **OR ALTERNATIVE**

**a) A civil engineering contractor has two payment options for a road project costing ₹50,00,000. (7 Marks)**

> **Option 1:** Pay ₹50,00,000 at the end of year 5 (single future payment).
> **Option 2:** Pay ₹12,00,000 at the end of every year for 5 years.
> **Assume $i = 8\%$. Draw the cash flow diagram for each option.**

**Model Answer (Diagram Description):**

**Option 1 Diagram:**
- $t=0$ to $t=4$: No cash flow.
- $t=5$: One large downward arrow of ₹50,00,000.

**Option 2 Diagram:**
- $t=0$: No cash flow.
- $t=1$ to $t=5$: Five equal downward arrows of ₹12,00,000 each.

**b) Using the Present Worth method at $i = 8\%$, determine which option is cheaper. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step Solution):**

**Step 1 — Present Worth of Option 1 (Single Future Payment):**
$$
P_1 = 50{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/F, 8\%, 5)
$$
$$
(P/F, 8\%, 5) = (1.08)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.46933} = 0.6806
$$
$$
P_1 = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6806 = \text{₹}34{,}02{,}920
$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth of Option 2 (Uniform Series):**
$$
P_2 = 12{,}00{,}000 \cdot (P/A, 8\%, 5)
$$
$$
(P/A, 8\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.08)^{5}-1}{0.08 \times (1.08)^{5}} = \dfrac{0.46933}{0.11755} = 3.9927
$$
$$
P_2 = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 3.9927 = \text{₹}47{,}91{,}270
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Decision:**
$$
P_1 = \text{₹}34{,}02{,}920 \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_2 = \text{₹}47{,}91{,}270
$$
$$
P_1 < P_2
$$

**Decision:** **Option 1 (pay ₹50,00,000 at end of year 5) is cheaper** because its present worth is approximately **₹13,88,350 less** than Option 2. The contractor should choose Option 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Mixing up +/− signs on the cash flow diagram. The KTU board deducts 1 full mark for every 2 wrong sign placements.
> 2. **Time-axis Placement:** Drawing cash flows at the *beginning* of the period instead of the *end* (ordinary annuity) leads to wrong answers. The KTU 2024 syllabus defaults to **ordinary annuity** unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Forgetting the Salvage Value:** Students frequently ignore the end-of-life salvage value while computing NPW, costing them up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Geometric with Arithmetic Gradient:** Geometric gradient uses a *percentage* $g$, arithmetic uses a *constant amount* $G$. Using the wrong formula is a 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Calculation Truncation:** KTU expects answers to **3 decimal places for factors** and **nearest rupee for cash flows**. Rounding intermediate values prematurely is penalized.
> 6. **Diagram Missing:** Even in 7-mark numerical sub-parts, a cash flow diagram is expected wherever a new scenario is introduced. **Always draw the diagram first** before computing.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Cash Flow Diagrams — Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- **Definition:** A CFD is a horizontal time axis with **vertical arrows** (up = inflow, down = outflow) showing the **timing, direction, and magnitude** of money flows.
- **KTU Convention:** $t=0$ is the present (left), $t=n$ is the planning horizon (right); arrows are placed at **end of period** by default.
- **Length of arrow $\propto$ magnitude of cash flow.** Never make all arrows the same length.
- **Six Core Factors (PIG FARM mnemonic):** SPCAF $(F/P, i, n)$, SPPWF $(P/F, i, n)$, USCAF $(F/A, i, n)$, USPWF $(P/A, i, n)$, CRF $(A/P, i, n)$, SFF $(A/F, i, n)$.
- **Reciprocal Pairs:** $(F/P) \times (P/F) = 1$ and $(F/A) \times (A/F) = 1$.
- **Gradient:** Arithmetic gradient $G$ has base 0 at $t=1$, value $G$ at $t=2$, etc. Geometric gradient grows by a fixed percentage $g$ — remember the special case $i = g$ makes the denominator zero.
- **Always draw the diagram first** before plugging numbers into any formula. This single habit will save you 2–3 marks per problem.
- **Ordinary vs Annuity Due:** KTU defaults to **ordinary annuity** (end-of-period). If a problem says "beginning of the year," multiply the ordinary result by $(1+i)$.
- **NPW Decision Rule:** If NPW $> 0$, **accept**; if NPW $< 0$, **reject**; if NPW $= 0$, the investor is **indifferent** at the MARR.
- **Conversion Check:** $P \leftrightarrow A$ uses CRF / USPWF; $P \leftrightarrow F$ uses SPCAF / SPPWF; $A \leftrightarrow F$ uses USCAF / SFF; **gradient** uses the gradient-to-P and gradient-to-A factors.
- **Final-answer format:** Always state the numerical value with units (₹ or $) and explicitly state the **decision** (accept/reject/cheaper option) — KTU awards the final 1 mark only when both the value and the verbal conclusion are present.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interest Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interest Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Interest? (The Cost of Money Over Time)

**Interest** is the monetary charge levied on a borrower for the use of borrowed money, or the compensation received by a lender for parting with liquidity for a defined period. In engineering economics, interest is rigorously treated as the *time value of money* — a fundamental concept asserting that **a Rupee today is worth more than a Rupee received tomorrow** because of its earning potential and inflation-adjusted purchasing power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Standard):** *"Interest is the rent paid for the use of borrowed capital, expressed as a percentage of the principal per unit of time."*

### Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary for Board Exams)

- **Principal (P):** The original sum of money borrowed, lent, or invested. Denoted by *P* or *PV*.
- **Interest Rate (i or r):** The percentage of principal charged per period, expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., 8% = 0.08).
- **Time Period (n or t):** The number of compounding periods (years, months, quarters).
- **Future Value (F or FV):** The total accumulated amount at the end of period *n* = Principal + Interest earned.
- **Present Value (P or PV):** The current worth of a future sum of money, discounted back to the present.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the symbol **i** (lowercase italic) is used for interest rate per period, while **n** represents the number of compounding periods. Always use *annual* equivalents unless otherwise specified.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Money-Rental" Concept

Imagine you borrow **₹10,000** from a friend, Karthik, for **1 year** to buy a bicycle. Karthik says: *"I am giving up the chance to spend this ₹10,000 on a new phone case or a movie ticket today. Pay me back the ₹10,000 plus an extra ₹1,000 as a thank-you for waiting."*

This extra ₹1,000 is the **interest**. Now extend this to a bank:
- The bank is a *wholesale money-rental service*.
- It charges a standardized "rental rate" (the interest rate) for the duration you hold the money.
- Just as a landlord charges *rent per month* for a house, a bank charges *interest per year* for capital.

**Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Consider a civil engineering firm that takes a ₹50 lakh loan at 12% per annum to buy a new excavator. The 12% is not arbitrary — it represents:
1. The **opportunity cost** of the bank's money (what it could earn elsewhere).
2. **Inflation compensation** (rupee depreciation).
3. **Default risk premium** (borrower might not repay).

## 1.3 Two Fundamental Types of Interest

There are two principal mechanisms for calculating interest in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Type | Mechanism | Where Interest is Calculated On |
|------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| **Simple Interest (SI)** | Linear growth | Only the **original principal** |
| **Compound Interest (CI)** | Exponential growth | **Principal + accumulated interest** |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Board Exam Heuristic:** *Simple Interest = Linear (straight-line). Compound Interest = Exponential (snowball/roller-coaster).* If a problem mentions reinvestment, compounding, or "interest on interest" → use CI. If a problem mentions flat interest on the original sum → use SI.

## 1.4 Why Interest Calculations Matter in Engineering

Every engineering project is essentially a **capital allocation problem**:
- A mechanical engineer must decide whether a ₹2 Crore CNC machine has a positive Net Present Value.
- An electrical engineer evaluates the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) for a solar farm.
- A computer science engineer must choose between AWS reserved instances vs. on-demand pricing.

**All of these decisions pivot on interest calculations.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Simple Interest — The Linear Model

In **Simple Interest (SI)**, the interest is computed *exclusively* on the original principal for every period. The interest earned in one period is **NOT** added to the principal to earn interest in subsequent periods.

### Step-by-Step Logic of SI

1. Borrower takes principal *P* at the start of period 1.
2. At the end of period 1, interest *I₁ = P × i* is paid.
3. At the end of period 2, interest *I₂ = P × i* is paid **again on the same P** (not on P + I₁).
4. After *n* periods, total interest = *P × i × n* (linear in *n*).
5. The future value grows as a **straight line** with slope *P × i*.

### Why SI is Used in Real Life

- **Short-term loans** (less than 1 year, e.g., personal loans, bridge loans).
- **T-bills and certain government bonds** in some jurisdictions.
- **Penalty calculations** in legal/contractual contexts.
- **Car loans and some auto-financing schemes** (flat-rate basis).

## 2.2 Compound Interest — The Exponential Model

In **Compound Interest (CI)**, the interest accrued in each period is added back to the principal, forming a new (larger) principal base for the next period. This creates the legendary *interest-on-interest* effect.

### Step-by-Step Logic of CI

1. Start with principal *P* at *t = 0*.
2. End of period 1: Interest = *P × i*. New principal = *P(1 + i)*.
3. End of period 2: Interest = *P(1 + i) × i*. New principal = *P(1 + i)²*.
4. End of period 3: New principal = *P(1 + i)³*.
5. After *n* periods: Future Value = *P(1 + i)ⁿ*. (Exponential growth.)

### The Compounding Frequency Variable (*m*)

The KTU 2024 scheme places heavy emphasis on the **compounding frequency** *m*:
- *m = 1* → Annual compounding
- *m = 2* → Semi-annual compounding
- *m = 4* → Quarterly compounding
- *m = 12* → Monthly compounding
- *m = 365* → Daily compounding
- *m → ∞* → **Continuous compounding** (special case)

When the nominal annual rate is *r* compounded *m* times per year, the **periodic rate** is *i = r/m*.

## 2.3 Effective Annual Interest Rate (EAR / EAY)

When compounding occurs more frequently than once a year, the **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** differs from the nominal rate. The EAR is the *true* interest rate earned in a year.

### Intuition for EAR

If a bank quotes "12% compounded monthly", you might naively think you earn 12% per year. But in reality, you earn 1% each month on a base that grows every month, so the effective annual yield is slightly **higher** than 12%.

## 2.4 Present Value (Discounting)

Since money has time value, future cash flows must be **discounted** back to the present. The present value of a future sum *F* receivable after *n* periods at rate *i* per period is:

$$PV = \frac{F}{(1+i)^n}$$

This is the **single-payment present worth factor (SPPWF)**, denoted in interest tables as *(P/F, i, n)*.

## 2.5 Annuities — The Heart of Engineering Economics

An **annuity** is a sequence of equal payments made at regular intervals over a specified number of periods. Annuities are categorized as follows:

| Type | Payment Timing | Cash Flow Diagram |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Ordinary Annuity (Annuity-Ordinary)** | End of each period | Payments at t=1, 2, ..., n |
| **Annuity Due** | Beginning of each period | Payments at t=0, 1, 2, ..., n-1 |
| **Deferred Annuity** | After a deferral period *k* | Payments at t=k+1, k+2, ..., k+n |
| **Perpetuity** | Indefinitely | Payments continue forever (n → ∞) |

## 2.6 Sinking Fund and Capital Recovery

- **Sinking Fund (SF):** A fund built by equal periodic deposits to accumulate a future sum *F* at compound interest.
- **Capital Recovery (CR):** The reverse — equal periodic payments to completely recover a present value *P* plus interest over *n* periods.

These are *mirror-image* problems and the formulas are reciprocals.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU 2024 Board Exams on Interest Calculations.** Memorize these formulas and their variable definitions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Simple Interest (SI) | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | Linear interest on principal |
| 2 | Simple Future Value | $F = P(1 + i \cdot n)$ | Future value under SI |
| 3 | Compound Future Value (Single Payment) | $F = P(1 + i)^n$ | Future value under CI |
| 4 | Compound Present Value (Single Payment) | $P = F(1 + i)^{-n}$ | Discounting to present |
| 5 | Periodic Rate from Nominal | $i = \dfrac{r}{m}$ | r = nominal annual rate |
| 6 | Compound Amount Factor | $(F/P, i, n) = (1 + i)^n$ | Single-payment compound amount |
| 7 | Present Worth Factor | $(P/F, i, n) = (1 + i)^{-n}$ | Single-payment present worth |
| 8 | Effective Annual Rate (EAR) | $i_{eff} = \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$ | True annual yield |
| 9 | Continuous Compounding FV | $F = P \cdot e^{r \cdot n}$ | Special case as $m \to \infty$ |
| 10 | Continuous EAR | $i_{eff} = e^r - 1$ | When compounding is continuous |
| 11 | Annuity Future Value (Ordinary) | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ | F of uniform A over n periods |
| 12 | Annuity Present Value (Ordinary) | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | P of uniform A over n periods |
| 13 | Annuity Future Value (Due) | $F_{due} = F_{ord} \cdot (1+i)$ | Multiply ordinary by (1+i) |
| 14 | Annuity Present Value (Due) | $P_{due} = P_{ord} \cdot (1+i)$ | Multiply ordinary by (1+i) |
| 15 | Sinking Fund Payment | $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | A needed to accumulate F |
| 16 | Capital Recovery Payment | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | A needed to recover P |
| 17 | Perpetuity (PV) | $P_{\infty} = \dfrac{A}{i}$ | PV of infinite A stream |
| 18 | Number of Periods | $n = \dfrac{\ln(F/P)}{\ln(1+i)}$ | Solve for n explicitly |
| 19 | Interest Rate (implicit) | $i = \left(\dfrac{F}{P}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Solve for i explicitly |
| 20 | Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost | $EUAC = P \cdot \left[\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}\right]$ | Same as Capital Recovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Alert:** In KTU textbooks, *i* is the interest per period (usually annual). When the problem says "*12% per annum compounded monthly*", you must first compute *i = 0.12/12 = 0.01* (1% per month) and use *n = 12 × (number of years)*.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Project Feasibility Analysis:** NPV, IRR, BCR calculations for infrastructure projects.
- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** Compare EUAC of old vs. new machinery.
- **Loan Amortization:** EMI calculations for housing/auto loans.
- **Retirement Planning:** Future value of SIPs (Systematic Investment Plans).
- **Inflation Adjustment:** Real vs. nominal interest rate calculations.
- **Bond Pricing:** Coupon rates, YTM (yield to maturity), zero-coupon bonds.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compound Interest Formula

We derive the future value formula $F = P(1 + i)^n$ from first principles.

**Setup:** Let $F_n$ be the future value at the end of period $n$, with initial principal $P$ and interest rate $i$ per period.

**Period 1:**
Interest accrued = $P \cdot i$
Future value at end of period 1 = $P + P \cdot i = P(1 + i)$

**Period 2:**
Interest accrued = $F_1 \cdot i = P(1 + i) \cdot i$
Future value at end of period 2 = $F_1 + F_1 \cdot i = P(1 + i) + P(1 + i) \cdot i = P(1 + i)(1 + i) = P(1 + i)^2$

**Period 3:**
Future value at end of period 3 = $F_2 + F_2 \cdot i = P(1 + i)^2(1 + i) = P(1 + i)^3$

**Inductive Generalization (Period n):**
By mathematical induction, for any positive integer $n$:

$$F_n = P(1 + i)^n$$

This is the **foundational compound interest equation**. Every other interest formula in engineering economics is a *direct consequence* or *algebraic rearrangement* of this master relation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Annual Rate (EAR) Formula

Let the nominal annual rate be $r$ compounded $m$ times per year. The periodic rate is $i = r/m$. There are $m$ compounding periods in one year.

**Start with principal $P$ at the beginning of the year.**

After 1 compounding period, the value is $P(1 + r/m)$.
After 2 compounding periods, the value is $P(1 + r/m)^2$.
...
After $m$ compounding periods (i.e., 1 full year), the value is $P(1 + r/m)^m$.

**Define EAR** as the single annual rate that would grow $P$ to the same value in one year. So:

$$P(1 + i_{eff}) = P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m$$

Dividing both sides by $P$ and solving for $i_{eff}$:

$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Continuous Compounding Formula

Continuous compounding is the limit as $m \to \infty$ of the EAR formula. Starting from:

$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

Take the limit:

$$\lim_{m \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

Using the standard limit identity $\lim_{x \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{a}{x}\right)^x = e^a$, we obtain:

$$i_{eff} = e^r - 1$$

The future value under continuous compounding is therefore:

$$F = P \cdot e^{r \cdot n}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Ordinary Annuity Future Value Formula

Consider an ordinary annuity of amount $A$ paid at the end of each period for $n$ periods, earning interest $i$ per period. We want the future value at the end of period $n$.

**The last payment** $A$ at the end of period $n$ earns zero interest. Its future value = $A$.

**The second-to-last payment** $A$ at the end of period $n-1$ earns interest for 1 period. Its future value = $A(1 + i)$.

**The third-to-last payment** $A$ at the end of period $n-2$ earns interest for 2 periods. Its future value = $A(1 + i)^2$.

**In general,** the payment at the end of period $k$ (where $k$ ranges from 1 to $n$) earns interest for $(n - k)$ periods. Its future value = $A(1 + i)^{n-k}$.

**Summing all payments (Geometric Series with ratio $(1+i)$ and $n$ terms):**

$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^1 + A(1+i)^0$$

This is a geometric series. Factoring out the last term $A$:

$$F = A \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^k$$

Using the standard geometric sum formula $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} r^k = \dfrac{r^n - 1}{r - 1}$ with $r = (1+i)$:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of the Ordinary Annuity Present Value Formula

The present value of an ordinary annuity is the sum of present values of all individual payments:

$$P = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{A}{(1+i)^k} = A \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^k}$$

Using the geometric series formula with first term $\frac{1}{1+i}$, ratio $\frac{1}{1+i}$, and $n$ terms:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{1}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+i}\right)^n}{1 - \frac{1}{1+i}} = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

## 3.6 Derivation of the Sinking Fund and Capital Recovery Formulas

**Sinking Fund (SF):** Given a desired future sum $F$ and rate $i$ over $n$ periods, find the periodic deposit $A$.

Starting from $F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$, solve for $A$:

$$A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

**Capital Recovery (CR):** Given a present value $P$ and rate $i$ over $n$ periods, find the periodic withdrawal $A$.

Starting from $P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$, solve for $A$:

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

**The crucial relation:** Capital Recovery = Sinking Fund + Interest on the present sum. This gives us the **Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost (EUAC)** identity, heavily tested in KTU 2024 exams.

## 3.7 Worked Example Matrix (Step-by-Step Solutions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1: Simple vs. Compound Interest Comparison**
>
> **Problem:** A sum of **₹50,000** is invested for **3 years** at **10% per annum**. Compute the maturity value under (a) Simple Interest, and (b) Compound Interest (annual compounding).

**Solution (a) — Simple Interest:**

$$I = P \cdot i \cdot n = 50{,}000 \times 0.10 \times 3 = 15{,}000$$

$$F = P + I = 50{,}000 + 15{,}000 = \mathbf{₹65{,}000}$$

**Solution (b) — Compound Interest:**

$$F = P(1 + i)^n = 50{,}000 \times (1.10)^3$$

Computing $(1.10)^3$ step by step:
- $(1.10)^1 = 1.10$
- $(1.10)^2 = 1.21$
- $(1.10)^3 = 1.331$

$$F = 50{,}000 \times 1.331 = \mathbf{₹66{,}550}$$

**Comparison:** Compound interest yields ₹1,550 *more* than simple interest over 3 years, due to reinvestment of interest.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 2: Effective Annual Rate with Monthly Compounding**
>
> **Problem:** A bank quotes **"12% per annum compounded monthly"**. Compute the Effective Annual Rate (EAR).

**Given:** $r = 0.12$, $m = 12$.

$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1 = \left(1 + \frac{0.12}{12}\right)^{12} - 1 = (1.01)^{12} - 1$$

Computing $(1.01)^{12}$:
- $(1.01)^2 = 1.0201$
- $(1.01)^4 = (1.0201)^2 = 1.04060401$
- $(1.01)^8 = (1.04060401)^2 = 1.082856706$
- $(1.01)^{12} = (1.01)^8 \times (1.01)^4 = 1.082856706 \times 1.04060401 \approx 1.126825$

$$i_{eff} = 1.126825 - 1 = 0.126825 = \mathbf{12.6825\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Monthly compounding at 12% nominal gives an **effective yield of 12.68%**, which is 0.68 percentage points higher. Banks and credit card companies use this differential to maximize returns.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 3: Sinking Fund Calculation**
>
> **Problem:** A company wants to accumulate **₹10,00,000** in 8 years to replace a piece of machinery. If the sinking fund earns **9% per annum compounded annually**, what equal annual deposit is required at the end of each year?

**Given:** $F = 10{,}00{,}000$, $n = 8$, $i = 0.09$.

$$A = F \cdot \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.09}{(1.09)^8 - 1}$$

Computing $(1.09)^8$:
- $(1.09)^2 = 1.1881$
- $(1.09)^4 = (1.1881)^2 = 1.41158161$
- $(1.09)^8 = (1.41158161)^2 = 1.992562642$

$$A = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.09}{1.992562642 - 1} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.09}{0.992562642}$$

$$A = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.090674 = \mathbf{₹90{,}674}$$

So the company must deposit approximately **₹90,674 per year** for 8 years to accumulate ₹10,00,000.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 4: Capital Recovery (EMI) Calculation**
>
> **Problem:** A startup takes a loan of **₹5,00,000** at **14% per annum** for **5 years** with equal annual payments at year-end. Calculate the annual instalment using the capital recovery formula.

**Given:** $P = 5{,}00{,}000$, $i = 0.14$, $n = 5$.

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.14 \times (1.14)^5}{(1.14)^5 - 1}$$

Computing $(1.14)^5$:
- $(1.14)^2 = 1.2996$
- $(1.14)^4 = (1.2996)^2 = 1.68896016$
- $(1.14)^5 = 1.68896016 \times 1.14 = 1.925414582$

$$A = 5{,}00{,000 \times \frac{0.14 \times 1.925414582}{1.925414582 - 1}} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.269558041}{0.925414582}$$

$$A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.291284 = \mathbf{₹1{,}45{,}642}$$

The annual instalment is **₹1,45,642**. Total paid = ₹1,45,642 × 5 = ₹7,28,210, of which ₹2,28,210 is interest.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 5: Continuous Compounding**
>
> **Problem:** Find the future value of **₹1,00,000** invested for **5 years** at **10% per annum** compounded continuously.

**Given:** $P = 1{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.10$, $n = 5$.

$$F = P \cdot e^{r \cdot n} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times e^{0.10 \times 5} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times e^{0.5}$$

Using $e^{0.5} \approx 1.648721$:

$$F = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.648721 = \mathbf{₹1{,}64{,}872}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** With annual compounding, $F = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.10)^5 = 1{,}61{,}051$. Continuous compounding yields a slightly higher value (₹1,64,872), as expected.

The block diagram below shows the **logical mapping** between different time-value-of-money transactions and their corresponding interest formulas.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A1[Single Payment] --> A2["SPPWF and SCAF"]

    B1[Uniform Series] --> B2["Annuity Formulas"]

    B2 --> B6["A = F * (A/F, i, n)"]

    C1[Infinite Series] --> C2[Perpetuity]

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Six Standard Factors

In engineering economics, every interest calculation problem maps to one of **six standard factors** recognized by KTU and globally by engineering economics textbooks. The following block diagram shows this architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Given Data: P, F, A, i, n] --> S{Select Factor Type}

    S -->|Single Payment| SP
    S -->|Uniform Series| US
    S -->|Arithmetic Gradient| AG
    S -->|Geometric Gradient| GG

    SP --> SP1["F = P * (1+i)^n<br/>SCAF"]
    SP --> SP2["P = F * (1+i)^(-n)<br/>SPPWF"]

    US --> US1["F = A * ((1+i)^n - 1) / i<br/>USCAF"]
    US --> US2["P = A * ((1+i)^n - 1) / (i*(1+i)^n)<br/>USPWF"]
    US --> US3["A = P * i*(1+i)^n / ((1+i)^n - 1)<br/>CRF"]
    US --> US4["A = F * i / ((1+i)^n - 1)<br/>SFF"]

    AG --> AG1["Arithmetic Gradient<br/>G Conversion to A"]

    GG --> GG1["Geometric Gradient<br/>g Conversion to P or A"]

    SP1 --> OUT[Solution: Unknown Variable]
    SP2 --> OUT
    US1 --> OUT
    US2 --> OUT
    US3 --> OUT
    US4 --> OUT
    AG1 --> OUT
    GG1 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Which Formula to Use?

Use this decision tree to determine the appropriate interest formula in KTU Board Exam problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is given?} --> Q1a[Future Value F]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Equal Payments A]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Both P and F]

    Q1a --> Q1a1{How many periods?}
    Q1a1 -->|One period| Q1a2[Simple Interest]
    Q1a1 -->|Multiple periods| Q1a3{Interest reinvested?}
    Q1a3 -->|Yes| Q1a4[Compound Interest]
    Q1a3 -->|No| Q1a2

    Q1b --> Q1b1{Timing of payment?}
    Q1b1 -->|End of period| Q1b2[Ordinary Annuity]
    Q1b1 -->|Beginning| Q1b3[Annuity Due]
    Q1b1 -->|Forever| Q1b4[Perpetuity]

    Q1c --> Q1c1[Single Payment Factor]
    Q1c1 --> Q1c2[SPPWF or SCAF]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Distinguish between Simple Interest and Compound Interest. Under what circumstances is Simple Interest preferred over Compound Interest in engineering economics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Simple Interest (SI) | Compound Interest (CI) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Formula | $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$ | $F = P(1 + i)^n$ |
| Base for interest | Always original principal | Principal + accumulated interest |
| Growth pattern | Linear (arithmetic) | Exponential (geometric) |
| Total interest over n periods | Proportional to n | Proportional to $(1+i)^n - 1$ |

**Circumstances where SI is preferred:**
1. **Short-duration loans** (less than 1 year), where the compounding effect is negligible.
2. **Government Treasury Bills** and certain debt instruments where interest is *not* reinvested.
3. **Penalty and statutory calculations** under contract law, where linear accrual is mandated.
4. **Flat-rate loan disclosures** in some consumer finance products for transparency.

**(1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for circumstances.)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the Effective Annual Rate (EAR)? Why does it differ from the nominal interest rate? Calculate the EAR when 18% per annum is compounded quarterly.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** The Effective Annual Rate (EAR) is the *actual* annual interest rate earned or paid when compounding occurs more frequently than once per year. It represents the true percentage growth of an investment over a one-year horizon.

**Why EAR differs from nominal rate:** The nominal rate is the *stated* annual rate, but when interest is compounded $m$ times per year, each compounding period adds interest that itself earns interest in subsequent periods. This compounding-on-compounding effect causes the effective yield to exceed the nominal rate whenever $m > 1$.

**Calculation:** Given $r = 0.18$, $m = 4$.

$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{0.18}{4}\right)^4 - 1 = (1.045)^4 - 1$$

Computing:
- $(1.045)^2 = 1.092025$
- $(1.045)^4 = (1.092025)^2 = 1.192518602$

$$i_{eff} = 1.192519 - 1 = 0.192519 = \mathbf{19.25\%}$$

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the reason, 1 mark for correct calculation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A construction engineering firm is evaluating two investment options for a new crane. Both options require an initial investment today.**

- **Option X:** Invest ₹8,00,000 today. Receive ₹2,50,000 at the end of every year for 4 years, plus a salvage value of ₹1,00,000 at the end of year 4.
- **Option Y:** Invest ₹8,00,000 today. Receive ₹3,00,000 at the end of every year for 4 years, with no salvage value.

The firm's Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) is **12% per annum**.

**(a)** Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) of both options. Which option is financially superior? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A contractor can instead invest the same ₹8,00,000 in a government bond scheme that yields 12% per annum compounded monthly for 5 years. Calculate the maturity value and the effective annual rate. Compare this with Option Y. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the present value of cash inflows for Option X.**

The annual receipts of ₹2,50,000 form an **ordinary annuity** for 4 years.
The salvage value of ₹1,00,000 is a **single future payment** at year 4.

Present value of annuity:
$$P_A = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{(1.12)^4 - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^4}$$

Computing $(1.12)^4$:
- $(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$
- $(1.12)^4 = (1.2544)^2 = 1.57351936$

$$P_A = 2{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{1.57351936 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.57351936} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times \frac{0.57351936}{0.18882232}$$

$$P_A = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3.037349 = \mathbf{₹7{,}59{,}337}$$

Present value of salvage value:
$$P_{SV} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^4} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1.57351936} = \mathbf{₹63{,}552}$$

**Total PV of inflows (Option X):**
$$P_X = 7{,}59{,}337 + 63{,}552 = ₹8{,}22{,}889$$

**NPV of Option X:**
$$NPV_X = P_X - \text{Initial Investment} = 8{,}22{,}889 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{+₹22{,}889}$$

**Step 2: Compute the NPV of Option Y.**

Present value of 4-year ordinary annuity of ₹3,00,000:
$$P_Y = 3{,}00{,}000 \times 3.037349 = ₹9{,}11{,}205$$

**NPV of Option Y:**
$$NPV_Y = 9{,}11{,}205 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{+₹1{,}11{,}205}$$

**Decision:** **Option Y is financially superior** because $NPV_Y = +₹1,11,205 > NPV_X = +₹22,889$. The higher annual cash flows of Option Y outweigh the additional salvage value of Option X.

**(Full marks: 1 mark for identifying annuity + single payment structure, 2 marks for Option X PV calculation, 2 marks for Option Y PV calculation, 1 mark for NPV computation, 1 mark for decision.)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the maturity value of the government bond.**

Given: $P = 8{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.12$ (nominal annual), $m = 12$, $n = 5$ years.

The periodic rate is $i = r/m = 0.12/12 = 0.01$ (1% per month).
The total number of compounding periods is $m \times n = 12 \times 5 = 60$.

$$F = P(1 + i)^{m \cdot n} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (1.01)^{60}$$

Using logarithms for precision:
$$\ln(1.01) = 0.00995033$$
$$60 \times \ln(1.01) = 0.597020$$
$$e^{0.597020} = 1.816697$$

$$F = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.816697 = \mathbf{₹14{,}53{,}358}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Effective Annual Rate (EAR).**

$$i_{eff} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1 = (1.01)^{12} - 1$$

Computing $(1.01)^{12}$:
$$\ln(1.01) \times 12 = 0.119404$$
$$e^{0.119404} = 1.126825$$

$$i_{eff} = 1.126825 - 1 = \mathbf{12.6825\%}$$

**Step 3: Comparison with Option Y.**

- The bond yields a maturity value of ₹14,53,358 in 5 years.
- Option Y yields a total nominal cash flow of ₹3,00,000 × 4 = ₹12,00,000 over 4 years (no salvage).

At first glance, the bond's absolute rupee return is higher. However, the comparison is **not strictly fair** because the time horizons differ (5 years vs. 4 years). For a like-for-like comparison, compute the EUAC (Equivalent Uniform Annual Cost) of the bond and compare to Option Y's annual inflow.

EUAC of bond over 5 years at 12.6825%:
$$A_{bond} = P \cdot \frac{i_{eff}(1 + i_{eff})^5}{(1 + i_{eff})^5 - 1} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.126825 \times (1.126825)^5}{(1.126825)^5 - 1}$$

Computing $(1.126825)^5$:
- $(1.126825)^2 = 1.269734$
- $(1.126825)^4 = (1.269734)^2 = 1.612225$
- $(1.126825)^5 = 1.612225 \times 1.126825 = 1.816703$

$$A_{bond} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.126825 \times 1.816703}{0.816703} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.230416}{0.816703} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.282135 = ₹2{,}25{,}708$$

**Comparison:** EUAC of bond = ₹2,25,708 < Option Y annual receipt = ₹3,00,000. Therefore, in annual equivalent terms, **Option Y still yields higher annual cash flow** and is preferable to the bond.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students frequently confuse $m$ (compounding frequency per year) with $n$ (number of years). The total number of compounding periods is $m \times n$, **not** $m$ or $n$ alone.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When comparing investments of different durations, direct rupee comparison is invalid. Use EUAC or extend both to a common analysis period.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to use $i = r/m$ (periodic rate) instead of the nominal $r$ in the formula. The nominal rate is **never** plugged directly into $F = P(1+i)^n$ when $m > 1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**An engineering graduate receives a job offer with two salary structures and a third alternative of further study. The relevant data is given below.**

**Option 1 (Private Sector Job):** Starting salary ₹6,00,000 per annum with an annual increment of ₹40,000. Duration: 5 years. Salary paid at the end of each year.

**Option 2 (PSU Job):** Starting salary ₹5,50,000 per annum with constant salary (no increment). Duration: 5 years. Additionally, a lumpsum bonus of ₹3,00,000 at the end of year 5.

**Option 3 (Higher Studies):** No income for 2 years, but assured placement thereafter at ₹9,00,000 per annum for the next 3 years.

Assume the MARR (discount rate) is **10% per annum**.

**(a)** Compute the Present Worth of each option. Which option is economically best? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bank offers the graduate a loan of ₹5,00,000 at 10% per annum compounded quarterly. The loan is to be repaid in 8 equal quarterly instalments. Calculate the quarterly instalment (EMI) and the total interest paid over the loan tenure. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Option 1 (Arithmetic Gradient Series):**

This is an **arithmetic gradient** problem where the salary increases by a constant $G = ₹40,000$ each year. The present worth has two components:

Component 1: Base uniform series $A_1 = ₹6,00,000$ for 5 years.
Component 2: Gradient series $G = ₹40,000$ starting at ₹0 in year 1, ₹40,000 in year 2, ..., ₹1,60,000 in year 5.

The present worth of the base uniform series:
$$P_1 = A_1 \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^5}$$

Computing $(1.10)^5$:
- $(1.10)^2 = 1.21$
- $(1.10)^4 = (1.21)^2 = 1.4641$
- $(1.10)^5 = 1.4641 \times 1.10 = 1.61051$

$$P_1 = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.61051}{0.161051} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 3.790787 = ₹22{,}74{,}472$$

The present worth of the gradient series uses the arithmetic gradient present worth factor $(P/G, i, n)$:

$$P_G = G \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n}$$

Substituting $G = 40{,}000$, $i = 0.10$, $n = 5$, $(1.10)^5 = 1.61051$:

$$P_G = 40{,}000 \times \frac{1.61051 - 0.10 \times 5 - 1}{(0.10)^2 \times 1.61051}$$

$$P_G = 40{,}000 \times \frac{1.61051 - 0.50 - 1}{0.0161051} = 40{,}000 \times \frac{0.11051}{0.0161051} = 40{,}000 \times 6.8618 = ₹2{,}74{,}472$$

**Total Present Worth of Option 1:**
$$PW_1 = 22{,}74{,}472 + 2{,}74{,}472 = \mathbf{₹25{,}48{,}944}$$

**Option 2 (Constant Salary + Lumpsum Bonus):**

Component 1: Uniform series of ₹5,50,000 for 5 years.
$$P_{2A} = 5{,}50{,}000 \times 3.790787 = ₹20{,}84{,}933$$

Component 2: Lumpsum bonus of ₹3,00,000 at end of year 5.
$$P_{2B} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.61051} = ₹1{,}86{,}277$$

**Total Present Worth of Option 2:**
$$PW_2 = 20{,}84{,}933 + 1{,}86{,}277 = \mathbf{₹22{,}71{,}210}$$

**Option 3 (Deferred Annuity):**

Cash flow: ₹0 for years 1-2, then ₹9,00,000 at end of years 3, 4, 5.

This is a 3-year ordinary annuity (years 3, 4, 5) deferred by 2 years.

Present worth at end of year 2 (i.e., start of annuity):
$$P_{at\ year\ 2} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{(1.10)^3 - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^3}$$

Computing $(1.10)^3 = 1.331$:

$$P_{at\ year\ 2} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{1.331 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.331} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.331}{0.1331} = 9{,}00{,}000 \times 2.486852 = ₹22{,}38{,}167$$

Discount this back 2 more years to present (year 0):
$$PW_3 = \frac{22{,}38{,}167}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{22{,}38{,}167}{1.21} = \mathbf{₹18{,}49{,}725}$$

**Decision:** Comparing present worths:
- $PW_1 = ₹25{,}48{,}944$ ✓ **Highest**
- $PW_2 = ₹22{,}71{,}210$
- $PW_3 = ₹18{,}49{,}725$

**Option 1 (Private Sector with increments) is the most economically attractive.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Determine the periodic interest rate and number of periods.**

Nominal annual rate $r = 10\% = 0.10$, compounded quarterly: $m = 4$.

Periodic rate: $i = r/m = 0.10/4 = 0.025$ (2.5% per quarter).
Number of periods: $n = 8$ quarters.

**Step 2: Apply the Capital Recovery Formula.**

$$A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.025 \times (1.025)^8}{(1.025)^8 - 1}$$

Computing $(1.025)^8$:
- $(1.025)^2 = 1.050625$
- $(1.025)^4 = (1.050625)^2 = 1.103812890625$
- $(1.025)^8 = (1.103812890625)^2 = 1.218402529$

$$A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.025 \times 1.218402529}{1.218402529 - 1} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.030460063}{0.218402529}$$

$$A = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.139470 = \mathbf{₹69{,}735}$$

The quarterly instalment is **₹69,735**.

**Step 3: Total amount paid and total interest.**

Total paid over 8 quarters:
$$\text{Total Paid} = 69{,}735 \times 8 = ₹5{,}57{,}880$$

Total interest paid:
$$\text{Interest} = \text{Total Paid} - \text{Principal} = 5{,}57{,}880 - 5{,}00{,}000 = \mathbf{₹57{,}880}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating the *nominal* 10% rate with the *periodic* 2.5% rate. Always compute $i = r/m$ first.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the wrong value of $n$. The problem says "8 quarterly instalments," so $n = 8$ quarters — not 2 years. (Though $n$ in years is 2, we use the total compounding periods.)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **deferred annuity** discount step in Option 3 of part (a). The annuity does not start at year 1, so it must be discounted back to year 0.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In arithmetic gradient problems, the **first gradient term is zero in year 1**, not $G$. The gradient $G$ is the *increment*, not the year-1 amount.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist in the last 15 minutes before your KTU exam to refresh the entire Module 4.**

**Core Concepts (Must Know):**
- [ ] **Simple Interest** is linear: $I = P \cdot i \cdot n$, used for short-term flat-rate loans.
- [ ] **Compound Interest** is exponential: $F = P(1 + i)^n$, interest is reinvested.
- [ ] **Periodic rate** $i = r/m$ is used in all formulas, never the nominal $r$ directly.
- [ ] **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** $= (1 + r/m)^m - 1$ captures the true annual yield.
- [ ] **Continuous compounding** uses $F = Pe^{rn}$ and $i_{eff} = e^r - 1$.

**Annuity Family (Most Tested):**
- [ ] **Ordinary Annuity** payments occur at the **end** of each period.
- [ ] **Annuity Due** payments occur at the **beginning** of each period; multiply ordinary formulas by $(1 + i)$.
- [ ] **Perpetuity** is an infinite annuity with $P = A / i$.
- [ ] **Sinking Fund** finds the periodic deposit $A$ to reach a target future sum $F$.
- [ ] **Capital Recovery** finds the periodic payment $A$ to amortize a present value $P$.
- [ ] **Deferred Annuity** is valued by first computing PV at the start of the annuity, then discounting back to year 0.

**Critical Pitfalls (Common Loss of Marks):**
- [ ] ❌ **Forgetting to use periodic rate** $i = r/m$ in compound interest formulas.
- [ ] ❌ **Confusing years with periods** when compounding frequency is not annual.
- [ ] ❌ **Mixing up ordinary annuity and annuity due** — the timing of payment shifts the value.
- [ ] ❌ **Direct rupee comparison across unequal time horizons** — always normalize using EUAC or extend to a common period.
- [ ] ❌ **Rounding intermediate steps** — keep 5-6 decimal places until the final answer.
- [ ] ❌ **Wrong formula selection** for the given problem type (e.g., using simple interest formula for a compound interest problem).

**Six Standard Factors (Engineer's Bible):**
- [ ] $(F/P, i, n) = (1 + i)^n$ — Single-payment compound amount factor
- [ ] $(P/F, i, n) = (1 + i)^{-n}$ — Single-payment present worth factor
- [ ] $(F/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ — Uniform series compound amount factor
- [ ] $(P/A, i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ — Uniform series present worth factor
- [ ] $(A/F, i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — Sinking fund factor
- [ ] $(A/P, i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ — Capital recovery factor

**Final Mantra:** *"In engineering economics, every rupee has a time-stamp. Discount the future to the present, then compare."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Interest Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compound Interest Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compound Interest** is the interest earned not only on the original principal amount but **also on the accumulated interest of prior periods**. The process of adding accumulated interest back to the principal so that interest is earned on interest is called **compounding**.

In engineering economics, a **Compound Interest Factor** is a mathematical multiplier that converts a cash flow occurring at one point in time into an *equivalent* cash flow at another point in time, assuming a constant interest rate $i$ per interest period and $n$ compounding periods.

The six standard factors (and two gradient factors) are written in the functional notation:

$$ (X/Y, i, n) $$

where $X$ is the cash flow we are *finding*, $Y$ is the cash flow we are *given*, $i$ is the interest rate per period, and $n$ is the number of periods.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Rolling Snowball

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you roll a small snowball down a snow-covered hill. The snowball picks up snow on the way, and that new snow itself picks up more snow. The growth is no longer linear; it is **accelerating**. Compound interest works exactly the same way — the interest you earned last year earns its *own* interest this year. The longer the snowball rolls, the faster it grows.

A more grounded finance analogy: deposit ₹1,000 in a savings account at **10 % per annum compounded yearly**.

- After Year 1: ₹1,000 + ₹100 = **₹1,100**
- After Year 2: ₹1,100 + ₹110 = **₹1,210**  *(interest itself earned ₹10)*
- After Year 3: ₹1,210 + ₹121 = **₹1,331**

The "₹10" and "₹21" are *interest on interest* — the hallmark of compounding.

### 1.3 Standard Cash Flow Symbols Used in KTU Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Position in Time |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Present worth (a single sum **now**, time 0) | $t = 0$ |
| $F$ | Future worth (a single sum at the **end** of period $n$) | $t = n$ |
| $A$ | End-of-period **uniform series** (equal cash flow every period) | End of each period, 1 through $n$ |
| $G$ | Arithmetic **gradient** (cash flow grows by $G$ each period) | End of each period, 1 through $n$ |
| $i$ | Interest rate per period (in **decimal**, e.g. 10 % = 0.10) | per period |
| $n$ | Number of interest periods (years, months, …) | dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Single-Payment Compounding

Suppose you invest a principal $P$ for one period at rate $i$. The future amount is:

$$F = P + P \cdot i = P(1+i)$$

If you reinvest this entire amount for a **second** period, the previous $F$ becomes the new $P$:

$$F = [P(1+i)](1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

Continuing this reasoning for $n$ periods yields the foundational formula for *all* other factors.

### 2.2 Why Six Factors? — The Engineering-Economics Logic

Every engineering project involves **unequal cash flows at unequal times**. To compare two alternatives fairly, the KTU syllabus requires bringing every cash flow to a **common datum** (the same point in time) at a **common rate $i$**. The six factors are the building blocks of this conversion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Single-Payment and Uniform-Series Factors** (with **Standard Notation and Standard Cash-Flow Signs**).

| # | Factor Name | Standard Notation | Algebraic Form | Read As |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{n}$ | "Find $F$, given $P$" |
| 2 | Single-Payment Present Worth | $(P/F, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{-n}$ | "Find $P$, given $F$" |
| 3 | Uniform-Series Compound Amount | $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | "Find $F$, given $A$" |
| 4 | Sinking-Fund Factor | $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | "Find $A$, given $F$" |
| 5 | Capital-Recovery Factor | $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | "Find $A$, given $P$" |
| 6 | Uniform-Series Present Worth | $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | "Find $P$, given $A$" |

#### 2.3.1 Arithmetic Gradient Factors (Frequently Tested in KTU Module 4)

| # | Factor Name | Standard Notation | Algebraic Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | Gradient Present Worth | $(P/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-i n-1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ |
| 8 | Gradient Uniform Series | $(A/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{i}-\dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions students must remember:**
> - If $i = 0$:  $(F/P, 0, n) = 1$,  $(F/A, 0, n) = n$,  $(P/A, 0, n) = n$.
> - If $n = 0$:  *all* factors equal **1** (a sum is itself).
> - All factors are **dimensionless multipliers**; they convert a cash flow of one form to another.

### 2.4 Reciprocal and Product Pairs (Examiners' Favourite)

$$(F/P, i, n) \cdot (P/F, i, n) = 1 \quad \text{(reciprocals)}$$

$$(F/A, i, n) \cdot (A/F, i, n) = 1$$

$$(P/A, i, n) = (P/F, i, n)\cdot(F/A, i, n)$$

$$(A/P, i, n) = (A/F, i, n)\cdot(F/P, i, n)$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Equipment Replacement Analysis:** Converting a one-time purchase price $P$ into an equivalent annual cost $A$ via the Capital-Recovery factor.
- **Bond Valuation:** Finding the present worth of a stream of coupon payments $A$ plus a face value $F$.
- **Project Justification:** Comparing alternatives with different cash-flow patterns by moving all values to a single datum (present or future).
- **Sinking-Fund Calculations:** Determining the equal annual deposit $A$ required to accumulate a target future amount $F$ (e.g., a plant decommissioning fund).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Payment Compound Amount $(F/P, i, n)$

**Starting point:** deposit $P$ at $t = 0$, rate $i$ per period, $n$ periods.

End of period 1:
$$F_1 = P + Pi = P(1+i)$$

End of period 2: previous balance becomes the new principal
$$F_2 = P(1+i) + P(1+i)\,i = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^2$$

End of period 3 (by induction):
$$F_3 = P(1+i)^3$$

Continuing for $n$ periods yields the master formula:

$$F = P(1+i)^{n}$$

$$\boxed{(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Uniform-Series Compound Amount $(F/A, i, n)$

Consider $n$ equal end-of-period deposits of $A$ at interest rate $i$. The last deposit occurs at the end of period $n$ and therefore earns **no interest**.

Each deposit $A$ grows to $F$ via the $(F/P, i, n)$ factor:

- $A$ at end of period 1 grows for $(n-1)$ periods → $A(1+i)^{n-1}$
- $A$ at end of period 2 grows for $(n-2)$ periods → $A(1+i)^{n-2}$
- $\;\;\vdots$
- $A$ at end of period $n$ grows for $0$ periods → $A(1+i)^{0} = A$

Summing the geometric series:

$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}$$

Factor $A$ from the right-hand side:

$$F = A \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^{k}$$

Apply the closed-form for a finite geometric series with first term $1$, ratio $(1+i)$, and $n$ terms:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+i)^{k} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Uniform-Series Present Worth $(P/A, i, n)$

We have $F = A(F/A, i, n)$ from Derivation 2. To find present worth, simply move the future amount back to time zero using the $(P/F, i, n)$ factor:

$$P = F \cdot (P/F, i, n) = A \cdot (F/A, i, n) \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

Substitute both closed forms:

$$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} \cdot (1+i)^{-n}$$

Simplify the numerator by factoring out $(1+i)^{-n}$:

$$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}(1+i)^{-n} - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = A \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

$$\boxed{(P/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Capital-Recovery Factor $(A/P, i, n)$

By definition, Capital Recovery is the reciprocal of the Present Worth factor:

$$(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{1}{(P/A, i, n)} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

We can also derive it by adding the interest on principal $i$ to the sinking-fund deposit $(A/F, i, n)$:

$$(A/P, i, n) = i + (A/F, i, n) = i + \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{i[(1+i)^{n} - 1] + i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$

This is a *very important* identity tested often in KTU:

$$\boxed{(A/P, i, n) = i + (A/F, i, n)}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive, KTU-style)

> **Problem (KTU module-level):** A company deposits ₹50,000 today into a fund that pays **9 % effective annual interest**. It also plans to deposit ₹20,000 at the end of every year for **8 years**. How much money will be in the fund immediately after the **8th deposit**?

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flows.**
- $P = $ ₹50,000 at $t = 0$
- $A = $ ₹20,000 at $t = 1, 2, \ldots, 8$
- $i = 9\% = 0.09$, $\;\; n = 8$

**Step 2 — Move the present sum $P$ to time $t = 8$ using $(F/P, i, n)$.**

$$F_P = P(1+i)^{n} = 50{,}000 \times (1.09)^{8}$$

Compute $(1.09)^{8}$ step by step:

$$(1.09)^{2} = 1.1881$$

$$(1.09)^{4} = (1.1881)^{2} = 1.41158161$$

$$(1.09)^{8} = (1.41158161)^{2} \approx 1.99256264$$

Therefore:

$$F_P = 50{,}000 \times 1.99256264 = \text{₹}99{,}628.13$$

**Step 3 — Move the uniform series $A$ to time $t = 8$ using $(F/A, i, n)$.**

$$F_A = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{1.99256264 - 1}{0.09}$$

$$F_A = 20{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.99256264}{0.09} = 20{,}000 \times 11.0284738$$

$$F_A = \text{₹}220{,}569.48$$

**Step 4 — Add the two future values (since they are both located at $t = 8$).**

$$F_{\text{total}} = F_P + F_A = 99{,}628.13 + 220{,}569.48$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} \approx \text{₹}320{,}197.61}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Factor

| Given | Find | Factor to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | $F$ | $(F/P, i, n)$ |
| $F$ | $P$ | $(P/F, i, n)$ |
| $A$ | $F$ | $(F/A, i, n)$ |
| $F$ | $A$ | $(A/F, i, n)$ |
| $A$ | $P$ | $(P/A, i, n)$ |
| $P$ | $A$ | $(A/P, i, n)$ |
| $G$ | $P$ | $(P/G, i, n)$ |
| $G$ | $A$ | $(A/G, i, n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cash-Flow Diagram Convention (KTU Standard)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the time-positioning of every standard cash-flow symbol on the horizontal time axis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0"] --> t1["t = 1"] --> t2["t = 2"] --> t3["t = 3"] --> tn["t = n"]

    Ppos["P (Present sum)"] -.-> t0
    Apos["A1, A2, A3, ..., An (Uniform series)"] -.-> t1
    Apos -.-> t2
    Apos -.-> t3
    Apos -.-> tn
    Gpos["G, 2G, 3G, ..., nG (Arithmetic gradient)"] -.-> t1
    Gpos -.-> t2
    Gpos -.-> t3
    Gpos -.-> tn
    Fpos["F (Future sum)"] -.-> tn
```

**Reading rule:** Arrows *up* = positive (receipt / income); arrows *down* = negative (disbursement / cost).

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Six-Factor Equivalence Engine

The next diagram shows the *logical conversions* between the three core variables $P$, $F$, and $A$, and the gradient extension $G$. The arrows represent the factor applied to move the value between time points.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph timeAxis["Time Axis (left = present, right = future)"]
        Pnode["P (t = 0)"]
        Anode["A (end of each period, 1 to n)"]
        Fnode["F (t = n)"]
        Gnode["G (gradient series)"]
    end

    Pnode -- "(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^n" --> Fnode
    Fnode -- "(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^-n" --> Pnode
    Pnode -- "(A/P, i, n) = i(1+i)^n / (1+i)^n - 1" --> Anode
    Anode -- "(P/A, i, n) = (1+i)^n - 1 / i(1+i)^n" --> Pnode
    Anode -- "(F/A, i, n) = (1+i)^n - 1 / i" --> Fnode
    Fnode -- "(A/F, i, n) = i / (1+i)^n - 1" --> Anode
    Gnode -- "(P/G, i, n)" --> Pnode
    Gnode -- "(A/G, i, n) = 1/i - n / (1+i)^n - 1" --> Anode
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the engine:** locate your *given* node, locate your *target* node, then apply the factor on the corresponding arrow. Two-step conversions (e.g. $G \rightarrow P \rightarrow A$) are valid because the factors are mathematically composable.

---

### 4.3 Decision Flow — "Which Factor Do I Pick?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is given?}
    Q1 -- Single sum at t=0 --> P["Use P-based factors"]
    Q1 -- Single sum at t=n --> F["Use F-based factors"]
    Q1 -- Equal series at end of each period --> A["Use A-based factors"]
    Q1 -- Linearly increasing series --> G["Use G-based factors"]

    P -- Find F --> FP["(F/P, i, n)"]
    P -- Find A --> AP["(A/P, i, n)"]
    F -- Find P --> PF["(P/F, i, n)"]
    F -- Find A --> AF["(A/F, i, n)"]
    A -- Find P --> PA["(P/A, i, n)"]
    A -- Find F --> FA["(F/A, i, n)"]
    G -- Find P --> PG["(P/G, i, n)"]
    G -- Find A --> AG["(A/G, i, n)"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor" $(F/P, i, n)$. State its formula and explain its engineering-economic significance in two lines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor** $(F/P, i, n)$ is the factor that converts a present sum $P$ into an equivalent future sum $F$ after $n$ periods at a constant interest rate $i$ per period. Its formula is:

$$(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$$

**Significance (2 lines):**
1. It quantifies the time-value of a single lumpsum investment/cost — every engineering project must convert its initial outlay $P$ into the future-worth $F$ it represents at the end of the planning horizon before fair comparison with alternatives.
2. It is the *primary* factor from which all other compound-interest factors are mathematically derived, making it the foundation of engineering-economic analysis.

---

**Q2.  [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the relationship $(A/P, i, n) = i + (A/F, i, n)$. Why is this identity useful in capital-recovery problems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement.** The capital-recovery factor equals the sum of the interest rate $i$ (interest on the original principal) and the sinking-fund factor $(A/F, i, n)$ (the periodic deposit that returns the principal at the end of $n$ periods):

$$(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = i + \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

**Usefulness (2 lines):**
1. It lets an analyst *split* an annualised cost into "interest" and "principal-recovery" components — a clear picture for management and tax-accounting decisions.
2. It provides an intuitive *sanity check*: at $n = 1$, the factor equals $1+i$ (pay back the principal plus one year's interest on it), and as $n \to \infty$ it tends to $i$ (interest only — principal is fully amortised by the infinite series).

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Frequently Lost Marks):**
> 1. **Mis-placing the cash flow at $t=0$ vs $t=1$:** In KTU valuation, the first uniform-series payment $A$ is *always* at the **end of period 1**, not at $t=0$. Drawing the wrong cash-flow diagram forfeits 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the gradient base year:** In a gradient series $0, G, 2G, \ldots, (n-1)G$, the **first non-zero** cash flow is at $t=2$, not $t=1$. The standard $(P/G, i, n)$ assumes the gradient *starts* with $G$ at $t=2$.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert $i$ to decimal:** A 12 % rate must be entered as 0.12, **not** 12. Common one-mark deduction.
> 4. **Rounding intermediates too early:** Keep at least **5–6 significant digits** until the final answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

A small engineering firm is evaluating the purchase of a CNC machine.

**(a)**  The machine costs **₹8,00,000** today. The firm borrows this amount at **12 % per annum effective**, to be repaid in **8 equal annual instalments** at the end of each year.
*Find the annual instalment using the capital-recovery factor.*   **(7 marks)**

**(b)**  Instead of borrowing, suppose the firm sets up a sinking fund into which it deposits an equal amount at the end of each year for 8 years. The sinking fund earns **9 % per annum**.
*Find the equal annual deposit that will accumulate to ₹8,00,000 at the end of 8 years, and compare it with the instalment in part (a). Comment on the result.*   **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Given:** $P = $ ₹8,00,000, $i = 12\% = 0.12$, $n = 8$.
**Required:** $A = ?$

Apply the **Capital-Recovery Factor**:

$$A = P \cdot (A/P, 12\%, 8) = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.12)^{8}$:

$$(1.12)^{2} = 1.2544$$

$$(1.12)^{4} = (1.2544)^{2} = 1.57351936$$

$$(1.12)^{8} = (1.57351936)^{2} \approx 2.47596317$$

Substitute:

$$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.47596317}{2.47596317 - 1}$$

$$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.297115580}{1.47596317}$$

$$A = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.20130282$$

$$\boxed{A \approx \text{₹}1{,}61{,}042.26 \text{ per year}}$$

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Given:** $F = $ ₹8,00,000, $i = 9\% = 0.09$, $n = 8$.
**Required:** $A_{\text{sink}} = ?$

Apply the **Sinking-Fund Factor**:

$$A_{\text{sink}} = F \cdot (A/F, 9\%, 8) = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$$

Compute $(1.09)^{8} \approx 1.99256264$ (computed earlier).

Substitute:

$$A_{\text{sink}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.09}{1.99256264 - 1}$$

$$A_{\text{sink}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.09}{0.99256264}$$

$$A_{\text{sink}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09067436$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{sink}} \approx \text{₹}72{,}539.49 \text{ per year}}$$

**Comment:**
The instalment in part (a) is much larger (₹1,61,042.26) because it includes **both** the sinking-fund deposit **and** the 12 % interest charge on the outstanding loan. In part (b), the firm finances the purchase from its own deposits and only needs the sinking-fund portion (₹72,539.49). The difference (₹1,61,042.26 − ₹72,539.49 ≈ ₹88,502.77) is essentially the annual interest burden of the borrowed money. **Conclusion:** if the firm has the discipline to invest annually, self-financing via a sinking fund is cheaper — but the cash-flow strain must be sustainable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

A civil-engineering project generates the following cash flow over **6 years**:

| Year | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash Flow (₹) | 0 | 10,000 | 20,000 | 30,000 | 40,000 | 50,000 |

The Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR) is **10 % per annum**.

**(a)**  Identify the cash-flow pattern, write the gradient form, and find the **present worth** at $i = 10\%$.   **(7 marks)**

**(b)**  Convert the same cash flow into an equivalent **uniform annual series** $A$ and find its value.   **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — Decompose the cash-flow pattern.**

The series is **0, G, 2G, 3G, 4G, 5G** with $G = $ ₹10,000. It is an **arithmetic gradient** (it increases by a constant $G$ each year). The first non-zero term is at $t = 2$ (consistent with the KTU standard $(P/G, i, n)$ convention).

**Step 2 — Apply the Gradient Present Worth Factor.**

For $i = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 6$, $G = $ ₹10,000:

$$(P/G, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - i n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$$

Compute $(1.10)^{6}$:

$$(1.10)^{2} = 1.21$$

$$(1.10)^{3} = 1.331$$

$$(1.10)^{6} = (1.331)^{2} = 1.771561$$

Compute numerator:

$$(1.10)^{6} - i n - 1 = 1.771561 - (0.10 \times 6) - 1 = 1.771561 - 0.60 - 1 = 0.171561$$

Compute denominator:

$$i^{2}(1+i)^{n} = (0.10)^{2} \times 1.771561 = 0.01 \times 1.771561 = 0.01771561$$

Therefore:

$$(P/G, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.171561}{0.01771561} \approx 9.6842$$

**Step 3 — Compute present worth.**

$$P = G \cdot (P/G, 10\%, 6) = 10{,}000 \times 9.6842$$

$$\boxed{P \approx \text{₹}96{,}842}$$

---

**Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Use the equivalence between $P$ and $A$ via the Capital-Recovery Factor.**

$$(A/P, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.771561}{1.771561 - 1} = \dfrac{0.1771561}{0.771561} \approx 0.22961$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A$ from $P$.**

$$A = P \cdot (A/P, 10\%, 6) = 96{,}842 \times 0.22961$$

$$\boxed{A \approx \text{₹}22{,}238.50 \text{ per year}}$$

*(Alternative direct method:* $A = G \cdot (A/G, 10\%, 6)$ where $(A/G, 10\%, 6) = (P/G, 10\%, 6)\cdot(A/P, 10\%, 6) = 9.6842 \times 0.22961 \approx 2.2238$, so $A = 10{,}000 \times 2.2238 \approx $ ₹22,238. Matches exactly.)*

**Interpretation:** A uniform annual receipt of ₹22,238.50 over 6 years is economically equivalent at $i=10\%$ to the original increasing cash-flow stream — useful for comparing this project with a flat-amount alternative.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Compound Interest Factors)**

- **Master factor** (foundation of all others): $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$. Memorise this *first*.
- **Reciprocal pairs (always true):** $(F/P) \cdot (P/F) = 1$, $(F/A) \cdot (A/F) = 1$, $(A/P) \cdot (P/A) = 1$.
- **Composition rule:** $(P/A, i, n) = (P/F, i, n) \cdot (F/A, i, n)$ — useful for two-step derivations in exam.
- **Capital-recovery identity:** $(A/P, i, n) = i + (A/F, i, n)$ — *favourite KTU short-answer question*.
- **Boundary cases:** $i = 0$ → factors collapse to their linear/numeric form; $n = 0$ → every factor equals 1.
- **Cash-flow diagram conventions:** $P$ at $t=0$, $A$ at the **end** of each period (starting $t=1$), $F$ at $t=n$, gradient $G$ starting at $t=2$ with increments of $G$ thereafter.
- **Decimal form for $i$:** Always convert percentages (e.g. 12 % → 0.12) before substituting.
- **Effective vs nominal rate:** If compounding is annual, the quoted rate *is* the effective rate. If compounding is monthly/quarterly, use $i_{\text{eff}} = (1 + i_{\text{nom}}/m)^{m} - 1$ *first*.
- **Numerical precision:** Carry at least 5–6 digits for intermediate factors; round only the **final answer**.
- **Most-examined conversions:** $P \rightarrow A$ (capital recovery), $F \rightarrow A$ (sinking fund), and gradient-to-uniform (real projects rarely have flat cash flows).
- **Typical KTU module weightage:** 14-mark long answers almost always use 2 of the 8 factors in one question; Part A 3-markers focus on the *meaning* of a single factor and a quick formula statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interest Tables and Evaluating Alternatives by Equivalence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interest Tables and Evaluating Alternatives by Equivalence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Interest Tables — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Interest Tables (Standard Engineering Economy Factor Tables):** Tabular compilations of numerical values of the **six (six plus two gradient) standard interest factors** used to convert cash flows occurring at different points in time into equivalent values at a common reference time, given a specified interest rate *i* and number of periods *n*.

The factors are functions of two variables: the **interest rate per compounding period (i)** and the **number of compounding periods (n)**. Engineers read these tables instead of computing $(1+i)^n$ manually, dramatically speeding up engineering economy calculations.

### 1.2 Equivalence — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence of Cash Flows:** Two cash flow patterns are **economically equivalent** at a specified interest rate *i* if their values are equal when both are referenced to the same point in time. Equivalence is therefore a function of **three things**: the interest rate used, the point in time chosen, and the cash flow values themselves.

**Symbolic Statement:**
$$\text{Cash Flow A} \equiv \text{Cash Flow B} \iff V_{\text{A at time } t} = V_{\text{B at time } t} \text{ at interest rate } i$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Money Time-Machine":**

Imagine you have a friend, **Rupee (₹)**, who can travel through time. ₹100 today is NOT the same as ₹100 received five years from now — because the ₹100 today could be invested at 10% per year and become ₹161.05 after 5 years. So ₹100 **today** is economically equivalent to ₹161.05 **after 5 years** at i = 10%.

* **Interest Tables** are like a **"translation dictionary"** that tells you exactly how much ₹1 today is worth in the future (or past, or annually, etc.) at any given interest rate.
* **Equivalence** is the principle that allows you to **compare apples and oranges** in engineering projects — a single lump-sum bid, a 10-year monthly lease, and a 30-year operating cost can all be "translated" into the same language (e.g., Present Worth) and then fairly compared.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Interest Rate Convention in KTU:** *i* is expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., 10% = **0.10**, not 10). The interest rate *i* and number of periods *n* must be in **the same units** (both years, both months, etc.). For effective annual rate, n = years; for monthly compounding at 12% annual, use i = 0.01 and n = 12·t.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six (Plus Two) Standard Interest Factors

Engineering economy is built on **six core interest factors** (sometimes extended to eight with the two gradient factors). Every factor is a function of *i* and *n* and represents a *ratio* of two equivalent cash flows.

#### Why These Six Factors?

* Engineers routinely encounter: a single payment now vs. later, equal annual payments, and a future sum.
* By combining these "primitives," any complex cash flow can be reduced to a single equivalent value.
* KTU examiners test these factors directly because they form the *spine* of Module 4.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Factor

**Factor 1 — Single Payment Compound Amount (F/P, i, n)**
* **What:** The future value of ₹1 invested at rate *i* for *n* periods.
* **How:** Each period multiplies the principal by (1+i). After *n* periods: $(1+i)^n$.
* **Use:** Convert a present single payment into a future value.

**Factor 2 — Single Payment Present Worth (P/F, i, n)**
* **What:** The present value of ₹1 received after *n* periods.
* **How:** Discount $(1+i)^n$ back to time zero. Mathematically the reciprocal of Factor 1.
* **Use:** Convert a future single payment into a present value.

**Factor 3 — Uniform Series Sinking Fund (A/F, i, n)**
* **What:** The equal annual end-of-period deposit *A* required to accumulate to ₹1 at the end of *n* periods.
* **How:** The future sum of an arithmetic gradient of *A*'s equals 1.
* **Use:** Bond retirement funds, replacement reserves.

**Factor 4 — Uniform Series Compound Amount (F/A, i, n)**
* **What:** The future value at the end of *n* periods of an annuity of ₹1 deposited at the end of each period.
* **How:** Geometric series sum of $1\cdot(1+i)^{n-1}$ terms.
* **Use:** Compute the future worth of equal annual payments.

**Factor 5 — Capital Recovery (A/P, i, n)**
* **What:** The equal annual end-of-period payment *A* required over *n* periods to recover (pay back) a present amount of ₹1 plus interest.
* **How:** Multiply (P/F, i, n) by (F/A, i, n) and combine.
* **Use:** Convert a present cost into equivalent annual cost — *the most-used factor in KTU problems*.

**Factor 6 — Uniform Series Present Worth (P/A, i, n)**
* **What:** The present value of an annuity of ₹1 received at the end of each of *n* consecutive periods.
* **How:** Multiply (P/F, i, n) by (F/A, i, n) and combine.
* **Use:** Convert annual operating costs into a single present capital equivalent.

**Factor 7 — Uniform Gradient Present Worth (P/G, i, n)**
* **What:** Present value of a cash flow series that increases linearly by ₹1 each period, starting from 0 in year 1.
* **Use:** Models costs that grow by a fixed amount annually (e.g., maintenance rising by ₹500/year).

**Factor 8 — Uniform Gradient to Uniform Series (A/G, i, n)**
* **What:** Annual equivalent of the same gradient series.
* **Use:** Used together with P/A to evaluate arithmetic-gradient cash flows.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL: no `|` symbols in tables)

| Factor Notation | Engineering Economy Symbol | Mathematical Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Single Payment Compound Amount | $(F/P,\,i,\,n)$ | $(1+i)^{n}$ | Find future value of a present sum |
| Single Payment Present Worth | $(P/F,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | Find present value of a future sum |
| Uniform Series Sinking Fund | $(A/F,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Find annual deposit to reach a future sum |
| Uniform Series Compound Amount | $(F/A,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | Find future value of equal annual payments |
| Capital Recovery | $(A/P,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Convert present cost to annual cost |
| Uniform Series Present Worth | $(P/A,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Convert annual series to present value |
| Uniform Gradient Present Worth | $(P/G,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-in-1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ | Present value of arithmetic gradient series |
| Uniform Gradient to Series | $(A/G,\,i,\,n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{i}-\dfrac{n}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Annual equivalent of arithmetic gradient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocal Rules (memorize these for KTU):**
> $(F/P,i,n)\cdot(P/F,i,n) = 1$   |   $(F/A,i,n)\cdot(A/F,i,n) = 1$   |   $(P/A,i,n)\cdot(A/P,i,n) = 1$
> **Product Rule:** $(P/A,i,n) = (P/F,i,n)\cdot(F/A,i,n)$

### 2.4 The Concept of Equivalence — Deeper

Two cash flow patterns are equivalent at interest rate *i* if they yield **the same economic value at the same point in time**. Three critical corollaries follow:

1. **Equivalence is interest-rate dependent.** Two cash flows that are equivalent at 8% may not be equivalent at 12%.
2. **Equivalence is reference-point dependent.** ₹100 today equals ₹110 in one year (at 10%) equals ₹121 in two years — but only at i = 10%.
3. **Equivalence is the foundation of all KTU evaluation methods:** PW, AW, FW, IRR, B/C ratio — all rely on reducing alternatives to a common reference at the same rate.

### 2.5 Methods of Evaluating Alternatives by Equivalence

KTU recognizes **seven equivalence-based methods**. The student must choose based on:
* Type of cash flow (single, uniform, gradient, mixed)
* Required decision (pick one, rank all, or replace)
* Available data (MARR known? cash flows equal lives?)

**Summary of Methods:**

| # | Method | Reference Point | Decision Rule | Best For |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Present Worth (PW) | Time 0 | Maximize PW (for revenue) / Minimize (for cost) | Mutually exclusive alternatives |
| 2 | Future Worth (FW) | Time *n* | Same as PW | When future is decision point |
| 3 | Annual Worth (AW) | Every year | Maximize AW / Minimize | Different-life alternatives, common MARR |
| 4 | Capitalized Cost (CC) | Infinite horizon | Minimize CC | Perpetual projects (dams, roads) |
| 5 | Rate of Return (ROR / IRR) | Internal | Select if ROR ≥ MARR | When MARR not given |
| 6 | Benefit-Cost (B–C) Ratio | B/C | Select if B/C ≥ 1 | Public-sector projects |
| 7 | Payback Period | Time to recover | Accept if ≤ target | Quick screening, not rigorous |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

* **Public infrastructure:** Comparing a bridge built now vs. a tunnel built later using **CC (Capitalized Cost)**.
* **Industrial machinery:** Choosing between two CNC machines using **AW (Annual Worth)** when their lives differ.
* **Energy projects:** Solar vs. wind farm selection using **PW (Present Worth)** over 25 years.
* **KTU pattern:** Most Module 4 numericals use PW or AW, with **incremental analysis** for mutually exclusive options.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Factor 1: Single Payment Compound Amount $(F/P, i, n)$

**Setup:** Invest ₹P today at interest rate *i* per period, compounded for *n* periods. Find F.

**Step 1 — Period 1:** $F_1 = P + P\cdot i = P(1+i)$

**Step 2 — Period 2:** $F_2 = F_1 + F_1\cdot i = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2}$

**Step 3 — General Period *k*:** $F_k = P(1+i)^{k}$

**Step 4 — Period *n* (final):** $F_n = P(1+i)^{n}$

$$\therefore (F/P,\,i,\,n) = (1+i)^{n} \quad\blacksquare$$
