**Conversion Logic:** Each period the principal is multiplied by $(1+i)$ because interest is earned on the *new* principal (compounding). After *n* such multiplications, the result is $(1+i)^n$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Factor 2: Single Payment Present Worth $(P/F, i, n)$

**Setup:** We know $F = P(1+i)^n$ from §3.1. Solve for P.

**Step 1 — Isolate P:** $P = F\div(1+i)^n$

**Step 2 — Rewrite as factor:**

$$\therefore (P/F,\,i,\,n) = \frac{1}{(1+i)^{n}} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Conversion Logic:** Discounting is the inverse of compounding. We "remove" *n* periods of growth by dividing by $(1+i)^n$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Factor 4: Uniform Series Compound Amount $(F/A, i, n)$

**Setup:** Deposit ₹A at the *end* of each year for *n* years at rate *i*. Find F (future value at end of year *n*).

**Step 1 — Cash Flow Diagram (conceptual):**
At the end of year 1, ₹A is deposited. It earns interest for (n−1) more years. At the end of year 2, ₹A is deposited. It earns interest for (n−2) more years, and so on. The last deposit (year *n*) earns no interest.

**Step 2 — Sum of each deposit's future value:**

$$F = A(1+i)^{n-1} + A(1+i)^{n-2} + \cdots + A(1+i)^{1} + A(1+i)^{0}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize geometric series** with first term $a_1 = A$, ratio $r = (1+i)$, and $n$ terms.

**Step 4 — Apply geometric sum formula** $S_n = a_1\cdot\dfrac{r^{n}-1}{r-1}$:

$$F = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify denominator:**

$$\therefore (F/A,\,i,\,n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i} \quad\blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Factor 6: Uniform Series Present Worth $(P/A, i, n)$

**Setup:** Receive ₹A at the end of each year for *n* years. Find P (present value at time 0).

**Step 1 — Discount each payment to time 0:**

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Geometric series with** $a_1 = A/(1+i)$, ratio $r = 1/(1+i)$, $n$ terms.

**Step 3 — Sum formula:**

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left[\frac{1}{(1+i)}\right]^{n}}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify denominator** $1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)} = \frac{i}{1+i}$:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$:**

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$\therefore (P/A,\,i,\,n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \quad\blacksquare$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Factor 5: Capital Recovery $(A/P, i, n)$

**Setup:** Borrow ₹P today at rate *i*. Repay in equal installments *A* at end of each of *n* years. Find A in terms of P.

**Step 1 — Use Factor 6 relationship** in reverse:

$$(A/P,\,i,\,n) = \frac{1}{(P/A,\,i,\,n)} = \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$

$$\therefore A = P \cdot \frac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Conversion Logic:** Capital recovery is the *reciprocal* of present worth of annuity. It tells us the *equivalent uniform annual cost* of a present investment — this is the workhorse of KTU's "AW method."

### 3.6 Derivation of Factor 3: Sinking Fund $(A/F, i, n)$

**Setup:** Accumulate ₹F in *n* years using equal end-of-year deposits *A* at rate *i*.

**Step 1 — From Factor 4:** $F = A\cdot\dfrac{(1+i)^n-1}{i}$

**Step 2 — Solve for A:**

$$\therefore (A/F,\,i,\,n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \quad\blacksquare$$

### 3.7 Derivation of Factor 7: Uniform Gradient Present Worth $(P/G, i, n)$

**Setup:** A series of cash flows: 0, G, 2G, 3G, ..., (n−1)G at end of years 1, 2, ..., n. Find P at i.

**Step 1 — Write the present value of each gradient cash flow:**

$$P = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{(k-1)G}{(1+i)^{k}}$$

**Step 2 — Re-index** with j = k−1:

$$P = G \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \frac{j}{(1+i)^{j+1}}$$

**Step 3 — Use the standard reduction** (see Park's Contemporary Engineering Economics derivation; omitting intermediate algebra to keep notes compact for KTU):

$$P = G \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - in - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$\therefore (P/G,\,i,\,n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n} - in - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Conversion Logic:** The gradient series is the difference of two shifted annuity series. Differentiating the (P/A) formula with respect to *i* gives the gradient result.

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive

**Problem:** A machine costs ₹50,000 today. It saves ₹15,000 per year in operating costs at the end of each year for 6 years, after which it has a salvage value of ₹8,000. The interest rate is 10% per year. Determine:
(a) Present Worth of the investment.
(b) Equivalent Uniform Annual Worth.
(c) Future Worth at end of year 6.
(d) State whether to accept (MARR = 10%).

**Solution:**

**(a) Present Worth**

**Step 1 — PW of savings (annuity of ₹15,000 for 6 years):**

$$PW_{\text{savings}} = 15{,}000 \cdot (P/A,\,10\%,\,6)$$

**Step 2 — From formula sheet:** $(P/A,\,10\%,\,6) = \dfrac{(1.10)^6 - 1}{0.10\cdot(1.10)^6} = \dfrac{1.771561 - 1}{0.10\cdot 1.771561} = \dfrac{0.771561}{0.1771561} = 4.3553$

$$PW_{\text{savings}} = 15{,}000 \cdot 4.3553 = 65{,}329.50$$

**Step 3 — PW of salvage value (single payment at year 6):**

$$PW_{\text{salvage}} = 8{,}000 \cdot (P/F,\,10\%,\,6) = 8{,}000 \cdot \frac{1}{(1.10)^{6}} = 8{,}000 \cdot 0.5645 = 4{,}515.85$$

**Step 4 — Total PW of benefits:**
$$PW_{\text{benefits}} = 65{,}329.50 + 4{,}515.85 = 69{,}845.35$$

**Step 5 — Net PW (benefit − cost):**
$$PW_{\text{net}} = 69{,}845.35 - 50{,}000 = 19{,}845.35$$

**(b) Annual Worth**

**Step 1 — Convert net PW to AW using Capital Recovery factor:**

$$AW = 19{,}845.35 \cdot (A/P,\,10\%,\,6)$$

**Step 2 — From formula sheet:** $(A/P,\,10\%,\,6) = \dfrac{0.10\cdot(1.10)^6}{(1.10)^6 - 1} = \dfrac{0.177156}{0.771561} = 0.22961$

$$AW = 19{,}845.35 \cdot 0.22961 = 4{,}556.65 \text{ per year}$$

**(c) Future Worth at end of Year 6**

**Step 1 — Convert net PW to FW:**

$$FW = 19{,}845.35 \cdot (F/P,\,10\%,\,6) = 19{,}845.35 \cdot (1.10)^{6} = 19{,}845.35 \cdot 1.771561 = 35{,}160.10$$

**(d) Decision**

Since $PW_{\text{net}} = 19{,}845.35 > 0$ and $AW = 4{,}556.65 > 0$ at MARR = 10%, **ACCEPT the machine.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Check:** All three methods yield the *same* accept/reject decision. This is the practical power of equivalence: $PW > 0 \iff AW > 0 \iff FW > 0$ at any common interest rate. KTU examiners may ask students to "verify equivalence."

### 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Equivalence Verification

| Method | Reference Time | Numerical Value | Decision |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| PW | t = 0 | ₹19,845.35 | Accept (PW > 0) |
| AW | End of each year | ₹4,556.65 / year | Accept (AW > 0) |
| FW | t = 6 years | ₹35,160.10 | Accept (FW > 0) |

**Proof of equivalence:** $AW = PW\cdot(A/P,i,n) = 19{,}845.35 \cdot 0.22961 = 4{,}556.65$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Equivalence-Based Evaluation Workflow

The diagram below maps the *decision workflow* a KTU student should follow when choosing an equivalence method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Project Cash Flow Data] --> B[Identify Cash Flow Pattern]
    B --> B1[Single Payment]
    B --> B2[Uniform Series]
    B --> B3[Arithmetic Gradient]
    B --> B4[Mixed Pattern]
    B1 --> C1[Use F-P or P-F Factor]
    B2 --> C2[Use F-A, A-F, P-A, or A-P Factor]
    B3 --> C3[Use P-G or A-G Factor]
    B4 --> C4[Decompose into Series and Sum]
    C1 --> D[Choose Reference Time Point]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> D1[Time Zero: Present Worth PW]
    D --> D2[End of Life: Future Worth FW]
    D --> D3[Per Year: Annual Worth AW]
    D1 --> E[Apply Decision Rule]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Revenue: Maximize Equivalent Value]
    E --> E2[Cost: Minimize Equivalent Value]
    E1 --> F[Recommended Alternative]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[END]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: Inter-relationships of the Six Interest Factors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[P at t=0] -->|Multiply F-P i n| F1[F at t=n]
    F1 -->|Multiply P-F i n| P1
    P2[P at t=0] -->|Multiply A-P i n| A2[A at end of each year]
    A2 -->|Multiply P-A i n| P2
    A2 -->|Multiply F-A i n| F2[F at t=n]
    F2 -->|Multiply A-F i n| A2
    G3[Gradient G] -->|Multiply P-G i n| P3[P at t=0]
    P3 -->|Multiply A-G i n times A-P| A3[Convert to Annual]
    P3 -->|Add to P-A component| A3
    style P1 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style F1 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style A2 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style F2 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style P3 fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style A3 fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Cash Flow Timeline (Block Schematic for Equivalence)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Cash Flow Equivalence Timeline (t=0 to t=n years)
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat %Y
    section Single Payment Pattern
    Initial Outlay P   :crit, p1, 2024, 1y
    Future Receipt F   :p2, 2029, 1y
    section Uniform Series Pattern
    Year 1 to n Annuity A :active, a1, 2024, 6y
    section Gradient Pattern
    Year 1 Zero G0    :g0, 2024, 1y
    Year 2 G1         :g1, 2025, 1y
    Year 3 G2         :g2, 2026, 1y
    Year n Gn minus 1 :gn, 2029, 1y
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents an *engineering decision node*. A KTU student can trace a problem from "START" → "Cash Flow Pattern" → "Reference Point" → "Decision Rule" to determine which method to use. The block schematic replaces complex physical drawings with functional architecture as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the concept of equivalence in engineering economy. Why is the interest rate critical in determining equivalence?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
* **Definition (2 marks):** Equivalence is the condition under which two different cash flow patterns have the same economic value at the same point in time when compared at a specified interest rate. Mathematically, Cash Flow A and Cash Flow B are equivalent at rate *i* and time *t* if $V_A(t) = V_B(t)$.
* **Role of Interest Rate (1 mark):** The interest rate determines the *time value of money*. Two cash flows equivalent at 8% will *not* be equivalent at 12%, because higher interest rates attach more value to near-term cash. Thus, the MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) is a critical input in every equivalence calculation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**State any three standard interest factors used in engineering economy and write their mathematical expressions in terms of *i* and *n*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Single Payment Compound Amount** $(F/P,i,n) = (1+i)^{n}$
2. **Uniform Series Present Worth** $(P/A,i,n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$
3. **Capital Recovery** $(A/P,i,n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — Alternative 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $(P/A, i, n)$ starting from first principles. Show every algebraic step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the cash flow series (1 mark):**
An annuity of ₹A received at the end of each year for *n* years. Present value P is sought at time 0.

**Step 2 — Discount each payment to time 0 (1 mark):**
The first payment at end of year 1 is discounted by 1 period; the second by 2 periods, ..., the *n*-th by *n* periods.

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^{1}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{2}} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{3}} + \cdots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize geometric series (1 mark):**
First term $a_1 = A/(1+i)$, common ratio $r = 1/(1+i)$, total terms $= n$.

**Step 4 — Apply geometric sum formula (2 marks):**

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left[\frac{1}{(1+i)}\right]^{n}}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify denominator (1 mark):**
$1 - \frac{1}{(1+i)} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{(1+i)} = \frac{i}{(1+i)}$

**Step 6 — Combine (1 mark):**
$$P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i} = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$$

$$\therefore (P/A,\,i,\,n) = \frac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} \quad\blacksquare$$

**(b) A company is evaluating two machines X and Y. Machine X costs ₹3,00,000 with annual operating cost ₹40,000 for 8 years. Machine Y costs ₹4,50,000 with annual operating cost ₹25,000 for 10 years. Both have negligible salvage. Using the Present Worth method at i = 12%, which machine should be selected? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify that lives differ (1 mark):**
X has life = 8 years, Y has life = 10 years. Use PW method with the stated MARR (no need to repeat lives if MARR is given and we just need a single PW).

**Step 2 — Compute PW of Machine X (2 marks):**
$(P/A, 12\%, 8) = \frac{(1.12)^{8}-1}{0.12\cdot(1.12)^{8}}$

$(1.12)^{8} = 2.4760$ (using standard factor table)

$(P/A, 12\%, 8) = \frac{2.4760 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 2.4760} = \frac{1.4760}{0.29712} = 4.9676$

$PW_X = 3{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}000 \cdot 4.9676 = 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}98{,}704 = 4{,}98{,}704$

**Step 3 — Compute PW of Machine Y (2 marks):**
$(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{(1.12)^{10}-1}{0.12\cdot(1.12)^{10}}$

$(1.12)^{10} = 3.1058$

$(P/A, 12\%, 10) = \frac{3.1058 - 1}{0.12 \cdot 3.1058} = \frac{2.1058}{0.37270} = 5.6502$

$PW_Y = 4{,}50{,}000 + 25{,}000 \cdot 5.6502 = 4{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}41{,}255 = 5{,}91{,}255$

**Step 4 — Compare (1 mark):**
$PW_X = 4{,}98{,}704 < PW_Y = 5{,}91{,}255$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**
Since Machine X has the lower PW of costs, **select Machine X.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B / 7-mark numericals):**
> * **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *i* notation — using 12 instead of 0.12 in the formula. KTU deducts **0.5 mark** for this.
> * **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the *initial* cost when computing PW of cost alternatives (it must be added, not replaced by an annuity).
> * **Common Mistake 3:** Comparing PWs of *unequal-life* alternatives directly without stating the assumption (either repeat lives on LCM basis or accept unequal lives with stated MARR).
> * **Common Mistake 4:** Not mentioning the *decision rule* explicitly — KTU awards the final 1 mark for a clear "select X because ..." sentence.

---

#### **QUESTION B — Alternative 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a) The maintenance cost of a machine is expected to be ₹5,000 in the first year, increasing by ₹1,000 every year thereafter up to the 6th year. At an interest rate of 10% per year, find the present worth of the maintenance cost using the gradient factor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flow pattern (1 mark):**
The series is 5,000, 6,000, 7,000, 8,000, 9,000, 10,000. This is a *base annuity* of ₹5,000 plus a *gradient* of 0, 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000, 5,000 (where G = 1,000).

**Step 2 — Decompose into two parts (1 mark):**
$PW = PW_{\text{base annuity}} + PW_{\text{gradient}}$

**Step 3 — Compute PW of base annuity (2 marks):**
$(P/A, 10\%, 6) = 4.3553$ (from formula or table)
$PW_{\text{base}} = 5{,}000 \cdot 4.3553 = 21{,}776.50$

**Step 4 — Compute PW of gradient (2 marks):**
$(P/G, 10\%, 6) = \frac{(1.10)^{6} - 6(0.10) - 1}{(0.10)^{2}\cdot(1.10)^{6}}$

$(1.10)^{6} = 1.771561$, so numerator $= 1.771561 - 0.6 - 1 = 0.171561$
Denominator $= 0.01 \cdot 1.771561 = 0.01771561$

$(P/G, 10\%, 6) = 0.171561 / 0.01771561 = 9.6842$

$PW_{\text{gradient}} = 1{,}000 \cdot 9.6842 = 9{,}684.20$

**Step 5 — Total PW (1 mark):**
$PW_{\text{total}} = 21{,}776.50 + 9{,}684.20 = 31{,}460.70$

$$\therefore PW_{\text{maintenance}} = ₹31{,}460.70 \quad\blacksquare$$

**(b) Two mutually exclusive investment proposals A and B are available. Proposal A requires an initial investment of ₹2,00,000 and generates a uniform annual benefit of ₹60,000 for 5 years. Proposal B requires ₹3,00,000 and gives an annual benefit of ₹85,000 for 6 years. Both have zero salvage. Using incremental Rate of Return analysis at MARR = 12%, recommend the better alternative. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute individual ROR for the cheaper alternative (Proposal A) (1 mark):**
Set $PW_A = 0$: $2{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}000 \cdot (P/A, i^{*}, 5)$
$(P/A, i^{*}, 5) = 3.3333$

From standard factor tables, this corresponds to $i^{*} \approx 15.24\%$ (between 15% and 16%).

**Step 2 — Test Proposal A against MARR (1 mark):**
Since $i^{*}_A = 15.24\% > 12\% = \text{MARR}$, Proposal A is *individually acceptable*. Proceed to incremental analysis.

**Step 3 — Compute incremental investment and incremental benefit (1 mark):**
$\Delta P = 3{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$
$\Delta A = 85{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 25{,}000$ per year for 6 years (use the LCM of lives or use shorter life with reinvestment assumption).

**Step 4 — Compute incremental ROR using 6-year life (1 mark):**
Set $1{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}000 \cdot (P/A, \Delta i, 6)$
$(P/A, \Delta i, 6) = 4.0000$

From standard tables, $(P/A, 8\%, 6) = 4.6229$ and $(P/A, 12\%, 6) = 4.1114$. So $\Delta i$ is between 12% and 8%. Closer to 12%:
$(P/A, 13\%, 6) = 3.9975 \approx 4.0000$ ✓

**Step 5 — Compare $\Delta i$ to MARR (1 mark):**
$\Delta i \approx 13\% > 12\% = \text{MARR}$

**Step 6 — Decision (1 mark):**
The incremental investment earns more than MARR, so choose the **higher-cost alternative — Proposal B.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Incremental ROR):**
> * **Common Mistake 1:** Computing the *individual* ROR and stopping there. KTU requires **incremental ROR** for mutually exclusive alternatives. Loss of up to **3 marks** if this is missed.
> * **Common Mistake 2:** Computing $\Delta i$ on the wrong life (use the *common* period of comparison or the LCM).
> * **Common Mistake 3:** Reversing the increment direction. Always subtract *defender* (lower-cost) from *challenger* (higher-cost). If $\Delta i < \text{MARR}$, keep the defender.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Six standard interest factors + 2 gradient factors** form the entire KTU Module 4 computational core.
- [ ] **Notation** $(X/Y, i, n)$ reads: "find X given Y at rate *i* over *n* periods."
- [ ] **Reciprocal pairs:** $(F/P)\cdot(P/F)=1$, $(F/A)\cdot(A/F)=1$, $(P/A)\cdot(A/P)=1$.
- [ ] **Product rule:** $(P/A,i,n) = (P/F,i,n)\cdot(F/A,i,n)$.
- [ ] **Equivalence** is rate-dependent and reference-point dependent — always state both.
- [ ] **PW = 0 ⇔ AW = 0 ⇔ FW = 0** at a common rate → same decision.
- [ ] **Use PW** for single-decision-point comparison; **use AW** for unequal-life alternatives; **use FW** when decision is at end of study period.
- [ ] **Capitalized Cost (CC)** is just PW with $n \to \infty$ → $CC = A/i$ for a perpetual uniform series.
- [ ] **Gradient factors** handle arithmetic-gradient cash flows — break any such series into a constant part + a gradient part.
- [ ] **Incremental ROR** is mandatory for *mutually exclusive* alternatives — never rely on individual ROR alone.
- [ ] **Interest rate convention:** Always use *decimal* form (10% → 0.10). Mismatch of units between *i* and *n* causes silent errors.
- [ ] **Most-tested KTU pattern:** (i) Compute PW, AW, or FW using two or three factors. (ii) Apply a decision rule. (iii) Verify equivalence as a cross-check.
- [ ] **Final-year project tips:** When unsure of method, choose **PW** — it is the most universally applicable equivalence method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Worth Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Present Worth Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Present Worth (PW) Comparison** is a fundamental technique in **Engineering Economics** used to evaluate and rank mutually exclusive investment alternatives by converting all future cash inflows and outflows to a **single equivalent value at time zero** (the present), using the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)** as the discount rate.

The technique is formally stated as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Present Worth is the equivalent worth, measured in today's monetary units, of all cash flows (revenues and disbursements) occurring over the life of a project, evaluated at an established interest rate `i` (MARR), with time zero taken as the reference point.*

When applied to **two or more alternatives**, the method is called **Present Worth Comparison** (or Present Worth Analysis). The **Net Present Worth (NPW / NPV)** is computed as:

$$NPW = PW \text{ of Benefits} - PW \text{ of Costs}$$

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Shopkeeper's Lens"

Imagine two shopkeepers, **Ravi** and **Sneha**, who both promise to pay you **₹1,00,000** — but in different years:
- Ravi: pays you in **5 years**
- Sneha: pays you in **10 years**

Which promise is worth more today? The answer is **Ravi's**, because money received earlier can be reinvested and grow.

Now scale this logic to a real engineering problem:
> An engineer choosing between **Machine A** (low cost, high operating expense) and **Machine B** (high cost, low operating expense) over a **10-year period** needs a single "today's rupee" value to compare them fairly. That single number is the **Present Worth**.

Just as the shopkeeper discounts future rupees back to today, the engineer discounts every future cash flow to **Year 0** to make a fair, apples-to-apples comparison.

## 1.3 Key Terms (Board-Exam Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Terminology for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - **MARR (i%):** Minimum Attractive Rate of Return — the lowest rate of return a project must earn to be acceptable.
> - **PW:** Present Worth of a single cash flow or series.
> - **NPW:** Net Present Worth = PW of all positive cash flows − PW of all negative cash flows.
> - **Service Life (n):** Useful life of the alternative in years.
> - **Salvage Value (SV):** Expected market value at the end of service life.
> - **Capitalized Cost (CC):** Present worth of an alternative assumed to **last forever** (perpetuity).
> - **LCM (Least Common Multiple):** Common analysis horizon when comparing alternatives with **unequal** service lives.

## 1.4 Why PW Comparison? (Board Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PW comparison is the **most theoretically sound** discounted-cash-flow method because:
> 1. It converts all flows to a **single time base** (Year 0).
> 2. It correctly accounts for the **time value of money**.
> 3. It is mathematically equivalent to **Annual Worth (AW)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** analysis when alternatives are **independent or revenue-generating** (mutually exclusive revenue projects are best evaluated on an **incremental** basis).
> 4. It is directly supported by **standard factor tables**: `(P/F, i, n)`, `(P/A, i, n)`, `(P/G, i, n)`, and `(F/P, i, n)`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Backbone — How PW Comparison Works

PW comparison follows a structured decision logic. Below is the step-by-step methodology the KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer:

1. **Establish the MARR (`i`)** — given in the problem (typically 8%, 10%, 12%, or 15%).
2. **Verify the alternatives are comparable**:
   - **Same service life?** → Direct PW comparison is valid.
   - **Different service lives?** → Use either (a) **LCM assumption (repeatability)**, or (b) a **stated study period**.
3. **Estimate all cash flows** for each alternative:
   - First cost (P) at `t = 0`
   - Annual operating & maintenance (AOM) costs
   - Annual revenues / savings
   - Salvage value (SV) at end of life
4. **Discount every future cash flow to `t = 0`** using the appropriate factor.
5. **Compute NPW** for each alternative.
6. **Apply the decision rule** (see §2.3).
7. **Sensitivity check** (if asked) — vary `i` to test robustness.

## 2.2 Core Mathematics — Discounting Logic

The fundamental discounting identity that underpins every PW calculation is:

$$P = F \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} = F \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

For uniform series of cash flows `A` over `n` periods:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$$

For a **uniform gradient series** G (arithmetic gradient):

$$P = G \cdot \left[ \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2 (1+i)^n} \right] = G \cdot (P/G, i, n)$$

## 2.3 Decision Rules for PW Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Decision Rules:**
>
> **For Independent Projects (Accept/Reject):**
> - Accept if `NPW ≥ 0` at the MARR.
>
> **For Mutually Exclusive Cost-Only Alternatives (Do-Nothing possible):**
> - Compute PW of **costs only**.
> - Select the alternative with the **least cost** (lowest |NPW| of costs, ignoring negative sign for cost).
>
> **For Mutually Exclusive Revenue/Service Alternatives:**
> - Compute NPW of benefits minus costs.
> - Select the alternative with the **maximum NPW (≥ 0)**.
> - If using **incremental analysis** (B − A): select the higher-cost alternative only if `ΔNPW(B−A) ≥ 0`.

## 2.4 Equal Service Life vs. Unequal Service Life

| Situation | Recommended Method | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Equal service life** | Direct PW comparison | Common horizon, no repeatability needed. |
| **Unequal service life, finite analysis** | **LCM (Least Common Multiple)** of lives | Assumes the chosen alternative is *repeated* up to the LCM year. |
| **Unequal service life, indefinite** | **Capitalized Cost (CC)** | Convert each alternative to a perpetual annual cost, compare AOCs. |
| **Stated study period** | Use that period explicitly | Common in replacement analysis. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **master formula table** for all PW-comparison problems. Memorize the factor notations — KTU uses the **standard factor symbols** in the question paper.

| # | Quantity Wanted | Given | Factor Name | Formula / Notation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Present Worth P | Future F, n yrs | Single Payment Present Worth | $P = F \cdot (P/F, i, n)$ |
| 2 | Future F | Present P, n yrs | Single Payment Compound Amount | $F = P \cdot (F/P, i, n)$ |
| 3 | Present P | Uniform A, n yrs | Uniform Series Present Worth | $P = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$ |
| 4 | Uniform A | Present P, n yrs | Capital Recovery | $A = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)$ |
| 5 | Present P | Gradient G, n yrs | Arithmetic Gradient PW | $P = G \cdot (P/G, i, n)$ |
| 6 | Capitalized Cost (perpetual) | Annual A | Capitalized Cost | $CC = \dfrac{A}{i}$ |
| 7 | NPW of project | Mixed flows | Net Present Worth | $NPW = \Sigma PW_\text{inflows} - \Sigma PW_\text{outflows}$ |
| 8 | Incremental NPW | Alt B − Alt A | Incremental PW | $\Delta NPW = NPW_B - NPW_A$ |

> Where `(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}`, `(P/A, i, n) = [(1+i)^n - 1] / [i(1+i)^n]`, `(P/G, i, n) = [(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1] / [i^2(1+i)^n]`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PW comparison is the **backbone of capital budgeting** in:
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Comparing machine tools, HVAC systems, and pumps.
- **Civil Engineering:** Bridge vs. tunnel route selection; pavement type selection.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Transformer replacement, renewable vs. conventional generation.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Equipment replacement, automation investment decisions.
- **Public Sector:** Highway project evaluation, water-supply schemes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Whenever a problem states *"compare two machines over their useful lives"*, the examiner is almost always testing **LCM-based PW comparison** for unequal lives. Always state the assumption: *"Each alternative will be replaced with an identical unit at the end of its service life."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Single Payment Present Worth Factor (P/F, i, n)

Starting from the definition of compound interest:

$$F = P \cdot (1 + i)^n$$

We want to find the present equivalent `P` of a future value `F`. Divide both sides by `(1+i)^n`:

$$\frac{F}{(1+i)^n} = P$$

$$\therefore P = F \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)^n} = F \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

**Interpretation:** One rupee receivable after `n` years is worth `\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n}` rupees today.

## 3.2 Derivation — Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (P/A, i, n)

We want to find the present worth of an **end-of-year** uniform series `A` for `n` years. Write out each term:

$$P = \frac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \dots + \frac{A}{(1+i)^n}$$

$$P = A \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+i)^k} = A \cdot \frac{1}{(1+i)} \cdot \frac{1 - \left[ \dfrac{1}{1+i} \right]^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by `(1+i)`:

$$P = A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

$$\therefore P = A \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n} = A \cdot (P/A, i, n)$$

**Interpretation:** `A` rupees received at the end of every year for `n` years is equivalent to receiving a single lump sum `P` today.

## 3.3 Derivation — Arithmetic Gradient Present Worth Factor (P/G, i, n)

In a **gradient series**, cash flows change by a constant amount `G` every year (0, G, 2G, 3G, ..., (n−1)G). The total present worth is:

$$P = 0 \cdot (P/F, i, 1) + G \cdot (P/F, i, 2) + 2G \cdot (P/F, i, 3) + \dots + (n-1)G \cdot (P/F, i, n)$$

Using the standard derivation (algebraic manipulation, KTU textbook approach):

$$P = G \cdot \left[ \frac{(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1}{i^2(1+i)^n} \right]$$

$$\therefore P = G \cdot (P/G, i, n)$$

## 3.4 Derivation — Capitalized Cost (Perpetual Present Worth)

For a public project (bridge, dam, monument) that is assumed to **last forever**, we seek the present worth of an infinite uniform annual series `A` at interest rate `i`:

$$CC = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{A}{(1+i)^k} = A \cdot \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

As `n → ∞`, the term `(1+i)^n` dominates the numerator. The ratio tends to `1/i`:

$$\therefore CC = \frac{A}{i}$$

**Decision Rule:** Select the alternative with the **lowest Capitalized Cost**.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — LCM-Based PW Comparison

> **Problem:** Two machines are being considered.
> - **Machine A:** First cost = **₹50,000**, Annual O&M cost = **₹8,000**, Salvage = **₹5,000**, Life = **5 years**.
> - **Machine B:** First cost = **₹80,000**, Annual O&M cost = **₹4,000**, Salvage = **₹8,000**, Life = **8 years**.
> - **MARR (i) = 10%**.
> - Compare the alternatives using **Present Worth analysis** (assume repeatability).

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Verify service lives.**
- Machine A: 5 years.
- Machine B: 8 years.
- They are **unequal**, so we use the **LCM method**.

$$\text{LCM}(5, 8) = 40 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: State the repeatability assumption.**
> "Both machines will be repeatedly purchased with the same first cost, O&M cost, and salvage value at the end of each service life over a 40-year horizon."

**Step 3: Determine the number of replacements within 40 years.**
- Machine A: 40 / 5 = **8 cycles** (purchased at `t = 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35`).
- Machine B: 40 / 8 = **5 cycles** (purchased at `t = 0, 8, 16, 24, 32`).

**Step 4: Compute the present worth of costs for Machine A over 40 years.**

For a 5-year life with salvage at year 5, and **repeated 8 times**, the present worth is the sum of the PWs of each cycle:

$$PW_A = \left[ P_A + A_A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 5) - SV_A \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 5) \right] \cdot \left[ 1 + (P/F, 10\%, 5) + (P/F, 10\%, 10) + \dots + (P/F, 10\%, 35) \right]$$

Evaluate the inside bracket (cost of one cycle):

$$P_A + A_A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 5) - SV_A \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 5)$$

Look up the factors (or compute):
- $(P/A, 10\%, 5) = \frac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10(1.10)^5} = \frac{1.61051 - 1}{0.10 \times 1.61051} = \frac{0.61051}{0.161051} = 3.7908$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 5) = (1.10)^{-5} = 0.6209$

$$PW_\text{1 cycle, A} = 50{,}000 + 8{,}000(3.7908) - 5{,}000(0.6209)$$

$$= 50{,}000 + 30{,}326.40 - 3{,}104.50 = 77{,}221.90$$

**Step 5: Sum the 8 cycles' present worths using the geometric series of `(P/F, 10%, 5)`.**

The 8 cycles are offset by 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35 years. The factor that brings cycle 2 to `t = 0` is `(P/F, 10%, 5)`, cycle 3 is `(P/F, 10%, 10)`, etc. The sum is a geometric series:

$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{7} (P/F, 10\%, 5k) = \sum_{k=0}^{7} (0.6209)^k$$

Using the geometric series formula: $S = \dfrac{1 - r^8}{1 - r}$, where `r = 0.6209`:

$$S = \frac{1 - (0.6209)^8}{1 - 0.6209} = \frac{1 - 0.02306}{0.3791} = \frac{0.97694}{0.3791} = 2.5770$$

Therefore:

$$PW_A(\text{over 40 yrs}) = 77{,}221.90 \times 2.5770 = 198{,}980.92$$

**Step 6: Compute the present worth of costs for Machine B over 40 years.**

Inside the bracket (one 8-year cycle):
- $(P/A, 10\%, 8) = \frac{(1.10)^8 - 1}{0.10(1.10)^8} = \frac{2.14359 - 1}{0.10 \times 2.14359} = \frac{1.14359}{0.214359} = 5.3349$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 8) = (1.10)^{-8} = 0.4665$

$$PW_\text{1 cycle, B} = 80{,}000 + 4{,}000(5.3349) - 8{,}000(0.4665)$$

$$= 80{,}000 + 21{,}339.60 - 3{,}732.00 = 97{,}607.60$$

**Step 7: Sum the 5 cycles' present worths using the geometric series of `(P/F, 10%, 8)`.**

Let `r = (P/F, 10%, 8) = 0.4665`. The 5 cycles are offset by 0, 8, 16, 24, 32 years:

$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{4} (0.4665)^k = \frac{1 - (0.4665)^5}{1 - 0.4665} = \frac{1 - 0.02207}{0.5335} = \frac{0.97793}{0.5335} = 1.8331$$

Therefore:

$$PW_B(\text{over 40 yrs}) = 97{,}607.60 \times 1.8331 = 178{,}915.07$$

**Step 8: Apply the decision rule.**

| Alternative | Total PW of Costs (₹) |
|---|---|
| Machine A | 1,98,980.92 |
| Machine B | **1,78,915.07** ✅ |

Since both alternatives are **cost-only** (no revenues), select the one with the **lowest PW of costs**.

**Decision:** Select **Machine B**. It saves ₹20,065.85 in present worth over 40 years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent AW Check (for validation):** Convert the cycle PWs to annual worth. The same ranking must be obtained, confirming PW and AW are mathematically equivalent. KTU examiners sometimes accept either method.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash-Flow Diagram — Machine A (One 5-Year Cycle, repeated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["t = 0 | P = 50000"]
    nodeT1["t = 1 | AOM = 8000"]
    nodeT2["t = 2 | AOM = 8000"]
    nodeT3["t = 3 | AOM = 8000"]
    nodeT4["t = 4 | AOM = 8000"]
    nodeT5["t = 5 | AOM 8000 minus SV 5000 = NET 3000"]

    nodeT0 --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5
```

```mermaid
    nodeT4B["t = 4 | AOM = 4000"]
    nodeT5B["t = 5 | AOM = 4000"]
    nodeT6B["t = 6 | AOM = 4000"]
    nodeT7B["t = 7 | AOM = 4000"]
    nodeT8B["t = 8 | AOM 4000 minus SV 8000 = NET minus 4000"]

    nodeT0B --> nodeT1B
    nodeT1B --> nodeT2B
    nodeT2B --> nodeT3B
    nodeT3B --> nodeT4B
    nodeT4B --> nodeT5B

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PW Comparison Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start PW Comparison"]
    stepA1["Identify MARR i percent and all alternatives"]
    stepA2["Verify service lives: equal or unequal"]
    stepA3a{"Equal lives?"}
    stepA3b["Direct PW calculation over the common life n"]
    stepA4a{"Unequal lives?"}
    stepA4b["Apply LCM of lives or stated study period"]
    stepA5["Discount all future cash flows to t = 0 using factor tables"]
    stepA6["Compute NPW for each alternative"]
    stepA7{"Project type?"}
    stepA8a["Cost only: select lowest PW of costs"]
    stepA8b["Revenue generating: select max NPW at MARR"]
    stepA9["Perform incremental analysis if mutually exclusive"]
    endA["Conclusion: Select the chosen alternative"]

    startA --> stepA1 --> stepA2 --> stepA3a
    stepA3a -- YES --> stepA3b --> stepA5
    stepA3a -- NO --> stepA4a
    stepA4a -- YES --> stepA4b --> stepA5
    stepA4a -- NO --> stepA5
    stepA5 --> stepA6 --> stepA7
    stepA7 -- COST --> stepA8a --> endA
    stepA7 -- REVENUE --> stepA8b --> endA
    stepA7 -- BOTH --> stepA8b --> stepA9 --> endA
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — PW Comparison and Its Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA["PW Comparison"]

    rootA --> branch1["Equal service life: Direct NPW"]
    rootA --> branch2["Unequal finite life: LCM Repeatability"]
    rootA --> branch3["Infinite life: Capitalized Cost CC = A divide i"]
    rootA --> branch4["Stated study period: Use n as given"]

    branch1 --> fact1["Factors: P F, P A, P G"]
    branch2 --> fact2["Geometric series of P F factors"]
    branch3 --> fact3["Perpetual annuity assumption"]
    branch4 --> fact4["Project-specific horizon"]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block Architecture — Discounting Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1["Cash Flow Stream"] --> proc1["Identify Type: Lump or Uniform or Gradient"]
    proc1 --> proc2["Apply Discount Factor at MARR i"]
    proc2 --> proc3["Convert all to t = 0 Present Worth"]
    proc3 --> out1["Sum NPW = Benefits minus Costs"]
    out1 --> judge{"NPW greater than or equal to 0?"}
    judge -- YES --> accept["Accept Alternative"]
    judge -- NO --> reject["Reject Alternative"]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define Present Worth (PW). State the decision rule for selecting an alternative using Present Worth Comparison for a cost-only project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Present Worth (PW):** It is the equivalent monetary value, at time zero (present), of all cash flows (revenues and/or costs) occurring over the life of a project, evaluated at the Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR, i%).
>
> **Decision Rule (cost-only project):**
> - Compute the PW of all costs for each alternative over its service life.
> - The alternative with the **least (most negative) PW of costs** is selected, provided it meets the required service output.
> - The PW of costs = First Cost + AOM·(P/A, i, n) − SV·(P/F, i, n).

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for decision rule, 1 mark for formula)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is Capitalized Cost? For a perpetual service project with an annual cost of ₹50,000 and MARR = 10%, compute the Capitalized Cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Capitalized Cost (CC):** It is the present worth of an alternative assumed to provide service **forever (perpetuity)**. It is computed as:
> $$CC = \frac{A}{i}$$
> where `A` is the equivalent uniform annual cost and `i` is the MARR.
>
> **Computation:**
> $$CC = \frac{50{,}000}{0.10} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$
> The Capitalized Cost of the project is **₹5,00,000**.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for final answer)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Two machines are under consideration. The details are given below:**

| Parameter | Machine X | Machine Y |
|---|---|---|
| First Cost (₹) | 1,00,000 | 1,50,000 |
| Annual O&M Cost (₹) | 20,000 | 12,000 |
| Salvage Value (₹) | 10,000 | 15,000 |
| Service Life (years) | 4 | 7 |

MARR = **12% per year**. Using the **Present Worth method** (LCM approach), determine which machine should be selected. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000 and generates an annual net cash inflow of ₹1,20,000 for 10 years. The salvage value at the end of year 10 is ₹50,000. If the MARR is 10%, determine:**
- (i) The Present Worth of the project.
- (ii) Whether the project is **acceptable** using the PW criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Machine Selection (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute LCM of service lives.**

$$\text{LCM}(4, 7) = 28 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the factors at i = 12%** (using standard tables or formulas):

For Machine X (n = 4):
- $(P/A, 12\%, 4) = 3.0373$
- $(P/F, 12\%, 4) = 0.6355$

For Machine Y (n = 7):
- $(P/A, 12\%, 7) = 4.5638$
- $(P/F, 12\%, 7) = 0.5066$

**Step 3: PW of costs for one cycle.**

Machine X:

$$PW_{X,\text{cycle}} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000(3.0373) - 10{,}000(0.6355)$$

$$= 1{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}746 - 6{,}355 = ₹1{,}54{,}391$$

Machine Y:

$$PW_{Y,\text{cycle}} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 12{,}000(4.5638) - 15{,}000(0.5066)$$

$$= 1{,}50{,}000 + 54{,}765.60 - 7{,}599 = ₹1{,}97{,}166.60$$

**Step 4: Compute the geometric series multiplier for repeated cycles.**

For X, repeated over LCM = 28 years: cycles at t = 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24 (i.e., 7 cycles). Geometric series with `r = (P/F, 12%, 4) = 0.6355`:

$$S_X = \frac{1 - (0.6355)^7}{1 - 0.6355} = \frac{1 - 0.04278}{0.3645} = \frac{0.95722}{0.3645} = 2.6260$$

For Y, repeated over LCM = 28 years: cycles at t = 0, 7, 14, 21 (i.e., 4 cycles). Geometric series with `r = (P/F, 12%, 7) = 0.5066`:

$$S_Y = \frac{1 - (0.5066)^4}{1 - 0.5066} = \frac{1 - 0.06588}{0.4934} = \frac{0.93412}{0.4934} = 1.8933$$

**Step 5: Compute total PW of costs over 28 years.**

$$PW_X = 1{,}54{,}391 \times 2.6260 = ₹4{,}05{,}430.77$$

$$PW_Y = 1{,}97{,}166.60 \times 1.8933 = ₹3{,}73{,}327.13$$

**Decision:** Machine Y has the lower PW of costs, so **select Machine Y**, saving ≈ ₹32,103.64 in present worth terms. ✅

*(1 mark for LCM, 1 mark for factors, 2 marks for cycle PWs, 2 marks for repeated series multiplier, 1 mark for decision)*

#### Part (b) — Single Project PW Evaluation (7 marks)

**Given:** P = ₹5,00,000; A = ₹1,20,000; SV = ₹50,000; n = 10 yrs; i = 10%.

**Step 1: Identify the factors.**
- $(P/A, 10\%, 10) = 6.1446$
- $(P/F, 10\%, 10) = 0.3855$

**Step 2: Compute PW of cash inflows.**

$$PW_\text{inflows} = A \cdot (P/A, 10\%, 10) + SV \cdot (P/F, 10\%, 10)$$

$$= 1{,}20{,}000(6.1446) + 50{,}000(0.3855)$$

$$= 7{,}37{,}352 + 19{,}275 = ₹7{,}56{,}627$$

**Step 3: Compute NPW.**

$$NPW = PW_\text{inflows} - P = 7{,}56{,}627 - 5{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}56{,}627$$

**Step 4: Decision rule.**

Since `NPW = ₹2,56,627 > 0` at the MARR of 10%, the project is **acceptable** ✅. It earns a rate of return **greater than 10%** and adds ₹2,56,627 in present-day value.

*(1 mark for factor selection, 2 marks for PW inflows, 1 mark for NPW, 1 mark for decision, 2 marks for correct calculations and units)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Alternate Model 2024]

**(a) A municipal corporation is evaluating two alternative routes for a 30-year highway project:**
- **Route P (Pavement):** Capital cost = ₹80,00,000; Annual maintenance = ₹4,00,000.
- **Route C (Concrete):** Capital cost = ₹1,20,00,000; Annual maintenance = ₹1,50,000.
- MARR = 10%. Using **Capitalized Cost (CC)**, recommend the preferred route. **(7 marks)**

**(b) An engineer is comparing two pumps with the following data at MARR = 15%:**

| Parameter | Pump M1 | Pump M2 |
|---|---|---|
| First Cost (₹) | 60,000 | 90,000 |
| Annual Net Revenue (₹) | 22,000 | 30,000 |
| Salvage Value (₹) | 5,000 | 8,000 |
| Life (years) | 5 | 5 |

Perform an **incremental Present Worth analysis** (M2 − M1) and recommend the better pump. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Capitalized Cost (7 marks)

For a **long-life public project**, we use the **Capitalized Cost** concept (perpetual service).

**Formula:**

$$CC = P + A \cdot \left(\frac{P}{A}, i, n\right) - SV \cdot \left(\frac{P}{F}, i, n\right) \;\; \text{then} \;\; CC_\infty = \frac{A_{eq}}{i}$$

A more direct approach: convert all cash flows to an **equivalent uniform annual cost (EUAC)** for the planned life, then capitalize it.

**Step 1: For Route P (assume n → ∞), EUAC is just the annual cost**, because the structure is long-lived:

- Capital cost ₹80,00,000 → perpetuity equivalent: $\dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{0.10} = ₹8{,}00{,}00{,}000$ (sunk permanent cost).

Since the project is essentially **perpetual**, the total annual cost = Annual maintenance + Equivalent annual capital recovery:

- Equivalent annual capital cost (perpetual) = $\dfrac{P \cdot i}{1} = 80{,}00{,}000$ (treating P as the capitalized cost itself).
- **Route P EUAC = ₹80,00,000 + ₹4,00,000 = ₹84,00,000/year.**

- **Route C EUAC = ₹1,20,00,000 + ₹1,50,000 = ₹1,21,50,000/year.**

Wait — for proper CC analysis, the capital cost is treated as the **lump sum that must be invested to fund perpetuity**. So:

$$CC_P = \text{Capital cost} + \frac{A_\text{maintenance}}{i} = 80{,}00{,}000 + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{0.10} = 80{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}20{,}00{,}000$$

$$CC_C = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{0.10} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = ₹1{,}35{,}00{,}000$$

**Decision:** Route P has the lower Capitalized Cost → **select Route P** ✅.

*(1 mark for CC concept, 2 marks for setup, 2 marks for computation, 1 mark for comparison, 1 mark for decision)*

#### Part (b) — Incremental PW Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1: Order alternatives by increasing first cost.**
- M1 = ₹60,000 (lower cost)
- M2 = ₹90,000 (higher cost)

**Step 2: Compute NPW of the lower-cost alternative (M1) alone** as a check (it must be ≥ 0 to be a viable base).

For M1 (n = 5, i = 15%):
- $(P/A, 15\%, 5) = 3.3522$
- $(P/F, 15\%, 5) = 0.4972$

$$NPW_{M1} = -60{,}000 + 22{,}000(3.3522) + 5{,}000(0.4972)$$

$$= -60{,}000 + 73{,}748.40 + 2{,}486 = ₹16{,}234.40 \;\; (\text{positive} \Rightarrow \text{base is acceptable})$$

**Step 3: Compute incremental cash flows (M2 − M1).**

| Item | Δ (M2 − M1) |
|---|---|
| Δ First Cost | ₹30,000 |
| Δ Annual Net Revenue | ₹8,000 |
| Δ Salvage Value | ₹3,000 |
| Common Life | 5 years |

**Step 4: Compute ΔNPW.**

$$\Delta NPW = -30{,}000 + 8{,}000(3.3522) + 3{,}000(0.4972)$$

$$= -30{,}000 + 26{,}817.60 + 1{,}491.60 = -₹1{,}690.80$$

**Step 5: Decision.**
- $\Delta NPW = -₹1{,}690.80 < 0$
- The **extra investment in M2 is NOT justified** at 15% MARR.
- **Select Pump M1** ✅ (lower-cost, NPW-positive alternative).

*(1 mark for ordering, 2 marks for base NPW, 2 marks for incremental analysis, 1 mark for sign-based decision, 1 mark for final answer)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PW Comparison:**
> 1. **Forgetting the LCM assumption.** If the question says "service life = 5 years" and another = "8 years" and you compare PWs *directly*, the examiner will deduct **2–3 marks** immediately. Always state the LCM and the repeatability assumption.
> 2. **Mixing up end-of-year and beginning-of-year cash flows.** PW formulas use **end-of-period** cash flows. The first cost is at `t = 0`; AOM is at `t = 1, 2, …, n`; salvage is at `t = n`.
> 3. **Sign errors in the salvage term.** Salvage is a **positive inflow** in PW computation: `PW = P + A(P/A) − SV(P/F)` only if `A` is cost. If `A` is net revenue, then the salvage is **added**, not subtracted.
> 4. **Forgetting to use `(P/F)` for the salvage value.** A common student error is to treat salvage as a present value, dropping the discounting factor. This loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the decision rule statement.** Always end with a clear "Select Alternative X because it has the lower PW of costs (or higher NPW) by ₹Y."
> 6. **For incremental analysis, forgetting to first check the base alternative's NPW ≥ 0.** If the base is not economically viable, the increment is meaningless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Present Worth Comparison (Module 4):**
> - **PW** = today's equivalent of all future cash flows at MARR `i`.
> - **NPW** = PW of Benefits − PW of Costs.
> - **Equal life?** → Direct PW comparison.
> - **Unequal life?** → LCM of lives with repeatability, OR Capitalized Cost for perpetuity.
> - **Decision Rule (Cost-only):** Select the alternative with the **least |PW of costs|**.
> - **Decision Rule (Revenue/Service):** Select the alternative with the **maximum NPW ≥ 0**.
> - **Mutually exclusive projects with different first costs:** Always perform **Incremental PW analysis** (B − A) to avoid the "bigger is better" bias.
> - **Factors to memorize:** `(P/F, i, n) = (1+i)^{-n}`, `(P/A, i, n) = [(1+i)^n - 1] / [i(1+i)^n]`, `(P/G, i, n) = [(1+i)^n - i \cdot n - 1] / [i^2(1+i)^n]`.
> - **Capitalized Cost** (perpetual): $CC = A / i$.
> - **Repeatability assumption** allows direct use of geometric series: $S = (1 - r^k) / (1 - r)$, where `r = (P/F, i, n_life)`.
> - **Always state the assumption** (LCM, repeatability, or study period) at the start of your solution.
> - **Always end with a clear decision** stating which alternative is chosen and the PW-based justification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Future Worth (FW) Comparison** is a method of comparing mutually exclusive engineering investment alternatives by converting all cash inflows and outflows of each alternative to a single equivalent value measured at the **end of the analysis horizon** (i.e., at a common future point in time, conventionally at year $n$ of the study period). The alternative with the **maximum Future Worth** (for revenue/benefit-oriented projects) or the **minimum Future Worth of costs** (for cost-only/service projects) is selected as the economically optimal choice.

Mathematically, Future Worth is governed by the time-value-of-money relationship:

$$FW = PW \cdot (1+i)^{n}$$

where $i$ is the effective interest rate per period and $n$ is the number of compounding periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Future Worth Comparison evaluates the equivalent future monetary value of all cash flows (both positive and negative) at a uniform future point in time, typically at the end of each alternative's useful life, using the appropriate **single-payment compound amount factor** $(F/P, i, n)$ and the **uniform series compound amount factor** $(F/A, i, n)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing between two savings schemes offered by two different banks for your daughter's college fund, which she will need 10 years from now:

- **Scheme A**: You invest ₹1,00,000 today, and the bank gives 8% compounded annually.
- **Scheme B**: You invest ₹10,000 every year for 10 years at 7% compounded annually.

You cannot directly compare a lump-sum "today" with a "stream of yearly payments" — they are measured at different times. **Future Worth Comparison** simply asks the magical question: *"If I let all my money grow undisturbed until the END of the 10 years, which scheme will leave my daughter with more money?"*

- Scheme A's ₹1,00,000 grows to ₹2,15,892 at the end of 10 years.
- Scheme B's yearly ₹10,000 deposits accumulate to ₹1,38,164 at the end of 10 years.

The first scheme wins because it generates a **higher Future Worth**. This is exactly how engineers compare machines, bridges, software systems, or factory layouts — by teleporting every rupee/dollar spent or earned into the **same future moment** and then picking the biggest pile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea in One Line:** *Future Worth Comparison is a "time-machine" valuation that brings every cash flow forward to year $n$, so that all alternatives can be weighed on the same scale.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

- **MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** — the benchmark interest rate $i$ used by the firm; default values in KTU problems are typically **$i = 8\%$, $10\%$, $12\%$, or $15\%$**.
- **n (Useful Life / Study Period)** — measured in **years**.
- **Salvage Value (SV)** — the residual value recovered at the end of useful life.
- **Inflation-adjusted real rate:** $i_{real} = \dfrac{1+i_{nom}}{1+f} - 1$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

Future Worth comparison of mutually exclusive alternatives follows a strict 7-step protocol. KTU examiners expect students to list these steps before solving any numerical problem.

1. **Identify the mutually exclusive alternatives** under consideration (e.g., Machine A vs Machine B vs Do-Nothing).
2. **Tabulate all cash flows** for each alternative — first cost ($P$), annual operating cost ($A$), salvage value ($SV$), and any periodic overhaul/replacement costs.
3. **Determine the analysis horizon**:
   - If all lives are **equal** ($n_A = n_B$) → use the common life.
   - If lives are **unequal** → use the **LCM of lives** (repeated-cycle assumption) or an explicit **study period**.
4. **Choose the MARR** ($i$) — given in the problem (commonly **10% to 15%** for KTU problems).
5. **Compute the Future Worth** of each alternative at year $n$ using:
   - The single-payment compound amount factor $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$
   - The uniform series compound amount factor $(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$
   - The sinking-fund factor $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$
6. **Apply the decision rule**:
   - **Revenue alternatives** (benefits > costs): select the alternative with **maximum FW**.
   - **Cost-only / service alternatives** (only costs, no revenue): select the alternative with **minimum FW of costs**.
7. **Perform incremental analysis** if required — compare the incremental future worth $(\Delta FW)_{B-A}$ against zero. If $(\Delta FW)_{B-A} > 0$, the higher-cost alternative $B$ is preferred.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Factor Name | Algebraic Form | Numerical Result at $i=10\%, n=5$ | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------|----------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Single-Payment Compound Amount $(F/P, i, n)$ | $(1+i)^{n}$ | $1.6105$ | Convert any present lump sum to future value |
| 2 | Single-Payment Present Worth $(P/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}$ | $0.6209$ | Convert any future lump sum to present value |
| 3 | Uniform Series Compound Amount $(F/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | $6.1051$ | Convert annual end-of-period deposits to future lump sum |
| 4 | Sinking Fund Factor $(A/F, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $0.1638$ | Find annual deposit to reach a future goal |
| 5 | Capital Recovery $(A/P, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | $0.2638$ | Convert first cost into equivalent annual cost |
| 6 | Uniform Series Present Worth $(P/A, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | $3.7908$ | Convert annuity into present lump sum |
| 7 | Gradient Future Worth $(F/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}}$ | $11.051$ | Convert arithmetic gradient series to future value |
| 8 | Gradient Present Worth $(P/G, i, n)$ | $\dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-i \cdot n - 1}{i^{2}(1+i)^{n}}$ | $6.8618$ | Convert arithmetic gradient to present value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** Cash outflows (costs, purchase price, annual O&M) carry a **negative sign**; cash inflows (revenue, salvage, salvage-like benefits) carry a **positive sign**. KTU examiners are strict about this.

## 2.3 The Composite FW Equation for One Project

For a typical single alternative with first cost $P$, annual net cash flow $A$ (could be +ve revenue or −ve cost), salvage $SV$, and life $n$:

$$FW(n) = -P \cdot (1+i)^{n} + A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} + SV$$

When the alternative produces **only costs** (no revenue), the equation becomes a **Future Worth of Cost**:

$$FW_{cost}(n) = P \cdot (1+i)^{n} + AOC \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} - SV$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The FW method is the most natural choice when the decision-maker is interested in the **terminal financial position** of a project — for example, projecting the closing balance of a power-plant maintenance escrow, an endowment fund, or a depreciation reserve at the end of its depreciation life. It is also the cleanest method when alternatives have **different lives** and the LCM assumption is realistic.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Single-Payment Compound Amount Factor $(F/P, i, n)$

Consider a principal $P$ invested at the end of year 0 at a compound interest rate $i$ per year. We want to find its equivalent value $F$ at the end of year $n$.

**Step 1 — Year 1 Value**

The interest earned in year 1 is $P \cdot i$. The balance at the end of year 1 is principal + interest:

$$F_1 = P + P \cdot i = P(1+i)$$

**Step 2 — Year 2 Value**

The new principal is $F_1$, which again earns interest $i$:

$$F_2 = F_1 + F_1 \cdot i = F_1(1+i) = P(1+i)(1+i) = P(1+i)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Inductive Generalization (for year $k$)**

Assume $F_{k-1} = P(1+i)^{k-1}$. Then at year $k$:

$$F_k = F_{k-1} + F_{k-1} \cdot i = F_{k-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^{k-1}(1+i) = P(1+i)^{k}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result for Year $n$**

Substituting $k = n$:

$$\boxed{F = P(1+i)^{n}}$$

This is the **Future Worth** of a present sum. The factor $(1+i)^{n}$ is the **single-payment compound amount factor**, written in standard engineering economics notation as $(F/P, i, n)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Uniform Series Compound Amount Factor $(F/A, i, n)$

Suppose we deposit an end-of-year amount $A$ for $n$ consecutive years into an account earning $i$ per year, with the first deposit at the end of year 1. Find the accumulated sum $F$ at the end of year $n$.

**Step 1 — Visualize the Cash-Flow Stream**

The last deposit (year $n$) earns **no interest** and contributes $A$ to the total. The second-to-last deposit (year $n-1$) earns interest for **1 year**. The first deposit (year 1) earns interest for **$n-1$ years**.

**Step 2 — Compound Each Deposit Forward to Year $n$**

The $k$-th deposit (made at the end of year $k$) compounds for $(n-k)$ years. Its future value at year $n$ is:

$$F_k = A(1+i)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3 — Sum Over All $n$ Deposits**

$$F = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A(1+i)^{n-k} = A \sum_{j=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^{j}$$

where the substitution $j = n - k$ flips the summation index.

**Step 4 — Apply the Geometric-Series Closed Form**

The sum $\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} r^{j}$ with $r = (1+i)$ is a finite geometric series:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}(1+i)^{j} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{(1+i) - 1} = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}}$$

The bracketed term is the **uniform series compound amount factor** $(F/A, i, n)$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A company must choose between two machines.
> - **Machine X:** First cost = ₹5,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹80,000; Salvage = ₹50,000; Life = 6 years.
> - **Machine Y:** First cost = ₹8,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹50,000; Salvage = ₹1,00,000; Life = 6 years.
> - MARR $i = 10\%$ per year. Use the **Future Worth Comparison** method to recommend the best machine. *(Assume both are cost-only service alternatives with identical lives.)*

**Solution — Step 1: Write the Composite FW Equation**

For a cost-only alternative, future worth of cost is:

$$FW_{cost} = P(1+i)^{n} + AOC \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i} - SV$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute Required Factors at $i = 10\%$, $n = 6$**

$$(1+i)^{n} = (1.10)^{6} = 1.7716$$

$$(F/A, 10\%, 6) = \dfrac{(1.10)^{6} - 1}{0.10} = \dfrac{1.7716 - 1}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.7716}{0.10} = 7.7156$$

**Solution — Step 3: FW of Cost for Machine X**

$$FW_X = 5{,}00{,}000(1.7716) + 80{,}000(7.7156) - 50{,}000$$

$$FW_X = 8{,}85{,}800 + 6{,}17{,}248 - 50{,}000 = 14{,}53{,}048$$

**Solution — Step 4: FW of Cost for Machine Y**

$$FW_Y = 8{,}00{,}000(1.7716) + 50{,}000(7.7156) - 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$FW_Y = 14{,}17{,}280 + 3{,}85{,}780 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 17{,}03{,}060$$

**Solution — Step 5: Decision**

| Machine | FW of Cost (₹) |
|---------|----------------|
| X | **14,53,048** |
| Y | 17,03,060 |

Since $FW_X < FW_Y$ and both are **cost-only** alternatives, **Machine X is preferred** (it has the lower future cost).

**Solution — Step 6: Incremental Verification (Best Practice)**

Let $B - A$ denote Y − X. Incremental first cost = ₹3,00,000 (Y is costlier upfront). Incremental annual saving in O&M = ₹30,000. Incremental salvage = ₹50,000.

$$(\Delta FW)_{Y-X} = -3{,}00{,}000(1.7716) + 30{,}000(7.7156) + 50{,}000$$

$$(\Delta FW)_{Y-X} = -5{,}31{,}480 + 2{,}31{,}468 + 50{,}000 = -2{,}50{,}012$$

Since $(\Delta FW)_{Y-X} < 0$, the extra investment in Y is **not justified** at $i = 10\%$. **Stick with X** — consistent with the FW result above.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The sign of $(\Delta FW)$ should be exactly opposite to that of $(\Delta PW)$ and $(\Delta AW)$ because all three measure the same economic truth, just at different time points.

## 3.4 Handling Unequal Lives — The LCM (Least Common Multiple) Method

When alternatives have different useful lives, the FW method requires that all alternatives be analyzed over a common horizon. The **repeated-cycle assumption** says: an identical replacement is bought at the end of the first cycle, and the analysis continues for $\text{LCM}(n_A, n_B, \dots)$ years.

**Example:** Machine A has $n = 4$ years; Machine B has $n = 6$ years. Their LCM is **12 years**. So we evaluate both alternatives over a 12-year horizon, replacing each machine at the end of its useful life with a replica of itself.

The composite FW equation over LCM years is:

$$FW_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} P_{k}(1+i)^{N - t_{k}} + A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{N} - 1}{i} - \sum_{k=1}^{m} SV_{k}$$

where $N = \text{LCM}$ and $m$ is the total number of purchase cycles. This is the most error-prone part of FW comparison in KTU exams; always draw a **cash-flow timeline** first.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of the FW Comparison Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Engineering Decision]) --> B[Identify mutually exclusive alternatives]
    B --> C[Tabulate first cost, AOC, salvage, life for each]
    C --> D{All lives equal?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use common life n]
    D -- No --> F[Compute LCM of all lives]
    F --> G[Adopt repeated-cycle assumption over LCM]
    E --> H[Choose MARR i from problem]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute FW using F/P and F/A factors at year n]
    I --> J{Alternatives are revenue or cost-only?}
    J -- Revenue --> K[Select alternative with MAX FW]
    J -- Cost-only --> L[Select alternative with MIN FW of cost]
    K --> M[Optional: Incremental FW analysis]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Document final recommendation]
    N --> O([End: Decision Communicated])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Future Worth Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Layer]
        I1[First Cost P]
        I2[Annual Cash Flow A]
        I3[Salvage SV]
        I4[Useful Life n]
        I5[MARR i]
    end

    subgraph Compute[Computational Core]
        C1[Compound Amount Engine: F = P to power 1+i raise to n]
        C2[Annuity Engine: F = A times F by A factor]
        C3[Sinking Fund Engine: A = F times A by F factor]
        C4[Gradient Engine: F = G times F by G factor]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Layer]
        O1[Single FW value at year n]
        O2[Comparative FW ranking across alternatives]
        O3[Incremental FW delta and go no-go flag]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I3 --> C2
    I4 --> C1
    I4 --> C2
    I5 --> C1
    I5 --> C2
    I5 --> C3
    I5 --> C4
    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O1
    C3 --> O1
    C4 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Cash-Flow Timeline Schematic (LCM Method with Two Machines)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Cash-Flow Timeline for FW Comparison (LCM = 12 years, i = 10 percent)
    dateFormat YYYY
    axisFormat Y%y
    section Machine A (n=4 yrs)
    A_Purchase_1    :a1, 2025, 4y
    A_Repl_2        :a2, 2029, 4y
    A_Repl_3        :a3, 2033, 4y
    section Machine B (n=6 yrs)
    B_Purchase_1    :b1, 2025, 6y
    B_Repl_2        :b2, 2031, 6y
    section Common Horizon
    FW_Eval_Year_12 :milestone, 2037, 0d
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents an active life-cycle of a machine. At the **end of year 12** (the LCM), all alternatives are brought to a common future point — this is the precise moment at which their FWs are compared. The milestone marker represents the evaluation point $n = 12$.
