# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Future Worth Comparison. State the two key interest factors used in this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Future Worth Comparison is a method of engineering economic analysis in which all cash flows associated with each mutually exclusive alternative are converted into an equivalent value at a common future point in time (usually at the end of the study period or LCM of useful lives), so that alternatives can be ranked on a uniform time-scale. **(2 marks)**

The two key factors are: **(i)** the single-payment compound amount factor $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$, used to escalate a present lump sum; and **(ii)** the uniform series compound amount factor $(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$, used to escalate a uniform end-of-period series. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**When is the Future Worth method preferred over the Present Worth method? Give two situations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The FW method is preferred in the following situations: **(i)** When the analysis horizon coincides with a **future financial milestone** such as a bond maturity, an endowment payout, or a sinking-fund target, and the decision-maker wants to know the **terminal balance**. **(2 marks)** **(ii)** When the alternatives have **different useful lives** and the analyst wishes to use the LCM-of-lives repeated-cycle assumption, since the FW of each replacement can simply be summed at the LCM year without re-discounting back to year 0. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, the student answers **ONE** of two alternatives. Both alternatives below are presented in full.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A manufacturing firm is evaluating two conveyor systems:

- **System P:** First cost = ₹3,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹40,000; Salvage = ₹30,000; Useful life = **5 years**.
- **System Q:** First cost = ₹5,00,000; Annual operating cost = ₹25,000; Salvage = ₹60,000; Useful life = **5 years**.

MARR is **12%** per year. **(a)** Compute the Future Worth of cost for both systems. **(b)** Which system should be selected? Justify using incremental FW analysis.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Future Worth of Cost (7 marks)

**Required factors at $i = 12\%$, $n = 5$:**

$$(1.12)^{5} = 1.7623$$

$$(F/A, 12\%, 5) = \dfrac{(1.12)^{5} - 1}{0.12} = \dfrac{1.7623 - 1}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.7623}{0.12} = 6.3528$$

**FW of Cost for System P:**

$$FW_P = 3{,}00{,}000(1.7623) + 40{,}000(6.3528) - 30{,}000$$

$$FW_P = 5{,}28{,}690 + 2{,}54{,}112 - 30{,}000 = 7{,}52{,}802$$

**FW of Cost for System Q:**

$$FW_Q = 5{,}00{,}000(1.7623) + 25{,}000(6.3528) - 60{,}000$$

$$FW_Q = 8{,}81{,}150 + 1{,}58{,}820 - 60{,}000 = 9{,}79{,}970$$

| System | FW of Cost (₹) |
|--------|----------------|
| P | **7,52,802** |
| Q | 9,79,970 |

#### Part (b) — Selection & Incremental Verification (7 marks)

Since $FW_P < FW_Q$, **System P is preferred** on a pure cost-minimization basis. **(2 marks)**

**Incremental Analysis (Q − P):**

$$\Delta P = 5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ (extra investment in Q)}$$

$$\Delta AOC = 40{,}000 - 25{,}000 = 15{,}000 \text{ (annual saving with Q)}$$

$$\Delta SV = 60{,}000 - 30{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ (extra salvage with Q)}$$

$$(\Delta FW)_{Q-P} = -2{,}00{,}000(1.7623) + 15{,}000(6.3528) + 30{,}000$$

$$(\Delta FW)_{Q-P} = -3{,}52{,}460 + 95{,}292 + 30{,}000 = -2{,}27{,}168$$

Since $(\Delta FW)_{Q-P} = -2{,}27{,}168 < 0$, the **incremental investment in Q is not justified** at 12% MARR. **Recommend System P.** **(3 marks)**

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The firm should purchase System P. The extra ₹2,00,000 invested in System Q fails to earn the minimum 12% return, and the firm ends up ₹2,27,168 worse off in future-value terms at the end of year 5.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A civil engineering company must choose between two earth-moving machines:

- **Machine M1:** First cost = ₹10,00,000; Annual revenue = ₹4,00,000; Annual expense = ₹1,50,000; Salvage = ₹2,00,000; Life = **4 years**.
- **Machine M2:** First cost = ₹15,00,000; Annual revenue = ₹6,00,000; Annual expense = ₹1,80,000; Salvage = ₹3,50,000; Life = **6 years**.

MARR = **10%** per year. **(a)** Using the LCM method, compute the Future Worth of each alternative. **(b)** Recommend the best alternative with a clear economic justification.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — FW Computation using LCM = 12 years (7 marks)

**Required factors at $i = 10\%$:**

| Factor | Value |
|--------|-------|
| $(1.10)^{4}$ | 1.4641 |
| $(1.10)^{6}$ | 1.7716 |
| $(1.10)^{12}$ | 3.1384 |
| $(F/A, 10\%, 4)$ | 4.6410 |
| $(F/A, 10\%, 6)$ | 7.7156 |
| $(F/A, 10\%, 12)$ | 21.3843 |

**Net Annual Cash Flow:**
- For M1: $A_{net} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}50{,}000$
- For M2: $A_{net} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000 = 4{,}20{,}000$

**Cycle Structure (LCM = 12 years):**
- M1 is replaced **3 times** (at $n = 0, 4, 8$); salvage occurs at $n = 4, 8, 12$.
- M2 is replaced **2 times** (at $n = 0, 6$); salvage occurs at $n = 6, 12$.

**FW of M1 at $n = 12$:**

First costs escalated to year 12:

$$10{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{12} + 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{8} + 10{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{4}$$

$$= 10{,}00{,}000(3.1384) + 10{,}00{,}000(2.1436) + 10{,}00{,}000(1.4641)$$

$$= 31{,}38{,}428 + 21{,}43{,}588 + 14{,}64{,}100 = 67{,}46{,}116$$

Net annual cash flow compounded:

$$2{,}50{,}000 \cdot (F/A, 10\%, 12) = 2{,}50{,}000 \cdot 21.3843 = 53{,}46{,}075$$

Salvage inflows at years 4, 8, 12:

$$2{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{8} + 2{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{4} + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$= 4{,}28{,}717 + 2{,}92{,}820 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}21{,}537$$

**Total FW of M1:**

$$FW_{M1} = -67{,}46{,}116 + 53{,}46{,}075 + 9{,}21{,}537 = -4{,}78{,}504$$

The negative sign indicates that M1's outflows exceed its inflows in future-value terms. **(2 marks)**

**FW of M2 at $n = 12$:**

First costs escalated to year 12:

$$15{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{12} + 15{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{6}$$

$$= 15{,}00{,}000(3.1384) + 15{,}00{,}000(1.7716) = 47{,}07{,}643 + 26{,}57{,}400 = 73{,}65{,}043$$

Net annual cash flow compounded:

$$4{,}20{,}000 \cdot (F/A, 10\%, 12) = 4{,}20{,}000 \cdot 21.3843 = 89{,}81{,}406$$

Salvage inflows at years 6 and 12:

$$3{,}50{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{6} + 3{,}50{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000(1.7716) + 3{,}50{,}000$$

$$= 6{,}20{,}060 + 3{,}50{,}000 = 9{,}70{,}060$$

**Total FW of M2:**

$$FW_{M2} = -73{,}65{,}043 + 89{,}81{,}406 + 9{,}70{,}060 = 25{,}86{,}423$$

**(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Decision (7 marks)

| Machine | FW at $n = 12$ (₹) |
|---------|--------------------|
| M1 | -4,78,504 (loss in future terms) |
| M2 | **+25,86,423** (gain in future terms) |

Since these are **revenue alternatives** (they generate positive net annual cash flow), we select the alternative with the **maximum FW**. **Machine M2 is recommended.** **(3 marks)**

**Economic Justification (4 marks):**
- M1 actually **erodes value** over the 12-year horizon (its FW is negative).
- M2 generates a future surplus of ₹25,86,423 over the LCM horizon.
- M2's higher first cost is fully recovered through superior annual net cash flow, and the surplus grows at the MARR of 10% per year.
- Even after accounting for two replacement cycles, M2 remains economically superior at the assumed MARR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors** — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks if students forget to treat first cost as a negative number. Always begin with `-P(1+i)^n`.
> 2. **LCM Mis-Calculation** — When lives are 4 and 6, LCM is 12 (not 10!). Many students wrongly add or take the maximum.
> 3. **Forgetting Intermediate Salvages** — In the LCM method, salvage must be added at **every replacement year**, not just the final LCM year. Missing intermediate salvages can flip the answer.
> 4. **Revenue vs Cost-Only Mix-up** — If the problem gives *both* annual revenue *and* annual expense, compute **net** cash flow first. Do not compare total revenue.
> 5. **Inconsistent Factor Lookup** — Use the same $n$ for both $(F/P)$ and $(F/A)$. Mixing $n = 5$ for one factor and $n = 6$ for another is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **No Incremental Verification** — KTU Part-B problems carrying 7+ marks almost always expect an **incremental analysis** to be shown in addition to the direct ranking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (high-priority):** Future Worth Comparison brings all cash flows forward to a **common future point** (usually year $n$ or LCM of lives).
- **Two Master Factors:** $(F/P, i, n) = (1+i)^{n}$ and $(F/A, i, n) = \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$. Memorize these formulas — KTU does not allow factor tables in most papers.
- **Composite FW equation for one project:** $FW = -P(F/P, i, n) + A(F/A, i, n) + SV$.
- **Decision Rules:**
  - Revenue alternatives → **maximum** FW wins.
  - Cost-only alternatives → **minimum** FW of cost wins.
- **Unequal lives → LCM of lives**, with the **repeated-cycle assumption** and salvage recognized at **every** replacement year.
- **Incremental analysis** confirms or refutes the basic ranking and is almost always expected for full marks.
- **Sign convention:** Outflows negative, inflows positive — strictly followed.
- **MARR is given** in the problem; do not invent one.
- **Mermaid timeline diagrams** and well-labeled cash-flow years are worth partial marks in KTU valuation — always draw a timeline before computing.
- **Equivalent forms:** FW, PW, and AW methods must give the **same decision** if applied correctly. If they disagree, recheck the math.
- **Common KTU interest rates:** $i = 8\%, 10\%, 12\%, 15\%$. Be fluent in computing $(1+i)^{n}$ for these values.
- **Avoid using** the FW method if the project has a very long horizon and the MARR is uncertain — small errors in $i$ get magnified by $(1+i)^{n}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annual Cost and Worth Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET727 - FINANCE FOR ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Annual Cost and Worth Comparison # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Engineering Economics**, the **Annual Cost (AC)** method — also called the **Equivalent Annual Cost (EAC)** or **Annual Equivalent Method** — is a technique used to evaluate mutually exclusive alternatives by converting all cash flows (initial investment, operating costs, salvage values, etc.) into a **uniform series of equal end-of-year amounts** over the life of each alternative. The **Annual Worth (AW)** is the uniform annual net benefit when revenues are involved, mathematically expressed as: $$\text{AW} = \text{Annual Benefits} - \text{Annual Equivalent Cost}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):** > *Annual Cost and Worth Comparison* refers to the systematic procedure of comparing engineering project alternatives by transforming all present and future cash flows into an equivalent uniform annual amount using the appropriate **Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n)** or **Sinking Fund Factor (A/F, i, n)**, and selecting the alternative with the **minimum annual cost** (for cost-only projects) or **maximum annual worth** (for revenue-generating projects) at the **Minimum Attractive Rate of Return (MARR)**. ## 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Minimum Attractive Rate of Return | **MARR (i)** | Lowest acceptable interest rate (cost of capital) | | Present Worth | **PW** | Equivalent value of all cash flows at time 0 | | Annual Worth | **AW** | Equivalent uniform annual cash flow over project life | | Future Worth | **FW** | Equivalent value of all cash flows at the end of project life | | Capitalized Cost | **CC** | PW of a perpetual series of annual costs | | Net Annual Worth | **NAW** | Annual revenue minus annual cost | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "EMI Analogy" Imagine you take two home loans for the same property. Loan A demands a huge down payment (₹20 lakh) with low monthly running cost. Loan B requires a smaller down payment (₹10 lakh) but higher monthly EMI for maintenance/insurance. To compare them fairly, the bank converts **both into equal monthly installments (EMIs)** over the same loan tenure. > The **Annual Cost method works exactly like this EMI comparison** — it asks: *"If I were to pay (or receive) the same amount every year for the life of the project, what would that uniform amount be?"* This makes heterogeneous cash flows directly comparable. ## 1.4 Why Use Annual Cost Instead of Present Worth? > [!NOTE] > 1. **Unequal Lives Handled Easily** — When alternatives have different useful lives, AW normalizes the comparison over a common period. > 2. **Intuitive Interpretation** — Decision-makers understand "₹/year" better than a one-time PW figure. > 3. **Direct Suitability** — Taxes, insurance, and operating costs are inherently annual. > 4. **Mathematical Equivalence** — AW and PW decisions always yield identical rankings because they are multiplied by a strictly positive factor (A/P, i, n). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Fundamental Time-Value Factors All annual cost/worth calculations depend on the six standard interest factors. Every factor is derived from the **Single Payment Compound Amount** (F/P, i, n) = (1+i)ⁿ. Let **i** = interest rate per period, **n** = number of periods, **P** = present value, **F** = future value, **A** = uniform annual amount. ### Factor Derivation Tree - **Given P, Find A** → Capital Recovery Factor $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ - **Given A, Find P** → Uniform Series Present Worth Factor $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ - **Given F, Find A** → Sinking Fund Factor $A = F \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ - **Given A, Find F** → Uniform Series Future Worth Factor $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}$ - **Given P, Find F** → Single Payment Compound Amount $F = P(1+i)^n$ - **Given F, Find P** → Single Payment Present Worth $P = \dfrac{F}{(1+i)^n}$ ## 2.2 Core Annual Worth Formulas > [!IMPORTANT] > The **master conversion equation** is the bridge between PW, FW, and AW: > $$\text{AW} = \text{PW} \cdot (A/P, i, n) = \text{FW} \cdot (A/F, i, n)$$ ### Standard AW Expression for a Project $$\text{AW} = -P(A/P, i, n) - \text{Annual Operating Cost} + S(A/F, i, n) + \text{Annual Revenue}$$ Where: - $P$ = First cost (Capital investment) - $S$ = Salvage value at end of life - $i$ = MARR (expressed as decimal) - $n$ = Project life in years ## 2.3 Capitalized Cost (Infinite-Life Annual Cost) When an alternative has a very long life (perpetuity-like, e.g., a bridge, dam, or monument), the **Capitalized Cost (CC)** is used, which is the PW when n → ∞. The equivalent **perpetual annual cost** is: $$\text{CC} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Cost}}{i} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{Annual Cost} = i \cdot \text{CC}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > This is the **most-tested formula set** in KTU Module 4. Memorize the table below and the sign convention: **Costs = negative, Revenues = positive.** | # | Conversion | Formula | Standard Notation | Sign Convention | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | PW → AW | $AW = PW \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $PW \cdot (A/P, i, n)$ | Multiply by positive factor | | 2 | FW → AW | $AW = FW \cdot \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | $FW \cdot (A/F, i, n)$ | Multiply by positive factor | | 3 | AW → PW | $PW = AW \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | $AW \cdot (P/A, i, n)$ | Multiply by positive factor | | 4 | Capitalized Cost | $CC = \dfrac{AW}{i}$ | Perpetuity AW | For infinite-life assets | | 5 | AW of first cost | $-P \cdot (A/P, i, n)$ | Negative (cost) | Use $A/P$ factor | | 6 | AW of salvage | $+S \cdot (A/F, i, n)$ | Positive (benefit) | Use $A/F$ factor | | 7 | Decision rule (cost) | Choose **min** AW | $\text{AW}_{\min}$ | For service alternatives | | 8 | Decision rule (revenue) | Choose **max** AW | $\text{AW}_{\max}$ | For revenue projects | | 9 | Incremental rule | $\Delta AW_{B-A} > 0$ | B preferred over A | For ranked alternatives | | 10 | AW with gradient | $AW_G = G \cdot \dfrac{1}{i} - \dfrac{nG}{i(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Arithmetic gradient | Escalating O&M | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields > [!TIP] > - **Civil Engineering** → Comparing bridge designs, pavement options, water-supply schemes. > - **Mechanical Engineering** → Selecting between two machine tools with different purchase prices and maintenance costs. > - **Electrical Engineering** → Evaluating transformer alternatives (purchase vs. operate). > - **Industrial Engineering** → Equipment replacement analysis (Defender vs. Challenger). > - **Public Sector** → Infrastructure projects evaluated over infinite life (CC method). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n) We want to find an equivalent uniform series **A** such that the sum of its present worth equals a single payment **P** at the end of **n** periods at interest rate **i**. ### Step 1 — Set up the equivalence equation The PW of a uniform series A occurring at the end of each year for n years must equal P: $$P = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^1} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^2} + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^3} + \cdots + \dfrac{A}{(1+i)^n}$$ ### Step 2 — Identify as a finite geometric progression The right side is a geometric series with first term $a = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)}$, common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}$, and number of terms $= n$. ### Step 3 — Apply the geometric sum formula For a geometric series: $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$ Substituting our terms: $$P = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left[\dfrac{1}{(1+i)}\right]^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)}}$$ ### Step 4 — Simplify the numerator and denominator The numerator becomes: $$1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^n} = \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i)^n}$$ The denominator simplifies to: $$1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)} = \dfrac{(1+i) - 1}{(1+i)} = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)}$$ ### Step 5 — Combine the expressions $$P = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i)^n}}{\dfrac{i}{(1+i)}}$$ $$P = \dfrac{A}{(1+i)} \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{(1+i)^n} \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)}{i}$$ The (1+i) terms cancel: $$P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$ ### Step 6 — Invert to get A in terms of P $$A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$ This is the **Capital Recovery Factor (A/P, i, n)** — the most-used factor in annual cost comparison. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The numerator $i(1+i)^n$ represents the "interest + principal repayment" portion, while the denominator $(1+i)^n - 1$ represents the cumulative present value of the recovery series. The factor grows with both **i** and **n**, meaning higher interest rates or longer lives increase the annualized cost of any capital investment. ## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU-Style Problem > **[Worked Example — Annual Cost Comparison of Two Machines]** > A manufacturing firm must choose between two machines for a new production line. The data are: | Parameter | Machine X | Machine Y | |---|---|---| | First Cost (P) | ₹ 2,00,000 | ₹ 3,50,000 | | Annual Operating Cost | ₹ 30,000 | ₹ 18,000 | | Salvage Value (S) | ₹ 20,000 | ₹ 50,000 | | Useful Life (n) | 5 years | 5 years | | MARR (i) | 10% | 10% | **Task:** Determine the economically superior machine using the **Annual Worth method**. ### Step 1 — Extract the standard interest factors at i = 10%, n = 5 Compute $(1+i)^n = (1.10)^5$: $$(1.10)^5 = 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 = 1.61051$$ Capital Recovery Factor: $$(A/P, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.10 \times 1.61051}{1.61051 - 1} = \dfrac{0.161051}{0.61051} = 0.26380$$ Sinking Fund Factor: $$(A/F, 10\%, 5) = \dfrac{0.10}{0.61051} = 0.16380$$ ### Step 2 — Compute Annual Worth of Machine X Using the master formula $AW = -P(A/P, i, n) - AOC + S(A/F, i, n)$: $$AW_X = -2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.26380 - 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 \times 0.16380$$ $$AW_X = -52{,}760 - 30{,}000 + 3{,}276$$ $$AW_X = -79{,}484 \text{ per year}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Annual Worth of Machine Y $$AW_Y = -3{,}50{,}000 \times 0.26380 - 18{,}000 + 50{,}000 \times 0.16380$$ $$AW_Y = -92{,}330 - 18{,}000 + 8{,}190$$ $$AW_Y = -1{,}02{,}140 \text{ per year}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Decision Rule Both AWs are negative (cost-only project). The rule is to select the **algebraically greater (less negative) AW**. $$\text{Since } AW_X = -79{,}484 > AW_Y = -1{,}02{,}140$$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** **Machine X is the better choice** because it incurs a lower equivalent annual cost of **₹ 79,484/year** versus **₹ 1,02,140/year** for Machine Y — a **saving of ₹ 22,656 per year** over the 5-year horizon. ### Step 5 — Verification using Present Worth Method For a sanity check, we convert AWs back to PW using $(P/A, 10\%, 5) = 1/0.26380 = 3.79079$. $$PW_X = -79{,}484 \times 3.79079 = -3{,}01{,}313$$ $$PW_Y = -1{,}02{,}140 \times 3.79079 = -3{,}86{,}967$$ Since $PW_X > PW_Y$ (less negative), the ranking matches the AW result — confirming internal consistency of the methods. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick Reference for KTU Valuation:** > Always explicitly state: (1) the formula used, (2) the factor value (with table reference), (3) the substituted numerical expression, and (4) the final computed value. Examiners award partial marks for each. | Step | Action | Marks Weightage | |---|---|---| | 1 | Identify cash flow components | 1 mark | | 2 | State the appropriate AW formula | 1 mark | | 3 | Compute/cite the interest factor value | 2 marks | | 4 | Substitute and compute the final AW | 2 marks | | 5 | State the decision rule applied | 1 mark | | **Total** | | **7 marks (Part-a)** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow — Annual Worth Comparison Procedure The following Mermaid diagram captures the **algorithmic decision flow** for solving any "Annual Cost and Worth Comparison" problem as required in KTU examinations. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([START: Problem Statement]) --> idA1[Identify all cash flows: P, AOC, S, Revenue] idA1 --> idA2[Determine MARR i and project life n] idA2 --> idA3{Is it a cost-only or revenue project?} idA3 -- Cost-only --> idA4[Use AW = -P(A/P,i,n) - AOC + S(A/F,i,n)] idA3 -- Revenue --> idA5[Use AW = -P(A/P,i,n) - AOC + S(A/F,i,n) + Annual Revenue] idA4 --> idA6[Compute factor (A/P, i, n) and (A/F, i, n) from tables] idA5 --> idA6 idA6 --> idA7[Substitute values and compute AW for each alternative] idA7 --> idA8[Compare AW values] idA8 -- Min AW for cost --> idA9[Select minimum AW as best alternative] idA8 -- Max AW for revenue --> idA10[Select maximum AW as best alternative] idA9 --> idA11[Verify using PW or FW method for consistency] idA10 --> idA11 idA11 --> idEnd([END: Recommendation Report]) ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Relationship Among PW, AW, and FW ```mermaid flowchart LR pwBlock[PW: Present Worth
at time t = 0] -- multiply by A/P,i,n --> awBlock[AW: Annual Worth
uniform over n years] awBlock -- multiply by P/A,i,n --> pwBlock awBlock -- multiply by F/A,i,n --> fwBlock[FW: Future Worth
at time t = n] fwBlock -- multiply by A/F,i,n --> awBlock pwBlock -- multiply by F/P,i,n --> fwBlock fwBlock -- multiply by P/F,i,n --> pwBlock pwBlock -. conversion via CC = AW / i .-> ccBlock[CC: Capitalized Cost
for perpetual assets] awBlock -. divide by i .-> ccBlock ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture — Decision Hierarchy in Alternative Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD rootQ{Alternatives under evaluation} rootQ --> q1{Do lives differ?} q1 -- No, equal lives --> r1[Direct AW comparison] q1 -- Yes, unequal lives --> r2[Use LCM of lives OR repeat assumption] r1 --> d1[Compute AW for each] r2 --> d1 d1 --> d2{Mutually exclusive?} d2 -- Yes --> d3[Pick min AW for cost OR max AW for revenue] d2 -- No, independent --> d4[Apply incremental AW analysis] d3 --> finalQ([Final Selection]) d4 --> finalQ ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is meant by Annual Worth (AW) method of comparing alternatives? When is it preferred over the Present Worth method?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Annual Worth method evaluates engineering alternatives by converting all cash flows (initial investment, operating costs, salvage, revenues) into a **uniform equivalent annual amount** over the project life using the factors $(A/P, i, n)$ and $(A/F, i, n)$. It is preferred over the PW method when: 1. Alternatives have **unequal useful lives**. 2. The decision-maker needs an intuitive **"cost per year"** interpretation. 3. Operating costs and revenues are inherently annual in nature. 4. The results must be communicated to **non-financial managers**. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the Capitalized Cost method. Give the formula and one engineering application.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** Capitalized Cost (CC) is the present worth of an alternative when its life is considered to be **perpetual (infinite)**, which equals the uniform annual cost divided by the interest rate. $$CC = \dfrac{AW}{i}$$ **Application:** Used for **public infrastructure** such as bridges, dams, monuments, and railway tracks whose service lives are practically infinite. The selection rule is to choose the alternative with the **minimum capitalized cost**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students must answer **one full question (a + b = 14 marks)** from a choice of two. Both alternatives are provided below for practice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cost-Only Alternative Comparison** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** Two pumps are being considered for a water-supply scheme. The relevant data are: | Parameter | Pump A | Pump B | |---|---|---| | First Cost (P) | ₹ 5,00,000 | ₹ 7,00,000 | | Annual Operating Cost | ₹ 60,000 | ₹ 35,000 | | Salvage Value | ₹ 50,000 | ₹ 80,000 | | Life | 6 years | 9 years | | MARR | 12% | 12% | **(a)** Compute the Annual Worth of each pump. (7 marks) **(b)** Which pump should be selected? Justify using the AW criterion. Also verify using the PW method over the **least common multiple (LCM) of lives**. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Required factors at i = 12%:** For Pump A (n = 6): $$(1.12)^6 = 1.97382$$ $$(A/P, 12\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1.97382}{0.97382} = 0.24323$$ $$(A/F, 12\%, 6) = \dfrac{0.12}{0.97382} = 0.12323$$ For Pump B (n = 9): $$(1.12)^9 = 2.77308$$ $$(A/P, 12\%, 9) = \dfrac{0.12 \times 2.77308}{1.77308} = 0.18768$$ $$(A/F, 12\%, 9) = \dfrac{0.12}{1.77308} = 0.06768$$ **Step 2 — Compute AW of Pump A:** $$AW_A = -5{,}00{,}000(0.24323) - 60{,}000 + 50{,}000(0.12323)$$ $$AW_A = -1{,}21{,}615 - 60{,}000 + 6{,}162$$ $$\boxed{AW_A = -1{,}75{,}453 \text{ per year}}$$ **Step 3 — Compute AW of Pump B:** $$AW_B = -7{,}00{,}000(0.18768) - 35{,}000 + 80{,}000(0.06768)$$ $$AW_B = -1{,}31{,}376 - 35{,}000 + 5{,}414$$ $$\boxed{AW_B = -1{,}60{,}962 \text{ per year}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Decision:** Since both are cost-only projects, select the alternative with the **less negative (algebraically greater) AW**. $$AW_B = -1{,}60{,}962 > AW_A = -1{,}75{,}453$$ **Pump B is selected** because it has a lower equivalent annual cost — saving ₹ 14,491 per year compared to Pump A. **Verification using PW method over LCM of lives:** LCM(6, 9) = 18 years. Repeat Pump A three times and Pump B two times. $$PW_A = -1{,}75{,}453 \times (P/A, 12\%, 18)$$ $$(P/A, 12\%, 18) = \dfrac{(1.12)^{18} - 1}{0.12(1.12)^{18}} = \dfrac{6.5438}{0.7853} = 7.8197$$ $$PW_A = -1{,}75{,}453 \times 7.8197 = -13{,}71{,}626$$ $$PW_B = -1{,}60{,}962 \times 7.8197 = -12{,}58{,}736$$ Since $PW_B > PW_A$, the recommendation matches — **Pump B is confirmed superior**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Revenue Project with Capitalized Cost** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** A municipal corporation is evaluating two water-treatment plants with the following data: | Parameter | Plant X | Plant Y | |---|---|---| | Initial Investment | ₹ 80,00,000 | ₹ 1,20,00,000 | | Annual Revenue | ₹ 12,00,000 | ₹ 16,00,000 | | Annual O&M Cost | ₹ 4,00,000 | ₹ 3,50,000 | | Life | 15 years | 20 years | | MARR | 10% | 10% | **(a)** Compute the Annual Worth of each plant. (7 marks) **(b)** Which plant should be selected? If both plants are to be retained for **perpetual use**, recompute the decision using the **Capitalized Cost** method. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Factors at i = 10%:** For Plant X (n = 15): $(A/P, 10\%, 15) = 0.13147$ For Plant Y (n = 20): $(A/P, 10\%, 20) = 0.11746$ **Step 2 — AW of Plant X:** $$AW_X = -80{,}00{,}000(0.13147) + 12{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000$$ $$AW_X = -10{,}51{,}760 + 8{,}00{,}000$$ $$\boxed{AW_X = -2{,}51{,}760 \text{ per year}}$$ **Step 3 — AW of Plant Y:** $$AW_Y = -1{,}20{,}00{,}000(0.11746) + 16{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}50{,}000$$ $$AW_Y = -14{,}09{,}520 + 12{,}50{,}000$$ $$\boxed{AW_Y = -1{,}59{,}520 \text{ per year}}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Finite-life decision:** Both AWs are negative; select the one with **less negative (higher) AW**. $$AW_Y = -1{,}59{,}520 > AW_X = -2{,}51{,}760$$ **Plant Y is selected**, with a lower annual cost deficit of ₹ 1,59,520/year. **Perpetual-life (Capitalized Cost) decision:** Net annual cost of Plant X = |AW_X| = ₹ 2,51,760/year Net annual cost of Plant Y = |AW_Y| = ₹ 1,59,520/year $$CC_X = \dfrac{2{,}51{,}760}{0.10} = ₹ 25{,}17{,}600$$ $$CC_Y = \dfrac{1{,}59{,}520}{0.10} = ₹ 15{,}95{,}200$$ **Decision:** $CC_Y < CC_X$, so **Plant Y remains the preferred choice** under perpetual use — saving ₹ 9,22,400 in capitalized cost. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Valuation:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors** — Forgetting the negative sign on first cost when subtracting $P(A/P, i, n)$ from annual revenue. This alone can cost **3 marks**. > 2. **Wrong Factor Selection** — Using $(P/A, i, n)$ when the question asks for AW. Always check: *"Do I want annual equivalent or present equivalent?"* > 3. **Unequal Lives Ignored** — When lives differ, students directly compare AWs **without justification**. Always state the assumption (repeat assumption or LCM). > 4. **Capitalized Cost Misuse** — Applying CC method to finite-life projects (e.g., a 10-year machine). CC is only valid for **perpetual or near-perpetual** assets. > 5. **No Unit Consistency** — Mixing percentage MARR (e.g., 10%) with decimal factor (0.10). Always use the **decimal form** in formulas. > 6. **Decision Rule Reversal** — Selecting the alternative with the **most negative AW** (smallest) instead of the **algebraically largest** (least negative). The rule is *not* "minimum" in absolute value — it is "maximum" algebraically for cost-only projects. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.** - ☐ **Annual Worth (AW)** = uniform annual equivalent of all cash flows at MARR. - ☐ **Master formula:** $AW = -P(A/P, i, n) - AOC + S(A/F, i, n) + R$ where $R$ = annual revenue. - ☐ **Capital Recovery Factor:** $(A/P, i, n) = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ - ☐ **Sinking Fund Factor:** $(A/F, i, n) = \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ - ☐ **Capitalized Cost (CC):** $CC = \dfrac{AW}{i}$ — used for perpetual assets only. - ☐ **Cost-only decision rule:** Select alternative with **maximum (least negative) AW**. - ☐ **Revenue project rule:** Select alternative with **maximum positive AW**. - ☐ **AW and PW are mathematically equivalent** — rankings are identical; only the magnitude differs. - ☐ **Unequal lives:** Use the **LCM of lives** method OR **repeat assumption** (study recommends repeat assumption only if technology remains stable). - ☐ **Gradient series:** $AW_G = G/i - nG/[(1+i)^n - 1]$ for arithmetic escalation in O&M. - ☐ **Always verify your answer** by cross-checking using a second method (PW or FW). - ☐ **Sign discipline:** Costs (outflows) = negative; Revenues/savings (inflows) = positive.
